High‑Level Answer

The new hybrid‑mode fix did work in the sense that Amazon analysis now runs (you can see full Amazon extraction in the first log), but it exposed a resume+chunking indexing bug.
On the resumed run, hybrid mode is enabled with chunk_size_categories=1 and persistent_category_index=2; the way _run_hybrid_processing_mode calls _extract_supplier_products means every chunk sees “0 categories from cursor”, so no new supplier products (and therefore no Amazon work) are scheduled for categories 2–326.
That’s why, after your first partially completed run, the second run appears to “skip all categories” and then finishes very quickly.

Removed the “RESUME GUARD” that disabled hybrid mode on resume, and replaced:
# Check hybrid processing configuration (from full config, not system section)
hybrid_config = full_config.get("hybrid_processing", {})
#  Resume context detection (for logging only - does NOT block hybrid mode)
sp_h = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {}) or {}

    self.log.info(f" HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled (resume_context={is_resuming})")
    return await self._run_hybrid_processing_mode(...)

So hybrid mode is now always used whenever hybrid_processing.enabled is true, including on resume.

HYBRID MODE: Chunked processing (chunk size: 1 categories)

The hybrid supplier path runs for category 1 as expected; you see:

Later, rich Amazon scraping logs (EAN search, sponsored filtering, title fallback, Keepa, etc.), e.g.:
Attempting Amazon search using EAN: 5023797209406
Processing 6 search result elements...
Extracting all product data for ASIN: B07RYXJZ3J
Mid‑run resume pointer (still category 1, Amazon phase):
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=1/326 url=... prod_idx=31/53
Termination:
There is no Workflow completed successfully in this log.
The log ends with:
Keeping browser persistent for next run - not calling close()
A cleanup warning about “no running event loop”.
Interpretation:

The first run successfully:
Initialised hybrid mode correctly.
Ran supplier extraction for category 1.
Entered and executed Amazon analysis for category 1.
It was interrupted (or stalled and then stopped) while still in Amazon analysis for category 1, not after completing the category or moving to category 2.
Run 2 (03:35 log) – Behaviour

File:
C:\Users\chris\Desktop\Amazon-FBA-Agent-System-v32 - latest good - Copy (8) - Copy - Copy - POSTLONGRUNPREKIRO2 beforecompletion-\logs\debug\run_custom_poundwholesale_20251130_033535.log

Same supplier: angelwholesale.co.uk.

This run has two clearly separated phases:

Phase‑aware Amazon resume for Category 1 (non‑hybrid resume finalizer)
Relevant lines:

At startup, state reflects unfinished Amazon work:
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=1/326 url=... prod_idx=32/53
RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: Starting from category index 1 at product 32 in phase 'amazon_analysis'.
PHASE-AWARE GATING: Routing to Amazon analysis phase
Category 1 queue & denominator alignment:
CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...Baby-Socks-and-Booties size=19 allowed_gate=off
DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED: Frozen denominator: 53 Actual queue size: 19 ... Action: Aligning state to actual queue size (19)
Clamp amazon offset: saved=32 start=19 (len=19)
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=1 total=19 start_batch=0 first_offset=19
Category 1 is then marked completed:
MARK_CATEGORY_COMPLETED CALLED
CATEGORY_COMPLETION_CALL: url=...Baby-Socks-a... absolute_cat_index=1
CATEGORY_INDEX_TRACKER: Category completed ...Baby-Socks-and-Booties next pci=2
WORKFLOW: Category 1 completed and finalized
Phase switch back to supplier:
PHASE TRANSITION: amazon_analysis supplier
PHASE SWITCH: amazon_analysis supplier (via resume finalizer)
RESUME COMPLETE: continuing main loop at cat_idx=2 phase=supplier
So, before hybrid kicks in, the system does a one‑off Amazon resume to finish category 1, then advances persistent_category_index to 2 and sets current_phase back to 'supplier'.

Hybrid mode restart from Category 2, with chunking bug

Immediately after the above:

State and cursor:
AUTHORITATIVE START POSITION: Category 2 in phase 'supplier'
WORKFLOW START CURSOR: category_index=2 (pci=2, cursor=1, max=2)
Hybrid re‑activation (your new behaviour):
HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled (resume_context=True)
HYBRID PROCESSING: Mode configuration loaded
HYBRID MODE: Chunked processing (chunk size: 1 categories)
Chunk loop logs:
Processing ALL 326 predefined categories (infinite mode)
Processing chunk 1: categories 1-1
PERFORMING SUPPLIER SCRAPING from 1 categories
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2
PROGRESS TRACKING: Extracting from 0 categories in 0 batches
Then the same pattern repeats for:
Processing chunk 2: categories 2-2
…
Processing chunk 306: categories 306-306
…
Processing chunk 326: categories 326-326
Each time: ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2 and Extracting from 0 categories in 0 batches.
Notice there are no AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED logs in this hybrid section, and CATEGORY QUEUE only appears for category 1 in the earlier Amazon resume snippet.
Final line:
Workflow completed successfully
Interpretation:

After finishing category 1’s Amazon analysis, the state says “start at category 2, phase supplier”.

Hybrid mode is then run with:

chunk_size_categories = 1
_start_category_index = 2
_run_hybrid_processing_mode splits the 326 categories into 326 single‑category chunks:

chunk 1 → [category 1]
chunk 2 → [category 2]
...
chunk 326 → [category 326]
_extract_supplier_products is then called on each chunk list, but it uses the global _start_category_index to slice that per‑chunk list:

In code (tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py):

# category_urls = chunk_categories (e.g. [category1] or [category2])
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]  # global index
self.log.info(
    f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {self._start_category_index}"
)
For the resumed run, _start_category_index is 2.
For every chunk, category_urls has length 1.
So category_urls[2-1:] → category_urls[1:] → empty list.
That matches the logs:
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2
PROGRESS TRACKING: Extracting from 0 categories in 0 batches
Because cursor_urls is always empty:

The inner loop that actually scrapes categories never runs (Scraping category: ... doesn’t appear in this run).
No new supplier products are discovered in hybrid mode for categories 2–326.
Consequently, no CATEGORY QUEUE is created and no AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED appears for those categories.
The outer hybrid loop still iterates through all 326 chunks, but each is a no‑op, so the run finishes almost immediately with “Workflow completed successfully”.
This is exactly what you observed: the second run “starts, appears to skip all categories, and ends the session within a few seconds.”

Root Cause Summary

The fix from CHAT FIXES.txt and your current code removed the guard that disabled hybrid on resume, allowing hybrid mode to be used consistently.
On a fresh session (run 1), this behaves correctly: _start_category_index=1, chunk_size=1, and hybrid processes category 1 with both supplier and Amazon phases.
On a resumed session after partially completing category 1:
The system correctly:
Resumes Amazon for category 1 via phase‑aware gating.
Marks category 1 complete and advances persistent_category_index to 2.
Then, when hybrid mode runs with chunk_size_categories=1 and _start_category_index=2, the interaction between:
Per‑chunk category lists ([category_n]), and
A global _start_category_index used directly in cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]
causes every supplier-scraping call in hybrid mode to see 0 categories from cursor.
Net effect:
Category 1 is properly finished via the resume finalizer.
Categories 2–326 are iterated in name only (chunks are logged), but no supplier or Amazon work runs for them because the effective worklist is empty in each chunk.
So “what happened” is:

Your change successfully re‑enabled hybrid mode on resume, but because _run_hybrid_processing_mode passes per‑chunk category lists into _extract_supplier_products while _extract_supplier_products uses a global _start_category_index, a resumed run from category 2 with chunk_size_categories=1 ends up with zero categories per chunk. That makes the resumed hybrid pass a no‑op, so the system appears to skip all categories and finishes almost immediately.

If you’d like, the next step would be to design a very small, surgical change to the hybrid/chunking code so that _start_category_index is correctly aligned with the chunked category lists (e.g. by adjusting the cursor per chunk or calling _extract_supplier_products on the full category list instead of per‑chunk lists), but I’ll hold off on any edits until you explicitly ask.

MEANING IN TERMS OF RESUMOTION,S INDEXES AND SO ON, SYSTEM WORKFLOW/LOGIC WAS BEHAVING AS EXPECTED. REFER TO THESE LOGS TO SEE WHAT IM REFERING TO :

2. How this baseline compares to the 30 Nov behaviour

Post-fix logs (30 Nov):

First 30 Nov run (…033208.log):
HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled (resume_context=False)
Category 1 supplier and Amazon phases both run in one hybrid session (you see full Amazon scraping, including EAN search, sponsored filtering, title search, ASIN details, etc.).
Second 30 Nov run (…033535.log):
A separate phase-aware resume first finishes just the remaining Amazon analysis for category 1, then:
CATEGORY_INDEX_TRACKER: Category completed ...Baby-Socks-and-Booties next pci=2
PHASE TRANSITION: amazon_analysis → supplier
RESUME COMPLETE: continuing main loop at cat_idx=2 phase=supplier
Then hybrid is re-enabled:
HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled (resume_context=True)
HYBRID MODE: Chunked processing (chunk size: 1 categories)
At this point, the behaviour diverges from your expected “correct” baseline:
_start_category_index is 2, but _run_hybrid_processing_mode passes per‑chunk 1‑element lists into _extract_supplier_products.
_extract_supplier_products uses _start_category_index (2) directly on each 1‑element category_urls list:
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]
With self._start_category_index=2, this becomes category_urls[1:] → empty.
This matches the logs:
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2
PROGRESS TRACKING: Extracting from 0 categories in 0 batches
Repeated for chunks 1..326.
Result: categories 2–326 are effectively no‑ops in hybrid mode, and the run ends quickly with “Workflow completed successfully”.
So:

Before the fix:
Hybrid was disabled on resume (HYBRID DISABLED FOR RESUME).
The “phase-aware routing” path continued supplier work with correct resumption/indexing, but failed to actually trigger Amazon, even when AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED was logged.
After the fix:
Hybrid is enabled on resume (HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled (resume_context=True)).
Category 1’s Amazon phase now runs correctly (big improvement).
However, because hybrid chunking now re-enters _run_hybrid_processing_mode for a resumed session with chunk_size=1 and _start_category_index>1, you get the new “skip all categories” symptom due to the per‑chunk cursor bug.
3. Does my previous explanation need adjustment?

Given your clarification (“resumption/indexes were behaving as expected; only Amazon triggering was wrong”) and the 29 Nov logs:

The core idea of my previous explanation still stands:

The new “skipping all categories” behaviour is caused by reusing a global _start_category_index inside _extract_supplier_products when it is called with chunked category lists in hybrid mode.
With chunk_size_categories=1 and _start_category_index=2, every chunk sees 0 categories from cursor, so hybrid does nothing after category 1.
What I’ll refine:

I should explicitly treat the “29 Nov behaviour” as the baseline you want to preserve for resumption/indexing:
PCI and per-category denominators behave correctly.
Resume pointers (phase, cat_idx, prod_idx) advance as expected.
Multi-category sequencing (Baby-Socks → All-Shop-by-age → A-To-Z) and mismatch handling (PCI/URL mismatch warnings) are correct.
The target behaviour is therefore:
Keep all of that baseline resumption/index logic.
Add “Amazon phase actually runs” when AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED appears, without introducing regressions like the hybrid-chunk cursor bug.
In other words:

You’re right: the original “world” had good resume/index behaviour but a missing Amazon trigger on resume.
The fix we introduced successfully solved the Amazon-trigger problem (as confirmed in the 30 Nov 03:32 and 03:35 logs for category 1).
But it also changed the high-level control flow (using hybrid for resumed runs) which exposed a pre‑existing design fragility in the interaction between:
_run_hybrid_processing_mode’s chunk loop, and
_extract_supplier_products’s use of _start_category_index.
So my earlier root-cause description is still accurate for the 30 Nov bug, but I would now explicitly anchor any suggested fix to preserve the 29 Nov resumption/index semantics you are happy with, and only adjust:

How hybrid is re-entered after a phase-aware Amazon resume.
How _start_category_index is applied in hybrid mode when chunk_size_categories is small (especially 1).
4. Summary in terms of your expectations

Your statement that “before the new implementation, resumption, indexes, and general workflow/logic were behaving as expected, except that Amazon processing was not launched” is fully supported by:

run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113518.log
run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113646.log
The new behaviour you’re seeing (30 Nov logs) is a regression introduced by enabling hybrid on resume without adjusting the chunking/cursor interaction.

Any fix plan going forward should therefore:

Treat the 29 Nov resume/index behaviour as the correct reference for:
persistent_category_index
prod_idx and per-category denominators
Category progression and PCI/URL mismatch handling
Preserve that behaviour,
While:
Ensuring Amazon analysis runs when AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED is logged, and
Avoiding the hybrid-chunk “0 categories from cursor” pattern seen on 30 Nov.
If you want, the next step (still without editing code) can be a concrete, file-referenced proposal for how to adjust _run_hybrid_processing_mode / _extract_supplier_products to meet those constraints, so you can review it before we touch any scripts.

SO TELL ME in a concise summary/report, what exactly happened, why system is behaving as such durin resumption, theroot casues, and suggested fixes.


What Happened

29 Nov (baseline):
First run (…113518.log) – Hybrid mode ON, supplier extraction for category 1 runs correctly; resume pointers, PCI, denominators, and category sequencing behave as expected. Amazon phase is never triggered despite no obvious resumption/index issues.
Second run (…113646.log) – On resume, you see HYBRID DISABLED FOR RESUME (phase-aware routing will handle resumption). Supplier continues correctly (category 1 finished, category 2 and onwards behave logically), but even when AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED appears (10 then 50 products), no Amazon extraction actually runs.
30 Nov (after removing the “RESUME GUARD” and enabling hybrid on resume):
First run (…033208.log) – Hybrid mode runs from a clean state; category 1 gets both supplier extraction and full Amazon analysis as intended.
Second run (…033535.log) – On resume, a phase‑aware Amazon resume does correctly finish category 1 and advance persistent_category_index to 2. Then hybrid mode restarts with chunk_size_categories=1 and _start_category_index=2. At this point the logs show ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2 and no supplier/Amazon work for categories 2–326, but the workflow still reports “completed successfully”. This is the “skipped all categories in a few seconds” behaviour you observed.
Why Resumption Now Behaves This Way

Original resume behaviour (29 Nov)

On resume, the code intentionally disables hybrid:
HYBRID DISABLED FOR RESUME (phase-aware routing will handle resumption)
The “phase‑aware routing” path resumes supplier work correctly, using:
persistent_category_index (PCI),
prod_idx,
frozen denominators,
all of which match your expectation in the logs.
However, when that path sees AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED, it does not actually call the Amazon analysis loop. It logs the need but then continues to the next supplier category. That’s why Amazon never ran on 29 Nov even though state/index logic looked fine.
New behaviour after enabling hybrid on resume (30 Nov)

On the resumed 03:35 run:
The state manager first does a non‑hybrid Amazon resume for category 1 (phase=amazon_analysis, cat_idx=1, prod_idx≈32/53) and completes it, then advances PCI to 2 and switches phase back to supplier.
After that, run() sees hybrid enabled and calls _run_hybrid_processing_mode with:
full category_urls_to_scrape (326 URLs),
chunk_size_categories = 1,
_start_category_index = 2 (from state: “start at category 2”).
Inside _run_hybrid_processing_mode:
It loops over categories in chunks of 1 (chunk 1: [cat1], chunk 2: [cat2], …).
For each chunk, it calls _extract_supplier_products(chunk_categories, …).
Inside _extract_supplier_products:
It uses _start_category_index as a global cursor into the per‑chunk list:
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index - 1:]
With _start_category_index = 2 and category_urls length 1 for every chunk, this slice is always empty.
That matches the logs: ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2 and Extracting from 0 categories in 0 batches for every chunk.
Result: after category 1’s Amazon resume, the hybrid phase sees 0 work for every chunk and effectively no‑ops through categories 2–326, then exits as “Workflow completed successfully”.
Root Causes

Original root cause (before your fix)

Design choice: On resume, hybrid mode was turned off (HYBRID DISABLED FOR RESUME), and “phase‑aware routing” was supposed to manage resumption.
Implementation bug: The phase‑aware resume path never invoked the Amazon analysis pipeline for categories where AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED was logged. That’s why the system skipped Amazon entirely in the 29 Nov logs even though resumption/indexing looked correct.
New root cause (after enabling hybrid on resume)

Enabling hybrid mode on resume changed the top‑level control flow so that _run_hybrid_processing_mode runs again after the one‑off Amazon resume for category 1.
_run_hybrid_processing_mode’s chunking (per‑chunk category lists) conflicts with _extract_supplier_products’s use of _start_category_index as a global index into each small chunk.
With chunk_size_categories = 1 and _start_category_index > 1, every chunk passes a 1‑element list into _extract_supplier_products, which then slices it starting at index 1 (0‑based) and yields an empty cursor_urls.
This interaction was not visible when hybrid was disabled on resume; it became visible only after your fix allowed hybrid to run in a resumed context with pci > 1.
Suggested Fix Direction (no edits yet)

All fixes should preserve the good behaviour you had on 29 Nov (correct resumption, PCI, denominators, category ordering), while:

Ensuring Amazon analysis actually runs whenever AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED is logged.
Preventing the hybrid chunk/cursor interaction from producing “0 categories from cursor”.
Concretely:

Fix the Amazon trigger on resume (core functional requirement)

Ensure that when AMAZON ANALYSIS NEEDED is logged for a category:
Either the phase‑aware routing path explicitly calls the Amazon analysis loop for that category; or
The system always routes through hybrid mode for that category, and hybrid guarantees “supplier completed → Amazon analysis runs” before advancing persistent_category_index.
The 30 Nov logs show that in a clean start, hybrid already does this correctly for category 1; the missing piece is consistent behaviour on resume.
Align hybrid chunking with _start_category_index (prevent category skipping)
Options that fit your constraints:

Option A: Make _start_category_index relative to the chunk.
For each chunk, compute a chunk‑local start index based on chunk_start (global index) and the saved PCI, and use that in place of the global _start_category_index when slicing category_urls inside _extract_supplier_products.
This ensures that if PCI is 2, the chunk containing category 2 starts at the correct element, and other chunks correctly no‑op only when they are fully “before PCI”.
Option B: Call _extract_supplier_products on the full category list instead of per‑chunk lists when resuming.
Keep chunking purely for Amazon processing or higher‑level scheduling, but let _extract_supplier_products see the complete category_urls_to_scrape and apply _start_category_index only once against the global list, as it was designed.
Option C: Don’t re‑enter hybrid immediately after a phase‑aware Amazon resume.
When state says current_phase == 'amazon_analysis' on startup, finish that category via the non‑hybrid resume path (as you already do), then jump directly to the next supplier category without re-running _run_hybrid_processing_mode for the rest of that chunk, or run hybrid in “from scratch” mode with _start_category_index reset appropriately.
Guardrail / observability improvements (optional but helpful):

Log a warning and short-circuit if _run_hybrid_processing_mode detects:
len(cursor_urls) == 0 and chunk_categories contains a category whose global index is ≥ _start_category_index.
This would turn the kind of “silent skips” seen on 30 Nov into explicit diagnostic signals.
In short: the system is currently behaving as it does because enabling hybrid on resume exposed a mismatch between global resume indices and per‑chunk category lists. The fix is to keep your existing, good resumption/index logic, restore or ensure proper Amazon triggering, and adjust how _start_category_index is applied in hybrid mode so that resumed runs don’t devolve into zero‑work chunks.

report 1:
Regression Analysis: Category Skipping in Hybrid Mode

Executive Summary
Issue: The system skips categories when running in "Hybrid Mode" (chunked processing). Root Cause: The
_extract_supplier_products
method applies a global start index (self._start_category_index) to a local chunk of categories. If the global index is greater than the size of the chunk (which is almost always true for chunks after the first one), the slicing logic results in an empty list, causing the chunk to be skipped. Status: Code is currently UNMODIFIED. This report outlines the findings and proposed fix for review.

Root Cause Analysis
The core issue lies in the mismatch between the scope of the index and the scope of the list being sliced.

The Problematic Logic
In
tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
:

Hybrid Mode (
_run_hybrid_processing_mode
):

Splits category_urls_to_scrape into smaller chunks (e.g., 1 category at a time).
Calls
_extract_supplier_products
with just this chunk.

chunk_categories is a list of length 1 (e.g., ['http://...'])
chunk_products = await self._extract_supplier_products(..., chunk_categories, ...)
Extraction Method (
_extract_supplier_products
):

Uses self._start_category_index (a global 1-based index, e.g., 355) to slice the input list.

self._start_category_index = 355
category_urls = chunk_categories (length 1)
Slicing logic (simplified):
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index - 1:]

Result: category_urls[354:] -> [] (Empty List!)
Result: The method thinks it needs to start at item 355 of a 1-item list, so it returns nothing.

Evidence from "Good" Logs
We analyzed logs where the system behaved correctly to understand the expected contract.
Log:
run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113646.log
Context: Normal run (non-hybrid or full list passed).
Observation:
WORKFLOW START CURSOR: category_index=1
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 326 categories starting from index 1
Insight: When the full list is passed,
category_urls
has length 326. Slicing from index 1 (0-based index 0) works perfectly.
Log:
run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113518.log
Context: Hybrid Mode run.
Observation:
HYBRID MODE: Chunked processing (chunk size: 1 categories)
Processing chunk 1: categories 1-1
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 1 categories starting from index 1
Insight: This worked only because it was the first chunk. self._start_category_index was 1. The chunk size was 1. category_urls[1-1:] -> category_urls[0:] -> Correctly took the item.
Failure Point: If this had been chunk 2 (global index 2), and _start_category_index was still 1 (or 2), and we passed a new list of length 1, the slicing would fail if not adjusted relative to the chunk's position.
4. Proposed Fix
We must tell
_extract_supplier_products
where the chunk fits into the global picture so it can adjust the slicing.

Plan
Update
_run_hybrid_processing_mode
: Pass the chunk_start_index (the global 1-based index of the first item in the chunk).
Update
_extract_supplier_products
: Accept chunk_start_index and use it to calculate a local slice.
Logic Change

OLD (Incorrect for chunks)
local_start = self._start_category_index - 1

NEW (Correct for chunks)
Calculate offset relative to the chunk's start
if self._start_category_index < chunk_start_index:
local_start = 0 # Resume point is before this chunk
elif self._start_category_index >= chunk_start_index + len(category_urls):
local_start = len(category_urls) # Resume point is after this chunk
else:
local_start = self._start_category_index - chunk_start_index # Inside chunk
Proposed Code Diffs
1.
tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
(Hybrid Mode Loop)

Process categories in chunks
        for chunk_start in range(0, len(category_urls_to_scrape), chunk_size):
            # ... (existing code) ...
            
            # [NEW] Calculate 1-based start index for this chunk
            chunk_start_index = chunk_start + 1
            chunk_products = await self._extract_supplier_products(
                # ... (existing args) ...
                chunk_start_index=chunk_start_index,  # [NEW] Pass chunk start
            )
2.
tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
(Extraction Method)
async def _extract_supplier_products(
self,
# ... (existing args) ...
chunk_start_index: int = 1, # [NEW] Default to 1 for backward compatibility
) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:

    # ...
    
    # [MODIFIED] Relative slicing logic
    if self._start_category_index < chunk_start_index:
        local_start_index = 0
    elif self._start_category_index >= chunk_start_index + len(category_urls):
        local_start_index = len(category_urls)
    else:
        local_start_index = self._start_category_index - chunk_start_index
    cursor_urls = category_urls[local_start_index:]
5. Verification
This fix ensures that:

Full List Runs: chunk_start_index defaults to 1. Logic becomes self._start_category_index - 1, preserving original behavior.
Hybrid Runs: Each chunk knows its global position. The global _start_category_index is correctly mapped to the local chunk's coordinate space.

If self._start_category_index = 2, then category_urls[2-1:] = category_urls[1:] which is empty if the list only has 1 element!
The core issue lies in the cursor slicing mechanism. In hybrid processing mode, the method receives individual category chunks, but the slicing logic assumes a full category list. This creates an empty cursor when attempting to slice a single-category list.

Look at line 5133:
pythoncursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:] # Adjust for 1-based indexing
THE PROBLEM:

Hybrid mode calls _extract_supplier_products with chunk_categories which is ONE category (since chunk_size=1)
The state has self._start_category_index = 2 (from previous run)
Inside _extract_supplier_products, it tries to slice: category_urls[2-1:] = category_urls[1:]
But category_urls only has 1 element (the single category from the chunk)
So category_urls[1:] = empty list!
Result: "0 categories starting from index 2"

This is why ALL categories are skipped - the cursor slicing logic assumes the FULL category list is passed, but hybrid mode passes INDIVIDUAL categories.

THE FIX
We need to disable the cursor slicing inside _extract_supplier_products when called from hybrid mode, because hybrid mode ALREADY handles which categories to pass via its chunk loop.

python# If we're receiving a chunk (less than total categories), don't slice - hybrid mode handles this

1. Feasibility of “removing chunk index/values” from resumption/progress logic

Clarifying what exists today:

The persistent resume state is global:
persistent_category_index and session_resume_cursor are handled by FixedEnhancedStateManager and bound to _start_category_index in run() (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 2118-2123)).
The chunk indices are local to hybrid mode:
In _run_hybrid_processing_mode, chunk_start / chunk_end are just loop variables over category_urls_to_scrape (around tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:6990+).
_extract_supplier_products applies the global _start_category_index to whatever category_urls it is given:
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:] (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (line 5134)).
So:

There is no chunk index stored in state; resumption/progress tracking is already based purely on global indices.
The bug is that we now call _extract_supplier_products with chunked lists (e.g. 1 category) while it still assumes it’s receiving the full list and applies the global cursor to that chunk.
Given that:

You do not need a cross-file rewrite to “remove chunk index/values” from resumption logic, because they are not persisted there today.
What you do need is a local fix inside tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py so that:
Either _run_hybrid_processing_mode passes global context (like chunk_start_index) and _extract_supplier_products maps the global cursor to the chunk (as in Report 1), or
_extract_supplier_products is only called with the full category list when it uses _start_category_index, and hybrid chunking is handled above it.
Removing hybrid chunking entirely from resumed flows is also feasible and mostly local to this file:
Chunking is configured and used in _run_hybrid_processing_mode (not in utils or config), and the overall state manager only cares about global indices.
Main external dependencies are tests and any docs around hybrid_processing.processing_modes.chunked.
So: Yes, it is feasible to keep resumption/progress tracking completely free of “chunk indices” and treat chunking as a purely local runtime concern. The changes are concentrated in passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, not “a lot of other files” – though you would want to re-run tests that cover hybrid mode.

2. Comparison with the two reports

Where we agree with Report 1

Root cause:
We are aligned: the core bug is exactly what Report 1 describes:

Hybrid mode passes per‑chunk lists (chunk_categories, often length 1) into _extract_supplier_products:
chunk_products = await self._extract_supplier_products(..., chunk_categories, ...) (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py near 7000).
_extract_supplier_products then slices category_urls using the global _start_category_index:
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:] (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (line 5134)).
When _start_category_index > 1 and category_urls is a 1‑element chunk, the slice is empty, which matches the logs:
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2 in run_custom_poundwholesale_20251130_033535.log.
Evidence from “good” logs:
Their use of the 29 Nov logs is consistent with what we saw:

run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113646.log:138/156 show:
WORKFLOW START CURSOR: category_index=1 (pci=1, cursor=1, max=1)
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 326 categories starting from index 1
run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113518.log (line 155) shows:
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 1 categories starting from index 1 (first hybrid chunk worked because _start_category_index was 1).
Proposed fix:
We agree the cleanest, explicit solution is the one they describe:

Pass a chunk_start_index (global 1‑based index of the first category in the chunk) from _run_hybrid_processing_mode into _extract_supplier_products.
Inside _extract_supplier_products, derive a local local_start_index from the global _start_category_index and chunk_start_index, then slice category_urls[local_start_index:].
This keeps:
Full list runs behaving as before.
Hybrid runs mapping the global cursor correctly into each chunk.
We did not explicitly spell out the exact chunk_start_index parameterization earlier, but conceptually our root cause and fix direction are the same.

Where we agree with Report 2

Report 2 is essentially a more “narrated” version of the same issue:
It correctly observes that in the 30 Nov log:
_start_category_index = 2 (from state),
hybrid mode passes a 1‑element chunk_categories list,
_extract_supplier_products slices that with self._start_category_index-1, yielding an empty cursor_urls and logs 0 categories starting from index 2.
It also correctly notes that in a normal full-list scenario, _start_category_index slicing is fine, and the bug only appears because the function is now being called with a subset (chunk) instead of the full list.
Their core diagnosis—that there is a list-slicing bug caused by applying a global index to a local chunk—is consistent with the code (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:2118, 5134, 6976+) and with the logs you provided.
Where I’m cautious / partially disagree (Report 2’s suggested heuristic)

Report 2 suggests a heuristic along the lines of:

“If len(category_urls) is less than the total number of predefined categories, assume we’re in a chunk and skip slicing (use the chunk as‑is); otherwise, apply _start_category_index slicing.”

I would not adopt that exact heuristic as-is, because:

category_urls is legitimately allowed to be a subset even in non-hybrid flows

The architecture summary explicitly mentions _extract_supplier_products being called with subset lists based on configuration and category selection (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py top-of-file summary and _hierarchical_category_selection at 4721, even if not used in your current custom runner).
In future or alternative workflows, category_urls could represent “a subset of predefined categories”, not a chunk, and you’d still want resume slicing.
Global category count can differ from the active set

The logs show the system happily processing different category URLs with consistent PCI/URL checks, e.g.:
Category 1: Baby-Socks-and-Booties
Category 2: All-Shop-by-age
Category 3: A-To-Z-wholesale
in run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113646.log (see PCI/URL mismatch warning near the end).
In that context, a “total categories” vs len(category_urls) check is not a robust indicator of “chunk vs full list”; it’s entirely possible to run with a filtered category set that is smaller than the total predefined count but still not be in hybrid chunk mode.
We already know when we are in hybrid chunk context

_run_hybrid_processing_mode constructs chunk_categories and calls _extract_supplier_products with those lists (e.g. around tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:6996+).
The more robust solution is to pass an explicit hint (chunk_start_index or a flag) from that caller, instead of guessing based on list length.
So I agree with their diagnosis, but I’d favour the more explicit fix pattern from Report 1 (or an equivalent) over a “len(category_urls) < total ⇒ hybrid” heuristic.

Bottom line

Your intuition is correct: originally, resumption/progress tracking thought only in terms of a global category index; hybrid chunking was an internal implementation detail. The regression appears because that assumption was broken when chunked lists were passed into _extract_supplier_products without adjusting how _start_category_index is applied.
Both external reports correctly identify the slicing of category_urls with a global _start_category_index as the root cause of category skipping in hybrid mode.
I’m aligned with their root causes and the general fix direction; my only caution is against using a “list length” heuristic to detect hybrid chunks, when we can instead explicitly pass global chunk position and keep resumption logic clean and global.

Short answer: chunked lists are not conceptually “required” for correct resume/index logic, but given how your current hybrid mode is written, removing them would be a larger, higher‑risk change than fixing the index/chunk interaction. The two “older” scripts you pointed at already depend on chunked lists in the same way, so there isn’t a clean, proven “no‑chunk” implementation to revert to.

Here’s the breakdown.

1. What the older versions actually do

Files checked:

backup\six_fixes_20251127\tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest - Copy (3).py
Findings:

Both versions already implement chunked hybrid mode exactly the same way as your current file:
_run_hybrid_processing_mode(...):
Splits category_urls_to_scrape into chunks:
for chunk_start in range(0, len(category_urls_to_scrape), chunk_size):
chunk_categories = category_urls_to_scrape[chunk_start:chunk_end]
Calls _extract_supplier_products with chunk_categories (sublist):
chunk_products = await self._extract_supplier_products(..., chunk_categories, ...)
_extract_supplier_products(...):
Still assumes category_urls is the global list and applies _start_category_index directly:
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]
So even in six_fixes_20251127 and Copy (3), there is already the same structural mismatch:
Global _start_category_index vs. local chunk_categories.
There is no version in these two files where hybrid mode runs without chunked lists, or where _extract_supplier_products has a separate, simpler “no chunk” resume path.

2. Are chunked lists “a must” for correct behaviour?

Conceptually, no:

Your resume and progress semantics are global:
persistent_category_index, session_resume_cursor, _start_category_index are all about the global category index.
They worked the way you expect in the 29 Nov logs when hybrid was disabled on resume.
Hybrid chunking is an implementation strategy for:
Limiting how many categories are processed per hybrid cycle.
Controlling when to switch from supplier to Amazon (per switch_to_amazon_after_categories).
So you could have a correct workflow that:

Uses the full category list in _extract_supplier_products (as originally intended), and
Drives “supplier vs Amazon” purely from global indices and counters, without ever passing sublists into _extract_supplier_products.
However, in your actual codebase:

Chunked lists are already wired into hybrid mode across these snapshots.
Tests, logs, and behaviour (including manifest generation per chunk) assume this shape.
That means:

Removing chunked lists at code level (so hybrid never passes per‑chunk lists) is a non‑trivial refactor:
You’d need to redesign _run_hybrid_processing_mode to:
Always call _extract_supplier_products with the full list,
Then apply “per‑chunk” behaviour only when deciding which categories to run Amazon on.
You’d also need to re‑audit category‑level logging, manifests, and any chunk‑specific behaviour already baked into the hybrid loop.
By contrast:

Keeping chunked lists and fixing the index/chunk mapping is a small, surgical change inside one file.
3. Low‑risk options vs. “no chunks”

Given your goals (minimal new fixes, avoid introducing discrepancies), I’d rank the options like this:

Option 1 – Smallest, safest change (keep chunked lists, fix their mapping)
(this is essentially Report 1’s approach)

Keep _run_hybrid_processing_mode and chunked lists.

Add a chunk_start_index parameter (or equivalent) and compute a local start index inside _extract_supplier_products:

From _run_hybrid_processing_mode:
chunk_start_index = chunk_start + 1 (global 1‑based index of first category in the chunk).
Pass chunk_start_index into _extract_supplier_products.
Inside _extract_supplier_products:
Map global _start_category_index (PCI/cursor) into the chunk’s coordinate space, then slice:
before the chunk → local_start_index = 0 (no skip within this chunk),
beyond the chunk → local_start_index = len(category_urls) (no work for this chunk),
inside the chunk → local_start_index = _start_category_index - chunk_start_index.
This:

Touches only tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.
Preserves all your existing resume/index behaviour.
Removes the “0 categories starting from index 2” bug without restructuring the workflow.
Option 2 – Disable chunked hybrid at runtime, rely on full‑list path

If you want to avoid chunked lists at runtime without large code surgery:

You can:
Turn off processing_modes.chunked.enabled in hybrid_processing config for now, or
Add a very small guard so that when is_resuming is true (or when pci > 1), hybrid either:
Falls back to a non-chunked mode, or
Uses _extract_supplier_products with the full category list instead of per‑chunk lists.
Effects:

Hybrid chunked mode stops being used on resume; _extract_supplier_products only ever sees the full category_urls_to_scrape.
This returns you closer to the 29 Nov “phase‑aware + full list” world, while still allowing us to fix the Amazon trigger path.
Risk: You lose the performance/architectural benefits of chunked hybrid on long runs, but the logic is simpler.
This is still less invasive than fully ripping chunking out of the code, but it doesn’t eliminate chunked code – it just bypasses it in the paths that hurt you.

Option 3 – Full “no chunked lists” refactor

Rewrite hybrid so that:
_extract_supplier_products is always given the full global list,
hybrid uses global indices and filters to decide which categories to “activate” for a given cycle,
chunking becomes purely a logical notion (e.g. “process categories i..j in this cycle”) instead of passing chunk_categories sublists.
This would be the cleanest architecture, but:
It’s the most intrusive,
It would likely require careful re‑testing of all hybrid scenarios,
It conflicts with your “surgical, minimal” constraint.
Given your constraints, I would not recommend Option 3 as the first move.

4. Answering your question directly

Are chunked lists a hard requirement for correct system logic?
No. They’re an implementation detail for hybrid scheduling, not the foundation of resumption/index correctness.

Can we get the behaviour you want without chunked lists, with high confidence and minimal new fixes?

Completely eliminating chunked lists from hybrid logic (Option 3) is not a low‑risk, minimal change.
Bypassing chunked mode on resume (Option 2) is feasible but still requires at least one small conditional path and a fix to the Amazon trigger.
The lowest‑risk, most surgical path that respects your current architecture is to keep chunked lists but fix their interaction with _start_category_index (Option 1). That’s exactly what Report 1 proposes and what the logs/code support.
If you want, the next step can be for me to write out a precise, line‑referenced patch plan for Option 1 (or Option 2) so you can review it before we touch any code.

does the below address/respect my requreiemnts ( removing chiunked list); or there are things that were not considerd ? which might cause discprencies and what not:
Feasibility Report: Removing Chunk Awareness from Resumption Logic

Analysis of Backup Files
File Checked: backup/six_fixes_20251127/tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
Finding: The backup version DID contain the problematic slicing logic:

Line 5133 in backup
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]
Implication: This means the "regression" (skipping categories in Hybrid Mode when start_index is high) was likely present in the backup version as well, but perhaps not triggered because:

Hybrid Mode wasn't used with a high resumption index.
Or the start_index was 1 (as seen in the "good" logs).
2. Feasibility of Removing Chunk Index/Values
User Question: Is it feasible to remove chunk index/values from resumption logic?

Answer: YES, it is feasible and likely cleaner.

Instead of "teaching" _extract_supplier_products about chunks (by passing chunk_start_index), we can:

Remove Slicing from _extract_supplier_products: Make this method "dumb". It should simply process whatever list of URLs it is given, starting from 0 (or a local offset if needed for product resumption, but not category resumption).
Move Logic to the Caller (_run_hybrid_processing_mode): The caller already knows the global context. It should decide which categories to pass.
Proposed "Chunk-Agnostic" Approach

In _run_hybrid_processing_mode (The Caller): Handle the "global resumption" logic here.
Pseudo-code for Caller
for chunk_start in range(0, total_categories, chunk_size):
chunk_end = chunk_start + chunk_size

# 1. Skip chunks that are fully "in the past"
if chunk_end < self._start_category_index:
    continue 
# 2. Slice the "current" chunk if we are resuming in the middle of it
current_chunk_urls = category_urls[chunk_start:chunk_end]
if chunk_start < self._start_category_index <= chunk_end:
    # We need to start part-way through this chunk
    local_offset = self._start_category_index - chunk_start - 1
    current_chunk_urls = current_chunk_urls[local_offset:]

# 3. Pass the (potentially sliced) list to extraction
# No need to pass chunk_start_index!
await self._extract_supplier_products(..., current_chunk_urls, ...)
2. In _extract_supplier_products (The Method): Remove the global slicing.

Pseudo-code for Method
REMOVE THIS: cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]
REPLACE WITH:
cursor_urls = category_urls # Process everything I was given
3. Benefits & Risks
Benefits:

Decoupling: _extract_supplier_products no longer needs to know about "chunks" or "global category indices" for the purpose of selecting what to scrape.
Simplicity: The method contract is simpler: "Scrape these URLs."
Alignment with User Request: This strictly follows the request to remove chunk values from the resumption logic inside the extraction step.
Risks:

State Tracking: We must ensure that _extract_supplier_products doesn't rely on self._start_category_index for logging or saving state in a way that assumes it's processing the "rest of the global list".
Mitigation: The backup shows that _run_hybrid_processing_mode handles the mark_category_completed call after extraction returns. This suggests the proposed change is safe.


Short answer: your proposal is close but not complete. It removes the worst chunk interaction, but a few important details are missing and could still cause subtle discrepancies if applied exactly as written.

What your proposal gets right

Moving all category‑resume slicing into _run_hybrid_processing_mode and making _extract_supplier_products process “whatever list I’m given” is conceptually sound.
It does respect your requirement that resumption logic should not depend on chunk indices inside _extract_supplier_products.
The basic cursor math in your pseudo‑code (skip chunks fully before PCI, slice the first “active” chunk, pass it down) is correct for 1‑based PCI + 0‑based chunk_start.
What is not fully considered / still risky

Non‑hybrid caller still depends on _start_category_index

run() calls _extract_supplier_products(category_urls_to_scrape, …) directly when hybrid is disabled.
If you remove cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:] but do not add any equivalent slicing in this non‑hybrid path, then:
Category resumption will rely only on the FIX D skip (absolute_cat_index < _start_category_index) inside _extract_supplier_products.
This will probably still work (earlier categories are skipped), but:
The log line ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {self._start_category_index} will become misleading (it will report the full len(category_urls) even though many are skipped).
You’re now splitting resume logic across two places: _run_hybrid_processing_mode (for hybrid) and FIX D inside _extract_supplier_products (for both). That’s more complex to reason about.
Global vs local category index mapping (_category_index_map)

In _extract_supplier_products, absolute_cat_index is computed from a mapping:

src_urls = (
    category_urls
    or getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", [])
    or (self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or [])
)
self._category_index_map = {normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(src_urls)}
In hybrid mode, when _extract_supplier_products is first called with a chunk, category_urls is that chunk list. Because it’s non‑empty, it wins over _active_category_urls, so src_urls can be chunk‑local, not global.

That means absolute_cat_index and the PCI/URL mismatch check may become relative to the chunk instead of the global category list — unless _category_index_map has already been built earlier from the full list.

Your proposal only changes the slicing, not how src_urls is chosen, so this subtle chunk/global mismatch is still there and could affect:

absolute_cat_index used in FIX D skip,
PCI/URL mismatch logging.
Chunked cache logic still uses the passed list as “chunk categories”

At the top of _extract_supplier_products there is hybrid‑specific cache logic:

chunk_category_urls = set(category_urls)
products_for_chunk_categories = [
    p for p in cached_products if p.get("source_url", "") in chunk_category_urls
]
This is fine if _run_hybrid_processing_mode always passes the correct per‑chunk list (your current_chunk_urls). Your proposal preserves that, so no issue — but it means _extract_supplier_products is still chunk‑aware in that sense. Only the resumption slicing becomes chunk‑agnostic.

Design consistency vs your “minimal change” goal

Right now, the code (and comments) are structured around:
“Resume logic handled inside _extract_supplier_products (lines ~5125+).”
Your proposal turns _run_hybrid_processing_mode into another resume decision‑maker. That’s not necessarily wrong, but it changes the division of responsibility:
Hybrid: resume slicing in _run_hybrid_processing_mode, gating in _extract_supplier_products.
Non‑hybrid: no slicing in caller, gating only in _extract_supplier_products.
Compared with the simpler chunk_start_index fix, this is more invasive in how the logic is organized, even if the code delta is similar.
Verdict

Your “chunk‑agnostic _extract_supplier_products” idea is technically feasible and mostly aligns with your requirement to keep resumption logic free of chunk indices inside that method.
However, as written, it does not fully account for:
How _category_index_map is built (global vs chunk),
The non‑hybrid call path (where resume slicing still needs to be coherent),
The fact that some chunk‑specific behaviour (cache logic, PCI/URL checks) still depends on which list is passed in.
Because of those points, I’d say:

The proposal is not yet “safe and discrepancy‑free” if applied literally.
To make it robust, you’d at least need to:
Ensure _category_index_map always uses the global list (_active_category_urls) as src_urls, not the chunk.
Be explicit about where resume slicing lives for the non‑hybrid path, so logic isn’t split in an ad‑hoc way.
Given your “minimal, surgical, lowest‑risk” requirement, the chunk_start_index approach (teach _extract_supplier_products how the chunk fits into the global index, and keep resume logic centralised there) is still the safer option unless you’re ready to also adjust the mapping and non‑hybrid caller.

1. Goals & Constraints

Keep the existing hybrid mode (chunked lists) but fix the “skips all categories on resume” bug.
Do not move resume logic out of _extract_supplier_products more than necessary.
Keep changes surgical, confined to tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, and compatible with:
non‑hybrid runs,
existing resume/index behaviour,
existing logs and state semantics.
2. Sources of Truth Checked

I based this plan on three independent “truth” sources:

Current implementation (tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py)

_extract_supplier_products (around lines 4963–5200):
Uses _start_category_index to slice category_urls globally:
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:].
Builds _category_index_map from category_urls or _active_category_urls.
_run_hybrid_processing_mode (around 6955+):
Creates chunk_categories = category_urls_to_scrape[chunk_start:chunk_end].
Calls _extract_supplier_products with chunk_categories.
Backup implementation

backup\six_fixes_20251127\tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
Shows the same pattern: hybrid passes per‑chunk lists into _extract_supplier_products, and _extract_supplier_products slices with _start_category_index. This confirms the bug pattern is stable and our fix does not conflict with earlier design.
Runtime behaviour in logs

run_custom_poundwholesale_20251129_113646.log (non‑hybrid / phase‑aware path):
WORKFLOW START CURSOR: category_index=1 (pci=1, cursor=1, max=1)
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 326 categories starting from index 1
⇒ Full‑list use of _extract_supplier_products + slicing works as expected.
run_custom_poundwholesale_20251130_033535.log (hybrid resume bug):
HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled (resume_context=True)
For every chunk: ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2
⇒ Confirms that _start_category_index=2 applied to 1‑element chunk_categories list yields empty cursor_urls, causing all chunks to no‑op.
These three together strongly support the diagnosis and guide a minimal fix.

3. Intended Behaviour After Fix (High‑Level)

Non‑hybrid path (caller passes the full category_urls_to_scrape):

_extract_supplier_products continues to slice with _start_category_index exactly as today.
Behaviour stays identical to “good” 29 Nov logs.
Hybrid path (caller passes per‑chunk lists):

_run_hybrid_processing_mode explicitly tells _extract_supplier_products where the chunk sits in the global list (via a new chunk_start_index argument).
_extract_supplier_products maps the global _start_category_index into a local index within each chunk:
Chunks entirely before persistent_category_index → no work (as today).
The chunk that contains persistent_category_index → start part‑way through that chunk.
Chunks after persistent_category_index → process fully.
This removes the “0 categories from index 2” bug while preserving the global resume semantics.
4. Surgical Code Changes (Diffs, No Edits Yet)

All diffs below are against tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.

4.1. Extend _extract_supplier_products signature (hybrid‑aware, backward compatible)

Add an optional chunk_start_index parameter. Non‑hybrid callers don’t pass it, so they keep current behaviour.

@@

-        self,
-        supplier_url: str,

Why this is safe:

Optional is already imported at the top of this file.
Existing call sites (non‑hybrid and hybrid) remain valid; hybrid will be updated in the next diff to pass chunk_start_index.
4.2. Fix cursor slicing inside _extract_supplier_products

Replace the single global slice with logic that respects per‑chunk context when chunk_start_index is provided, and preserves existing slicing when it is not.

*** Proposed diff 2: cursor slicing logic in _extract_supplier_products

@@
-        #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
-        # Use cursor-adjusted category URLs starting from session_resume_cursor
-        cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]  # Adjust for 1-based indexing
-        self.log.info(f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {self._start_category_index}")
+        #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
+        # When called with a full global list (non-hybrid), use _start_category_index directly.
+        # When called with a chunk (hybrid), map the global _start_category_index into the chunk.
+        if chunk_start_index is None:
+            # Full-list path (non-hybrid): preserve existing behaviour
+            cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index - 1 :]  # 1-based -> 0-based
+            enum_start_index = self._start_category_index
+        else:
+            # Hybrid path: chunk_start_index is the global 1-based index of the first category in this chunk.
+            global_start = self._start_category_index
+            chunk_first = chunk_start_index
+            chunk_last = chunk_start_index + len(category_urls) - 1
+
+            if global_start <= chunk_first:
+                # Resume index is at or before this chunk: process from the first category in the chunk.
+                local_start_index = 0
+            elif global_start > chunk_last:
+                # Resume index is after this chunk: nothing to do in this chunk.
+                local_start_index = len(category_urls)
+            else:
+                # Resume index falls inside this chunk: skip categories before it.
+                local_start_index = global_start - chunk_first
+
+            cursor_urls = category_urls[local_start_index:]
+            # For logging, show the first global index that will actually be considered.
+            enum_start_index = max(global_start, chunk_first)
+
+        self.log.info(
+            f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {enum_start_index}"
+        )
Why this is safe:

Non‑hybrid callers (chunk_start_index is None):
Behaviour is byte‑for‑byte equivalent: same slice, same start index in logs.
Hybrid callers:
chunk_start_index explicitly anchors the chunk in the global category list.
The three cases are exactly what we want:
chunks before PCI → 0 work (as intended),
chunk containing PCI → partial work from PCI onward,
chunks after PCI → full work.
This directly addresses the 30 Nov symptom (ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 2 on every chunk) by allowing later chunks to see non‑empty cursor_urls.
Note: we intentionally do not change _category_index_offset logic (self._category_index_offset = self._start_category_index - 1), because that governs display indices and is already correct in both logs and state.

4.3. Pass chunk_start_index from _run_hybrid_processing_mode

Update the hybrid chunk loop to tell _extract_supplier_products where each chunk begins in the global sequence.

*** Proposed diff 3: _run_hybrid_processing_mode chunk loop

@@
-            # Process categories in chunks
-            for chunk_start in range(0, len(category_urls_to_scrape), chunk_size):
-                chunk_end = min(chunk_start + chunk_size, len(category_urls_to_scrape))
-                chunk_categories = category_urls_to_scrape[chunk_start:chunk_end]
+            # Process categories in chunks
+            for chunk_start in range(0, len(category_urls_to_scrape), chunk_size):
+                chunk_end = min(chunk_start + chunk_size, len(category_urls_to_scrape))
+                chunk_categories = category_urls_to_scrape[chunk_start:chunk_end]
@@
-                chunk_products = await self._extract_supplier_products(
+                # chunk_start_index is the global 1-based index of the first category in this chunk.
+                chunk_start_index = chunk_start + 1
+

                     supplier_url,
                     supplier_name,
                     chunk_categories,
                     max_products_per_category,
                     max_products_to_process,
                     supplier_extraction_batch_size,
+                    chunk_start_index=chunk_start_index,
                 )
Why this is safe:

Only the hybrid path is affected; non‑hybrid calls (e.g. from run() when hybrid is disabled) remain unchanged.
chunk_start + 1 is the correct 1‑based global index for the first category in this chunk (since chunk_start is 0‑based).
The mapping logic in diff 2 guarantees correct behaviour for all three chunk positions relative to _start_category_index.
5. Expected Behaviour After Applying These Changes

Using the 30 Nov scenario as a test case:

State: persistent_category_index = 2, resume_phase='supplier', chunk_size_categories=1.
Hybrid run:
Chunk 1 (cat 1):
chunk_start_index=1, global_start=2, chunk_last=1 ⇒ local_start_index = len(chunk)=1 ⇒ cursor_urls=[].
No work, as category 1 has already been completed.
Chunk 2 (cat 2):
chunk_start_index=2, chunk_last=2, global_start=2 <= chunk_first=2 ⇒ local_start_index=0 ⇒ cursor_urls=[cat2].
Category 2 is processed as expected.
Chunk 3+ (cat 3, 4, …):
Each has chunk_start_index >= 3, global_start=2 <= chunk_first ⇒ local_start_index=0 ⇒ full processing.
All subsequent categories are processed normally; the “0 categories from cursor=2” bug disappears.
For 29 Nov “good” full‑list behaviour:

Non‑hybrid run, _run_hybrid_processing_mode is either disabled for resume or not in play.
_extract_supplier_products is called once with the full category_urls_to_scrape.
chunk_start_index is None, so we fall through to the existing slice:
category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:] with _start_category_index=1, resulting in 326 categories enumerated, as in the logs.
6. Risk & Regression Assessment

Resumption semantics:
_start_category_index is still computed exactly as before in run() and stored in self._start_category_index.
We do not change persistent_category_index, session_resume_cursor, or any state manager writes.
Non‑hybrid workflows:
The slicing logic for chunk_start_index=None is identical to current behaviour, so non‑hybrid runs and phase‑aware routing remain unchanged.
Hybrid workflows:
The only behavioural change is to allow chunks at and after PCI to see non‑empty cursor_urls; this directly addresses the “skip everything” regression and aligns with the intended semantics of persistent_category_index.
Category index & logging:
_category_index_map, absolute_cat_index, PCI/URL mismatch logging, frozen denominators, and CATEGORY PROCESSING logs all continue to use global indices and category_urls exactly as they do now.
The ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR log is now more accurate in hybrid mode: it reports the first global index that might be processed in that chunk, not the raw _start_category_index which may be before the chunk.
Given the three sources of truth (current code, backup code, and concrete logs), and the fact that non‑hybrid behaviour is strictly preserved, I’m confident this plan is both minimal and correct for fixing the hybrid resume skipping issue.

If you’re happy with this plan and the diffs, I can next apply these exact changes to tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py as requested.

NOTE : I SEE YOUT STATED:
For 29 Nov “good” full‑list behaviour:

Non‑hybrid run, _run_hybrid_processing_mode is either disabled for resume or not in play.

For 29 Nov “good” full‑list behaviour:

You’re right to question that line; my wording there was sloppy and could be read as saying “29 Nov = non‑hybrid,” which is not what we want.

What actually matters

On 29 Nov:
First run (…113518.log): hybrid mode was ON, chunk_size_categories=1, _start_category_index=1. This run behaved as you like (except Amazon not triggering later).
Second run (…113646.log): hybrid was explicitly disabled for resume (HYBRID DISABLED FOR RESUME), and the phase‑aware path used the full category list.
How the plan relates to that

The plan is designed so that:

Fresh hybrid runs (like 29 Nov first run)

_start_category_index = 1
For chunk 1, chunk_start_index = 1, so the new hybrid path computes:
local_start_index = 0, cursor_urls = [that category]
Behaviour is identical to what you saw on 29 Nov (same category processed, same indexing, same logs, just with a slightly more explicit “start index” in the log).
Full‑list (non‑chunked) calls into _extract_supplier_products

When the caller passes the full category_urls_to_scrape (as in the 29 Nov phase‑aware path), chunk_start_index is None and we keep exactly the old slice:
cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]
That’s the “good full‑list behaviour” I was referring to; my phrase “non‑hybrid run” was imprecise. The crucial point is “full list passed,” not “hybrid disabled forever.”
Hybrid + resume (the new requirement)

This is the only place behaviour changes: when _start_category_index > 1 and hybrid is ON, we no longer apply the global index blindly to a 1‑item chunk.
Instead we map _start_category_index into each chunk’s global position (via chunk_start_index) so:
chunks before PCI do nothing,
the chunk containing PCI starts mid‑chunk,
later chunks run fully.
So, to your core concern:

We are not reverting to a non‑hybrid world.
The fix is specifically crafted so that:
Hybrid with _start_category_index = 1 behaves as on 29 Nov.
Full‑list calls behave as on 29 Nov.
Only hybrid + resume (which never worked correctly) gets new logic.
Agreement / disagreement with the quoted analysis

I partially disagree with the sentence as written (“Non‑hybrid run…”): it’s misleading. The correct statement is:
“For the 29 Nov behaviour where _extract_supplier_products sees the full category list, our ‘full‑list branch’ preserves that behaviour exactly.”
I fully agree with your requirement:
When hybrid processing is enabled, product processing, indexing, and state updates should stay consistent with the 29 Nov runs, except for fixing the Amazon‑phase triggering and the resume skipping bug.
The proposed chunk_start_index changes are specifically designed to satisfy that: they don’t alter the 29 Nov fresh‑start hybrid semantics, only the broken resume scenario.
If you’d like, I can next annotate the key log lines (29 Nov and 30 Nov) against the proposed diffs so you can see, step‑by‑step, that the indices and category transitions stay identical except where we explicitly fix the bug.

Status: I PARTIALLY AGREE with your plan, but I have identified a CRITICAL GAP that will cause the fix to fail (skipping will persist) if not addressed.

Points of Agreement
Slicing Logic: Your proposed changes to _extract_supplier_products (adding chunk_start_index and the relative slicing logic) are CORRECT. This correctly solves the "empty list" problem.
Backward Compatibility: The changes are safe for non-hybrid modes.
Points of Disagreement (The Critical Gap)
You stated: "Category index ... continue to use global indices ... exactly as they do now."
I DISAGREE. Based on my analysis of tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:

The Evidence (Source of Truth: Code): Lines 5175-5181 in _extract_supplier_products:

python
5175: if not hasattr(self, "_category_index_map"):
5176: # Prefer the list already in-scope...
5177: src_urls = (category_urls # <--- THIS IS THE CHUNK!
5178: or getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", []) ...)
5180: self._category_index_map = {normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(src_urls)}
5181: absolute_cat_index = self._category_index_map.get(normalize_url(category_url), 1)
The Failure Mechanism:

In Hybrid Mode, category_urls is the chunk (e.g., 1 item).
src_urls becomes the chunk.
_category_index_map is created locally for that chunk (Index = 1).
absolute_cat_index becomes 1.
The Skip Check (Line 5186):
python
if absolute_cat_index < getattr(self, "_start_category_index", 1):
If _start_category_index is 2 (Global Resume), and absolute_cat_index is 1 (Local), IT WILL SKIP.
3. Required Addition to Plan
To fix this, you MUST initialize the global map in _run_hybrid_processing_mode before the loop, so _extract_supplier_products uses the correct global indices.

Add this to _run_hybrid_processing_mode (before the loop):

python
# Initialize authoritative category index map for the FULL list
self._active_category_urls = category_urls_to_scrape
self._category_index_map = {
self.normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(category_urls_to_scrape)
}


You’re right: your analysis caught a real gap in my earlier plan.

Where I agree with your analysis

Your reasoning about _category_index_map is correct:

if not hasattr(self, "_category_index_map"):
    src_urls = (category_urls  # <-- chunk in hybrid
                or getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", [])
                or (self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or []))
    self._category_index_map = {normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(src_urls)}
absolute_cat_index = self._category_index_map.get(normalize_url(category_url), 1)
In hybrid mode, when _extract_supplier_products is first called, category_urls is the chunk (e.g. 1 URL).

src_urls therefore becomes the chunk, not the full list.

absolute_cat_index becomes chunk‑local (1 for that category), while _start_category_index is global (e.g. 2).

The skip check:

if absolute_cat_index < getattr(self, "_start_category_index", 1):
    ...
    continue
will wrongly skip that category (1 < 2), even though we already fixed the cursor slicing to include it.

Conclusion: my previous plan (slicing fix + chunk_start_index) is incomplete on its own. Without fixing _category_index_map to use global indices, skipping can still occur due to FIX D.

Where I differ slightly from your suggested addition

You suggested building _category_index_map directly in _run_hybrid_processing_mode:

self._active_category_urls = category_urls_to_scrape
self._category_index_map = {
    self.normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(category_urls_to_scrape)
}
Conceptually this is fine (prebuild a global map), but there are two minor issues:

In this file, normalize_url is imported as a module‑level function, not a method, so it should be normalize_url(u), not self.normalize_url(u).
A more general, slightly smaller change is to adjust _extract_supplier_products so that it prefers the global list already stored in _active_category_urls when building the map, instead of the chunk.
Concretely (conceptual diff, not applying it yet):

-    src_urls = (category_urls
-                or getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", [])
-                or (self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or []))
+    src_urls = (getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", [])
+                or category_urls
+                or (self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or []))
run() already sets self._active_category_urls = category_urls to the full predefined list before any extraction.
With this priority:
In non‑hybrid calls, _active_category_urls and category_urls are the same full list → behaviour unchanged.
In hybrid calls, _active_category_urls ensures src_urls is the full global list, so:
absolute_cat_index is the global category index,
the skip check (absolute_cat_index < _start_category_index) now compares global vs global, as intended.
So:

Yes, you correctly identified that I missed the _category_index_map / absolute_cat_index interaction; the slicing fix alone is not enough.
I agree we need an additional small change so category indices remain global in hybrid mode.
I’d implement that by adjusting how src_urls is chosen (as above), which achieves the same goal as pre‑building the map in _run_hybrid_processing_mode, but in a slightly more localized and reusable way.
If you approve, the final surgical plan would be:

Add chunk_start_index and the relative slicing logic (as in my previous message).
Adjust src_urls priority in _extract_supplier_products so _category_index_map is built from the full list via _active_category_urls.
Together, those two changes keep 29 Nov behaviour intact and fix the hybrid‑resume skipping without introducing new discrepancies.

Here’s the full, surgical fix plan that incorporates everything we’ve discussed, including the extra point about _active_category_urls. This is a plan only with exact diffs; I will not edit any files until you explicitly tell me to.

1. Objectives

Keep hybrid (chunked) processing enabled and working.
Fix the resume bug where hybrid mode skips all categories after a resumed run.
Preserve the 29 Nov behaviour for:
Fresh hybrid runs (_start_category_index = 1),
Full‑list (non‑chunked) runs,
Resume semantics (PCI, denominators, PCI/URL checks, category sequencing).
Make only minimal, localized changes in tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.
2. Fix Set Overview

This plan makes four small, coordinated changes:

Extend _extract_supplier_products to accept an optional chunk_start_index, so it can map the global resume index into each chunk.
Implement hybrid‑aware cursor slicing inside _extract_supplier_products:
Full‑list path: keep current behaviour.
Hybrid path: use chunk_start_index to compute a local slice, so chunks at/after PCI are processed correctly.
Ensure category indices stay global in hybrid mode by:
Preferring _active_category_urls over the local chunk when building _category_index_map.
Explicitly (re)setting _active_category_urls in _run_hybrid_processing_mode for robustness.
Wire chunk_start_index in _run_hybrid_processing_mode when calling _extract_supplier_products.
Together, these fix both the “0 categories from cursor” problem and the _category_index_map / absolute_cat_index mismatch, while leaving non‑hybrid behaviour unchanged.

3. Surgical Diffs

All paths are relative to the repo root. No changes will be applied until you approve.

3.1. Extend _extract_supplier_products signature

File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (around line 4963)

@@

-        self,

+        max_products_per_category: int,

Notes:

Existing callers remain valid; the hybrid caller will be updated in Diff 4.
3.2. Hybrid‑aware cursor slicing in _extract_supplier_products

File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (the cursor section you saw in your logs, around lines 5125–5145).

Current code:

        #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
        # Use cursor-adjusted category URLs starting from session_resume_cursor
        cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]  # Adjust for 1-based indexing
        self.log.info(f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {self._start_category_index}")
Replace with:

*** Diff 2: hybrid-aware cursor slicing

@@
-        #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
-        # Use cursor-adjusted category URLs starting from session_resume_cursor
-        cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]  # Adjust for 1-based indexing
-        self.log.info(f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {self._start_category_index}")
+        #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
+        # Full-list path (non-hybrid): use _start_category_index directly.
+        # Hybrid path: map the global _start_category_index into the chunk using chunk_start_index.
+        if chunk_start_index is None:
+            # Non-hybrid / full-list call: preserve existing behaviour
+            cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index - 1 :]  # 1-based -> 0-based
+            enum_start_index = self._start_category_index
+        else:
+            # Hybrid call: chunk_start_index is the global 1-based index of the first category in this chunk.
+            global_start = self._start_category_index
+            chunk_first = chunk_start_index
+            chunk_last = chunk_start_index + len(category_urls) - 1
+
+            if global_start <= chunk_first:
+                # Resume index is at or before this chunk: process from the first category in the chunk.
+                local_start_index = 0
+            elif global_start > chunk_last:
+                # Resume index is after this chunk: nothing to do in this chunk.
+                local_start_index = len(category_urls)
+            else:
+                # Resume index falls inside this chunk: skip categories before it.
+                local_start_index = global_start - chunk_first
+
+            cursor_urls = category_urls[local_start_index:]
+            # Logging: show the first global category index considered for this chunk.
+            enum_start_index = max(global_start, chunk_first)
+
+        self.log.info(
+            f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {enum_start_index}"
+        )
Effect:

Full‑list (non‑chunked) calls behave exactly as today (chunk_start_index=None).
Hybrid calls now correctly:
Do nothing for chunks entirely before PCI,
Start mid‑chunk for the chunk that contains PCI,
Run fully for chunks after PCI.
This directly fixes the “0 categories starting from index 2” symptom in your 30 Nov log.
