4. Behaviour Summary After These Fixes

29 Nov “good” hybrid (fresh start, _start_category_index=1):

Hybrid mode ON, chunk_size=1:
For chunk 1: chunk_start_index=1, global_start=1 ⇒ local_start_index=0 ⇒ category 1 processed as before.
All other logic (frozen denominators, PCI, PCI/URL checks, supplier caching) unchanged.
29 Nov phase‑aware resume (full list, hybrid disabled):

The run() → _extract_supplier_products(category_urls_to_scrape, …) call has chunk_start_index=None.
_extract_supplier_products uses the original full‑list slice and global map; behaviour identical.
30 Nov hybrid resume (newly fixed case):

State: persistent_category_index = 2, hybrid ON, chunk_size_categories=1.
For chunks:
Chunk 1 (cat 1): local_start_index=len(chunk)=1 ⇒ 0 cursor_urls ⇒ correctly skipped (category already completed).
Chunk 2 (cat 2): local_start_index=0 ⇒ cat 2 processed.
Later chunks (cat 3+): local_start_index=0 ⇒ processed fully.
_category_index_map now uses _active_category_urls and maintains global indices, so:
absolute_cat_index matches global category position,
FIX D (absolute_cat_index < _start_category_index) remains valid and works with the new slicing.
The net effect is:

Hybrid behaviour with _start_category_index=1 remains the same as your 29 Nov “good” run.
Full‑list behaviour remains the same.
Hybrid+resume no longer skips everything and respects the global resume point.
If you approve this plan and these diffs, I can next apply them to tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py exactly as specified.

are you sure your plan is accruat and truly "surgucal" ( in the sense where it kept code edits to the absolute necessary and has the least risk possible in terms of causing discprencies for other steps and higher chances of addressing the issues at hand ? - basically that ts not "over egineering


Yes – given everything we’ve uncovered, this really is about as surgical and low‑risk as we can make it while still actually fixing the bug.

Here’s why I’m confident it’s accurate and not over‑engineered:

Scope is tightly bounded

Only one file is touched: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.
Only two methods are changed in a meaningful way:
_extract_supplier_products (signature + cursor + index‑map logic),
_run_hybrid_processing_mode (one assignment + the param passed into _extract_supplier_products).
No new classes, no new config, no new state keys.
Behaviour preserved by design

Non‑hybrid / full‑list calls still take the exact old path:
chunk_start_index=None → same category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:] slice.
Fresh hybrid runs with _start_category_index=1 behave like your 29 Nov “good” runs:
For the first chunk, global start = 1, slice from 0 → identical to today.
We do not touch:
How products are scraped,
Amazon extraction,
Financial calculations,
Frozen denominators, PCI/URL mismatch logging, or phase management.
Fixes are minimal but necessary

The empty‑chunk bug can’t be fixed without:
Telling _extract_supplier_products where each chunk sits globally (chunk_start_index), and
Adjusting the slice so global _start_category_index is mapped into that chunk.
The index‑map bug (your _category_index_map finding) can’t be fixed without:
Ensuring _category_index_map is built from the global category list, not the chunk.
That’s the small priority change on src_urls + reinforcing _active_category_urls in hybrid mode.
Anything less (e.g., only changing the slice, or only the map) would still leave one of the two failure modes in place.
We deliberately avoided heavier options

We didn’t:
Remove or redesign hybrid mode,
Move resume logic out to a new “controller” function,
Introduce new state fields or config options.
Those would all be more invasive and higher‑risk compared to adjusting the two existing functions that already own this logic.
Cross‑checked against three “truths”

Current code structure,
Backup version (same hybrid pattern),
Actual logs from 29 Nov and 30 Nov.
The planned changes are justified directly by mismatches we saw in those logs (empty cursor_urls and chunk‑local vs global absolute_cat_index), and they’re constructed to leave all other behaviour intact.
So, in short: yes, I’m confident this plan is both accurate and surgical—it changes only what’s strictly needed to make hybrid+resume behave like your 29 Nov expectations, without over‑engineering or reshaping the rest of the workflow.

i want you to proceed with the below plan, and once you are done, you will trace back your steps GO THROUGH THE SCRIPT AND TELL ME IF THE FIXES HAVE BEEN CORRECTLY ACRUATELY AND SURGICALLY IMPELMETED.AND OF COURSE IF THAT WAS DONE IN THE CORRECT SECITONS OF THE SCRIPT. MAKE SURE NO OTHER SECTIONS WERE INCORRECTLY UPDATED (nd make sure that everything was infact integrated. )

MAKE SURE TO SRGICALLY IMPELMETED THE DIFFS, AND ONLY EDIT THE REELVATNSECTIONS OF THE SCRIPT ( NOTHING MORE NOTHING LESS).

3.2. Hybrid‑aware cursor slicing in _extract_supplier_products

File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (the cursor section you saw in your logs, around lines 5125–5145).

Current code:

    #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
    # Use cursor-adjusted category URLs starting from session_resume_cursor
    cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]  # Adjust for 1-based indexing
    self.log.info(f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {self._start_category_index}")
Replace with:

*** Diff 2: hybrid-aware cursor slicing

@@

   #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
   # Use cursor-adjusted category URLs starting from session_resume_cursor
   cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index-1:]  # Adjust for 1-based indexing
   self.log.info(f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {self._start_category_index}")
   #  REVISED: Start enumeration from cursor, not from beginning
   # Full-list path (non-hybrid): use _start_category_index directly.
   # Hybrid path: map the global _start_category_index into the chunk using chunk_start_index.
   if chunk_start_index is None:
       # Non-hybrid / full-list call: preserve existing behaviour
       cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index - 1 :]  # 1-based -> 0-based
       enum_start_index = self._start_category_index
   else:
       # Hybrid call: chunk_start_index is the global 1-based index of the first category in this chunk.
       global_start = self._start_category_index
       chunk_first = chunk_start_index
       chunk_last = chunk_start_index + len(category_urls) - 1
       if global_start <= chunk_first:
           # Resume index is at or before this chunk: process from the first category in the chunk.
           local_start_index = 0
       elif global_start > chunk_last:
           # Resume index is after this chunk: nothing to do in this chunk.
           local_start_index = len(category_urls)
       else:
           # Resume index falls inside this chunk: skip categories before it.
           local_start_index = global_start - chunk_first
       cursor_urls = category_urls[local_start_index:]
       # Logging: show the first global category index considered for this chunk.
       enum_start_index = max(global_start, chunk_first)
   self.log.info(
       f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {enum_start_index}"
   )
Effect:

Full‑list (non‑chunked) calls behave exactly as today (chunk_start_index=None).
Hybrid calls now correctly:
Do nothing for chunks entirely before PCI,
Start mid‑chunk for the chunk that contains PCI,
Run fully for chunks after PCI.
This directly fixes the “0 categories starting from index 2” symptom in your 30 Nov log.

3.3. Keep category indices global by preferring _active_category_urls

File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (the _category_index_map block inside _extract_supplier_products, around lines 5175–5181):

Current code:

            if not hasattr(self, "_category_index_map"):
                # Prefer the list already in-scope; otherwise fall back to cached/global sources
                src_urls = (category_urls
                            or getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", [])
                            or (self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or []))
                self._category_index_map = {normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(src_urls)}
            absolute_cat_index = self._category_index_map.get(normalize_url(category_url), 1)
Replace with:

*** Diff 3: global category index mapping

@@

           if not hasattr(self, "_category_index_map"):
               # Prefer the list already in-scope; otherwise fall back to cached/global sources
               src_urls = (category_urls
                           or getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", [])
                           or (self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or []))
               self._category_index_map = {normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(src_urls)}
           if not hasattr(self, "_category_index_map"):
               # Prefer the authoritative global list over any per-chunk list
               src_urls = (getattr(self, "_active_category_urls", [])
                           or category_urls
                           or (self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or []))
               self._category_index_map = {normalize_url(u): i + 1 for i, u in enumerate(src_urls)}
           absolute_cat_index = self._category_index_map.get(normalize_url(category_url), 1)
Effect:

In normal runs, run() sets:

category_urls = self._get_predefined_categories(self.supplier_name) or []
self._active_category_urls = category_urls
(see run() around line 1985), so:

src_urls becomes the full category list,

_category_index_map maps URLs → global indices,

absolute_cat_index is global, and the skip check:

if absolute_cat_index < getattr(self, "_start_category_index", 1):
compares global vs global, as intended.

In hybrid mode, even though category_urls is a chunk, this change ensures we don’t build the map from the chunk (which is the misbehaviour you correctly flagged in your analysis).

# Hybrid path: map the global _start_category_index into the chunk using chunk_start_index.
if chunk_start_index is None:
    # Non-hybrid / full-list call: preserve existing behaviour
    cursor_urls = category_urls[self._start_category_index - 1 :]  # 1-based -> 0-based
    enum_start_index = self._start_category_index
else:
    # Hybrid call: chunk_start_index is the global 1-based index of the first category in this chunk.
    global_start = self._start_category_index
    chunk_first = chunk_start_index
    chunk_last = chunk_start_index + len(category_urls) - 1

    if global_start <= chunk_first:
        # Resume index is at or before this chunk: process from the first category in the chunk.
        local_start_index = 0
    elif global_start > chunk_last:
        # Resume index is after this chunk: nothing to do in this chunk.
        local_start_index = len(category_urls)
    else:
        # Resume index falls inside this chunk: skip categories before it.
        local_start_index = global_start - chunk_first

    cursor_urls = category_urls[local_start_index:]
    # Logging: show the first global category index considered for this chunk.
    enum_start_index = max(global_start, chunk_first)

self.log.info(
    f" ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: {len(cursor_urls)} categories starting from index {enum_start_index}"
)
The old two lines (cursor_urls = … and the original self.log.info) are gone; only this block is present.

A. Amazon Resumption Bug (Critical)

Triangular analysis (3 sources of truth)

supplier_products_needing_extraction: 53
supplier_products_completed: 53
amazon_products_needing_analysis: 53
amazon_products_completed: 33
Interpretation: First run finished all supplier extraction for category 1 and had done 33/53 Amazon analyses; 20 Amazon products were still pending. Phase is correctly persisted as amazon_analysis.

Resume run log (run_custom_poundwholesale_20251130_050820.log)
Startup:

START MODE: is_fresh_start=False, pci=1, session_cursor=1, url=...Baby-Socks-and-Booties...
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=1/326 ... prod_idx=33/53

This matches _run_amazon_phase_from_resume: saved_amazon_offset=33, total=19 → clamped_amazon_offset=19.

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...Baby-Socks-and-Booties size=19 allowed_gate=off (second rebuild)

Code path (_run_amazon_phase_from_resume and finalizer)
File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

cat_idx = int(getattr(rp, "cat_idx", 1))
prod_offset = int(getattr(rp, "prod_idx", 0))

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
total = len(products)  # becomes 19 after filtering

# Denominator mismatch: updates amazon_products_needing_analysis to 19
sp_update["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] = total

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
saved_amazon_offset = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0))  # 33 from state
clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(saved_amazon_offset, 0), total)    # min(33,19) = 19
if saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    ... log Clamp ...  # matches log line
    prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset  # now 19

batch_size = ...  # typically 100
start_batch = prod_offset // batch_size    # 19 // 100 = 0
first_offset = prod_offset % batch_size    # 19
total_batches = ceil(total / batch_size)   # ceil(19 / 100) = 1

for batch_num in range(start_batch, total_batches):  # range(0,1)
    ...
    start_i = first_offset if batch_num == start_batch else 0  # start_i = 19
    for i in range(start_i, len(batch_products)):  # range(19, len=batch_products=19)
        # NO iterations; _analyze_with_amazon never runs
Finalizer:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
total = len(products)                   # still 19
done = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)  # 19

if (total == 0 and supplier_count == 0) or (total > 0 and done >= total):
    self._finalize_category_completion(category_url, int(cat_idx))
    self.state_manager.commit_phase_switch(new_phase="supplier")
This matches exactly what the log shows: 0 processed in the loop, but done == total == 19, so the category is finalized and phase switches to supplier.

Net effect (confirmed bug)

3 sources (first-run state, resumed log, code) all agree:
At the time of interruption, category 1 had 20 Amazon products still pending.
On resume, the system:
shrank the queue to 19,
clamped amazon_products_completed to 19,
iterated zero products (because first_offset == total),
then treated category 1 as fully complete and moved to category 2.
This is exactly the bug you described: remaining Amazon products for the interrupted category were skipped and the system started Amazon work on the next category.
B. Root Cause (Amazon resume)

Semantic mismatch of counters when denominator shrinks.

amazon_products_completed in state is stored relative to the original denominator (53).
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume:
shrinks amazon_products_needing_analysis to the new total=19 based on the rebuilt queue.
then claps saved_amazon_offset (33) down to the new total (19) and writes that back to amazon_products_completed.
This effectively reinterprets “33 out of 53 done” as “19 out of 19 done”, which means:
prod_offset == total
the resume loop doesn’t run,
finalizer sees done >= total and finalizes the category.
Loop indexing expects offset < total.

The resume logic uses prod_offset both as:
the “last completed index” (for [RESUME_HONORED]), and
the starting offset for the next item (first_offset).
That assumption only holds if prod_offset < total. Once clamped to total, first_offset == len(batch_products) and the loop runs zero iterations.
No per-item Amazon commits in this resume.

Because the loop is empty, commit_amazon_progress is never called for category 1 in the resumed run, but amazon_products_completed has already been changed to 19 and used to force completion.
C. Suggested Fix (Amazon resume) – high-level, no code changes yet

To meet your requirement—“resume exactly where it left off in Amazon phase, never skip remaining products”—the safest, minimal approach is:

Treat denominator alignment and resume offset separately.

When frozen_denom and total differ, it is unsafe to simply clamp amazon_products_completed and treat that as “done”.
Instead, for Amazon resume when denominator shrinks:
Either:
Reset the offset: if saved_amazon_offset >= total, set:
prod_offset = 0
sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
and reprocess the queue of total items. This guarantees no skip, at the cost of reprocessing.
Or:
Use a safer mapping rule (e.g., start from max(0, total - N) for some small N) but the simplest and safest is full reprocess.
Ensure prod_offset < total before entering the loop.

After any adjustment, enforce:

if prod_offset >= total:
    prod_offset = 0
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
This guarantees that:

start_batch and first_offset are within bounds,
the inner loop will actually run.
Only finalize a category when the loop has had a chance to run.

After the loop, the finalizer’s condition:

if (total == 0 and supplier_count == 0) or (total > 0 and done >= total):
    _finalize_category_completion(...)
becomes safe again because done will be based on actual commit_amazon_progress calls for the current total, not on a clamped legacy value.

This change is local to _run_amazon_phase_from_resume and uses existing state fields. It does not touch hybrid chunking or supplier phases and is consistent with your preference for minimal, surgical fixes.

E. Summary

The hybrid chunk/resume fixes you approved are behaving as designed and have resolved the earlier “skip all categories after resume” problem at the category level.
There remains a separate, critical resume bug in the Amazon phase:
When the Amazon queue shrinks on resume (denominator mismatch), the current logic clamps the prior amazon_products_completed count to the new queue length, causing the system to see the queue as fully processed and finalize the category without actually iterating the remaining items.
The proposed fix for this Amazon resume issue is to adjust _run_amazon_phase_from_resume so that:
In the mismatch case where saved_amazon_offset >= total, it resets the offset (e.g., to 0) instead of clamping to total, ensuring the resume loop actually processes the entire rebuilt queue before the finalizer is allowed to mark the category as complete.
If you’d like, I can next draft a small, surgical diff plan just for _run_amazon_phase_from_resume to address this Amazon resume bug, similar in style to the earlier hybrid fixes—without touching any other parts of the workflow.

2025-11-29 11:35:48,359 - utils.fixed_enhanced_state_manager - INFO - RESUME PTR: phase=supplier cat_idx=1/326 url=https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Socks-and-Booties prod_idx=0/19

I’ll split this into three parts: (1) what exactly goes wrong in Amazon resume, with concrete numbers, (2) how products in multiple categories fit into this, and (3) why supplier resume works and how the Amazon fix would mirror it.

1. Exact cause of the Amazon‑phase resume bug (with concrete numbers)

Using the state and logs you gave for Baby-Socks-and-Booties:

End of first run (saved state) – first run angel state.json

"system_progression": {
  "current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
  "persistent_category_index": 1,
  "current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Socks-and-Booties",
  "supplier_products_needing_extraction": 53,
  "supplier_products_completed": 53,
  "amazon_products_needing_analysis": 53,
  "amazon_products_completed": 33,
  ...
}
This means:

Category 1 (Baby-Socks-and-Booties) had 53 products.
Supplier extraction: 53/53 done.
Amazon analysis: 33/53 done → 20 products still need Amazon analysis.
Phase is correctly recorded as amazon_analysis.
Start of second run (resume) – run_custom_poundwholesale_20251130_050820.log

Startup lines:

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=1/326 ... prod_idx=33/53
RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: Starting from category index 1 at product 33 in phase 'amazon_analysis'.
PHASE-AWARE GATING: Routing to Amazon analysis phase
So the system explicitly decides: “Category 1, resume at product index 33 in Amazon phase”.

Inside Amazon resume logic (_run_amazon_phase_from_resume in code)**

The code does roughly this for the current category:

Rebuild the Amazon queue for that category:

Logs show it ends up with 19 products in the queue:

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...Baby-Socks-and-Booties size=19 allowed_gate=off
DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED:
  Frozen denominator: 53
  Actual queue size:  19
  Difference: 34 products
  Action: Aligning state to actual queue size (19)
So now:

total = 19 (queue size)
amazon_products_needing_analysis is updated to 19 in system_progression.
Read the saved Amazon offset from state:

From state: amazon_products_completed = 33.

The code does:

saved_amazon_offset = 33
clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(33, 0), total=19)  # => 19
if saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset  # 19
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 19
After this:

prod_offset = 19
amazon_products_completed is overwritten to 19.
In other words, “33/53 done” is now treated as “19/19 done” on the shrunken queue.

Calculate where to start in the queue:

With total = 19, prod_offset = 19 and typical batch_size (e.g. 100):

start_batch = prod_offset // batch_size = 19 // 100 = 0
first_offset = prod_offset % batch_size = 19
total_batches = ceil(19 / 100) = 1
The log shows this:

AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=1 total=19 start_batch=0 first_offset=19
[RESUME_ENTER] cat_idx=1 total=19 ptr=19 start_batch=0 first_offset=19
Loop over the queue:

The loop is:

for batch_num in range(start_batch, total_batches):  # range(0,1)
    batch_products = products[start_idx:end_idx]  # len(batch_products) = 19
    start_i = first_offset if batch_num == start_batch else 0  # 19
    for i in range(start_i, len(batch_products)):  # range(19, 19) -> empty
        ...
        await self._analyze_with_amazon(p)
        self.state_manager.commit_amazon_progress(...)
Because start_i == len(batch_products) == 19, the inner loop runs zero iterations.
No Amazon products are analyzed in this resumed call.
There are no [PROGRESS_COMMIT] logs for category 1 in this second run.
Finalizer decides the category is complete:

After the loop, it re-checks the queue:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)  # still 19
total = 19
done = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)  # 19 (we set it earlier)
if total > 0 and done >= total:
    _finalize_category_completion(...)
    commit_phase_switch(new_phase="supplier")
This matches the logs:

MARK_CATEGORY_COMPLETED CALLED
CATEGORY_COMPLETION_CALL: url=...Baby-Socks-and-Booties absolute_cat_index=1
CATEGORY_INDEX_TRACKER: Category completed ... next pci=2
PHASE TRANSITION: amazon_analysis  supplier
PHASE SWITCH: amazon_analysis  supplier (via resume finalizer)
RESUME COMPLETE: continuing main loop at cat_idx=2 phase=supplier
Simplified summary with numbers

Before interruption: 53 products in category, Amazon had processed 33, so 20 remain.
On resume:
The queue rebuild yields 19 items (likely due to category/queue filtering and/or dedup).
The code:
Changes amazon_products_completed from 33 to 19,
Uses 19 both as “last completed index” and as the starting offset,
So the loop never runs,
Then decides done (19) ≥ total (19) and finalizes the category.
Result: those remaining 19 products are never processed in this run, and the system jumps to category 2. That’s the exact bug.
2. How multi‑category products relate to this (and why the fix won’t create new issues)

Your note: some products appear in multiple categories. That can affect how many items are in the per‑category Amazon queue.

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, the queue is rebuilt with:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
# then filtered by category_url and allowed keys
Because of this:

If the same product appears in several categories, and the queue is deduplicated by stable_key or filtered by category, the actual per-category queue (total) may be smaller than the original frozen denominator (53).

That is exactly why we see:

Frozen denominator: 53
Actual queue size:  19
Those 34 missing entries can be due to:

Products moved out of this category’s queue into another category’s queue (multi-category reassignment),
Products excluded by the new filtering rules,
Or both.
What matters for the bug:

The bug is not that the queue is 19 instead of 20 or 53. Queue size can legitimately change when:
factors like multi-category assignments and filtering rules change.
The real bug is:
After the queue shrinks, we still trust the old amazon_products_completed value (33) and convert it into “19/19 done” by clamping.
That logic is fragile whenever queue size (total) changes, regardless of why it changed (multi-category, filtering, etc.).
Will the proposed fix break anything when products appear in multiple categories?

The fix I suggested is:

When denominator mismatch is detected and we find saved_amazon_offset >= total:

Do not treat saved_amazon_offset as “all done”.
Instead, for this category’s Amazon queue:
Set prod_offset = 0,
Set sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0,
Then reprocess the entire queue of total products for this category.
In numbers:

If queue shrinks to total=19 and you had amazon_products_completed=33:
You ignore the 33 for this per-category resume and restart Amazon analysis from index 0 for this 19‑item queue.
This is safe even when products are in multiple categories because:
The queue for each category is rebuilt and filtered per category.
If a product appears in both Baby-Socks and All-Shop-by-age, it will have its own queue position in each category’s queue, and resuming that queue from 0 simply means “analyze every item currently in this category’s queue”.
You may re‑analyze some products that were already processed in a previous run, but you won’t skip any.
So multi-category products change the size and composition of the per-category queue, but the proposed fix:

Always bases resume on the actual queue for the current category in the current run (total),
And ensures the loop starts from a valid offset (0 <= prod_offset < total), which guarantees all queued items are reachable.
3. Why supplier resume works and why we can mirror it for Amazon

Your supplier-phase log from 29 Nov (with earlier code) shows how supplier resume behaves correctly, even with complex filtering and multi-category products:

Scenario (from your log):

Category: Baby-Socks-and-Booties
Frozen denominator: 53
Linking map: 29 products already fully processed
Supplier cache: 5 products with supplier data
Need full extraction: 19 products
Supplier resume state:

RESUME PTR: phase=supplier cat_idx=1/326 ... prod_idx=34/53
...
RESUME STATE: phase=supplier ... supplier=34/53 amazon=29/53
So supplier had processed 34 of the 53 original positions.

Supplier resume logic:

The log shows the supplier side does not rely directly on the old 34/53 to skip part of the new worklist. Instead, it:

Recalculates the new worklist (19 full‑extraction products) based on current linking map + cache.

Clamps the old “saved supplier offset” into the new worklist as a local index; but because that offset is too large, it resets to 0:

WARNING - Clamp supplier offset: saved=34  start=0 (len=19)
Treats this as “start from beginning of the new 19‑item worklist”:

RESUME CATEGORY: category 1 at resume point, total products=19, resume index=0, remaining=19
PROCEEDING WITH EXTRACTION: 19 products need full extraction
FULL CATEGORY PROCESSING: Processing all 19 products
Key differences vs current Amazon resume:

Supplier:

Builds a new list of items that need work (19).
If the old offset is beyond that new list, it sets the starting index to 0.
The loop actually processes all 19 items.
Amazon (current behaviour):

Builds a new queue (19).
Clamps the old Amazon completed count (33) to 19 and writes it into state as “completed”.
Uses 19 as the starting offset, which is equal to the queue length → no items processed.
Then uses that same 19 to declare the queue complete and finalize the category.
What the Amazon fix would do, mirroring supplier

Change Amazon resume logic so that in the mismatch case:

After you get total from the rebuilt queue:
If saved_amazon_offset >= total:
Set prod_offset = 0,
Set sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0,
Then start the Amazon loop from first_offset = 0.
That makes Amazon behave like supplier resume: when the queue has changed significantly, it trusts the new queue more than the old offset and reprocesses from the beginning of the new queue instead of treating it as complete.
This ensures:

No remaining Amazon items for the category are skipped.
Multi-category and filtering effects (which caused the queue to shrink to 19) are safely handled, because the resume loop walks the queue that actually exists now.
Summary

The Amazon resume bug is caused by clamping the old amazon_products_completed value to the new queue length and then using that clamped value as both:
the “current completed” count, and
the starting offset for the loop.
In the concrete Baby-Socks example, this turns “33/53 done, 20 left” into “19/19 done, 0 left” without actually processing any of the remaining 19 items.
Products being in multiple categories influence how many items end up in the Amazon queue for a given category, but the fundamental bug is in how the resume offset is interpreted after the queue shrinks.
The fix is to make Amazon resume behave like supplier resume:
after rebuilding the queue, if the old offset is beyond the new queue length, reset the Amazon offset for that category to 0 and reprocess the actual queue, rather than treating it as complete.

1. How the indexes behave today (supplier vs Amazon)

I’ll use the Baby‑Socks example with the real numbers you’ve seen.

Supplier phase (behaviour you like)

First discovery for Baby‑Socks:

53 URLs discovered in that category.

State stores:

supplier_products_needing_extraction = 53
supplier_products_completed increases as you scrape; at one point it was 34/53.
After filtering on resume (from your 29 Nov supplier log):

Total URLs extracted: 53
Linking map (skip): 29
Supplier cache: 5
Need full extraction: 19
...
EXTRACTION NEEDED: 19/53 = 35.8% need fresh extraction
...
Clamp supplier offset: saved=34  start=0 (len=19)
PROCEEDING WITH EXTRACTION: 19 products need full extraction
FULL CATEGORY PROCESSING: Processing all 19 products
Conceptually, two layers of “indexes” exist:

Global discovered denominator (53)

Stored in system_progression.supplier_products_needing_extraction and in frozen_category_denominators.
Used for high‑level progress prints like supplier=34/53.
Filtered worklist denominator (19)

Derived at runtime from linking map + cache.
Supplier resume logic then:
clamps the old offset (34) against the new list length (19),
but in your log it decides to start at index 0 for this new 19‑item list,
then processes 0..18 over that list.
In the saved state (e.g. first run angel state.json), you see:

system_progression.supplier_products_needing_extraction = 53 (global)
supplier_extraction_progress.supplier_products_needing_extraction = 17, supplier_products_completed = 2 (17+2=19) (filtered worklist).
So for supplier:

System‑level fields (under system_progression) keep the original 53‑based semantics.
A separate progress subtree tracks the current filtered worklist (19‑based semantics).
The loops themselves resume using the filtered denominator (19), not the original 53.
Amazon phase (current behaviour that’s wrong)

For Baby‑Socks at the end of the first run (from first run angel state.json):

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 53
amazon_products_completed = 33
→ 33/53 done, 20 remaining.
On resume (050820.log):

The Amazon resume builds a category queue and finds only 19 items for Baby‑Socks:

CATEGORY QUEUE: ... size=19 allowed_gate=off
DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED:
  Frozen denominator: 53
  Actual queue size:  19
  ...
  Action: Aligning state to actual queue size (19)
The code then does:

total = 19

saved_amazon_offset = 33 (from state)

clamped_amazon_offset = min(33, 19) = 19

Sets:

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 19 (overwriting 53)
amazon_products_completed = 19
Then it computes:

start_batch = 19 // batch_size (0)

first_offset = 19

Loop runs with start_i = 19 and len(batch_products) = 19 → no iterations.

Finalizer sees:

total = 19
done = amazon_products_completed = 19
and finalizes the category as complete without analyzing any of those 19.

So for Amazon right now:

The system‑level fields under system_progression are being repurposed to the filtered semantics (19) and, worse, clamped in a way that makes the queue look fully processed.
That’s the key difference:

Supplier: global 53 preserved; filtered 19 used in a way that still walks all 19 items.
Amazon: filtered 19 used, but amazon_products_completed is clamped to 19 and used as both “already done” and “starting offset”, so nothing runs.
2. How multi‑category products affect this

You asked specifically whether products appearing in multiple categories were part of the root cause.

The Amazon queue for a category is built via _rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url) and then filtered by category_url and allowed keys.

If a product exists in multiple categories (common in AngelWholesale), dedup and filtering can reduce the per‑category queue size versus the original “53 discovered URLs”:

e.g. a product might be attached to both Baby‑Socks and a generic “All Shop-by-age” category.
When building the Baby‑Socks queue, the filter ensures only Baby‑Socks entries remain; others removed.
That’s what causes the mismatch:

Original count (53) vs current queue (19).
But regardless of why the queue shrank (multi‑category assignment, filtering rules, or both), the real bug is:

the resume logic assumes it can take the old amazon_products_completed value (33) and reinterpret it against the new queue size (19) via clamping, which is unsafe once the queue changes.
The fix I’ll propose does not depend on why the queue shrank; it only enforces that:

the resume offset is always within the current queue (0 <= prod_offset < total),
and that amazon_products_completed reflects actual work done on the current queue, not a clamped legacy value.
So multi‑category products change queue size, but the fix is robust to that: rebuilt queue size is treated as authoritative, and the offset is adjusted to ensure we actually process the queue.

3. Proposed Amazon‑resume fix (surgical diff + state behaviour)

Goal:

Keep behaviour for the “easy” case (no queue shrink) unchanged.
When the queue shrinks enough that the saved offset is beyond the new end, restart the Amazon queue for that category from 0, instead of treating it as complete.
We change only a small block in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume inside tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py. Do not apply this yet; this is just the proposed diff.

Current block (simplified):

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
saved_amazon_offset = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)) if isinstance(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0), (int, float)) else int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(saved_amazon_offset, 0), total)
if saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    self.log.warning(
        f" Clamp amazon offset: saved={saved_amazon_offset}  start={clamped_amazon_offset} (len={total})"
    )
    prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset

-        clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(saved_amazon_offset, 0), total)
-        if saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
-            self.log.warning(

-            )
-            prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset
-            sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
+        sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
+        raw_completed = sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)
+        saved_amazon_offset = int(raw_completed) if isinstance(raw_completed, (int, float)) else int(raw_completed or 0)
+        clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(saved_amazon_offset, 0), total)
+
+        if total == 0:
+            # Empty queue: nothing to resume; normal logic will handle completion check.
+            prod_offset = 0
+            sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0

+            # Queue has shrunk below the saved offset; restart this category's Amazon queue from 0.
+            self.log.warning(
+                f" AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset {saved_amazon_offset} >= queue length {total}; restarting from 0"
+            )
+            prod_offset = 0
+            sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
+        elif saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
+            # Slight mismatch (e.g., negative or just beyond bounds): clamp and continue.
+            self.log.warning(
+                f" Clamp amazon offset: saved={saved_amazon_offset}  start={clamped_amazon_offset} (len={total})"
+            )
+            prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset
+            sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
+        else:
+            # Saved offset is valid within the current queue; honour it.
+            prod_offset = saved_amazon_offset
What this does, with the Baby‑Socks numbers

For Baby‑Socks on resume:

total = 19 (queue size after filtering),
saved_amazon_offset = 33.
We hit the elif saved_amazon_offset >= total branch:

Log: AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 33 >= queue length 19; restarting from 0
Set:
prod_offset = 0,
amazon_products_completed = 0.
Then:

start_batch = 0
first_offset = 0
Loop iterates all items in the 19‑item queue (0..18), calling _analyze_with_amazon and commit_amazon_progress on each.
Finalizer:

After the loop, amazon_products_completed will be incremented to 19 by commit_amazon_progress.
Rebuild queue (still 19), so:
total = 19
done = 19
Finalizer condition (total > 0 and done >= total) is true only after we actually processed the queue, and the category is correctly marked as completed and phase switched to supplier.
How state indexes “behave” after this change

For Amazon with queue mismatch (like Baby‑Socks):

Right before processing:

system_progression.amazon_products_needing_analysis will be set to the current queue size (19).
(This is already happening today in the mismatch block.)
system_progression.amazon_products_completed will be reset to 0 for that category.
During processing:

amazon_products_completed increments from 0 up to total (19) as each item is processed.
So:

In the resumed run, Amazon progress for that category is expressed as 0..19 rather than continuing from 33..53.
Across the two runs, your overall story is:
First run: 33/53 for Amazon on that category.
Second run: 0..19 on the new, cleaned queue; all those are processed.
This mirrors supplier behaviour where:

You had 53 discovered, but for the filtered worklist you tracked 0..19 again on resume.
It does mean:

For Amazon, the system_progression fields become tied to the current filtered queue after mismatch, not the original global denominator—but that’s already the direction the code was taking.
The difference is that now those fields will consistently reflect real work on the current queue, instead of a clamped legacy value that can make the queue appear fully processed when it isn’t.
Given your note that there’s no “right” answer between “stick with 53 + offset” vs “normalize to 19”, and that your top priority is never skipping remaining work, this approach is:

Surgical (one small block in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume),
Keeps supplier semantics intact,
Aligns Amazon resume with how supplier resume behaves when the worklist changes (restart from 0 in the new queue),
And is robust to multi‑category products causing queue size changes.

I started from the existing implementation in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume and its finalizer, not from a blank design.

I explicitly compared with the supplier resume logic (from your 29 Nov log and the current supplier code) and used that as the reference pattern, so we’re aligning Amazon behaviour to a path that already exists and is known to work.

1. Goal of this implementation plan

Fix the Amazon-phase resume bug where the system:
Resumes in amazon_analysis for category 1 (Baby-Socks-and-Booties),
Rebuilds a smaller queue (19 items),
Clamps amazon_products_completed=33 down to 19,
Then skips all remaining Amazon work and marks the category complete.
Keep the fix local and surgical inside the existing Amazon resume function.
Preserve:
Supplier resume behaviour (which already correctly resumes from a recalculated worklist),
Normal Amazon resume when the queue length hasn’t changed much.
No code edits are made yet; this is the exact plan and diffs you can choose to apply.

2. Intended index behaviour in processing state after the fix

For Amazon when the queue has changed (e.g. from 53 down to 19):

system_progression.amazon_products_needing_analysis will be updated to the current queue size (19).
This is already happening as part of the denominator mismatch handling.

system_progression.amazon_products_completed will be treated as follows:

If the previously saved value (saved_amazon_offset) is within the new queue (0 <= saved < total):
It will be used as the starting offset (prod_offset = saved), and the loop will resume from that position.
If saved_amazon_offset >= total (the Baby‑Socks case: 33 vs new total 19):
We will reset for that category’s Amazon queue:
prod_offset = 0
amazon_products_completed = 0
Then the loop will process all total items (0..total‑1), and amazon_products_completed will be incremented by commit_amazon_progress as each product is analyzed.
So:

Across runs, the global story for that category will be:
First run: 33/53 (old denominator), no skipping because the first 33 are done.
Resume run: 0..19 on the new queue (19 items), all processed in this run.
Within a single resumed run, Amazon indexes stored in system_progression will be consistent with the current filtered queue length; the system will no longer interpret a clamped offset as “already completed”.
This mirrors how supplier behaves when the worklist changes: supplier resumes from 0 on the new 19‑item list and processes all 19; Amazon will now do the same for its queue when needed.

3. Surgical fix plan (single function, minimal changes)

We modify only a small block inside _run_amazon_phase_from_resume in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.

Context in current code

Inside _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, after rebuilding the queue and handling denominator mismatch, you currently have:

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
saved_amazon_offset = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)) if isinstance(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0), (int, float)) else int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(saved_amazon_offset, 0), total)
if saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    self.log.warning(
        f" Clamp amazon offset: saved={saved_amazon_offset}  start={clamped_amazon_offset} (len={total})"
    )
    prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
And earlier at the top of the function:

cat_idx = int(getattr(rp, "cat_idx", 1))
prod_offset = int(getattr(rp, "prod_idx", 0))

We keep the structure but make the offset logic robust to queue shrinkage:

How this behaves in the Baby‑Socks scenario

total = 19 (queue size),

saved_amazon_offset = 33,

saved_amazon_offset >= total → triggers the “safety restart” branch:

Logs:

AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 33 >= queue length 19; restarting from 0
Sets:

prod_offset = 0
amazon_products_completed = 0
Loop then processes all 19 items in the queue (index 0 to 18), with commit_amazon_progress updating amazon_products_completed as usual.

Finalizer logic remains unchanged, but now:

After processing, amazon_products_completed reflects the number of items actually processed for the current queue (19),
So the category is only finalized as complete after the remaining work has been done.
Non‑mismatch scenarios

If total is equal or close to the original denominator and saved_amazon_offset < total:

The saved_amazon_offset >= total branch is skipped,
If any minor clamp is needed (e.g., negative values), we use the elif saved != clamped branch,
Otherwise, prod_offset = saved_amazon_offset and the existing resume behaviour is preserved.
Impact on processing state

After this change:

system_progression.amazon_products_needing_analysis continues to be updated to the actual queue size for the current category (this already happens in the denominator mismatch block).
system_progression.amazon_products_completed:
will be reset to 0 only when the old offset is beyond the new queue,
then incremented by commit_amazon_progress on each processed item.
For progress tracking:

Supplier continues to use its existing “53 discovered / 19 filtered” pattern:
system‑level fields for the global denominator,
filtered counters for the current worklist.
Amazon will behave similarly when queues change:
store the current queue length in state,
and track amazon_products_completed relative to that queue for the resumed run,
without ever declaring a queue complete without actually processing the items.

is the below suggestion enough or not ? if not, then state the reason, what it would do and so on

Current Problematic Code:

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
saved_amazon_offset = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)) if isinstance(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0), (int, float)) else int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(saved_amazon_offset, 0), total)
if saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
self.log.warning(
f" Clamp amazon offset: saved={saved_amazon_offset} start={clamped_amazon_offset} (len={total})"
)
prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset
sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
Suggested Fix:

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
saved_amazon_offset = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)) if isinstance(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0), (int, float)) else int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)

    # FIX: If saved offset is beyond current total, it means the queue has changed/shrunk.
    # We must reset to 0 to ensure we process the products that ARE in the queue.
    if saved_amazon_offset > total:
        self.log.warning(
            f" OFFSET MISMATCH: saved={saved_amazon_offset} > total={total}. Resetting offset to 0 to ensure processing."
        )
        prod_offset = 0
        sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
    else:
        prod_offset = max(saved_amazon_offset, 0)

It’s a good start, but it’s not enough by itself.

What your suggested change does well

For the exact scenario we’re seeing now (Baby‑Socks):

Before: saved_amazon_offset = 33, total = 19

With your fix:

if saved_amazon_offset > total:
    # 33 > 19 -> true
    prod_offset = 0
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
else:
    prod_offset = max(saved_amazon_offset, 0)
Result: prod_offset becomes 0, so the Amazon resume loop will process all 19 items instead of skipping them.

So, for this > total case, your fix correctly avoids skipping the queue.

Why it’s not fully safe

There are still mismatch cases it does not handle:

Saved offset equal to new total (saved == total)

Example:

First run: amazon_products_needing_analysis = 53, amazon_products_completed = 33.
For whatever reason (multi‑category reassignments, stricter filtering, dedup), the rebuilt Amazon queue now has total = 33.
State still has saved_amazon_offset = 33.
With your fix:

if saved_amazon_offset > total:   # 33 > 33 -> false
    ...
else:
    prod_offset = max(saved_amazon_offset, 0)  # 33
prod_offset is set to 33, which equals total.
The loop uses for i in range(prod_offset, total) so it runs from 33 to 33 → no iterations.
Finalizer sees done == total and finalizes the category without actually processing any of the 33 items in the current queue.
This is the same structural problem we saw with 33/19; it just shows up when the new length matches the old completed count.

So the bug is not only “saved > total”; it’s “saved ≥ total” once the queue has changed composition.

State consistency and logging

In the current code, when it clamps, it also updates amazon_products_completed to the clamped value and logs the clamp.

Your version:

Updates the state only when saved_amazon_offset > total (not when it’s negative), and drops the clamp log.
That’s not inherently wrong, but it slightly weakens the observability and leaves amazon_products_completed un-normalised in some edge cases.
What your fix guarantees

It does fix the exact Baby‑Socks bug (33 vs 19).
It does not guarantee safety for all denominator mismatch scenarios, especially when saved_amazon_offset == total after a queue shrink.
What’s missing to make it robust

At minimum, we need:

To treat saved_amazon_offset >= total as the “queue changed, restart this category’s Amazon queue” case, not just > total:

if saved_amazon_offset >= total:
    # queue shrank or aligns exactly with old completed count; restart safely
    prod_offset = 0
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
else:
    prod_offset = max(saved_amazon_offset, 0)
And ideally keep or adapt the clamp log so we can see when this path is taken.

This still stays in the same small block, built on the existing implementation, and keeps the fix surgical, but makes sure:

We never start the loop at prod_offset == total (which inevitably leads to zero iterations and premature completion), regardless of whether saved > total or saved == total.
If you want, I can rewrite the full diff using this stricter >= total condition and preserving a simple warning log, still within the same block.

if we were to implement the "missing" opoints making it robust, which of the sugested plan/fixes would you suggest we use and why


I’d go with the slightly more explicit version I proposed (the one with the total == 0, saved >= total, saved != clamped branches), not the minimal “if saved > total” variant.

Why that version is safer and still surgical

Covers all problematic cases, not just the one we saw

Handles saved > total (your Baby‑Socks case).
Also handles saved == total, which leads to the same “loop doesn’t run” problem.
Handles negative or malformed values by clamping and logging.
Keeps behaviour unchanged when things are clean

When 0 ≤ saved < total and the queue hasn’t really changed, it just sets prod_offset = saved and keeps current behaviour.
It only resets to 0 when the saved offset is clearly incompatible with the new queue (saved >= total).
Keeps observability

Logs a clear AMAZON RESUME SAFETY when it has to restart from 0.
Keeps a Clamp amazon offset warning for small mismatches.
Minimal blast radius

Entire change is confined to the existing clamping block in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume.
No new fields, no changes to other phases, and no new control paths elsewhere.
If you want the shortest robust version, the core rule is:

Use saved_amazon_offset >= total as the trigger for “restart this queue from 0” (reset counter and offset), not just >.
My branchy version just makes that rule explicit and keeps good logging and handling of edge cases, which is why I’d choose it over the simpler if saved > total tweak.

The new Amazon‑resume safety fix is integrated only in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, replacing the old clamp block while preserving surrounding logic.

Behaviour now:

The per‑category Amazon queue is still rebuilt and filtered (total is the current queue length).
amazon_products_needing_analysis is still aligned to total when a denominator mismatch is detected.
The resume offset is derived from system_progression.amazon_products_completed but handled as follows:
total == 0 → prod_offset = 0, amazon_products_completed = 0, finalizer decides if category should complete.
saved_amazon_offset >= total (e.g. saved=33, total=19 or saved=19, total=19) → log AMAZON RESUME SAFETY, reset this category’s Amazon progress to 0 (prod_offset = 0, amazon_products_completed = 0) and reprocess the entire queue.
0 <= saved_amazon_offset < total, but needs clamping (e.g. negative, malformed) → clamp and log Clamp amazon offset, then resume from the clamped value.
Otherwise → honour the saved offset and resume from that position.
The loop then uses this prod_offset to compute start_batch and first_offset, guaranteeing 0 ≤ prod_offset < total in all resume scenarios where the queue is non‑empty, so there is always at least a potential iteration.
This keeps the rest of the workflow intact:

commit_amazon_progress in utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py is unchanged; it still:
increments amazon_products_completed,
writes the per‑item pointer (queue_idx) and per‑category context,
emits first‑after‑resume proofs.
The Amazon finalizer at the end of _run_amazon_phase_from_resume still:
rebuilds the queue for the category,
checks total and done = amazon_products_completed,
only finalizes the category when the queue is actually empty or all queue items have been processed.
Net effect relative to the bug:

In denominator‑mismatch cases (like Baby‑Socks where queue shrinks from 53 to 19), the system will no longer interpret the old offset as “everything done”.
Instead, it restarts Amazon processing for the current category’s queue from 0 if the saved offset is at or beyond the new queue length, ensuring no remaining products are skipped.
In clean cases where the queue size hasn’t changed and the saved offset is within bounds, behaviour is unchanged: resume starts from the saved offset.
I traced the workflow and related files:

Only _run_amazon_phase_from_resume’s offset logic was changed; all supplier resume logic, hybrid chunking, state writes, and finalization rules are untouched.
The change does not introduce any new callers or new state fields, and it stays compatible with the existing resume pointer (rp.prod_idx) and system_progression structure.
Given these constraints, the fix is as surgical as possible while closing the skip‑on‑resume gap for Amazon analysis.

Here’s the concise, file‑grounded summary.

1. How the 53 → 19 index change behaves

Using the two state files and the 090352/090519 logs:

After the fresh run (Baby‑Socks, first run – reflected in your description, not in the two JSON snapshots above):

amazon_products_needing_analysis ≈ 53
amazon_products_completed ≈ 38
Log 090352:
Supplier phase: prod_idx=0/53 → prod_idx=53/53
ISS‑004 runs once:
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 17 → 12 products
Amazon phase runs and is interrupted at prod_idx=38/53.
After the resumption run (secondrun-categoy1.json + 090519.log):

system_progression now has:
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 19
"amazon_products_completed": 3
"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 19
"supplier_products_completed": 53
"frozen_category_denominators" (under system_progression) still records 53 for Baby-Socks-and-Booties.
Top‑level frozen_category_denominators records 19 for the same category.
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume recalculates the current Amazon queue length for the category:
Code ( verified at tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 7715-7760)):
Rebuilds the category’s Amazon queue (products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)).
Applies category‑scope + allowed‑key filtering.
Sets total = len(products).
Writes:
sp_update["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] = total
sp_update["supplier_products_needing_extraction"] = total
frozen_cats[nurl] = total (top‑level).
In this run total == 19, hence the 53 → 19 transition.
Conclusion:
*_products_needing_* reflect the current filtered queue length, not the original discovered total. The original 53 is preserved in frozen_category_denominators (and gap metrics), but the active resume contract is “0..19 pending in this queue”. This matches the supplier‑phase pattern you pasted (where the denominator becomes 19 for the resumed worklist).

2. What happens to “non‑filtered” products and multi‑category products

From code and logs:

Fresh Baby‑Socks run (090352.log):
ISS‑004 executes once, in _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic
(tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:10850+):
It builds normalized_cats from category_urls.
It keeps a product p only if its source_url/category_url normalizes into normalized_cats.
Log line:
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 17 → 12 products
means 5 products were discarded because their source belonged to a different category (multi‑category / mis‑scoped).
Resumed Baby‑Socks run (090519.log):
The Amazon queue is rebuilt in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume with two filters:
Category match (n_src == ncat).
Step‑2 allowed key set (k in _category_allowed_keys).
ISS‑004 does not log here, which is expected: the queue is already category‑scoped, and this path uses its own category filter.
For products not filtered out by ISS‑004:

They remain in the queue for that category as long as they:
Match the category URL (source_url/category_url normalizes to the category).
Are in the allowed key set (NEEDS_AMAZON_ONLY / NEEDS_FULL_EXTRACTION).
On resumed runs, some of them will likely be reprocessed (because we reset the Amazon offset when the queue shrinks).
commit_amazon_progress (utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py (lines 1685-1765)) treats amazon_products_completed as a queue‑relative pointer (0..19 in your example), not as a global “ever processed” count.
Linking map and cache are the real source of truth:
is_product_processed is forced to return False (fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py (lines 2837-2845)), so state alone never suppresses reprocessing.
De‑duplication happens in the linking‑map layer (hash indices built over URL/EAN – see HASH INDEX BUILT logs in 090352/090809).
Reprocessing a product that already has a linking‑map entry will:
Either update the existing entry or be a benign duplicate.
Not create gaps in the indexing because mark_category_completed and resume pointers are based on queue length and category URL, not on linking‑map index positions.
For multi‑category products:

On fresh runs:
ISS‑004 explicitly removes products whose source_url doesn’t belong to the current category’s normalized URL set.
On resumed runs:
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume re‑enforces the same idea:
It normalizes category_url to ncat.
It skips any product where normalized source_url doesn’t equal ncat.
Result:
A product can still appear in multiple categories overall (one entry per category), but for any given category’s Amazon queue, only the matching entries remain.
This can cause extra re‑processing, but not skipping: the filtering is “only this category”, never “exclude items that belong to this category”.
3. Why ISS‑004 runs only in the fresh run

Triangular check (code + logs + state):

Code location: _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 10850-10883)).
ISS‑004 is only called inside this helper, which is used for hybrid chunk processing when you’re running the main workflow over products.
Fresh Baby‑Socks run (090352.log):
ISS‑004 logs once, right after supplier phase finishes and before Amazon processing starts:
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 17 → 12 products
Resumed run (090519.log):
Workflow does not enter _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic for this category.
Instead you see:
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=1/326 ... prod_idx=38/53
Then _run_amazon_phase_from_resume kicks in and logs:
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 38 >= queue length 19; restarting from 0
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=1 total=19 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
Because this path rebuilds and filters the queue itself, ISS‑004 is not invoked again. The category scoping is enforced by the code at tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 7715-7734).
So: ISS‑004 is a one‑time, pre‑Amazon safeguard in the main workflow. Resumes use a dedicated, already category‑scoped path, which is why you don’t see ISS‑004 logging again.

4. Are your fixes actually active in these three runs?

Using the “triangular protocol” (code + logs + state):

Hybrid chunk + resume (supplier side)

Code:
_extract_supplier_products signature includes chunk_start_index: Optional[int] = None.
Hybrid cursor logic present (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 5133-5161)).
_run_hybrid_processing_mode sets _active_category_urls and passes chunk_start_index (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 6997-7042)).
Logs:
090352.log (Baby‑Socks fresh run):
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 1 categories starting from index 1
CATEGORY PROCESSING: abs_index=1, display_index=1, url=...Baby-Socks-and-Booties...
090809.log (A‑To‑Z fresh run):
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 1 categories starting from index 1
CATEGORY PROCESSING: abs_index=1, display_index=1, url=...A-To-Z-wholesale...
Verdict:
Confirmed working for hybrid fresh runs (chunk size 1, PCI=1).
Resume of later categories (PCI>1) is not exercised in these three logs, so that specific scenario is not yet verified, but nothing in the logs contradicts the intended behavior.
Amazon resume safety (saved offset vs queue length)

Code: _run_amazon_phase_from_resume logic at tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 7753-7789).
Logs (090519.log):
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis ... prod_idx=38/53
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 38 >= queue length 19; restarting from 0
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=1 total=19 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
Then RESUME PTR lines for prod_idx=1/19, 2/19, 3/19.
State (secondrun-categoy1.json):
amazon_products_needing_analysis": 19
amazon_products_completed": 3
Verdict:
Confirmed working: the dangerous “skip everything because offset>queue_len” behaviour has been replaced by “restart queue at 0 if offset>=total”.
We have not yet seen a log where this resumed queue fully completes and triggers the Amazon finalizer + mark_category_completed, so we cannot yet fully verify end‑to‑end completion on resume; but the entry behaviour is correct and no skipping occurs.
Index semantics / processing_state counters

Code:
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume updates amazon_products_needing_analysis (queue length) and supplier_products_needing_extraction (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 7755-7761)).
commit_amazon_progress writes amazon_products_completed = queue_idx + 1 (utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py (lines 1740-1742)).
mark_category_completed resets all per‑category counters to 0 for the next category (utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py (lines 2783-2788)).
State files:
first run angel state.json and angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json show the same pattern for A‑To‑Z: denominators preserved as 62, counters reset to 0 once category is complete.
secondrun-categoy1.json shows the 19/3 situation for Baby‑Socks as described.
Verdict:
Behaviour matches the “queue‑relative” model: denominators for *_products_needing_* follow the active queue, while frozen denominators keep the original totals.
This is consistent and does not block category completion, because completion is driven by done >= amazon_products_needing_analysis and mark_category_completed, not by earlier 53‑based numbers.
New bug: absolute_cat_index NameError

Code: _run_hybrid_processing_mode calls
self.state_manager.mark_category_completed(category_url, absolute_cat_index) at tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (line 7179) but absolute_cat_index is not in scope there.
Logs (090352.log and 090809.log tails are identical here):
ERROR - Unexpected error occurred during workflow execution: name 'absolute_cat_index' is not defined
Traceback points to _run_hybrid_processing_mode line 7179.
Verdict:
This fix is not yet implemented. It is independent of the index/queue logic above.
It explains the crash at the end of the first category in both Baby‑Socks and A‑To‑Z scenarios, but does not affect the Amazon resume offset behaviour you’re asking about.
5. Direct answers to your specific questions

Are the 53→19 changes “wrong” or dangerous by themselves?
No. They reflect a deliberate choice: *_products_needing_* track the current filtered queue, while frozen_category_denominators and gap metrics preserve the original “discovered” totals. Category completion is controlled by the queue‑relative denom (amazon_products_needing_analysis) and mark_category_completed, not by the old 53, so there’s no intrinsic inconsistency.

On resume, what happens to products not filtered out by ISS‑004?
They remain in the rebuilt, category‑scoped queue. Because of the Amazon resume safety fix, if the saved offset is too high (like 38 vs 19), we restart from 0 and reprocess some items. The de‑dup logic in linking map/cache absorbs duplicates; there is no index gap that could cause missing products.

Why didn’t ISS‑004 run again on the resumed run?
Because the resumed run enters _run_amazon_phase_from_resume directly (you can see the AMAZON RESUME SAFETY and AMAZON RESUME logs), which rebuilds and filters the queue itself. ISS‑004 sits in _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic and is only used on the fresh hybrid path, not on resume.

Did the implemented fixes match your “surgical & minimal change” requirement?
Yes for the parts we can see in action:

Hybrid fixes were inserted exactly where category slicing and index mapping already lived, reusing existing mechanisms.
Amazon resume safety was implemented by extending the existing offset‑clamp block, not introducing a new subsystem.
No unrelated sections were changed for the behaviours visible in these logs.
The one remaining gap is the missing absolute_cat_index wiring in _run_hybrid_processing_mode, which we can fix surgically once you approve code changes.
If you’d like, next step I can propose the minimal code change to fix the absolute_cat_index crash (only that one call site) and outline a short test scenario to confirm: (1) Baby‑Socks completes on resume with no skipped Amazon items, and (2) the workflow then moves cleanly to the next category.
