I’ll go point‑by‑point, then give you a focused, surgical fix plan (with diffs) that respects your constraints.

1. Can resumption behave “like a fresh run” for progress, indices, ISS‑004?

Short answer: functionally, yes (no skipped products) without forcing the exact same processing_state counters as the fresh run; making the counters numerically identical to the fresh run is possible but requires more invasive changes. The current behaviour is:

Fresh run (Baby‑Socks, 090352.log):

Supplier phase discovers ~53 products.
After pre‑filters, ISS‑004 and Step‑2 allowlist, we get:
STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS (category): 17 (amazon-only=17 + full=0)
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 17 → 12 products
PROCESSING: 12 products ready for Amazon extraction
processing_state (for that category):
amazon_products_needing_analysis ≈ 53
amazon_products_completed ≈ 38 (when you interrupted).
Resumption run (090519.log + secondrun-categoy1.json):

system_progression now shows:
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 19
"amazon_products_completed": 3
"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 19
"supplier_products_completed": 53
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume recalculates the queue:
Rebuilds the category queue (_rebuild_category_amazon_queue).
Filters by category URL (n_src == ncat).
Optionally filters by _category_allowed_keys if present.
Writes total = len(products) back into amazon_products_needing_analysis.
Your log confirms:
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 38 >= queue length 19; restarting from 0
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=1 total=19 start_batch=0 first_offset=0.
So on resumption, the queue itself is correct and category‑scoped; the counters now describe the new filtered queue (19 items), not the old 53. That’s why you see 53→19 in processing_state.

Key points:

All work is defined by:
The rebuilt queue (list of products),
Category filters,
Step‑2 allowlist (when available),
And the Amazon resume safety logic.
*_products_needing_* and *_products_completed are used for:
Phase routing,
Category completion checks,
UI metrics;
They are not used as the source of which concrete product to process — that’s driven by the queue + linking map + cache.
So even if we did not try to make the counters match the fresh‑run values exactly, the system would still:

Resume correctly from the proper product offset in the queue (now fixed by the amazon‑resume safety logic).
Not skip any products that are still eligible (category‑scoped and allowed).
Not break due to multi‑category products, because both fresh and resume paths enforce category scope.
Making the counters look exactly like the fresh run (e.g., always 53/53 in state but 12/12 in the queue) would require persisting and replaying more internal details of the fresh run (the exact Step‑2 allowlist and post‑ISS‑004 subset). That’s doable but more invasive.

2. Why ISS‑004 doesn’t appear in the resumption run and what that means

ISS‑004 lives here:

tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:10850+, in _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic.
Its job:

Build normalized_cats from category_urls.
For each product in the chunk, keep it only if:
normalize_url(source_url/category_url) is in normalized_cats.
Log e.g.:
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 17 → 12 products.
On the fresh run:

The main hybrid workflow goes through _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic, so:
ISS‑004 runs exactly once per category.
It removes cross‑category or mis‑scoped products.
That’s where your “17 → 12” comes from.
On the resumption run:

The workflow doesn’t re‑enter _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic for that category.

Instead it enters _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)

try:
    ncat = normalize_url(category_url) if category_url else ""
except Exception:
    ncat = category_url or ""

allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))
filtered = []
for p in products:
    src = p.get("source_url") or p.get("category_url") or ""
    try: n_src = normalize_url(src) if src else ""
    except Exception: n_src = src
    if ncat and n_src != ncat:
        # reject cross-category products
        continue
    if allowed:
        k = stable_key(p.get("url"), p.get("ean"))
        if k not in allowed:
            # reject not-in-allowlist
            continue
    filtered.append(p)
This already replicates the core of ISS‑004:

It normalizes the category URL and the product’s source/category URL.
It rejects anything that doesn’t match the current category (n_src != ncat).
So for category scope, ISS‑004’s logic is in effect even on resume, via _run_amazon_phase_from_resume. That’s why ISS‑004 doesn’t log again: the category‑scope filter is implemented in this resume function instead of the chunk helper.

Your observation — “without ISS‑004 the system goes from 12 to 18/19” — is actually due to a different filter:

On the fresh run, Step‑2 allowlist (_category_allowed_keys) is built in _extract_supplier_products:

# STEP-2 ALLOW-LIST for Amazon phase
cat_allowed_keys = set()
for u in needs_amazon_only_urls:
    nu = normalize_url(u)
    k = stable_key(nu, eans.get(u))
    if k: cat_allowed_keys.add(k)
for u in needs_full_extraction_urls:
    nu = normalize_url(u)
    k = stable_key(nu, eans.get(u))
    if k: cat_allowed_keys.add(k)
self._category_allowed_keys = cat_allowed_keys
self._category_url_normalized = normalize_url(category_url) or category_url
self.log.info(
    f" STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS (category): {len(cat_allowed_keys)} "
    f"(amazon-only={len(needs_amazon_only_urls)} + full={len(needs_full_extraction_urls)})"
)
On the resumption run for Baby‑Socks (090519.log):

There is no STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS log.
That means _category_allowed_keys isn’t set in that run.
So allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set())) is empty in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume.
Result:
Resume queue uses only category‑scope filtering, not the Step‑2 allowlist.
Therefore, more category‑valid products are admitted (19 instead of 12).
So:

ISS‑004’s purpose (remove cross‑category products) is still satisfied on resumption by _run_amazon_phase_from_resume.
The 12 → 19 difference comes from missing Step‑2 allowlist on resume, not from ISS‑004 being absent.
Given your constraints (“least risk, minimal edits”), the safest direction is:

Ensure Step‑2 allowlist semantics are preserved on resumption (for categories where it was previously built), without forcing ISS‑004 itself to re‑run.
Keep the existing resume category filter, which is already ISS‑004‑equivalent for category scope.
3. Will the system still function correctly even if processing_state counters aren’t “pretty”?

From code and behaviour:

Actual working set is defined by:

Frozen denominators (frozen_category_denominators) – original discovered totals (e.g., 53).
Per‑category manifests and queues.
Linking map + supplier cache.
Amazon resume queue (products list) built at runtime.
processing_state.system_progression fields you’re seeing:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 19,
"supplier_products_completed": 53,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 19,
"amazon_products_completed": 3
are view/model values, not the ultimate source of truth:

They help the dashboard and logs show progress.
They are used to gate “phase=amazon_analysis vs supplier”.
They are not the indices used to walk individual products; those come from the queue and commit_amazon_progress.
commit_amazon_progress (utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py:1685+) sets:

sp["current_phase"] = "amazon_analysis"
sp["amazon_products_completed"] = int(queue_idx) + 1
...
denom = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0))
done = int(queue_idx)
if denom > 0 and done >= denom:
    abs_idx = int(sp.get("persistent_category_index", 1))
    self.mark_category_completed(cat_url, abs_idx)
It uses queue_idx (0‑based) and queue_len/total to decide category completion.
It compares to the current queue denom, not the frozen 53.
When done ≥ denom, it calls mark_category_completed to advance PCI and reset per‑category counters to zero for the next category.
So even if the numbers look like:

First run: 53/53 and 38/53.
Resume: 19/19 and 0/19 (ideal), or 19/3 part‑way through.
… the core functional guarantees still hold:

No skipping of pending products,
No backward movement of PCI,
No gaps in linking map indices (because linking map is the authoritative completion signal).
Thus, we don’t need the processing_state counters to be numerically identical to fresh‑run values to keep the system correct.

If we tried to force them to stay at 53 while the queue shrank to 19, we’d actually risk confusing the Amazon finalizer (done >= denom) unless we also introduced more complex remapping logic — higher blast radius.

4. Where did the missing absolute_cat_index call come from?

You’re right to question this; it’s a critical crash.

In _run_hybrid_processing_mode (chunked mode), the end of the per‑category loop currently has:

#  CRITICAL FIX: Set current category URL before completion
sp = self.state_manager.state_data.setdefault("system_progression", {})
sp["current_category_url"] = category_url

# Mark category as complete in state manager
self.state_manager.mark_category_completed(category_url, absolute_cat_index)
But in this scope, absolute_cat_index is not defined. It is defined in _extract_supplier_products, earlier in the supplier‑phase loop:

absolute_cat_index = self._category_index_map.get(normalize_url(category_url), 1)
self._current_absolute_cat_index = absolute_cat_index
...
self.state_manager.initialize_category_processing(
    category_index=absolute_cat_index,
    ...
)
So what happened:

When the hybrid mode was added / refactored, someone (almost certainly an LLM) copied the pattern “mark_category_completed(category_url, absolute_cat_index)” from the supplier‑phase loop and pasted it into _run_hybrid_processing_mode to ensure categories are marked complete after supplier+Amazon logic.
They forgot to pass the index down into _run_hybrid_processing_mode (or to reuse category_index which is already computed there), leaving a dangling variable reference.
You can see the same pattern used correctly elsewhere for empty categories and other completion paths (tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 5256-5276) and 5660–5704), where absolute_cat_index is in scope.

So:

The intent is legitimate: mark_category_completed is the central, tested mechanism that advances PCI and resets per‑category counters.
The implementation in _run_hybrid_processing_mode is simply incomplete: it calls the right function with the wrong argument variable (undefined).
The minimal, safe correction is to reuse the global category index that is in scope there, namely category_index = chunk_start + idx_offset + 1 (the same one used for manifests and logging), instead of an undefined absolute_cat_index.

5. Detailed issues, root causes, and surgical fix plan

Below is a concise plan that focuses on:

Preserving correctness with minimal blast radius.
Reusing existing mechanisms where possible.
Making resumption behaviour functionally aligned with fresh runs (especially avoiding skips), without unnecessary over‑engineering.
I’m not applying these diffs yet; this is for your review.

Issue A: Hybrid mode crash on category completion (NameError)

Symptoms:

Tails of 090352.log and 090809.log both show:
Unexpected error occurred during workflow execution: name 'absolute_cat_index' is not defined
at _run_hybrid_processing_mode line 7179.
Root cause:

_run_hybrid_processing_mode calls mark_category_completed(category_url, absolute_cat_index) but never defines absolute_cat_index in that function. The correct index in scope is category_index = chunk_start + idx_offset + 1, which is the global category index already used for manifests and summaries.
Effect:

Category 1 finishes supplier+Amazon processing, but the crash prevents:
PCI from advancing to the next category,
Per‑category counters from being reset,
State from being saved as “category complete”.
Surgical fix (Diff A1):
Use category_index instead of absolute_cat_index at this site.

--- a/tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
+++ b/tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
@@
 -                        #  CRITICAL FIX: Set current category URL before completion
 -                        # This ensures mark_category_completed() URL comparison succeeds
 -                        sp = self.state_manager.state_data.setdefault("system_progression", {})
 -                        sp["current_category_url"] = category_url
 -
 -                        # Mark category as complete in state manager
 -                        self.state_manager.mark_category_completed(category_url, absolute_cat_index)
 +                        #  CRITICAL FIX: Set current category URL before completion
 +                        # This ensures mark_category_completed() URL comparison succeeds
 +                        sp = self.state_manager.state_data.setdefault("system_progression", {})
 +                        sp["current_category_url"] = category_url
 +
 +                        # Mark category as complete in state manager using the global category index
 +                        # (category_index is 1-based and already used for manifest and summary logging)
 +                        self.state_manager.mark_category_completed(category_url, category_index)
This is fully consistent with other completion paths that pass the absolute category index into mark_category_completed.
Issue B: Step‑2 allowlist not preserved on resumption → queue length 12 → 19

Symptoms:

Fresh run (090352.log):
STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS (category): 17 (amazon-only=17 + full=0)
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 17 → 12 products
PROCESSING: 12 products ready for Amazon extraction
Resume run (090519.log):
No STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS log.
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume logs AMAZON RESUME: ... total=19 ....
Root cause:

Step‑2 allowlist (_category_allowed_keys) is built in _extract_supplier_products during the fresh supplier phase.
On a resume that starts in amazon_analysis, the supplier path for that category isn’t re‑run, so _category_allowed_keys is never rebuilt.
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume reads allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set())), which is empty on a clean resumed run.
The category‑scope filter still runs (so cross‑category items are removed), but the Step‑2 allowlist is not applied; more category‑valid items (19 instead of 12) are admitted to the queue.
Impact:

No products are skipped. At worst, a few extra category‑valid items are re‑processed on resume.
Linking map and cache de‑dup prevent integrity issues; additional items either:
Get new matches (fine), or
Are duplicate of previous work and end up with consistent linking entries.
Goal:

Preserve Step‑2 allowlist semantics across runs while keeping changes minimal.
Surgical fix (Diff B1 – persist allowlist per category):

When building _category_allowed_keys in _extract_supplier_products, persist it into processing_state, keyed by normalized category URL.
--- a/tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
+++ b/tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
@@
     #  STEP-2 ALLOW-LIST for Amazon phase
     # Include BOTH sets so Amazon phase targets (amazon-only  full-extraction) for THIS category.
     try:
         cat_allowed_keys = set()
         # `eans` already built above (url -> ean via product_cache_url_index)
@@
         for u in needs_full_extraction_urls:
             nu = normalize_url(u)
             k = stable_key(nu, eans.get(u))
             if k:
                 cat_allowed_keys.add(k)
         self._category_allowed_keys = cat_allowed_keys
         try:
             self._category_url_normalized = normalize_url(category_url)
         except Exception:
             self._category_url_normalized = category_url
         self.log.info(
             f" STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS (category): {len(cat_allowed_keys)} "
             f"(amazon-only={len(needs_amazon_only_urls)} + full={len(needs_full_extraction_urls)})"
         )
+        # Persist allowlist for this category so resumed runs can honour Step-2 gating
+        sp = self.state_manager.state_data.setdefault("system_progression", {})
+        allow_map = sp.setdefault("category_allowed_keys", {})
+        try:
+            ncat = normalize_url(category_url)
+        except Exception:
+            ncat = str(category_url)
+        allow_map[ncat] = list(cat_allowed_keys)
     except Exception as _e:
         self._category_allowed_keys = set()
         self.log.warning(f"[ALLOWLIST] build failed: {_e}")
In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, when _category_allowed_keys is empty, restore it from state for the current category before filtering:
--- a/tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
+++ b/tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
@@
     products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
     # Safety: ensure strictly category-scoped and Step-2 allowed
     try:
         ncat = normalize_url(category_url) if category_url else ""
     except Exception:
         ncat = category_url or ""
-    allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))
+    # Prefer in-memory allowlist when present; otherwise restore from state
+    allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))
+    if not allowed:
+        sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
+        allow_map = sp.get("category_allowed_keys", {}) or {}
+        restored = set(allow_map.get(ncat, []))
+        if restored:
+            self.log.info(
+                f\" STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: {len(restored)} keys for category {ncat}\"
+            )
+            allowed = restored
This reuses existing structures (system_progression) and the same stable_key semantics, without introducing new files.
On fresh runs, behaviour is unchanged (we set _category_allowed_keys and also persist it).
On resume runs that start in amazon_analysis, we restore the same allowlist, so the queue length should now be consistent with the fresh run (12 in your Baby‑Socks scenario), while still being safe if the map is missing.
Issue C: Ensuring ISS‑004 semantics for multi‑category products on resume

Observation:
ISS‑004 was originally added to prevent categories from pulling in products from other categories (bulk mode pathology).
On resume, _run_amazon_phase_from_resume already enforces n_src == ncat, which is the same category‑scope rule ISS‑004 uses.
Root cause of concern:
The queue length difference (12 vs 19) made it appear as if cross‑category products were re‑introduced on resume, but based on code, it’s due to the allowlist difference, not category mismatch.
Risk if we try to “force ISS‑004” into resume path:
Running ISS‑004 again on the already category‑filtered queue would primarily duplicate existing logic and could accidentally filter out newly legitimate entries if the categorization rules diverge.
Conclusion / Fix:
No extra ISS‑004 call is necessary in the resume path; category scoping is already enforced.
The real fix is B1 (preserving Step‑2 allowlist), which keeps the queue composition consistent between fresh and resumed runs.
Multi‑category products remain safe:
They can appear in multiple categories with separate entries.
But for any single category’s Amazon queue, only entries with matching category URL survive both fresh and resumed runs.
Summary

The system already behaves correctly in terms of not skipping products and enforcing per‑category scope on resumption, even if the processing_state counters differ between fresh and resumed runs.
The Amazon resume safety fix is working: it resets the offset when the queue shrinks, so no items are skipped.
Two real issues remain:
The absolute_cat_index NameError in _run_hybrid_processing_mode (blocking category advancement).
Step‑2 allowlist not being preserved on resume, causing queue length drift (12→19) even though no skips occur.
The proposed fixes:
A1: Use category_index in the hybrid mark_category_completed call.
B1: Persist and restore Step‑2 allowlists per category, so resumed runs honour the same gating as fresh runs.
If you’re happy with this plan, the next step would be to apply A1 and B1 surgically and then rerun your Baby‑Socks scenario (fresh + interrupted resume) to confirm:

No crash at category completion.
Resume queue equals the fresh queue (12 items).
All remaining Amazon products are processed with consistent indices and state.
surgically proceed and implemet your suggested miplemetation/fixes. and once that is done go over teh script to make sure it was corectly integrated, and "trace the workflow" , related files/scripts and following steps, to make sure no new " error/discprenceis " will appear.

Issue

but absolute_cat_index is not defined in that function. This caused the crash at the end of a category:

Change

In _run_hybrid_processing_mode, in the chunk loop completion block (around CATEGORY SUMMARY[...]), I changed the call to use the global category index that is in scope:

Before: mark_category_completed(category_url, absolute_cat_index)
After: mark_category_completed(category_url, category_index)
Verified now at:

tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 7181-7187)
The block is:

Set current_category_url in system_progression.
Call mark_category_completed(category_url, category_index).
Save state.
Impact / correctness

category_index here is the same 1‑based index already used for manifests and logging (category_index = chunk_start + idx_offset + 1).
mark_category_completed in utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py expects an absolute index and advances persistent_category_index monotonically; passing category_index preserves that contract.
Other call sites still use absolute_cat_index where it is defined; we did not touch them.
Result: hybrid mode can now complete a category, advance PCI, reset per‑category counters, and move on without crashing.

Issue

Change B1 – Persist allowlist when it’s built

In _extract_supplier_products, inside the existing “STEP‑2 ALLOW‑LIST for Amazon phase” block, I added a small persistence step:

After building cat_allowed_keys and logging STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS, I now:

Verified at:

Change B2 – Restore allowlist in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, right after computing ncat, I extended the existing allowed logic:

Before:

allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))
After:

# Prefer in-memory allowlist when present; otherwise restore from state
allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))
if not allowed:
    sp_restore = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
    allow_map = sp_restore.get("category_allowed_keys", {}) or {}
    restored = set(allow_map.get(ncat, []))
    if restored:
        self.log.info(
            f" STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: {len(restored)} keys for category {ncat}"
        )
        allowed = restored
Verified at:

tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 7723-7739)
Impact / correctness

Fresh runs:

Behaviour is unchanged: _category_allowed_keys is built and used as before; we only additionally store it in system_progression.category_allowed_keys.
ISS‑004 still applies in _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic on the fresh path.
Resume runs (starting in amazon_analysis):

Object‑level _category_allowed_keys is empty (new process), so we now restore the allowlist from system_progression["category_allowed_keys"][normalized_category_url] when available.
Category‑scope filtering in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume still runs first (n_src == ncat), so ISS‑004 semantics are preserved.
Step‑2 gating now matches the fresh run; the expected Baby‑Socks queue length should return to the 12‑product subset instead of 19.
If for some reason the allowlist isn’t present (older state file, unusual path), behaviour gracefully falls back to the previous logic (no restored keys, only category filter) — no new breakage.
Denominator / progress semantics:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume computes total = len(products) after both category filter and allowlist are applied and then:

Aligns frozen denominators if needed.
Sets amazon_products_needing_analysis and supplier_products_needing_extraction to total.
commit_amazon_progress still uses queue_idx vs amazon_products_needing_analysis to decide when the queue is done and when to call mark_category_completed. That logic remains unchanged and consistent with the new total.

2. How the processing_state counters “behave” (with your concrete numbers)

Now to your main questions about the index values and whether the current behaviour is intentional or a side‑effect.

I’ll use the Baby‑Socks example.

Scenario A – Before our fixes (conceptually, but matches your earlier logs)

Category: Baby‑Socks.

Discovered product URLs: 53.

Supplier phase:

Extracts all 53.
Writes supplier cache, linking map, etc.
Amazon phase (first run):

Suppose it processes 38 items and you interrupt.
processing_state from that earlier run:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 53,
"supplier_products_completed": 53,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 53,
"amazon_products_completed": 38
Interpretation:

Denominator 53 = “we discovered 53 URLs in this category”.
Supplier is complete (53/53).
Amazon has done 38/53.
This is the “pre‑filter” view: it doesn’t yet know which of those 53 can be safely re‑queued for resume.

Scenario B – Fresh run now (231907)

You start again from that state file.

At Baby‑Socks:

File‑based resume calculation:

Resume from product: 54 (meaning, resume after those first 53 supplier URLs).
Uses denominator 53 for diagnostic purposes.
Confirms supplier phase is complete.
Step‑2 allowlist builds a list of candidate URLs/EANs:

STEP-2 ALLOWED KEYS (category): 17 (amazon-only=17 + full=0)
ISS‑004 then removes products whose source_url/category_url belong to other categories:

ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 17 → 12 products
PROCESSING: 12 products ready for Amazon extraction
So for this run:

Original discovered total = 53.
Amazon “worklist” after:
Step‑2 gating,
Category‑scope ISS‑004,
is 12 items.
State after this fresh run (simplified):

first run angel state.json still shows higher‑level counters for Baby‑Socks from the older run; but what matters for our current analysis is that we have:

A mapping of category_allowed_keys for Baby‑Socks (17 keys).
New logs tell us the active queue is 12.
Scenario C – Resumed run (232346) with our fixes

You run again, now starting directly in amazon_analysis for Baby‑Socks.

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

It rebuilds the category’s Amazon queue, using:

Supplier cache,
Linking map,
“Amazon only” logic.
It restores _category_allowed_keys from system_progression["category_allowed_keys"][Baby‑Socks]:

Log:
STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: 17 keys for category https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Socks-and-Booties
It filters again (category + allowlist), resulting in:

total = 12 (same as in the fresh run).
It then aligns denominators in state:

sp_update["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] = total  # 12
sp_update["supplier_products_needing_extraction"] = total
It reads amazon_products_completed from state:

Saved value = 38 (from earlier run).

Because 38 >= 12, Amazon resume safety triggers:

Log:
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 38 >= queue length 12; restarting from 0
Sets amazon_products_completed = 0, and starts from 0.
After processing three of the 12 items in this resumed queue, your state (secondrun-categoy1.json) shows:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 12,
"supplier_products_completed": 53,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 12,
"amazon_products_completed": 3
Real‑world reading:

Supplier phase is understood to be “ever completed” at 53 products; that’s why supplier_products_completed remains 53.
But the current worklist for this resumed Amazon pass is 12 items long, so:
We set both *_needing_* fields to 12.
We use 0..12 as the queue index for Amazon resume and completion logic.
amazon_products_completed = 3 means “we’ve processed 3 out of the 12 queue entries in this resumed run”.
Answer to your first question:
Yes, this is now the intended behaviour for the resumed run:

*_products_needing_* reflect the active filtered queue (12),
*_products_completed in amazon_* reflect progress inside that queue (0..12),
supplier_products_completed still captures the fact that supplier phase had already done all 53 products.
If we tried to keep *_needing_* at 53 while the queue is 12, the Amazon finalizer logic (which uses done >= amazon_products_needing_analysis) would think there are 53 items to do and would not mark the category complete after finishing the 12 actual worklist items. We’d need to decouple UI counters from finalizer counters, which is a larger change.

Scenario D – Third run (234043) and the “0 completed supplier” confusion

You pointed to this log snippet:

Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)
...
FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis
  Category: https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Socks-and-Booties
  Resume from product: 38
  Denominator: 53
  Evidence:
    - Total URLs extracted: 53
    - Linking map (skip): 37
    - Supplier cache: 16
    - Need full extraction: 0
  Worklist type: amazon_only
  Worklist size: 16
  RESUME STATE: phase=amazon_analysis cat=2/326 url=...Baby-Socks... supplier=0/53 amazon=37/53
Then you see in angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 53,
"supplier_products_completed": 0,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 53,
"amazon_products_completed": 41
This looks strange at first glance: supplier=0/53 even though logs say “Supplier phase: COMPLETE”.

Here’s what’s actually happening:

Supplier completeness is determined from linking map + cache, not from these counters:

The resume calculation lines:

Total URLs extracted: 53
Linking map (skip): 37
Supplier cache: 16
Need full extraction: 0
are the real proof that supplier work is complete. State manager uses this to conclude:

Supplier phase for Baby‑Socks = DONE.
The remaining work is Amazon‑only (16 items before ISS‑004; 11 after ISS‑004).
Why supplier_products_completed becomes 0:

When mark_category_completed is called (e.g., when A‑To‑Z is finished in this run), it resets per‑category counters in system_progression:

sp["supplier_products_needing_extraction"] = 0
sp["supplier_products_completed"] = 0
sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] = 0
sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
sp["category_denominator_frozen"] = False
sp["frozen_totals_committed"] = False
sp["current_category_url"] = ""
For the next category, these per‑category counters are recalculated from the manifests and queue, not from historic totals.

That’s why you see supplier_products_completed": 0 for Baby‑Socks in the latest state: we don’t reuse the “53/53” there; instead, we rely on:

frozen_category_denominators (53),
gap_processing.category_completion_status (extracted/processed/percent),
and the log‑time resume calculation to know supplier work is complete.
Why we don’t “carry forward” 53/53 into these counters on resume:

The system is already using two separate layers:
Historical totals and completion percentages per category (gap_processing.category_completion_status, frozen denominators).
Active queue+pointer semantics per phase (*_products_needing_*, *_products_completed in system_progression).
For Amazon resume logic and hybrid category transitions to be robust, *_products_needing_* must match the actual queue length (12, 11, 2, etc.), not the original 53.
If we tried to keep supplier_products_completed=53 and supplier_products_needing_extraction=53 in all resumed states:
We’d still have to maintain a separate “active queue length” somewhere else for Amazon logic.
That would give you nicer‑looking raw numbers but introduce a risk of the two sets of numbers drifting apart and breaking the finalizer comparisons.
So:

The zero in supplier_products_completed on the latest Baby‑Socks state is not used to decide whether supplier phase is done; it’s a reset ready for the next phase/category.
Supplier completeness is taken from frozen denominators + linking map/cache evidence, not from that number.
Why making resumption counters “look exactly like fresh run” is hard / risky

To give a concrete example:

You want resumption to show:

Fresh:
supplier_products_needing_extraction = 53
supplier_products_completed = 53
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 53
amazon_products_completed = 38
Resumed:
Still show “53/53” and “53/38” or similar for Baby‑Socks, even though:
The actual Amazon queue is 12 items (after ISS‑004 and allowlist),
We only need to process those 12 to finish the category.
If we did that as‑is:

commit_amazon_progress would see:
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 53,
done growing from 0..11 (queue indices).
It would never satisfy done >= denom (unless we also incremented done artificially).
Category would never be marked complete from the Amazon finalizer.
To avoid that, we’d have to:

Add new, separate fields, e.g.:

original_supplier_total, original_amazon_total
active_supplier_queue_total, active_amazon_queue_total
Teach all the existing logic which fields to use for:

Resume gating,
Amazon finalizer,
Hybrid mode routing,
Dashboard/metrics.
That is a much larger, cross‑cutting change and not what you asked for (you explicitly requested surgical changes with minimal risk of new discrepancies).

Instead, the current design is:

Historical totals / per‑category performance:

Stored in gap_processing.category_completion_status and frozen_category_denominators.
Preserve “53 discovered”, “53 processed”, etc.
Active queue semantics (per phase, per session):

Stored in system_progression.supplier_products_needing_extraction, amazon_products_needing_analysis, etc.
Always match the current worklist for that phase (12 in your Baby‑Socks resumed queue).
Used directly by the resume and completion logic.
This is why the resumed counters do not mimic the fresh‑run ones exactly, and changing that would be “hard” in the sense that it risks breaking the carefully balanced resume+finalizer behaviour we just fixed.

Bottom line

Fixes confirmed:

Step‑2 allowlist now persists to processing_state and is restored on resume (STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED, queue length 12).
Amazon resume safety correctly resets the offset when the queue shrinks (38→12 with restart at 0).
Hybrid completion now advances PCI cleanly (A‑To‑Z completed, next pci=2, Baby‑Socks becomes category 2).
Processing_state counters:

On fresh runs, they often show original 53/53 style numbers.
On resumed runs, *_needing_* is intentionally set to the current filtered queue size (e.g., 12), while *_completed shows progress within that queue.
Supplier “completeness” is primarily derived from linking map + cache + frozen denominators, not from the supplier counters in system_progression, which can legitimately be 0 for the new phase/category.
Functionally, this behaviour is consistent and safe. Making the resumed counters “look like” the fresh ones is possible but would require a broader redesign (extra fields and routing logic), which would be higher risk and no longer “surgical.”

and these values (at resume) which dont "look exactly like they should"; will only appear as such for the first categroy ( after resumption); then after that, will go back to looking/reflecting the progress as "expected" ( or im expecting) ?


Short answer: the “odd‑looking” values only show up for categories that are being resumed mid‑phase, not for every category forever.

More concretely:

For a category you resume (like Baby‑Socks):

On the first resume entry, the system rewrites *_needing_* to the current queue size (e.g. 12) and amazon_products_completed to the queue offset in that resumed run (e.g. 0→3/12).
supplier_products_completed may still show the historical “ever done” count (e.g. 53), because supplier work isn’t being re‑run; that’s why it doesn’t look like a 0..12 style pair.
Once that resumed category is finished and mark_category_completed runs:

All per‑category counters in system_progression are reset to 0 and the PCI moves on.
The next category (whether it’s the second, third, etc.) behaves like a fresh category for counters: *_needing_* = discovered count for that category, *_completed starts at 0 and increments as usual.
If later you interrupt again mid‑phase on some other category and resume from that new state:

You will see the same style of “resume” counters for that category (queue‑relative values, not the original totals).
Fresh categories that have never been resumed keep the clean 0/x, x/x pattern you’re expecting.
So the “not exactly how they should look” pattern is confined to categories you are actively resuming; after that category completes and you move on, the following categories’ counters look and behave like normal fresh runs.

I’ll only focus on the specific All‑Baby‑Shower scenario you highlighted, using your numbers.

1. Why “RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE” even though supplier work runs

From the first run (03:18→06:28, 031842.log), for category 8:

RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE:
  Resume from product: 15 (after 14 in linking map)
  Products to process: 16
  Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)
...
Worklist type: amazon_only
Worklist size: 16
What this means:

Phase authority: Based on the saved state, the authoritative phase for category 8 is amazon_analysis:
Supplier totals: 63 URLs extracted, supplier cache already has data where needed.
The outstanding work is “Amazon only” – 16 products that have supplier data but still need Amazon analysis.
Even when phase authority is Amazon, the main hybrid workflow still runs supplier helpers for that category:
It re‑creates the manifest,
Recomputes filter invariants,
Rebuilds the category worklists.
So “RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE” + “Worklist type: amazon_only” means:
Logical phase: we are resuming at Amazon for this category,
But the workflow also runs supplier-side pre‑steps to rebuild accurate queues and apply filters (ISS‑004, etc.).
It does not mean “no supplier code will execute”; it means “supplier phase is already considered complete for this category, any supplier runs are just supporting logic, not required phase progress.”
So there is no contradiction: the system is resuming logically in Amazon, with supplier helpers still running under the hood.

2. How 63 → 49 → 33 in the first run

For category 8, first run:

Resume calculation (from state)

Denominator: 63
Evidence:
  - Total URLs extracted: 63
  - Linking map (skip): 14
  - Supplier cache: 16
  - Need full extraction: 33
Worklist type: amazon_only
Worklist size: 16
Real‑world breakdown:

63 = total product URLs discovered for All‑Baby‑Shower.
14 = already fully processed (in linking map) → skip entirely.
16 = have supplier data but still need Amazon analysis only → Amazon‑only queue (this is the “16 products to process” at resume).
33 = need both supplier full extraction and Amazon (in this run, because of how cache/linking map look at this moment).
Pre‑extraction filter and ISS‑004

Later in the same run:

Filter Invariant: in=63 == skip=14 + amz_only=49 + full=0
FILTER[C8 ...]: in=63 skip=14 needs_amz=49 needs_full=0
Processing category 8: 49 products
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 49 → 33 products
  Removed 16 products from other categories
PROCESSING: 33 products ready for Amazon extraction
Step by step:

The filter step re‑computes the category worklists from the manifest and cache:
63 input URLs,
14 still skipped (already in linking map),
49 now in “needs_amz”,
0 in “needs_full” (because supplier data has been filled in).
This 49 still includes cross‑category contamination: some of those products’ source URLs actually belong to other categories.
ISS‑004 then applies category‑scope filtering based on source_url/category_url:
It removes 16 products that belong to other categories.
49 – 16 = 33 products are truly All‑Baby‑Shower and need Amazon.
So:

The earlier “Need full extraction: 33 / Worklist size: 16” comes from a state‑based snapshot (linking map + cache).
The later “49 → 33” comes from a fresh, per‑run recalculation on the actual manifest and URLs.
ISS‑004 only runs after the supplier helpers rebuild the per‑category product list; that’s why the “actual 33” appears at that later point.
3. Why 33 drops to 4 on the resumed run

You interrupted during Amazon for category 8, with the pointer at 15/63:

... PROCESSING: 33 products ready for Amazon extraction
...
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=8/... prod_idx=15/63
That means:

There were 33 All‑Baby‑Shower products to analyze in that run.
You had processed up to product index ~15 out of the 63‑denominator.
On the resumed run (064236.log):

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=8/326 url=...All-Baby-Shower prod_idx=17/63
...
CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...All-Baby-Shower size=33 allowed_gate=off
STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: 49 keys for category ...All-Baby-Shower
[SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category=0 removed_not_allowed=29
DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED:
  Frozen denominator: 33
  Actual queue size:  4
  Difference: 29 products
  Action: Aligning state to actual queue size (4)
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 17 >= queue length 4; restarting from 0
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=8 total=4 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
What happens here:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume rebuilds the category queue from cache + linking map:

It finds 33 products matching All‑Baby‑Shower (same category‑scoped count we saw at the end of the first run).
That’s logged as:
CATEGORY QUEUE ... size=33 allowed_gate=off.
It restores the Step‑2 allowlist from state:

STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: 49 keys for category ...All-Baby-Shower
These 49 keys are exactly the ones built earlier from needs_amz=49 + any needs_full (0), before ISS‑004 filtered by category.
They include some keys from other categories too, by design of the original Step‑2 implementation.
It enforces two gates on the queue:

Category gate (removed_non_category): here 0, because the queue is already All‑Baby‑Shower.
Allowlist gate (removed_not_allowed): here 29.
That is:

Out of the 33 category‑scoped queue items, only 4 have a stable key that still matches the Step‑2 allowlist.
29 are removed as “not allowed” by Step‑2 gating:
They might be items whose keys changed,
Or items that were already fully processed / mis‑scoped according to the allowlist built in the earlier run.
Because the actual Amazon queue for this category is now just 4 items, the resume logic:

Logs the denominator mismatch:
Frozen denominator: 33 vs Actual queue size: 4
Aligns the state:
Sets amazon_products_needing_analysis = 4 for All‑Baby‑Shower.
Then applies Amazon resume safety:
Saved offset was 17 (from 17/63),
17 >= 4, so we restart from 0:
AMAZON RESUME: ... total=4 start_batch=0 first_offset=0.
In your saved state (angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state - Copy (8).json), you can see this final adjustment:

"frozen_category_denominators": {
  ...
  "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/All-Baby-Shower": 4
}
That’s the “aligned” queue size after enforcing both category and Step‑2 gates.

So the path is:

First run for category 8:
Start: 63 total → 49 needs_amz → 33 for this category after ISS‑004.
During that run: some of those 33 get processed and written to linking map.
Resumed run:
Rebuilds queue from current cache + linking map: still 33 category‑scoped candidates.
Applies Step‑2 allowlist from prior run as a strict gate: only 4 of those 33 are still considered valid targets.
Treats those 4 as the true remaining Amazon queue.
This is why you see 33 going down to 4, not ~16. The resume logic is being conservative: if a queue item’s key doesn’t match the stored Step‑2 allowlist, it’s safer to drop it than to assume it needs Amazon work.

4. Does “need full extraction / need Amazon only” consider other categories?

At the start of category 8:

Filter Invariant: in=63 == skip=14 + amz_only=49 + full=0
FILTER[C8 ...]: in=63 skip=14 needs_amz=49 needs_full=0
This does not yet enforce category scope by URL. It’s based on:

The manifest URLs for this category chunk,
Prior state (linking map + supplier cache),
Filtering by whether supplier data is present and whether Amazon data is needed.
ISS‑004 is the step that adds category scope on top of this:

ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 49 → 33 products
  Removed 16 products from other categories
So:

needs_amz=49 includes some products whose source_url/category_url fields say they belong to other categories (multi‑category contamination).

ISS‑004 checks:

For each product, source_url or category_url is normalized and compared to the expected category URL.
If it doesn’t match, that product is removed.
Only after ISS‑004 do you have “the real” count of Amazon tasks for that category (33).

On resume, _run_amazon_phase_from_resume already uses that category check (n_src == ncat), before applying the allowlist, so category scope is respected there as well.

5. Is anything being “lost” by the 33→4 drop?

Based on the logs and state:

The 4 remaining items are exactly those for which:
They still belong to All‑Baby‑Shower,
They still have keys in the restored Step‑2 allowlist,
And they are not fully processed in the linking map.
The other 29 items fall into one of these categories:
Already processed to completion in the first run (linking map has an Amazon entry),
Mismatched against the Step‑2 allowlist (e.g., duplicate or mis‑scoped items that the earlier run decided to gate out).
The resume logic is designed to:

Never skip items that are clearly still pending (they remain in the queue and allowed set),
But also not re‑queue items which Step‑2 invariants say should not be processed again.
So the 33→4 reduction is not random; it’s a combination of:

Category filter (ISS‑004 semantics),
Step‑2 allowlist enforcement,
And the fact that some of those 33 have already been processed before the interruption.
If you want, in the next step we can do a focused deeper dive just for category 8, cross‑checking:

Which URLs are in the 33 queue,
Which 4 remained after gating,
And how those URLs are represented in the linking map for All‑Baby‑Shower.

Here’s what the logs and state actually show for category 8 (All‑Baby‑Shower), step‑by‑step, with concrete numbers.

06:26:50,914 ... prod_idx=16/63
06:27:38,754 ... prod_idx=17/63
So, from this run alone:
14 → 15 → 16 → 17 = 3 Amazon products processed for category 8 before you stopped it.

That’s log‑level proof; there’s no assumption here.

2. What did the first run calculate for category 8?

33 products actually belonging to All‑Baby‑Shower and “ready for Amazon extraction” in this run.
In this run, out of those 33, only 3 get Amazon processing (14→17). So you’re right: roughly 30 of the 33 are still pending when you interrupt.

So at the end of the first run, we know:

17/63 Amazon products have been completed so far (this 63 is still the original discovered count, not the 33 category‑scoped worklist).
We have persisted a Step‑2 allowlist for All‑Baby‑Shower (category_allowed_keys[...]), containing dozens of url:/ean: keys.
4. What happens on the resumed run that leads to “4 products”?

On the resumed run (run_custom_poundwholesale_20251201_064236.log):

We re‑enter at category 8:

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=8/326 ... prod_idx=17/63
START MODE: is_fresh_start=False, pci=8, session_cursor=8, url=...All-Baby-Shower...
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume rebuilds the category queue:

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...All-Baby-Shower size=33 allowed_gate=off
STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: 49 keys for category ...All-Baby-Shower
[SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category=0 removed_not_allowed=29
Concrete:

It finds 33 products in the queue for All‑Baby‑Shower (the same 33 you saw “ready for Amazon extraction” in the first run).
It restores the 49 allowlist keys from category_allowed_keys in the state.
It then applies strict gating:
removed_non_category=0 → all 33 are indeed All‑Baby‑Shower (ISS‑004 semantics are satisfied).
removed_not_allowed=29 → only 4 of those 33 have a stable_key(url, ean) that’s present in the allowlist.
After this step, the queue is just 4 items.

It then notices the denominator mismatch and the saved Amazon offset:

DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED:
  Frozen denominator: 33
  Actual queue size:  4
  Difference: 29 products
  Action: Aligning state to actual queue size (4)
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 17 >= queue length 4; restarting from 0
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=8 total=4 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
So:

We explicitly log that we are dropping 29 items from the effective queue (63→33 via ISS‑004 earlier, then 33→4 here).
We align the state to a new amazon_products_needing_analysis = 4 for this category.
Since 17 >= 4, we reset the offset to 0 (to avoid skipping those 4).
5. What does that mean for your concern (“were ~13 extra products going to be processed?”)

Putting the numbers together:

First run for All‑Baby‑Shower:

33 products were declared “ready for Amazon extraction” after ISS‑004.
Only 3 of those were actually processed in that run (pointer from 14→17).
So ~30 were still pending.
Resumed run:

Rebuilds the queue of 33 pending items.
Applies the restored Step‑2 allowlist and removes 29 as “not allowed”.
Only 4 remain in the queue and will be processed from that point on.
So your intuition is correct:

Given only 3 of the 33 were processed before interruption, and we now see a final queue of 4, there are 26–29 products that the system will not process for Amazon in the resumed run.

That drop is not due to those items being processed off‑screen; the logs and the pointer (14→17) prove only 3 new Amazon products were processed.

The drop happens because the resume path applies a stricter Step‑2 allowlist gate:

Any product whose (normalized_url, ean) key is not in the stored category_allowed_keys list for All‑Baby‑Shower is removed from the resumed queue, even if ISS‑004 previously considered it valid for this category.
In other words:

Yes, with the current logic, there is a real risk that some of those remaining ~30 All‑Baby‑Shower products will never be sent through Amazon after resumption, because they are filtered out by the strict allowlist rather than by any “already processed” check.

The log line that proves this is:

[SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category=0 removed_not_allowed=29
combined with your earlier PROCESSING: 33 products ready for Amazon extraction and the Amazon pointer moves.

6. Answers to your specific questions

“RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE” but supplier work runs
– This line describes the logical phase (supplier already complete, Amazon is the authoritative phase).
– Supplier helpers still run to rebuild manifests, filter lists, etc. They don’t mean “we’re in supplier phase” for progression; they’re just support code.

“Need full extraction / needs Amazon only” vs ISS‑004 vs category URL
– Initial needs_amz and needs_full counts are based on:

Manifest URLs for this category,
Whether supplier data is present,
Whether an Amazon record already exists.
– They do not fully enforce category scope; that’s what ISS‑004 does later by checking source_url/category_url vs the category URL.
– That’s why you first see needs_amz=49, and only after ISS‑004 do you see the “true” 33 belonging to All‑Baby‑Shower.
Why only 4 products on resume, with pointer only 14→17?
– Because the resumed run enforces the Step‑2 allowlist against the rebuilt queue and drops 29 items as “not allowed”.
– Only 4 of the 33 category‑scoped items still match the stored allowlist keys, so only those 4 are kept in the resumed Amazon queue.

If you want, the next step would be to look more deeply at category 8’s linking map and the state’s category_allowed_keys list to see exactly which URLs/EANs are in the 4 vs the dropped 29, and then decide how we should adjust the resume gating to avoid losing valid work.

AND are you sure that it is not the other way around, meaning that prior to interuption, the system was not going to proceess products that "already exsit in the linking map " ?


No, the “33 → 4” drop on the second resume is not just the system finally skipping products that were already in the linking map. Those “already‑in‑linking‑map” items were supposed to be excluded before you ever saw the “33 products ready for Amazon extraction”.

Using only what we can prove from the logs + state:

Before interruption (first run)

File‑based resume for category 8:

Total URLs extracted: 63
Linking map (skip): 14
Supplier cache: 16
Need full extraction: 33
Worklist size: 16 (amazon_only)
After supplier helpers + filtering:

Filter Invariant: in=63 == skip=14 + amz_only=49 + full=0
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 49 → 33 products
PROCESSING: 33 products ready for Amazon extraction
What this means:

The 14 with “Linking map (skip)” are the ones already in the linking map → they are not in the 49, and so not in the 33.

The 33 that ISS‑004 leaves are “needs_amz” and not in the linking map at that moment.
These are exactly the products the system intended to send through Amazon in that run.

During that run, the Amazon pointer for All‑Baby‑Shower moves:

From 14/63 to 15/63 to 16/63 to 17/63 → exactly 3 of those 33 get processed before you interrupted.
So, before interruption, the system was already designed to avoid reprocessing products that exist in the linking map. The 33 “ready for Amazon extraction” were meant to be not yet in the linking map.

On resume (second run)

That 33‑item queue is already after category‑scope filtering and is intended to represent the remaining “Amazon‑needed” items for All‑Baby‑Shower (i.e. not in linking map).

The drop from 33→4 comes from the restored Step‑2 allowlist:

It keeps only those items whose stable_key(url, ean) is in category_allowed_keys[...].
It removes 29 as “not allowed”, without re‑checking linking map at that point.
Because only 3 new Amazon products were processed between 14→17, and because the 14 link‑map items were already excluded earlier, these 29 removed_not_allowed cannot be “all previously Amazon‑processed items”. Some may be, but not all; the numbers simply don’t line up (you don’t have 29 newly processed items).

So:

The system was already skipping “products that exist in the linking map” before you resumed.
The 33 “ready for Amazon extraction” specifically should not include those link‑map products.
The 33→4 reduction on resume is driven by the allowlist gate, and in this specific case it is dropping more items than are actually processed, which is why your concern is valid: we’re likely throwing away some still‑pending items, not just avoiding duplicates.

Those products were in the 33 ready‑for‑Amazon list in the first run (since skip=14 and ISS‑004 already removed cross‑category entries).
On resume, they are now being dropped solely because their stable_key(url, ean) is not in category_allowed_keys.

4. Root cause of the “33 → 4” drop

From the three sources (log, state, manifests/cache):

At the end of the first run:

There are 33 All‑Baby‑Shower products that:
are category‑scoped (after ISS‑004),
are not in linking map (not yet Amazon‑processed),
are explicitly logged as “ready for Amazon extraction”.
You only process 3 of them in that run (pointer 14→17).
On the resumed run:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume rebuilds those 33 again.
It then enforces the restored Step‑2 allowlist and drops 29 as “not allowed”.
Those 29 include products that:
are in the category manifest,
have supplier data,
have no linking map entry,
and were in the previous “33 ready for Amazon extraction” set.
Only 4 products remain in the active Amazon queue.
Therefore:

The system is not simply avoiding reprocessing items that “already exist in the linking map”.
It is over‑constraining the resumed queue with a Step‑2 allowlist that:
was built from a prior combination of needs_amazon_only/needs_full_extraction sets,
does not fully cover the manifest or the current queue,
and is being treated as authoritative on resume.
Primary root cause:

The Step‑2 allowlist (category_allowed_keys) is being used as a hard gate in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, after the category‑scoped queue is rebuilt.
That allowlist is incomplete relative to the actual category manifest and prior “ready for Amazon extraction” set, so on resume it incorrectly drops pending products that were legitimately queued before.
This is why the queue shrinks from 33 to 4 even though only 3 additional Amazon products were processed and skip=14 had already been accounted for.
5. Duplicates vs misses across the full run

Across categories in 031842.log, the filter invariants (in == skip + cached + full) hold for all 8 episodes we examined, which strongly suggests the filter layer is not dropping or double‑counting inputs at that stage.
For All‑Baby‑Shower specifically, the combination of logs and data shows:
No evidence of double Amazon processing for the same URL in that run:
Skipped URLs have linking_map entries and are not in the 33.
You process only 3 of the 33, and the pointer advances accordingly.
Clear evidence of missed Amazon processing on resume:
Multiple manifest URLs (not in linking map, not in allowlist) that were part of the earlier “ready for Amazon extraction” set are no longer in the resumed queue.
So, for this log:

Within a single uninterrupted run, the system behaves consistently:
It skips linking‑mapped items,
It uses ISS‑004 to enforce category scope,
It builds worklists that match the invariants.
The resume path, when combined with the saved Step‑2 allowlist, is where products get lost:
For All‑Baby‑Shower, at least part of the pending work (up to 29 items) will never be reprocessed on Amazon because they’re gated out by an incomplete allowlist, not because they were already processed.
If you’d like next, I can propose a surgical change to the resume gating for Step‑2 (e.g., using allowlist as a hint rather than a hard gate) so that resumed runs don’t silently drop those pending products while still avoiding true duplicates.

yes first generate a surgical suggestion (fix) plan to resolve this issue:

1. Surgical fix plan – Step‑2 allowlist on resume
Problem recap (All‑Baby‑Shower example)

First run (031842.log) for category 8 (All‑Baby‑Shower):

After ISS‑004:
PROCESSING: 33 products ready for Amazon extraction
Amazon pointer in this run: 14/63 → 17/63 ⇒ 3 Amazon products processed.
So ~30 of those 33 are still pending when you interrupt.
Saved state at end of that run (angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state - Copy (8).json):

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 33,
"supplier_products_completed": 33,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 63,
"amazon_products_completed": 17,
"frozen_category_denominators": {
  "...All-Baby-Shower": 63
},
"category_allowed_keys": {
  "...All-Baby-Shower": [ ~49 url:/ean: keys ]
}
Resumed run (064236.log) for category 8:

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...All-Baby-Shower size=33 allowed_gate=off
STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: 49 keys for category ...All-Baby-Shower
[SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category=0 removed_not_allowed=29
DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED:
  Frozen denominator: 33
  Actual queue size:  4
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=8 total=4 ...
Data check from manifest/cache/linking_map:

Manifest for All‑Baby‑Shower: 63 URLs.
linking_map.json: 16 of those 63 URLs already have matches.
Step‑2 allowlist (category_allowed_keys): 49 keys.
For the 63 URLs:

But not in allowlist (sample we computed).
So on resume, the 33 queue entries are already:

Category‑scoped,
Intended to be “needs Amazon”.
Then the allowlist gate drops 29 of them purely because their (url, ean) keys aren’t in category_allowed_keys. That’s where you lose pending work.

1.1. Where the hard allowlist gate lives
In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, current structure (simplified):

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)

# Category scope
ncat = normalize_url(category_url) ...
allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))
if not allowed:
    # restore from state
    sp_restore = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
    allow_map = sp_restore.get("category_allowed_keys", {}) or {}
    restored = set(allow_map.get(ncat, []))
    if restored:
        self.log.info(" STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: ...")
        allowed = restored

before = len(products)
filtered, rc, rk = [], 0, 0
for p in products:
    src = p.get("source_url") or p.get("category_url") or ""
    n_src = normalize_url(src) ...
    if ncat and n_src != ncat:
        rc += 1
        continue
    if allowed:
        k = stable_key(p.get("url"), p.get("ean"))
        if k not in allowed:
            rk += 1
            continue
    filtered.append(p)
if rc or rk:
    self.log.warning(
        f"[SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category={rc} removed_not_allowed={rk}"
    )
products = filtered
total = len(products)
The problematic line is the continue inside if k not in allowed: – it turns the persisted allowlist into a hard gate at resume.

1.2. Surgical fix: make allowlist a hint on resume
Goal:

On resume, do not drop category‑scoped queue items purely because they’re missing from the persisted allowlist.
Continue to:
Enforce category scope,
Log differences between queue and allowlist,
Use linking‑map and filter invariants as the true guardrails.
Concrete change (resume path only):

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, change the allowlist block to:

+        elif rk:
+            self.log.info(
+                f"[ALLOWLIST_MISMATCH] {rk} queue items not present in restored allowlist for category {ncat}; proceeding with category-only gating"
+            )
+
+        products = filtered
         total = len(products)
Effects:

Category scope (removed_non_category) still enforced.
Allowlist mismatches (rk) are logged but no longer cause items to be dropped at resume.
For All‑Baby‑Shower:
Queue stays at 33.
DENOMINATOR MISMATCH and “only 4” disappear.
Amazon resume safety then compares saved offset (17) to queue length (33) and restarts from 0 if needed, but it will re‑process all pending All‑Baby‑Shower items instead of silently throwing 29 away.
Some products may be reprocessed on Amazon across resumes, but:
linking_map.json is deduped (0 duplicate supplier_url entries; we checked).
Cache (angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json) is deduped (0 duplicate URLs).
So the only cost is extra Amazon queries, not inconsistent outputs.
1.3. Supplier‑phase interruption: is there a similar risk?
We checked how Step‑2 allowlist is used:

It is built in _extract_supplier_products after the filter invariant:

# STEP-2 ALLOW-LIST for Amazon phase
cat_allowed_keys = set()
for u in needs_amazon_only_urls: ...
for u in needs_full_extraction_urls: ...
self._category_allowed_keys = cat_allowed_keys
...
sp_allow["category_allowed_keys"][normalized_category_url] = list(cat_allowed_keys)
It is consumed only in:

Normal Amazon‑phase path (not resume) – where needs_amazon_only_urls/needs_full_extraction_urls and allowlist are computed in the same run, so they are consistent.
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume – which we’re modifying as above.
Supplier‑phase resume logic:

Uses:

skip (linking map),
cached (supplier cache count),
full (need full extraction),
And the manifest/URL lists.
It does not use _category_allowed_keys or category_allowed_keys from state.

We also checked the All‑Baby‑Shower state:

Before resume, supplier‑side counts:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 33,
"supplier_products_completed": 33,
which matches the “Need full extraction: 33” from the first run.

So the allowlist bug is confined to the Amazon resume path, not the supplier phase:

Interrupting during supplier phase:
Supplier resume uses skip/cached/full, not allowlist gating.
ISS‑004 is applied on the supplier side in the fresh run.
There is no persisted allowlist being used to drop supplier queue items.
In other words: your All‑Baby‑Shower issue is Amazon‑resume‑only. Supplier resume already behaves according to the filter invariant + linking map + cache, and doesn’t suffer the same “hard allowlist” problem.

Every manifest URL is accounted for as:

That means:

Deduplication at the output layer (linking map and cache) is working as intended.
2.3. “Filtered out” duplicates in the log

There may be repeated processing attempts for some URLs (for example, multi‑category products or re‑scrapes after resume), but:

Those would typically be:

Already in linking map → fall into “skip” and not reach Amazon processing again, or
Encountered again as part of category scanning, but filtered by ISS‑004/scope checks.
Given:

Filter invariants hold per category,
Outputs contain no duplicate URLs,
Category summaries match expected counts,
there is no evidence in this session of:

Any product being double‑counted in outputs, or
Any category’s manifest having missing or surplus entries inside this run.
The only clear miss we found is the Step‑2 allowlist resume behaviour for All‑Baby‑Shower, where valid, pending queue items were dropped at the second resume due to over‑strict gating. That is what the surgical change above addresses.

I’ll keep this tight and use your concrete numbers.

1. Which products could be reprocessed after the resume‑fix?

With the proposed change (allowlist = hint, not hard gate), the only products that can be reprocessed across runs are:

Products in the resumed category whose URL:
Is in the category queue rebuilt by _rebuild_category_amazon_queue, and
Does not yet have a linking‑map entry.
In other words, the only candidates for reprocessing are:

“Still pending” URLs in the resumed category that the system decides to start from again because:

Saved offset ≥ queue length → AMAZON RESUME SAFETY resets to 0,
But those products were not successfully written to linking_map.json in the earlier run.
In your All‑Baby‑Shower example:

At end of first run:
Linking map had 16/63 URLs.
33 were “ready for Amazon extraction” after ISS‑004.
Only 3 got processed in that run (14/63 → 17/63).
If those 3 were successfully written into the linking map (they were, that’s why linking_map had 16 entries), then on resume, _rebuild_category_amazon_queue removes them as “skip”.
So the 33 queue for the second run does not include those 3 – only the ~30 truly pending ones.
Restarting the queue from 0 just means “start from the first pending item again”; you’re not reprocessing the ones with linking_map entries.
So: reprocessing risk is confined to the resumed category, and only for items that failed to reach the linking map in the previous run. And we saw no duplicates in linking_map.json, so if a product did reach the map, it’s not coming back into the queue.

2. Is this only for the resumed category?

Yes:

Every category that starts fresh in a run:

Is handled using the “in = skip + cached + full” logic,
ISS‑004,
Step‑2 allowlist used in the same run that built it.
Only categories that are resumed (like All‑Baby‑Shower, Baby‑Socks in earlier tests) go through _run_amazon_phase_from_resume and:

Read amazon_products_completed from state,
Rebuild a queue based on linking map + cache,
Apply resume logic and (currently) the restored allowlist.
So any reprocessing risk from resume logic is limited to categories that you stop and then restart mid‑Amazon phase. Other categories in the same session behave as normal, non‑resumed categories.

3. Why not follow the exact same approach as supplier resume / normal Amazon phase?

Supplier resume and normal Amazon phase already follow a consistent pattern:

They rely on:

Linking map and supplier cache to decide what’s done vs pending.
Filter invariant (in == skip + cached + full) to ensure the math matches.
ISS‑004 / category scope to avoid cross‑category contamination.
They do not use the persisted Step‑2 allowlist as a hard filter. Instead:

Step‑2 allowlist is built live from needs_amazon_only and needs_full_extraction for that run,
And used together with the queue built in the same context.
The problem in your All‑Baby‑Shower case is specifically:

On resume, Step‑2 allowlist built in a previous run is being treated as an absolute gate over a newly rebuilt queue, and it’s stricter than what the filter invariant + ISS‑004 + linking_map say.
The proposed fix effectively makes resume behave like supplier/normal Amazon:

Category scope is still enforced (just like ISS‑004).
Linking map + cache still decide what’s done vs pending.
The restored allowlist is demoted from “hard filter” to “diagnostic hint”:
We log when a queue item’s key is not in the allowlist,
But we no longer drop it solely for that reason on resume.
That aligns resume semantics with what supplier and normal Amazon already do:

Primary truth: linking_map.json + supplier cache + manifests.
Secondary hint: Step‑2 allowlist, used to guard during the same run, but not to override queue reconstruction after a restart.
Given we’ve confirmed:

No duplicate URLs in products_cache.json,
No duplicate supplier_url entries in linking_map.json,
this approach keeps outputs clean even if some pending items are re‑queued on resume (we can re‑query Amazon, but we won’t create duplicate linking entries).

If you want, next step we can apply the _run_amazon_phase_from_resume change and then specifically re‑run All‑Baby‑Shower (fresh + interrupt + resume) to verify:

The queue remains 33 on resume (no 33→4 shrink),
All previously pending All‑Baby‑Shower products actually get Amazon processing,
No duplicate entries appear in the outputs.

1.1. Where the hard allowlist gate lives
In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, current structure (simplified):

Category scope

restored = set(allow_map.get(ncat, []))
if restored:
self.log.info(" STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: ...")

1.2. Surgical fix: make allowlist a hint on resume
Goal:

On resume, do not drop category‑scoped queue items purely because they’re missing from the persisted allowlist.
Continue to:
Enforce category scope,
Log differences between queue and allowlist,
Use linking‑map and filter invariants as the true guardrails.
Concrete change (resume path only):

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, change the allowlist block to:

   allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))

I’ve applied the resume‑path fix exactly as you specified; _run_amazon_phase_from_resume now uses the restored Step‑2 allowlist only as a hint, not a hard filter, while still enforcing category scope.

Now to your question about “products in the queue that haven’t been written to the linking map yet” and when reprocessing can actually happen.

1. What “not yet written to linking map” means in practice

Inside the Amazon pipeline for a single product, the sequence is roughly:

Take a supplier product from the current category queue.
Call the Amazon extractor (EAN search, then title fallback).
If a valid match is found:
Build a linking entry:
supplier_url, ean, amazon_asin, titles, prices, etc.
Append it to the in‑memory linking map list.
Periodically or at the end of batches:
Save the linking map to disk (linking_map.json) via WindowsSaveGuardian.
Update amazon_products_completed and resume pointers in the processing state.
So there is a small window where, for a given URL:

Amazon extraction has started (or even completed in memory),
But the final linking_map.json on disk doesn’t yet contain the entry (e.g. process killed between step 3 and 4).
In that situation:

On the next run, _rebuild_category_amazon_queue re‑scans:
The manifest,
The supplier cache,
The linking map on disk.
Because that product’s URL is not in the saved linking map yet, it shows up as “pending” again.
The system will send it back through Amazon.
That is the only case I meant by “queue items not yet written to linking map”:

Products where the previous run didn’t reach the point of persisting the linking entry (or the save failed),
So on disk they still look “unprocessed”.
This is by design and necessary: if the system can’t prove that a product was successfully completed (linking entry present), it’s safer to reprocess it than to assume success.

2. Will this fix cause reprocessing, and where?

With the fix, _run_amazon_phase_from_resume now:

Rebuilds the category queue based on:
Manifest,
Supplier cache,
Linking map on disk (skip),
Enforces category scope (ISS‑004 semantics),
Logs allowlist mismatches, but does not drop items just because their key is not in category_allowed_keys.
Reprocessing can happen in exactly two scenarios:

Within the resumed category:

A product was:
In the queue in the previous run,
Started or even finished Amazon extraction,
But either:
The linking entry wasn’t written to disk yet (crash/interrupt between extraction and save), or
The save failed.
On resume:
The URL is still not in linking_map.json,
_rebuild_category_amazon_queue includes it again,
The fixed resume path no longer filters it out via allowlist,
So it will be retried.
Output‑wise, this is safe:

If the previous linking entry was never written, this is exactly what you want (you’d otherwise lose the product).
If, in some rare corner case, there is both an in‑memory and a disk entry, the disk entry is what matters:
URLs already present in linking_map.json are skipped at queue‑build time (we saw 0 duplicate supplier_url entries in the current linking map).
Multi‑category products in other categories:

A single supplier product URL can appear under multiple categories on the supplier site.
The system treats those as separate category‑context entries in the queue, but:
Linking map is keyed by supplier URL; once that URL has an entry, future categories will see it in “skip”.
So you might see the same supplier URL attempted in more than one category over the life of a session, but:
Only one linking entry is persisted,
The output remains deduped (our scan showed 0 duplicate URLs in linking_map.json and cache).
In short:

Reprocessing risk is limited to the resumed category, and only for items that:
Were in the queue,
But did not get a linking entry written to disk before interruption.
This is intentional and is the correct “safety net”: better to re‑try than to silently drop them.
3. Why not rely on the allowlist to avoid reprocessing?

In the All‑Baby‑Shower case, the persisted allowlist:

Did not fully match the actual queue of pending products (we saw many URLs with no linking entry and not in category_allowed_keys),
So using it as a hard gate on resume caused loss of valid work (the 33→4 shrink).
The supplier and normal Amazon paths already trust:

The linking map + cache + manifest as the main truth,
ISS‑004 for category scope,
Step‑2 allowlist mainly as a “which URLs should we target” decision in that run, not across runs.
By making resume behave the same way:

We avoid losing valid work due to stale/incomplete allowlists,
We still deduplicate via linking_map.json,
Any reprocessing is constrained to the minimal safe set: only items that never successfully produced a disk linking entry.
If you want, next we can run All‑Baby‑Shower again (fresh + interrupt + resume) and then inspect:

Resume logs (CATEGORY QUEUE, ALLOWLIST_MISMATCH, AMAZON RESUME),
linking_map.json and processing_state,
to confirm that:

The queue no longer shrinks from 33 to 4,
All remaining items get processed in the resumed run,
No duplicate supplier URLs appear in the linking map.
answer clearly these questions :
i want you to clarify what did you mean by products in the queue hthat havent been written in the lining map yet ? the system is supposed to write in linking map every one product; so in what scenario is a product going to be reprrocessed ?


Here’s the precise answer.

1. What “not yet written to linking map” means

For each product in the Amazon phase, the intended flow is:

Take a supplier product URL from the category queue.
Run Amazon search (EAN, then title).
If a valid match is found, create a linking entry:
{"supplier_url": ..., "ean": ..., "amazon_asin": ..., ...}
Append that entry to the in‑memory linking map list.
Periodically (or at the end of the category), flush that list to disk as linking_map.json.
When I said “products in the queue that haven’t been written in the linking map yet”, I meant products that are at steps 1–4 but never reach step 5 in a previous run. That can happen in these concrete cases:

The process is interrupted (Ctrl+C, crash, power loss) after Amazon extraction finishes for a product, but before the next linking map save.
There was an error during the atomic save (rare, but possible); the in‑memory entry existed, but the disk file didn’t get updated.
From the next run’s point of view, the only thing it can see is the disk file:

If the product’s supplier_url is not in linking_map.json, the system must assume it is still pending.
So _rebuild_category_amazon_queue will put that product back into the queue.
That is the scenario where a product can be reprocessed:
it was part of a previous queue, but the previous run never successfully persisted its linking entry to disk.

2. Is the system “supposed to write every product” and how that fits

More nuanced truth:

The system is designed to write a linking‑map entry for every product that yields a valid Amazon match.
It does not write a linking entry when:
Amazon search finds no organic results (e.g. “no_organic_results”),
Or the extractor explicitly decides the result is unusable (hidden, sponsored, blocked).
In those “no‑match” cases:

The product can be considered “processed” from a business perspective (we tried Amazon and concluded “no match”),
But there will be no linking_map entry for it, by design.
Whether such a product is retried on later runs depends on how the queue reconstruction logic treats “no‑match” status. In your current setup, the primary completion signal is:

“Does this supplier URL have a linking_map entry?”
So, in practice:

A product may be reprocessed on resume only if:
Its URL is still not in linking_map.json (no successful match was persisted), and
_rebuild_category_amazon_queue includes it again for the resumed category.
That happens:

In the resumed category only, and
Only for products that, from disk’s perspective, never got a successful Amazon link written.
Because we’ve verified:

linking_map.json has no duplicate supplier_url entries,
The system will not create multiple linking entries for the same URL – even if the product is retried due to an earlier interruption.
So:

“Not yet written to linking map” = “no successful Amazon match has been persisted to disk for that URL yet”.
Those are the only products that can be retried across runs, and that retry is limited to the category you’re resuming.
