I’ll answer your two points separately.

1. “Write every 1 product” vs reprocessing

Given your config:

Linking map save frequency = 1 (commit after every product),
And products are processed strictly one‑by‑one,
then in a clean run, the only product that could plausibly be “processed but not yet written” is the one that is in‑flight at the exact moment of interruption:

Example:
Product N:
Amazon extraction finishes.
Linking map entry is appended in memory.
Before the per‑product save runs, the process is killed (hard crash, power outage).
When you restart, disk linking_map.json still only has entries up to product N‑1.
Product N’s URL is then seen as “not in linking map” and will be re‑queued.
So with save_every=1, the “processed but not written” scenario is limited essentially to the interrupted product in the worst case.

Separately, there are products that:

Went through Amazon search,
Did not get a valid match (no organic results, or all candidates rejected),
And, by design, do not get a linking_map entry at all.
Those are “processed” in the sense that Amazon was queried, but they’re indistinguishable from “never processed” if you only look at linking_map.json. On resume, they can be re‑queued unless there’s extra state saying “no match already tried”. That’s a different category from the interrupted product, but still falls under “no linking entry → candidate for reprocessing”.

Crucially:

We confirmed linking_map.json has 0 duplicate supplier_url entries.
That means even if we re‑queue something on resume, once it does produce a valid link, it will only ever appear once in the outputs.
So you’re right: with your config, there won’t be “many” products in the situation I described; in practice it’s:

The interrupted one,
Plus any “no match” items that you are comfortable re‑trying on a later session.
2. Why amazon_products_needing_analysis can be 63 while supplier totals drop

Your state snippet for All‑Baby‑Shower after the first (long) run:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 33,
"supplier_products_completed": 33,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 63,
"amazon_products_completed": 18,
This is before you resume.

What’s going on:

For supplier:

After the manifest and filtering, the system concludes:
33 products in this category actually needed supplier extraction in that run.
So supplier_products_needing_extraction is set to 33 (the filtered worklist size).
That is a “queue‑length” style counter.
For Amazon (in the same, non‑resumed run):

amazon_products_needing_analysis remains at the original discovered total: 63.
amazon_products_completed = 18 means “we have advanced 18 positions in the 63‑denominator space”.
The logs show the finer‑grained picture:
14 already skipped by linking map,
Some subset cached,
33 effectively “ready for Amazon extraction” after ISS‑004.
The reason we’re stricter about aligning denominators for supplier/amazon on resume, but not forcing that during the first long run, is:

In the first run:
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 63 doesn’t cause harm:
Progress and summaries are driven by the queue and linking_map; category completion is also driven by the hybrid controller calling mark_category_completed.
The fact that 33 of 63 are actually pending Amazon tasks is captured by the log (“PROCESSING: 33 products ready for Amazon extraction”) and by the queue, not just by that counter.
On resume:
We rebuild a new queue (after some items have been processed in previous runs).
Some of the original 63 are now skip, some have no‑match statuses, some might be filtered out.
Keeping amazon_products_needing_analysis = 63 at that point can confuse the resume finalizer, which decides “category Amazon queue is done” based on:
done >= amazon_products_needing_analysis
That’s why _run_amazon_phase_from_resume explicitly aligns the counter to the current rebuilt queue length (total) before using it for resume completion.
You saw this happen in the All‑Baby‑Shower second resume before the fix:

It logged:

DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED:
  Frozen denominator: 33
  Actual queue size: 4
  Action: Aligning state to actual queue size (4)
That code is doing exactly the “align denom to queue length for resume” that I described. The 63 in your snippet is simply the pre‑resume, pre‑alignment state from the first run; after we resume, we adjust it to match the actual queue.

So:

It’s not that Amazon is “allowed” to keep the old 63 while supplier must use the filtered count.
It’s that:
During the initial long run, *_needing_* can be a discovered total (63) while the real worklist and progress are tracked via queue + linking_map.
On resume, we now align those *_needing_* counters to the rebuilt queue length, for both supplier and Amazon paths, to keep the resume logic internally consistent.
The All‑Baby‑Shower bug you saw (33→4) wasn’t caused by that 63; it was caused by the allowlist being used as a hard filter on the resumed queue. That is what we just fixed.

You’re right to push on this; your current state + log show something important.

1. What’s actually happening in this resumed run

From the latest state (angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json):

"persistent_category_index": 8,
"current_category_index": 8,
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/All-Baby-Shower",

  "...All-Baby-Shower": 63
}
From run_custom_poundwholesale_20251201_094557.log for All‑Baby‑Shower:

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...All-Baby-Shower size=33 allowed_gate=off

...

RESUME PTR ... prod_idx=28/63
Key facts:

The actual resumed Amazon queue length is 33 (CATEGORY QUEUE size=33).
commit_amazon_progress is logging frozen_queue_len=33, so it is using 33 for its internal logic (the queue_len argument).
The processing state still shows amazon_products_needing_analysis = 63 because we have not changed that field in this code path.
So your observation is correct: after resume, amazon_products_needing_analysis stayed at 63, while the real queue for this resumed category is 33.

2. Why my earlier explanation needs adjusting

Earlier I simplified and said: “on resume we align the denominator to the queue length.” What the code actually does is more subtle:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume calls:

frozen_denom = self.state_manager.get_frozen_denominator(category_url)
get_frozen_denominator uses supplier_products_needing_extraction as the authoritative denominator for the current category when category_denominator_frozen is true:

if sp.get("category_denominator_frozen") and sp.get("current_category_url") == nurl:
    return int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0)) or None
In your state, for All‑Baby‑Shower:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 33
So get_frozen_denominator(...) returns 33, not 63.

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume then compares:

total = len(products)  # 33
frozen_denom = 33
if frozen_denom is not None and frozen_denom != total:
    # log DENOMINATOR MISMATCH and align; NOT triggered here
Because frozen_denom (from supplier) and the rebuilt queue length total both equal 33, there is no mismatch, and that block does not run. As a result:

amazon_products_needing_analysis stays at its old value (63) in system_progression.
The resume logic for Amazon uses the queue_len argument (33) instead, which is why you see AMAZON PROGRESS ... frozen_queue_len=33 in the log.
So the truth is:

Supplier denominator and queue length are aligned at 33 → no “denom mismatch” event.
Amazon denominator (amazon_products_needing_analysis=63) is not being corrected here; it’s acting more like a legacy “total discovered” value.
The actual resume and progress for Amazon are driven by queue_len=33, not by the 63.
That’s why you’re seeing:

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 63 in state,
But the system behaving correctly on resume with a 33‑item queue.
3. Does this 63 cause functional problems now?

In this resumed Amazon path:

Queue construction + category gating → 33 items.

commit_amazon_progress uses:

queue_len = 33 (you see it in the log),
queue_idx = 17..27 as it walks the queue.
The Amazon finalizer inside _perform_amazon_commit:

denom = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0))
done = int(queue_idx)
if denom > 0 and done >= denom:
    mark_category_completed(...)
would indeed need denom to match the queue length to auto‑complete based purely on these fields. However:

In your hybrid flow, category completion is also triggered by the surrounding workflow (e.g., after queue is exhausted in _run_hybrid_processing_mode), not only by this finalizer.

You haven’t finished the category 8 Amazon queue yet in this run (pointer is only 28/63), so we haven’t hit the completion condition to see which path actually fires.

So as of now:

The 63 is not blocking the resumed queue from running correctly.
It could make a pure “Amazon finalizer completes category when done >= denom” less accurate for resumed categories; that’s something we can tighten up later, but it’s separate from the allowlist issue you saw.
4. Summary

Yes, after your latest resume, amazon_products_needing_analysis is still 63 for All‑Baby‑Shower.
That is because the denominator‑alignment logic in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume uses supplier_products_needing_extraction (33), not the Amazon field, to decide whether to adjust anything, and in your case 33 == queue length, so no alignment happens.
The resumed Amazon work is driven by the 33‑item queue (CATEGORY QUEUE size=33, AMAZON PROGRESS ... frozen_queue_len=33), not by the 63.
So while the counter is cosmetically confusing and could be improved, it’s not in conflict with the new allowlist fix or the resumed queue behaviour you’re seeing now.

No – it will not process more than the 33 for this category, and it will not “keep going to 63” and re‑run 33–63.

In your current run:

The actual Amazon worklist for All‑Baby‑Shower is the queue rebuilt by _rebuild_category_amazon_queue, which the log shows as:

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...All-Baby-Shower size=33 allowed_gate=off
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume uses this size=33 as the queue length when it loops and when it calls commit_amazon_progress:

AMAZON PROGRESS: cat=8/326 idx=17->17 (frozen_queue_len=33) ...
...
AMAZON PROGRESS: cat=8/326 idx=27->27 (frozen_queue_len=33) ...
Here:

idx is the 0‑based index in the 33‑item queue (17, 18, …),
frozen_queue_len=33 is the queue length passed into the state manager.
The prod_idx=/63 lines (e.g. prod_idx=28/63) are just logging the original discovered index (63 from the manifest), not the number of items the loop will process.

Crucially:

The resume code iterates over total = len(products), which is 33, and stops when it has walked that queue.
There is no code that iterates to 63 or re‑queues items beyond the 33 in CATEGORY QUEUE.
Once those 33 queue entries have been attempted (and the category is marked complete by the hybrid controller / state manager), All‑Baby‑Shower is done; it does not then process “33–63” again.
So your primary concern is safe: for this category, the system will process at most the 33 items in the rebuilt queue, not all 63 originally discovered items, and it will not keep going past that to reprocess products 33–63.

You’re seeing two different “views” of the same category, depending on phase.

Using your examples:

Supplier‑phase snapshot

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 53,
"supplier_products_completed": 63,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 63,
"amazon_products_completed": 0
Amazon‑phase (later) snapshot

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 33,
"supplier_products_completed": 33,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 63,
"amazon_products_completed": 28
What these mean:

supplier_products_needing_extraction
In supplier phase:
Initially set to the discovered count for that category (e.g. 53 or 63).
After the file‑based resume + filters, it is reset to the active worklist size for that category.
In your Amazon‑phase snapshot for All‑Baby‑Shower:
It is 33 because the system has concluded “33 supplier products are the authoritative denominator for this category” (after ISS‑004 and filtering).
supplier_products_completed = 33 means supplier work for those 33 is done.
amazon_products_needing_analysis
This is currently still carrying the original discovered total for that category (63 in your case).
It does not get updated to 33 in the normal path, even though the actual Amazon queue is smaller.
The Amazon resume logic uses the queue length from the rebuilt queue (total / frozen_queue_len=33), not this 63, to control how many items it actually processes.
So there is an asymmetry:

Supplier fields are being reused as the filtered denominator (33) once the system knows how many products really belong in that category’s worklist.
Amazon fields are still showing the discovered total (63), while the actual Amazon work is driven by the 33‑item queue.
This is why:

In supplier phase you see “nice” denominators that match the filtered worklist (e.g. 33/33).
In Amazon phase you see:
supplier_* reflecting the filtered denominator (33/33),
amazon_* still reflecting “63 discovered”, while the queue and logs (CATEGORY QUEUE size=33, frozen_queue_len=33) reflect the real Amazon worklist size.
The important part for correctness is:

The system will only process the 33 queue entries for that category on Amazon; it does not keep going to 63 or reprocess 33–63.
The 63 in amazon_products_needing_analysis is legacy “total discovered” counter; it’s confusing, but it’s not driving the resumed queue length now that we’ve fixed the allowlist gating.

Here’s what I can confirm from code + logs + state.

1. Supplier vs Amazon counters – what really happens

From the code:

get_frozen_denominator(category_url) (used by Amazon resume) uses supplier_products_needing_extraction as the authoritative denominator for the current category when category_denominator_frozen is true:

if sp["category_denominator_frozen"] and sp["current_category_url"] == nurl:
    return int(sp["supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0]) or None
amazon_products_needing_analysis is not touched in the no‑mismatch case; it keeps whatever value it had (often the original discovered total).

So for resumed categories:

Supplier fields (supplier_products_needing_extraction, supplier_products_completed) reflect the filtered denominator (e.g. 33 for All‑Baby‑Shower).
Amazon fields (amazon_products_needing_analysis, amazon_products_completed) can still show the old “discovered total” (63) and progress against that total, even though the real resumed queue length is smaller (33). The resumed Amazon logic uses the queue length argument (frozen_queue_len in the logs), not that 63.
You were right to doubt my earlier simplification; for Amazon, the denominator field is not being aligned here in the no‑mismatch case.

The system considered supplier_products_needing_extraction = 55 and supplier_products_completed = 55 for All‑Bears.
The 54 you’re seeing now in the state (supplier_products_needing_extraction: 54) is a later artifact; it does not match the resume log, and the frozen denominators map still has 55 for All‑Bears.

Short, factual answer: in those runs, for those categories, there simply weren’t any cross‑category products to remove, so the ISS‑004 log line never appeared.

Here’s the breakdown.

1. All‑Bears in 094557 (first run)

There is no:

ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: X → Y products
In the ISS‑004 code, that line is only logged if the category filter actually changes the count:

original_count = len(products)
category_filtered = [...]
if len(category_filtered) != original_count:
    self.log.warning("ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: ...")
So for All‑Bears in this run:

Either:
ISS‑004 ran but len(category_filtered) == original_count → nothing removed, so no log; or
The path that uses _process_chunk_with_main_workflow_logic (where ISS‑004 lives) wasn’t involved, and the category list was already clean.
In both cases, the outcome is the same: no products from other categories were present in that worklist.
The filter invariant and the separate SKIP_ENTIRELY / NEEDS_AMAZON_ONLY lists show no “extra” items being thrown away.

2. All‑Bears in 100633 (resumed Amazon run)

On resume, the category‑scope filter sits in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

for p in products:
    src = p.get("source_url") or p.get("category_url") or ""
    n_src = normalize_url(src) ...
    if ncat and n_src != ncat:
        rc += 1
        continue
    ...
if rc or rk:
    log.warning("[SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category={rc} ...")
We never see that [SAFETY] log, and CATEGORY QUEUE size=54 equals the number of allowlist keys (54). That tells us:

When the queue was rebuilt for All‑Bears on resume:
No items had source_url/category_url pointing to a different category (removed_non_category=0),
The queue was already category‑pure.
So again: there was nothing for ISS‑004‑style logic to remove.

3. So is this a bug or not?

For these category 9 runs, based on code + logs:

There is no evidence of cross‑category contamination in All‑Bears.
The supplier filter invariant shows a clean split (1 skip, 54 cached, 0 full).
The Amazon resume queue is 54 All‑Bears items, and the resume filter reports no non‑category removals.
ISS‑004’s log line only appears when it actually removes something; in All‑Bears it had nothing to remove, so it’s silent.
For categories where cross‑category products exist (e.g. All‑Baby‑Shower, Baby‑Blankets), you do see:

ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: X → Y products
And/or [SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category=...
So:

For All‑Bears in these logs: no cross‑category products → no ISS‑004 effect to log.
This is expected behaviour, not a bug.
isnt it the other way ? shouldnt iss004 occure for system to know if there are products from other categroies ?


You’re mixing two slightly different things:

The filter logic itself (which checks for cross‑category products).
The ISS‑004 log line, which is only emitted when that logic actually removes something.
For ISS‑004 as implemented:

original_count = len(products)
category_filtered = [...]
if len(category_filtered) != original_count:
    self.log.warning("ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: X → Y products")
The check runs whenever that code path is used (it looks at source_url/category_url vs the expected category).
The log line only appears if len(category_filtered) != original_count – i.e., if at least one product was actually removed.
So:

If there are products from other categories in the list, ISS‑004 both runs and logs (ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 49 → 33 products etc.).
If there are zero such products, ISS‑004 (or the equivalent category‑scope filter in the resume path) still runs the comparison, but:
It finds nothing to drop (removed_non_category=0),
It leaves the list unchanged,
It does not emit the ISS‑004 log, because there’s no change.
For All‑Bears in your two logs:

Supplier side:
FILTER INVARIANT VALIDATED: in=55 == skip=1 + cached=54 + full=0
No ISS‑004 line → the supplier‑side product set was already category‑clean.
Amazon resume (100633):
CATEGORY QUEUE: ... size=54 allowed_gate=off
STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: 54 keys
No [SAFETY] category_queue_enforced removed_non_category=...
That means the resume‑path category filter (n_src == ncat) also found 0 cross‑category items.
So it’s not that “ISS‑004 didn’t run so we don’t know.”
It’s that the category filters ran and found nothing to remove, so they didn’t log ISS‑004 / removed_non_category lines.

if i were to add entries in linking map, adter having interupted the system ( before reusmption); would the system handle those entries, specfically fo the cageroy at which we itneurpted?


Yes – manual edits to the linking map for the interrupted category will be taken into account on the next resume, as long as you do them before starting the new run and the entries are well‑formed.

Here’s how it works, specifically for “the category we interrupted at”:

On startup / resume, the workflow recomputes per‑category skip/cached/full from files, not just from the old counters:

For each category (including the interrupted one), it does a file‑based analysis like:

Total URLs extracted: 55
Linking map (skip): 1
Supplier cache: 54
Need full extraction: 0
Filter invariant: PASS
Those numbers come from:

The manifest for that category,
The current linking_map.json,
The current supplier cache.
So if you’ve added new linking‑map entries for URLs in that category, the next resume will see those URLs as “skip” and they won’t be put into the “needs_amz” set.

Queue rebuild for the interrupted category uses the updated linking map

When resuming Amazon for that category, _rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url):

Loads the manifest URLs for that category,
Filters out any URL whose supplier_url already appears in linking_map.json,
Builds the Amazon queue only from the remaining (not yet linked) URLs.
So:

If you add a linking‑map entry for the interrupted product before the resume, it will be treated as “already processed” and will not be retried.
If you add entries for other URLs in that category, those URLs will also be skipped and will not appear in the resumed queue.
Counters may look “off”, but behaviour follows the files

supplier_products_* and amazon_products_* in system_progression may still show older totals (e.g. 63) until the resume logic recomputes and/or advances them.
The actual behaviour of the resumed run is driven by:
The manifest,
linking_map.json,
The supplier cache,
Not by those counters alone.
That’s why you see filter invariants and “RESUME STATE” logs recomputed from files on each run.

Caveats when editing the linking map

Only add entries for URLs you truly want to treat as “already processed” for that category.
Make sure supplier_url matches exactly the manifest URL (normalization is based on that field).
Do the edit while the system is not running; then start a fresh run so the state manager reads the new file once at startup.
If you follow that, the system will respect your manual linking‑map edits for the interrupted category: those products will be considered done and will not be reprocessed on resume.

if i want to have the system perform the supploer phase in parallel with the amazpm extraction phase. how much work are realsitically talkigabout ? would i need to edit theexitign script or can i crate a additonal script instead or ?


Short version: doing “real” parallel supplier + Amazon in this codebase is non‑trivial and absolutely requires touching the existing workflow/state code. A small extra script alone won’t safely give you that behaviour.

I’ll break it down.

1. What you already have today

The current design is a hybrid, interleaved model, not true parallelism:

_run_hybrid_processing_mode:
Scrapes a category (supplier phase),
Builds manifests, applies filters, caches products,
Then runs Amazon for that category,
Then moves to the next category chunk.
So supplier and Amazon work are interleaved for each category, but not running at the same time.

Under the hood you also already have async I/O (Playwright, CDP), so there is some concurrency in the browser level, but the core workflow is still “supplier, then Amazon” per category.

2. What “parallel” really implies here

If you mean:

One part of the system scraping supplier categories while at the same time
Another part is doing Amazon extraction on previously scraped products,
then you’re talking about:

Two pipelines running concurrently and sharing:
The same OUTPUTS/ tree,
The same linking_map.json,
The same processing state file (angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json),
The same BrowserManager/Playwright resources.
To do that safely, you’d need:

Concurrency‑safe state management:
fixed_enhanced_state_manager is designed for single‑writer atomic updates with WindowsSaveGuardian.
Introducing concurrent writers would require:
Locking protocol (file locks or in‑process mutex),
Or splitting state per phase/category to avoid collisions.
Concurrency‑safe linking‑map writes:
linking_map.json is saved atomically as a whole file.
If supplier and Amazon logic were writing to it at the same time, you’d risk races and lost updates.
Browser/session considerations:
BrowserManager assumes a single workflow is using its CDP context.
Two concurrent workflows would need either:
Shared, re‑entrant BrowserManager,
Or separate Playwright/browser contexts.
That’s already several core components.

3. Editing existing workflow vs “extra script”

Extra script only (e.g. new Python file that runs supplier in one process and Amazon in another) is not enough because:
Both processes would still be reading/writing the same:
processing_state,
linking_map.json,
supplier cache.
Those components currently assume a single active workflow and will not magically coordinate with another process.
To do this correctly you would have to:

Either:
Refactor the existing workflow to schedule supplier and Amazon tasks concurrently inside a single process (e.g. multiple async tasks with careful locking around state and linking map),
Or:
Plan a multi‑process setup and modify:
fixed_enhanced_state_manager to support per‑phase/per‑category state files or explicit locks,
passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py to avoid both phases writing to the same files at the same time.
So you cannot realistically get safe parallel supplier+Amazon by “just adding another script” without touching the core files.

4. How much work are we talking about?

Realistically, in this codebase:

Small, surgical change (like the resume fixes we just did): touching 1–2 functions, a few dozen lines.

True parallel supplier + Amazon (end‑to‑end):

Requires design work:
Decide on concurrency model (multi‑task async vs multi‑process).
Decide how to partition state and outputs.
Requires code changes in multiple files:
tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (workflow/control flow),
utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py (state semantics, locking),
utils/windows_save_guardian.py and/or new locking helpers for shared files,
Potentially utils/browser_manager.py if both phases hit the browser concurrently.
That’s on the order of hundreds of lines across several core modules, plus careful testing:

Not a “quick tweak”, more like a mini‑feature project.
5. Practical recommendation

Given the complexity and your current goals (robust resume, no skipped products):

The safest path is to keep using the current hybrid, interleaved model and refine it (which we’ve been doing) rather than trying to fully parallelize supplier + Amazon.
If you really want parallelism later, it should be approached as a separate, planned refactor with clear decisions on:
State partitioning/locking,
File ownership,
Browser concurrency.
If you want, I can sketch a concrete architecture for a “supplier worker + Amazon worker” model tailored to this project, but implementing it will definitely mean editing the existing workflow and state manager, not just adding one new script.

when i mean in parallel, i dont mean the systme running in parallel on the same catgeoy, i mean i would have extracted a few catgeoies lets say, and then run the system .

and what about having the eystem run for multiple website simutlaniosuyl ? what would that require exactly ? AND TELL ME IN TERMS OF CAUSING "ISSUES" WITH AMAOZIN WEBSITE, IF THAT WOULD MAYBE TRIGGER THEIR ANTIBOT MEASURE OR SOMETHING LIKE THAT


I’ll answer in two parts, matching your scenarios.

1. “Parallel” supplier vs Amazon across categories for the SAME site

You clarified you don’t mean “supplier and Amazon on the same category at once”, but something like:

First run: scrape supplier for several categories (fill cache/manifests).
Later runs: let Amazon chew through those categories, while supplier keeps scraping new categories.
Key points given the current code:

Today, the hybrid mode already interleaves supplier and Amazon per category, but only one category at a time and in a single process.

To truly have:

Process A: scraping new categories for angelwholesale,
Process B: doing Amazon on categories already scraped for angelwholesale,
you’d end up with two processes touching the same files:

Same OUTPUTS/CACHE/processing_states/angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json.
Same OUTPUTS/FBA_ANALYSIS/linking_maps/angelwholesale.co.uk/linking_map.json.
Same OUTPUTS/cached_products/angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json.
Those files are managed under the assumption of one active writer:

fixed_enhanced_state_manager and windows_save_guardian give you atomic writes, but not cross‑process locking.
Two processes can still overwrite each other’s updates even if each individual write is atomic.
Realistically:

Doing this safely for a single supplier would require changes in the core code:

Either:
Splitting state per phase or per category (e.g. separate state files for supplier vs Amazon workers), and
Making sure only one process ever writes a given file.
Or:
Adding proper file‑based locking or a small coordinator so only one process can modify the shared state at a time.
Just “adding another script” that runs Amazon while the existing one scrapes new categories, without editing the state/linking‑map code, is not safe: you’d risk subtle state races and lost updates.

So for one supplier, parallel supplier+Amazon across categories is not a small tweak; it needs intentional refactoring, not a quick wrapper script.

2. Running multiple websites (suppliers) simultaneously

This is much more feasible, because the architecture already separates suppliers:

Each supplier has:
Its own state file (<supplier>_processing_state.json),
Its own linking map (linking_maps/<supplier>/linking_map.json),
Its own supplier cache (<supplier>_products_cache.json).
So if you run:

Process A: run_custom_poundwholesale.py for angelwholesale,
Process B: run_custom_poundwholesale.py for some_other_supplier,
and make sure:

They use different output roots (or at least their default per‑supplier paths),
They do not share the same supplier_name,
then:

File‑wise, they’re independent.
State‑wise, they’re independent.
The Amazon side and anti‑bot risk

Where they are not independent is Amazon:

All suppliers ultimately feed into the same Amazon extractor:
Same IP address (your machine),
Same browser/Playwright session,
Same Amazon site (amazon.co.uk), possibly same account/cookies.
If you run multiple suppliers at once:

The Amazon request rate increases roughly linearly with the number of concurrent workflows.
That’s exactly the kind of pattern Amazon’s anti‑bot systems look for:
Many searches per minute,
Consistent user agent / IP,
Automated navigation patterns.
You already see in the logs:

“Checking for CAPTCHA…”
“No CAPTCHA page detected…”
which means the code is prepared for anti‑bot checks, but not for a much higher request rate.

Practically:

One supplier at a time (sequential) is the lowest‑risk pattern: Amazon sees one stream of human‑like traffic.
Multiple suppliers in parallel increases risk:
2 concurrent suppliers roughly doubles your Amazon query load,
3 or more start to look much more like a scraper cluster.
If you still want to run multiple suppliers at once, the safest way is to:

Keep only one Amazon worker active at a time:
E.g., configure other processes to run supplier‑only phases (no Amazon) or to pause Amazon until the first finishes.
Or, if you really want parallel Amazon across suppliers:
Add aggressive throttling (sleep between searches, maybe use max_products_per_cycle/batch size to keep the rate low),
Accept that CAPTCHAs and temporary blocking are more likely.
Bottom line

Parallel supplier+Amazon for the same supplier (across different categories) is non‑trivial and unsafe without changing the core state/linking‑map code; an extra script alone is not enough.
Running multiple different websites simultaneously is structurally easier (separate state/outputs), but you must treat Amazon as a shared bottleneck:
One Amazon worker at a time is safest.
More concurrency → higher risk of triggering Amazon’s antibot measures.
GIVE Me a detailed analysis and report, on how much would i benifit from having an mcp created for my tool, (tell me what the pros are and list me the different taks/feature i would have doing so); take into consideration that i dont want something extremely advanced, as i gave not done this before.

what would an mcp for my tool take to be create ? would i need ot adjsut existing scripts, or creain new scirpts would suffce ? give me a breakdown on what would be needed, if any exisitng files would need to be edited, other scrips what owul be requried, and so on .


I’ll treat “MCP for your tool” as: a small, dedicated MCP server that wraps your existing Amazon FBA Agent System so an LLM (or other MCP‑aware client) can:

Inspect its state (processing_state, logs, linking map, cache),
Trigger runs/resumes/diagnostics,
Get structured answers without you manually copying paths and snippets.
Given your constraints (no huge refactor, first time doing this), I’ll keep it realistic and incremental.

1. What you’d actually gain from an MCP for this repo
Think in terms of concrete things you do manually today.

1.1. Day‑to‑day tasks that become easier
With an MCP server for this system, you could ask your LLM client things like:

“Show me the current processing state for angelwholesale.co.uk: which category, which phase, which indices?”
“List all categories where supplier is done but Amazon is still pending.”
“For All‑Baby‑Shower, show me the 33 URLs currently in the Amazon queue, and which ones are already in the linking map.”
“Run a resume pass for the current supplier and show me the first 50 lines of the new log.”
“Compare angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state - Copy (8).json with the latest state and summarize differences in frozen denominators and allowed keys.”
Right now you:

Manually copy Windows paths into the prompt,
Ask the model to read specific files,
Manually scan logs and JSON.
With MCP, the client can call your server’s tools to:

Read a logical resource like fba/state/angelwholesale instead of you pasting the JSON,
Run fba/list-categories or fba/category-queue instead of you grepping logs,
Kick off fba/run-resume instead of you calling python run_custom_poundwholesale.py yourself.
1.2. Pros in your specific situation
Given the complexity of your workflow (hybrid mode, resume, ISS‑004/allowlists), the big benefits are:

Structured visibility:
Expose processing_state, linking_map, cache, and key metrics as structured resources.
Let the LLM query “per category” views without you hand‑copying paths and context.
Replay and forensic tools:
Expose a tool for “explain what happened in the last run for category X”, using logs + state + outputs.
You already do this manually; an MCP tool can automate the correlation step.
Safer interventions:
Tools like:
fba/add-linking-entry (with explicit parameters),
fba/fix-state-category-denominator,
can be guarded and audited instead of ad‑hoc JSON edits.
Multi‑supplier scaling:
The same MCP tools can work for any supplier, just by passing supplier_name.
You don’t need different ad‑hoc prompts per supplier; the server knows where each supplier’s state and outputs live.
You don’t need “extremely advanced” behaviour to get value; even read‑only resources + a couple of simple commands are a big quality‑of‑life upgrade for debugging and monitoring.

2. Minimal feature set I’d recommend (v1 MCP server)
Here’s a realistic first version, given where your codebase is now.

2.1. Read‑only resources
fba/state/<supplier>

Returns the current OUTPUTS/CACHE/processing_states/<supplier>_processing_state.json.
Useful for “what phase/category/indices are we at?”
fba/logs/latest/<supplier>

Returns the last N KB of the latest logs/debug/run_custom_poundwholesale_*.log for that supplier.
Optionally parameters for date/time or filename.
fba/linking-map/<supplier>

Returns a filtered or summarized view of:
OUTPUTS/FBA_ANALYSIS/linking_maps/<supplier>/linking_map.json.
E.g. query by category URL, or by supplier URL/EAN.
fba/cache-products/<supplier> (optional)

Returns a small subset (sample or filtered) from OUTPUTS/cached_products/<supplier>_products_cache.json.
2.2. Simple actions
fba/run-resume/<supplier>

Runs python run_custom_poundwholesale.py --supplier <name> in “resume” mode.
Returns:
Command status (success/failure),
Path to the new log file,
Maybe the first and last lines.
fba/explain-category/<supplier, category_url> (read‑only)

Combines:
Current state,
Linking map entries for that category,
Manifest (if present),
Recent logs.
Returns a structured summary:
Supplier vs Amazon completion,
Queue sizes,
Any ISS‑004 / allowlist mismatches.
This is basically what you’re asking me to do manually right now.
You can stop here for v1. No need to build full control or editing tools until you’re comfortable.

3. What it would take to implement (realistically, for you)
You have two main choices for the MCP server implementation:

Node/TypeScript server using the official MCP SDKs.
Python server (there are emerging libraries, or you can implement the protocol yourself).
Given your repo is Python‑heavy and you’re on Windows, a small Python MCP server alongside your existing code may be easiest mentally, but the most mature examples today are Node‑based. I’ll describe the structure in implementation‑agnostic terms; you can pick language later.

3.1. Core idea: wrap existing scripts, don’t rewrite them
For v1, you do not need to modify the main workflow scripts:

run_custom_poundwholesale.py
tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py
You can:

Create a new MCP server file, e.g.:
mcp_servers/fba_server.py or mcp_servers/fba_server.ts.
In that file:
Implement handlers that:
Read JSON files from OUTPUTS/,
Tail log files under logs/debug/,
Invoke python run_custom_poundwholesale.py as a subprocess when needed.
This is a thin layer: it doesn’t change your business logic, it just exposes it in a machine‑friendly way.

3.2. Files / scripts you’d add
At minimum:

mcp_servers/fba_server.py (or TS/JS equivalent):

Implements MCP protocol (discovery, resources, tools).
Hardcodes (or configures) the paths:
Processing states,
Logs,
Linking maps,
Cache files.
mcp_servers/config.json (optional):

Simple config with:
Base directory for repo,
Mapping from supplier_name to processing_state/log prefix if needed.
You might also add:

A tiny helper script to list the “latest log” for a supplier (or you can implement that logic inside the MCP server).
3.3. Do you need to edit existing scripts?
For v1, no, if you accept these limitations:

run-resume MCP tool will just trigger python run_custom_poundwholesale.py with the existing CLI options:
You won’t have fine‑grained control (per‑category runs) unless you add new flags to your runner.
Log format and state schema are used as‑is:
The MCP server will parse them; if you later change format, you’ll need to update the MCP parser.
If later you want more precise control (e.g. “run only Amazon resume for category 8”):

You’d then add small, focused options to your existing codebase:
E.g. extend run_custom_poundwholesale.py to accept --category-url or --phase amazon_only.
Then wire those into the MCP tools.
But that’s a second phase; you don’t need it to get started.

4. How “hard” is this for you, given you haven’t done MCP before?
Complexity level (if you keep it minimal):

Low–medium:
You’re not rewriting your engine.
You’re writing a small “adapter” that:
Reads JSON,
Reads text logs,
Spawns a subprocess.
The main new learning curve is:
Understanding the MCP server skeleton,
Registering resources and tools with the MCP client.
If you:

Start with read‑only resources (state, logs, linking map),
Add one action (run-resume),
you’ll get immediate benefit:

You can stop manually copying Windows paths into prompts.
You can ask a model to inspect and correlate state/logs for you, via a clean interface.
5. Summary
Benefits:

Structured access to state, logs, linking map and cache.
Automated “explain what happened” tools for categories and runs.
Less manual path‑copying and log‑grepping.
Reusable across suppliers.
Effort:

v1 MCP server = new small server file + maybe a simple config file.
No need to modify your main scraping/matching scripts initially.
Later, if you want more precise control, you can add small CLI options to your runner and expose them via MCP.
If you’d like, I can next sketch a concrete MCP server outline (in Python or Node) tailored to your repo structure, so you can see exactly what code you’d be writing.

I’ll break this into three parts:

What the supplier-onboarding Claude skill actually does.
What kinds of files/scripts it generates (with wizard vs manual work).
Whether an MCP server would make these tasks more seamless, and how hard it would be.
1. What the supplier-onboarding skill does
The skill is a guided workflow for adding a new wholesale supplier to your Amazon FBA Agent System. It is designed to let an LLM walk you through:

Collecting input data (URLs, selectors, auth),
Generating config + runner + auth helper via the wizard,
Validating everything,
Doing a smoke‑test run.
It does not contain “live” code; it’s instructions + templates + examples that tell the LLM how to drive your real wizard script: utils/supplier_onboarding_wizard.py.

1.1. High‑level workflow (from SKILL.md)
The skill defines a 7‑step process:

Initial handshake

LLM asks you five key questions:
Does supplier require login?
Category URLs (list or file path)?
One test product URL?
Do you already have CSS selectors?
How does pagination work?
If you don’t provide something, the skill tells the LLM to use browser tools / DevTools to inspect.
Data preprocessing & validation (manual LLM work)
LLM uses file read/write tools to:

Open your category URL list (setup/categories_{supplier}.txt or similar).
Validate:
Every line is an http(s) URL.
Domain matches the supplier.
No foreign supplier URLs.
No obvious syntax issues.
Similarly, validate selectors:
Are they valid CSS, not Playwright‑only selectors?
Are the required fields present (item, title, price, url, image, identifier, ean, etc.)?
Prepare configurations (manual + wizard inputs)

LLM prepares the inputs the wizard needs:

A categories config JSON: e.g. config/{workflow_key}_categories.json listing category URLs.
A supplier config under config/supplier_configs/{domain}.json with:
base_url,
login_config (if any),
price_selectors, etc.
Ensures config/system_config.json has a workflow entry with:
workflow_key (like kdwholesale_workflow),
supplier_name,
supplier_url,
categories_config_path.
Invoke wizard (real Python script)

The skill instructs the LLM to run the wizard script:

python utils/supplier_onboarding_wizard.py \
    --domain <supplier_domain> \
    --workflow-key <workflow_key> \
    --categories-config <path> \
    --selectors-config <path> \
    [--authentication-required true/false] ...
The wizard:

Normalizes domain (dot / hyphen / underscore forms via SupplierForms).
Resolves file paths based on naming conventions (NamingConventions).
Creates / updates:
Category config JSON,
Supplier config JSON,
Runner script (run_custom_{supplier-id}.py) using templates/runner_template.py.txt,
Auth helper script (tools/{supplier_id}/supplier_authentication_service.py) using templates/auth_helper_template.py.txt (if auth required),
System config entries.
Performs a sanity check run (short test), collects results, and writes them to session output JSON for the skill.
Validate generated files (manual LLM checks)

The skill tells the LLM to open and inspect generated files, not just check they exist:

Runner script:

Length: 117–143 lines (must be full implementation, not a 26‑line shim).
workflow_key is correct and not left as poundwholesale_workflow or another supplier.
Imports are correct, including PassiveExtractionWorkflow from tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.
Uses correct naming forms (dot, hyphen, underscore).
Supplier config:

JSON valid,
Required keys present (base_url, login_config, price_selectors, etc.),
Domain matches.
Auth helper:

File in tools/{supplier_id}/supplier_authentication_service.py,
Class and method names aligned with template,
TODOs clearly marked for manual completion.
Report results

The skill describes how Claude should summarise:

What files were created,
Any discrepancies found,
Whether the wizard created a full runner or a shim,
If more manual fixes are needed.
Pre‑run verification

LLM checks before you run a full pipeline:

Chrome CDP connectivity config (chrome_debug_port),
System config has the new workflow,
Paths in output directories (linking maps, cache) look correct,
No obvious mistakes in names or selectors.
User decision

Skill ends with a menu:

Test run (20s smoke test):
python run_custom_{supplier-id}.py and stop after 20 seconds.
Main run (full extraction and analysis).
Fix issues first (go back to earlier steps).
1.2. Types of outputs / scripts it generates
Via the wizard:

Runner script – run_custom_{supplier-id}.py

Generated from templates/runner_template.py.txt, which:

Sets up logging (setup_logger).
Loads system config (SystemConfigLoader).
Loads workflow config by workflow_key.
Detects platform, prints debug info.
Connects to Chrome via BrowserManager.
Optionally integrates auth helper ({{IMPORT_AUTH_HELPER}}, {{AUTH_SECTION}}).
Instantiates PassiveExtractionWorkflow and calls await workflow.run().
For example: your existing run_custom_angelwholesale-co-uk.py is “Auto‑generated by supplier_onboarding_wizard.py”.

Auth helper – tools/{supplier_id}/supplier_authentication_service.py

Generated from templates/auth_helper_template.py.txt:

Contains class {{SUPPLIER_CLASS_NAME}}AuthenticationHelper with:
is_authenticated(self) -> bool,
login(self, credentials: dict) -> bool.
Both have TODO comments; they’re templates requiring manual selector/login logic.
Configs:

config/{workflow_key}_categories.json – categories and perhaps weights/flags.
config/supplier_configs/{domain}.json – supplier‑specific settings:
base_url,
login_config (paths, selectors),
price_selectors, etc.
config/system_config.json – updated to include:
New workflow entry,
Possibly default category config path if missing.
Via manual LLM work (before/after wizard):

Category list .txt files,
Selector files,
Sometimes JSON configs if the wizard is bypassed or needs help.
2. Would an MCP integration make this more seamless?
Comparing this Claude skill to an MCP server:

This skill is instructions + a Python wizard.
MCP is a tool protocol + server that can do the same kinds of file/CLI work but through structured tool calls instead of long textual instructions.
2.1. What’s already good about the skill
The skill already gives you:

A deterministic, step‑by‑step onboarding process.
Clear validation rules and docs (FILE_SPECIFICATIONS, NAMING_CONVENTIONS, TROUBLESHOOTING).
An automated backend (wizard) that actually writes the files.
For Claude Code, this is already quite “seamless”:

Claude can read SKILL.md,
Ask the right questions,
Call python utils/supplier_onboarding_wizard.py directly,
Open and validate the generated files.
If you only ever use Claude Code in this repo, the marginal benefit of MCP is smaller, because this skill is tailor‑made for that environment.

2.2. Where MCP would improve the experience
An MCP server sitting next to this repo could:

Expose real tools like:

onboard_supplier (takes domain, categories path, selectors path, auth info),
validate_supplier_files,
list_runners, show_runner, compare_to_reference_runner,
run_test (short smoke test with logs).
Return structured JSON instead of free‑form logs, e.g.:

{
  "runner_path": "run_custom_kdwholesale-co-uk.py",
  "auth_path": "tools/kdwholesale-co-uk/supplier_authentication_service.py",
  "workflow_key": "kdwholesale_workflow",
  "warnings": ["Selectors missing image_url", "Pagination pattern not standard"]
}
Benefits:

Less prompt glue:
Instead of SKILL.md telling the LLM “open this file, check these lines, make sure it’s 117–143 lines…”, a tool can encapsulate that logic and return pass/fail + reasons.
Reusability across agents:
Any MCP‑aware client (Claude, ChatGPT, others) could call the same onboarding tools.
Auditability:
Tools can be logged and versioned; you can see exactly which onboarding actions were run when, with what arguments.
For the tasks you’re doing now (onboarding and verifying suppliers), MCP would make it:

More structured: fewer ambiguous textual steps, more explicit tool actions.
More portable: not tied to one vendor’s skill system.
Easier to script: you could call the MCP tools from outside the chat environment too.
2.3. Where MCP would be roughly “the same”
Some parts are fundamentally manual/creative and won’t get easier just because of MCP:

Designing correct CSS selectors for a messy supplier site.
Understanding authentication flows (captchas, multi‑step login).
Business decisions (which categories to include, rate limiting, etc.).
An MCP server can help with:

Fetching the HTML,
Running checks,
Saving configs,
but it doesn’t replace the need to reason about the site.

3. How complex would an MCP be for this, concretely?
Given you already have:

The wizard (utils/supplier_onboarding_wizard.py),
Templates (.claude/skills/supplier-onboarding/templates),
Examples (sample_data/reference_runners, reference_auth),
Detailed file specs in .claude/skills/supplier-onboarding/docs,
building an MCP server for supplier onboarding is moderate complexity, not massive.

You’d need:

3.1. New code
One MCP server module, e.g. mcp_servers/fba_supplier_onboarding.py that:

Offers tools like:

onboard_supplier:

Input: { "domain": "...", "categories_path": "...", "selectors_path": "...", "auth": { ... } }
Implementation:
Normalize domain via generate_supplier_forms (you can import from utils/supplier_onboarding_wizard.py or reimplement).
Construct the same JSON input arguments the wizard expects.
Call the wizard as a subprocess: python utils/supplier_onboarding_wizard.py ....
Read wizard’s session output JSON and return a structured response.
validate_onboarding:

Opens the generated runner / configs and runs the same checks SKILL.md describes (line count, workflow_key, naming conventions), but as code instead of manual LLM reasoning.
list_suppliers:

Reads config/supplier_configs/*.json and returns a list of onboarded suppliers.
get_supplier_docs:

Returns the relevant sections of FILE_SPECIFICATIONS.md or NAMING_CONVENTIONS.md for a supplier.
Optional: a tiny config file for the server (base paths, etc).

3.2. Changes to existing scripts
For a minimal MCP:

You do not need to modify run_custom_*.py, passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, or fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py.
You may want to refactor a bit of supplier_onboarding_wizard.py to make it easier to call programmatically (e.g. expose a main(args) function returning a dict instead of only writing to session files), but even that is optional; you can parse its output JSON as designed.
The most you’d likely do is:

Factor the wizard’s “session I/O” logic into a helper so your MCP tool can read/write from memory instead of a temporary JSON file if you want it cleaner.
3.3. Complexity summary
Small–medium scope if you:
Start with 1–2 tools that just wrap the existing wizard,
Keep everything read‑only except the wizard’s own writes.
Medium+ scope if you also:
Re‑implement all the validation logic from SKILL.md as separate MCP tools,
Add more interactive tools (e.g. “probe this supplier URL’s selectors” in real time).
Given that you already have a fully working wizard + skill, the fastest path to MCP is:

Wrap the wizard as a single onboard_supplier tool.
Add a simple validate_files tool that uses the same checks as FILE_SPECIFICATIONS.md.
Leave the rest of the workflow (selector design, category list cleanup) as manual LLM tasks for now.
Bottom line

The supplier-onboarding skill already gives you a robust, Claude‑centric onboarding process with templates, wizard integration, and manual validation.
An MCP server on top would make those operations more structured and reusable, especially if you want multiple agents/clients to onboard suppliers in a consistent way.
For your use case, a minimal MCP wrapper around supplier_onboarding_wizard.py + a couple of validation tools is quite achievable without touching core scraping logic; complexity is moderate and largely additive, not a rewrite.
i need you to clarify, aince the system is setup to have some "data" saved on memeory( ram) during the run; is there a specificc "duration" and/or actio ( like restarting my computer/OS) that would maybe clear this information of my memory ? if so, then what exactly might cause such a thing and what information can be lost and how will it affect thebsystem ( behavior/workflow)


Short answer: there is no time‑based expiry of the important state; what matters are events (process exit, OS restart, hard crash) and where you are relative to the last disk save.

I’ll separate what lives only in memory vs what is persisted, and what happens if you restart.

1. What lives only in RAM during a run
While run_custom_*.py is running, Python holds a lot of state in memory:

In PassiveExtractionWorkflow:

Current category queue (products list).
Per‑category helpers: _active_category_urls, _category_index_map, _category_allowed_keys, etc.
In‑memory linking map list, before it’s flushed by WindowsSaveGuardian.
In fixed_enhanced_state_manager:

The state_data dict in RAM mirrors the JSON file, but can be ahead of what’s been written.
Debounced/“runtime mode” changes between atomic saves.
Browser side:

Playwright / Chrome pages, cookies, login session, pagination position.
These only exist as long as the Python process + browser context are alive.

There is no built‑in timer that clears them after X minutes. They are cleared when:

You stop the Python run (Ctrl+C or closing the terminal),
The Python process crashes,
Chrome/Playwright crashes and is restarted,
You restart or shut down Windows,
Power loss.
2. What is persisted to disk (and survives restarts)
Critical data is written to disk via atomic saves:

Processing state:
OUTPUTS/CACHE/processing_states/angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state*.json

Contains:

current_phase, persistent_category_index, current_category_url.
supplier_products_needing_extraction/completed.
amazon_products_needing_analysis/completed.
frozen_category_denominators, category_allowed_keys, etc.
Linking map:
OUTPUTS/FBA_ANALYSIS/linking_maps/angelwholesale.co.uk/linking_map.json

Supplier cache:
OUTPUTS/cached_products/angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json

Manifests, logs, financial CSVs.

These survive:

Restarting Python,
Rebooting Windows,
Turning the machine off and on again,
as long as the last atomic write finished.

The workflow on next run always re‑derives its view from these files, not from any previous RAM state.

3. What can be lost on a restart/crash, and how it affects behaviour
Because your config sets “save every 1 product” for linking map, the only realistic losses are:

The product that was in flight when you killed the run or Windows died

If extraction finished but the linking entry wasn’t yet flushed to disk:
On the next run, that URL is not in linking_map.json,
So _rebuild_category_amazon_queue will treat it as pending and re‑process it.
Outputs stay clean:
We checked: linking_map.json has 0 duplicate supplier_url entries.
Products that hit “no match” / error states

For those, by design:
No linking entry is written (because there is no usable Amazon match).
On the next run, if the queue logic doesn’t remember “we already tried and got no match”, they can be retried.
Again, no duplicates in linking map, just extra Amazon calls.
The very last resume pointer / counters right before interruption

You see this in the logs as:
RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=...,
Followed by ATOMIC SAVE.
If a crash happens between pointer update and save, you can be off by a product or two.
On the next run, the resume logic will start from the last saved amazon_products_completed and queue index.
Effect on workflow:

You do not lose whole categories or large ranges, because:
Manifests and frozen_category_denominators are written once per category.
Linking map saves every product.
Worst case after a restart/crash:
One or a few products get retried in the resumed category,
Some “no match” products might be attempted again,
The system sees everything else from disk and skips already‑linked URLs.
4. Is there any “duration” you need to worry about?
No:

If you leave the process running for hours, state stays in RAM until it finishes or crashes.
Sleep vs. restart:
Sleep: RAM contents stay; Python + browser keep running (though network/CDP connections might get flaky).
Hibernate: Windows dumps RAM to disk and restores it; your processes resume where they left off.
Restart / shutdown: all RAM is cleared; only files under OUTPUTS/, logs/, config/ remain.
So the only things that matter for data loss are events (kill/crash/reboot), not time.

Summary

Important state for resume (phase, category, indices, denominators, linking map) is persisted to disk and survives restarts.
In‑memory state is lost whenever the Python process or OS stops, but because you save linking and state frequently (per product/per commit), at most you re‑process a small number of products in the interrupted category.
There’s no hidden “RAM‑only master state” that, if lost after some time, would break the whole workflow; the system is designed to rebuild its view from the processing state + linking map + cache on every new run.

You’re right to call out the confusion. Let me straighten it out precisely, based on the actual code and your config.

1. RAM vs disk: what is really lost when a run stops or OS restarts
When the Python process stops (Ctrl+C, crash, or OS shutdown/restart), all in‑memory state is gone:

PassiveExtractionWorkflow instance,
Its in‑memory linking_map list and hash indexes,
The current category queue (products),
Any in‑flight Amazon response or temporary variables.
The only things that survive are what has already been written to disk:

OUTPUTS/CACHE/processing_states/*.json
OUTPUTS/FBA_ANALYSIS/linking_maps/.../linking_map.json
OUTPUTS/cached_products/*.json
OUTPUTS/manifests/...json
Logs, CSVs, etc.
There is no time‑based retention in RAM; persistence depends only on whether a given piece of data has been flushed to disk before the process/OS stops.

If you restart Windows or stop the run, everything that was only in memory and not yet written to disk is lost. There is no “memory survives restart” in this design.

2. What exactly lives in memory vs disk during a run
2.1. In memory
Examples of in‑memory only data (until saved):

PassiveExtractionWorkflow.linking_map

Python list of all linking entries accumulated so far in this run.
Backed by hash indexes via self.hash_optimizer.
This matches what will be written to linking_map.json at each save point.
Current category queues:

Supplier side: lists of product URLs per category.
Amazon side: per‑category products lists built by _rebuild_category_amazon_queue.
Helper indexes:

_category_index_map (category URL → absolute index),
_active_category_urls,
_category_allowed_keys (for Step‑2 gating during the run).
In‑flight Amazon responses and financial calculations.

None of this is “magical”; it’s just the working state of the process. It is recomputed on the next run from:

Manifests,
Supplier cache,
Linking map,
Processing state.
2.2. On disk
The same information, or a summary of it, exists on disk as:

Manifests (per category):

Written by _save_category_manifest() to OUTPUTS/manifests/<supplier>/<category_slug>_manifest.json.
This is the “ground truth” list of URLs per category.
Always persisted before the main work for that category.
Linking map:

Written by _save_linking_map(self.supplier_name) to OUTPUTS/FBA_ANALYSIS/linking_maps/<supplier>/linking_map.json.
Processing state:

Written atomically by the state manager (save_state_atomic) to OUTPUTS/CACHE/processing_states/<supplier>_processing_state.json.
These files are what the next run uses to rebuild queues and resume safely.

3. With your config (linking_map_batch_size = 1), what can actually be lost?
Your system_config.json:

"linking_map_batch_size": 1,
"update_frequency_products": 1,
...
In tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, linking map saving is wired like this (main chunk processing):

overall_product_index = start_idx + i + 1
linking_map_batch = self.system_config.get("linking_map_batch_size", 1)

if overall_product_index % linking_map_batch == 0:
    self._save_linking_map(self.supplier_name)
    self.log.info(
        f" Periodic save at product {overall_product_index} (linking_map_batch_size: {linking_map_batch})"
    )
    self._perform_smart_memory_management(...)
And again in another path:

linking_map_batch = self.system_config.get("linking_map_batch_size", 1)
if current_index % linking_map_batch == 0:
    self.state_manager.save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True)
    self._save_linking_map(self.supplier_name)
    self.log.info(
        f" Periodic save at product {current_index} (linking_map_batch_size: {linking_map_batch})"
    )
Given your linking_map_batch_size = 1:

After every product, the workflow calls _save_linking_map(self.supplier_name), which:
Writes the full self.linking_map list to linking_map.json using WindowsSaveGuardian (atomic write),
Rebuilds hash indexes from disk,
Logs verification.
So the only way you lose a linking entry is:

The process crashes between:

Adding the entry in memory via _add_linking_map_entry_optimized(entry), and
The very next _save_linking_map(...) call for that product.
That window is small — essentially “in the middle of processing the current product.”

Everything up to the previous product has already been written to disk and will not be lost.

4. “No‑match” products: are they written to linking map or not?
Yes. In this version, no‑match products do create linking map entries in all the relevant paths. That’s explicit in the code.

4.1. Main Amazon phase (non‑resume)
At lines ~2549–2595:

if not amazon_data or "error" in amazon_data:
    self.log.warning(
        f"Could not retrieve valid Amazon data for '{product_data.get('title')}'. Creating no-match linking entry to prevent reprocessing."
    )
    ...

    # Determine the reason for no match
    error_info = (
        amazon_data.get("error")
        if isinstance(amazon_data, dict)
        else "No Amazon data returned"
    )
    no_match_reason = f"Amazon search failed: {error_info}"

    # Create no-match linking entry
    no_match_entry = {
        "supplier_ean": supplier_ean,
        "amazon_asin": None,
        "supplier_title": product_data.get("title"),
        "amazon_title": None,
        "supplier_price": product_data.get("price"),
        "amazon_price": None,
        "match_method": "none",
        "confidence": "0",
        "created_at": datetime.now().isoformat(),
        "supplier_url": product_data.get("url"),
        "no_match_reason": no_match_reason,
    }

    # Add no-match entry to linking map using optimized method
    self._add_linking_map_entry_optimized(no_match_entry)
    self.log.info(
        f" Added NO-MATCH linking entry: {supplier_ean or 'NO_EAN'}  NO MATCH ({no_match_reason})"
    )
    ...

    self.state_manager.mark_product_processed(
        product_data.get("url"), "failed_amazon_extraction"
    )
    continue
So:

Every time Amazon search fails or returns an error:
A linking entry is created with:
amazon_asin = None,
match_method = "none",
no_match_reason = "Amazon search failed: ..."
This is added to self.linking_map and then saved to disk at the next _save_linking_map() (which, with batch size 1, is the same product).
4.2. Gap processing
Around 3140–3155:

no_match_entry = {
    "supplier_ean": supplier_ean,
    "amazon_asin": None,
    "supplier_title": product_data.get("title"),
    "amazon_title": None,
    "supplier_price": product_data.get("price"),
    "amazon_price": None,
    "match_method": "none",
    "confidence": 0,
    "created_at": datetime.now().isoformat(),
    "supplier_url": product_data.get("url"),
    "no_match_reason": no_match_reason,
}

self._add_linking_map_entry_optimized(no_match_entry)
self.log.info(
    f" No Amazon match for gap product: {product_data.get('title')} - Added no-match linking entry"
)
Again, gap products that fail on Amazon get no‑match entries.

4.3. Resume‑phase _analyze_with_amazon (what you saw earlier)
Lines 7592–7626:

if not amazon_data or "error" in amazon_data:
    ...
    no_match_reason = f"Resumption processing - Amazon search failed: {error_info}"

    no_match_entry = {
        "supplier_ean": supplier_ean,
        "amazon_asin": None,
        "supplier_title": product_data.get("title"),
        "amazon_title": None,
        "supplier_price": product_data.get("price"),
        "amazon_price": None,
        "match_method": "none",
        "confidence": 0,
        "created_at": datetime.now().isoformat(),
        "supplier_url": product_data.get("url"),
        "no_match_reason": no_match_reason,
    }

    self._add_linking_map_entry_optimized(no_match_entry)
    self.log.info(
        f" Added NO-MATCH linking entry: {supplier_ean or 'NO_EAN'}  NO MATCH"
    )

    self.state_manager.mark_product_processed(
        product_data.get("url"), "failed_amazon_extraction"
    )
    return None
Same pattern: failed resume products get no‑match linking entries.

Conclusion:
By design (and in the current code), every Amazon attempt that fails or yields no usable result creates a no‑match linking entry. With your linking_map_batch_size = 1, each of those entries is written to linking_map.json after that product.

This ensures:

Failed/no‑match products will not be re‑queued endlessly on future runs.
Reprocessing only happens for:
The product in flight at the moment of a hard crash or forced kill between in‑memory add and next _save_linking_map(),
Or if the linking_map write fails catastrophically (which logs as CRITICAL).
5. What can be lost, exactly, with your current config
Given all of the above, and assuming normal behaviour of WindowsSaveGuardian:

Linking map:

At most one product’s linking entry for the currently processed item if:
The process/OS dies in the tiny window between _add_linking_map_entry_optimized() and the next _save_linking_map() for that product.
Everything up to the previous product is safely on disk (because you save every product).
Processing state:

commit_amazon_progress is called per product and uses save_state_atomic inside fixed_enhanced_state_manager.
So state is also persisted very frequently; worst‑case, amazon_products_completed might lag by one product if a crash occurs right after a commit but before the OS completes the write.
Queues and helper structures:

Category queues (products lists), _category_index_map, _category_allowed_keys exist only in memory for each run.
On the next run, they are rebuilt from:
manifests,
linking_map,
supplier cache,
processing state.
There is no large “mystery chunk of state” that can vanish and corrupt the workflow.
With your settings, the window for losing anything is narrowed down to:

The very last incomplete product of the current batch/loop at the moment of a crash or kill,
Which will then simply be retried on the next resume because it has no linking_map entry yet.
Everything else is grounded in files that are re‑read on the next run.
