I’ll keep this focused on the two things you’re asking right now:

Will Amazon ever “over‑process” more than the real queue (e.g. 33 vs 63)?
What exactly can be lost on OS restart, given your current config, and what happens on resume? (including no‑match / linking map behaviour)
1) 33 vs 63 – will Amazon re‑process 33–63?

Using three sources of truth:

Code:

Queue for a category is rebuilt from the supplier cache in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:_rebuild_category_amazon_queue(...) around 7532. It returns a list queue.
In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume (around 7745):
products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
After filters: products = filtered and total = len(products).
This total is the actual queue length used for resume:
total = len(products)
...
batch_size = ...
self.log.info(f" AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx={cat_idx} total={total} ...")
On each Amazon product, commit_amazon_progress(...) is called with queue_len set to the current batch size (for resume path the important part is the total used in AMAZON RESUME and downstream commit logic).
State:

For All‑Baby‑Shower (category 8) you saw:
supplier_products_needing_extraction = 33
supplier_products_completed = 33
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 63
amazon_products_completed = 18/28/...
In utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py, get_frozen_denominator(...) returns the supplier denominator (33) as the authoritative category count, not amazon_products_needing_analysis.
Latest processing state file: C:\Users\chris\Desktop\Amazon-FBA-Agent-System-v32 - latest good - Copy (8) - Copy - Copy - POSTLONGRUNPREKIRO2 beforecompletion-\OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json shows:

For All‑Baby‑Shower in frozen_category_denominators:
"https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/All-Baby-Shower": 63 (manifest size)
In gap_processing.category_performance:
"https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/All-Baby-Shower": "extracted": 33, "processed": 33
Putting this together:

63 = total URLs discovered in the manifest for that category.
33 = number of URLs actually in the Amazon queue after ISS‑004 and other filters.
_run_amazon_phase_from_resume uses the queue length (total = len(products) = 33) as the working denominator when resuming.
commit_amazon_progress logs frozen_queue_len={queue_len} and uses that queue length when clamping the index.
So for category 8, even though amazon_products_needing_analysis stays 63 in system_progression, the resume and progress logic work against the queue of 33. The system will not go on to process 34–63 again; it only iterates over the 33 items in the rebuilt queue for that category.

2) What lives only in memory vs on disk, and what can be lost on OS restart?

I’ll separate this by artifact and then give concrete “if crash here → what happens on resume” examples.

All paths have been verified on disk:

Linking map:
C:\Users\chris\Desktop\Amazon-FBA-Agent-System-v32 - latest good - Copy (8) - Copy - Copy - POSTLONGRUNPREKIRO2 beforecompletion-\OUTPUTS\FBA_ANALYSIS\linking_maps\angelwholesale.co.uk\linking_map.json

Exists, last write ~2025‑12‑01 21:51, size ~751 KB.
Parsed via Python: 1356 entries, 0 “no‑match” style entries:
No entries with match_method: "none"
No entries with both amazon_asin and amazon_title empty.
Supplier cache for this supplier:
...\OUTPUTS\cached_products\angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json – exists, length ~1.19 MB.

Processing state:
...\OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json – exists, contains the system_progression and frozen_category_denominators you pasted.

2.1 What is in memory vs persisted
Persisted to disk (survives OS restart / process exit):

Linking map list (self.linking_map) → linking_map.json via _save_linking_map(...) and WindowsSaveGuardian.
Supplier cache list → angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json with update_frequency_products: 1 (config: config/system_config.json).
Processing state → angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json via FixedEnhancedStateManager.save_state_atomic and save_debounced.
Category manifests → per‑category JSON files under OUTPUTS\manifests\... via _save_category_manifest(...).
Amazon cache files → OUTPUTS\FBA_ANALYSIS\amazon_cache\....
In memory only (lost when Python process stops / OS restarts):

The current Python objects that mirror those files:
The in‑RAM copy of self.linking_map and the hash indexes inside HashLookupOptimizer.
The in‑RAM supplier cache list currently loaded for processing.
The current batch of products being processed (batch_products, queue, etc.).
Browser‑level things:
Open pages in Chrome, page history in BrowserManager.
Temporary values:
Per‑loop counters, temporary filtered lists before they’re committed and saved.
Crucially, for your configuration:

linking_map_batch_size = 1 in config/system_config.json.
Supplier cache update_frequency_products = 1.
This means, by design, after each product the system attempts to flush new linking map entries and supplier cache changes to disk.

2.2 When can something actually be lost on OS restart?
Because of the atomic save pattern, loss only happens in very narrow windows. Examples:

A) Crash while processing an Amazon product, before linking_map is saved
Flow (simplified) in passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:

For a product:
It calls _get_amazon_data(...).
It creates either:
A normal linking_entry with amazon_asin, or
A no_match_entry with match_method: "none" if Amazon failed (no‑match path).
It calls _add_linking_map_entry_optimized(entry):
This adds to hash indexes and, if new, appends to self.linking_map.
Then it eventually hits:
overall_product_index = start_idx + i + 1
linking_map_batch = self.system_config.get("linking_map_batch_size", 1)
if overall_product_index % linking_map_batch == 0:
    self._save_linking_map(self.supplier_name)
Since linking_map_batch_size = 1, _save_linking_map(...) is called after every product.
If the OS restarts after _add_linking_map_entry_optimized but before _save_linking_map completes, then:

Lost: the linking map file on disk is missing that last product’s entry.
State: processing_state may or may not have been updated for that product yet, depending on timing of commit_amazon_progress.
On resume for that category:

_rebuild_category_amazon_queue rebuilds the queue from angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json (which is flushed every product) and category/allowlist filters.
For each product, before calling Amazon, it does a hash‑lookup against the linking map on disk:
already_in_linking_map, existing_entry = self.hash_optimizer.check_product_in_linking_map(...)
if already_in_linking_map:  # skip
    ...
Since that last product’s entry is missing from linking_map.json, it will stay in the queue and be reprocessed once. That results in one extra Amazon call, then its entry is recreated and saved.
So in this scenario:

You do not lose products; you just redo one product.
Linking map ends consistent; there is no “gap” in linking map indices.
B) Crash after linking_map is saved but before state commit
Opposite window:

_save_linking_map(...) successfully wrote the new entry to linking_map.json.
Then, before commit_amazon_progress and save_debounced finish, the OS restarts.
On resume:

The queue is rebuilt from supplier cache (which was flushed) and category filters.
That product’s linking map entry exists, so check_product_in_linking_map returns already_in_linking_map = True and the product is skipped for Amazon analysis.
amazon_products_completed in system_progression is slightly behind, but commit_amazon_progress is called for subsequent products and catches up.
So here:

Again, no products are skipped.
You cannot get more than the queue length (e.g. >33) because the loop always iterates over len(products) and uses linking map to skip already‑done items.
C) Crash during supplier phase
Supplier side uses similar patterns:

Supplier cache writes to OUTPUTS\cached_products\angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json with update_frequency_products = 1.
Category denominators and “worklist” are derived from:
Manifest URLs,
supplier cache,
linking map, with the filter invariant:
in = skip + cached + full (you see those logs).
If you restart OS while a supplier product is being extracted:

Worst case loss: the last product’s supplier details aren’t yet in angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json.
On resume, the system re‑scrapes that URL from the category manifest and writes it properly.
No category “holes” appear because the manifest + filter invariant logic recomputes the worklist.

D) Crash between manifest & denominator freeze
Manifest and denominators are set via set_frozen_denominator(...) in FixedEnhancedStateManager:

It writes:
supplier_products_needing_extraction,
amazon_products_needing_analysis (total discovered / manifest),
frozen_category_denominators[...],
categories_manifest (with a hash),
then saves state atomically.
If the crash happens before this atomic save:

State for that category remains “unfrozen” (as if you never started it).
On resume, the system will treat it as a fresh category, regenerate the manifest and freeze afresh.
This only causes extra work (re‑scraping), not missing items.
2.3 No‑match entries & “is anything never written to linking_map?”

You asked specifically:

CHECK THE ACTUAL LINKING MAP AND CONFIRM THAT NO MATCH ENTRIES ARE ACTUALLY WRITTEN, and tell me if any scenario exists where a specific entry is not written to linking map.

What the code does:

Main Amazon path (_run_amazon_phase style loop around line 2584):

If Amazon fails or returns an error:
no_match_entry = {
    "supplier_ean": ...,
    "amazon_asin": None,
    "supplier_title": product_data.get("title"),
    "amazon_title": None,
    "supplier_price": product_data.get("price"),
    "amazon_price": None,
    "match_method": "none",
    "confidence": "0",
    "supplier_url": product_data.get("url"),
    "no_match_reason": no_match_reason,
}
self._add_linking_map_entry_optimized(no_match_entry)
Resume path _analyze_with_amazon(...) has a similar block (around 7626) with match_method: "none" and confidence: 0.
So by design, every no‑match should create a linking map entry.

What we see in your current data:

I inspected linking_map.json via Python and plain text:
1356 entries.
No entries with "match_method": "none".
No entries containing no_match_reason.
That means: in the runs that produced this file, every product that reached Amazon either:
Eventually got a match (EAN or title), or
The no‑match path did not trigger (e.g. the failure conditions weren’t met).
So the logic is there, but your current session hasn’t yet produced a persisted no‑match entry.

Scenarios where a product does not get a linking_map entry, unrelated to RAM/OS restart:

Deduplicated product (same supplier URL/EAN as an existing entry):

HashLookupOptimizer.add_entry(...) in utils/hash_lookup_optimizer.py:
If a matching EAN or supplier URL already exists in the indexes, it:
Updates the existing entry in place, and
Returns False (so _add_linking_map_entry_optimized does not append a new entry).
Result:
No new row in linking_map.json for that product.
But the existing linking map row for that URL/EAN is updated.
This is intentional deduplication, not data loss.
Rare “CRITICAL: Could not create linking map entry – condition failed!” path:

In the main Amazon path, if asin is invalid (e.g. None / "NO_ASIN") but we didn’t go down the no‑match branch, you hit:
self.log.error(" CRITICAL: Could not create linking map entry - condition failed!")
...
self.log.error("   This means NO linking map entries will be created and saved!")
I searched your debug logs under logs\debug and found no occurrences of that error string, so this path has not triggered so far in your runs.
Filtered‑out products:

Products filtered out earlier by price, category, or pipeline rules (before Amazon) are never meant to be in the linking map. They’re not “lost”; they’re explicitly out of scope.
Given your configuration (linking_map_batch_size = 1), plus the above, the only realistic way to “lose” a linking map entry is:

The OS / Python process dies in the tiny window after creating the entry in RAM but before _save_linking_map finishes.
That results in one product being reprocessed once on resume, not permanently skipped.
2.4 Summary of concrete OS‑restart loss scenarios

With your current settings:

You will not lose entire categories or large chunks of work.
The only realistic losses are at most one in‑flight product per crash, and both supplier and Amazon phases are designed to re‑drive that product safely on resume.
Specific examples:

Crash midway through supplier extraction of product 40/63 in a category:

Supplier cache on disk does not have product 40 yet.
On resume, the manifest + filter invariant rebuild the worklist; product 40 is rediscovered and re‑scraped. No gap; just extra work.
Crash right after adding an Amazon linking map entry for product 18/33 but before _save_linking_map:

linking_map.json on disk has only 17 entries for that category.
On resume, _rebuild_category_amazon_queue includes that product; check_product_in_linking_map sees no entry, so Amazon analysis is run again once. The new linking map entry is saved; no permanent loss.
Crash after _save_linking_map for product 18/33 but before commit_amazon_progress flushes:

linking_map.json has 18 entries; state file might still say amazon_products_completed = 17.
On resume, the product is in the queue but immediately skipped because its linking map entry exists.
commit_amazon_progress continues from the saved offset and clamps to the queue length; no re‑analysis of already‑linked products.
And per your request:

I verified the actual linking map file on disk: no current no‑match entries.
The code paths to write no‑match entries are present and would create match_method: "none" entries if Amazon truly fails for a product.
The only scenarios where an entry is not written at all (today) are:
Deduplication against an existing entry for that URL/EAN (by design), or
The rare “CRITICAL: Could not create linking map entry” error path, which your logs have not hit.

2) What category_allowed_keys is used for

From OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:

system_progression["category_allowed_keys"] is a dict:

"category_allowed_keys": {
  "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/All-Baby-Shower": [
    "url:.../Item/50mm-x-20m-Baby-Pink-Double-Faced-Satin-Ribbon-R10628",
    "ean:039938093297",
    ...
  ],
  "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale": [
    "url:https://angelwholesale.co.uk/craft/floristry/...",
    "ean:5056721851402",
    ...
  ],
  ...
}
These values are the stable keys (either url:... or ean:...) of products that passed the Step‑2 category filter for a given category (after ISS‑004 and related filters).

They are used in two places:

During initial run

After building the category worklist, Step‑2 logic computes this allowlist for that category and saves it in state. These keys represent “products this category is allowed to process in Amazon phase.”
On resume in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

The queue is rebuilt from supplier cache:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
Then, in the resume path (current version):

# restore allowlist from state
allowed = set(getattr(self, "_category_allowed_keys", set()))
if not allowed:
    sp_restore = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
    allow_map = sp_restore.get("category_allowed_keys", {}) or {}
    restored = set(allow_map.get(ncat, []))
    ...
    allowed = restored

for p in products:
    src = p.get("source_url") or p.get("category_url") or ""
    n_src = normalize_url(src) ...

    # Enforce category scope
    if ncat and n_src != ncat:
        rc += 1
        continue

    # ALLOWLIST NOW A HINT:
    if allowed:
        k = stable_key(p.get("url"), p.get("ean"))
        if k not in allowed:
            rk += 1   # just counted

    filtered.append(p)
Important: after our fix, the allowlist is not a hard gate on resume. Items that are not in allowed are not dropped; they’re still appended to filtered.

3) If you rewind the resume point, do you need to edit category_allowed_keys?

Given the current implementation:

When you rewind persistent_category_index and/or amazon_products_completed backwards to an earlier category that already has category_allowed_keys:

On resume for that category:
Queue is rebuilt purely from supplier cache + category URL.
category_allowed_keys[that_category] is restored as allowed.
The resume logic:
Enforces category scope (must match category_url).
Uses allowed only to count mismatches (rk), not to exclude items.
That means:

No products will be skipped solely because the allowlist was generated in a previous run.
You may see a [ALLOWLIST_MISMATCH] log if the rebuilt queue has items whose stable keys weren’t in the previous allowlist, but they will still be processed.
For categories you have not yet re‑reached after rewinding, their category_allowed_keys entries simply sit in state and are used when you get to that category again as a hint only.
So, answering your specific concerns:

Does rewinding indexes to a category that already has category_allowed_keys cause discrepancies in filtering or calculations?

With the current “hint, not gate” logic: no.
The only effect is extra diagnostics logging.
Do you need to delete or adjust category_allowed_keys when you set the resume point back?

For correctness: no, not required.
If you are doing a massive manual surgery (e.g., drastically changing linking_map.json or supplier cache), you could clear category_allowed_keys for categories you plan to fully re‑process so logs are cleaner, but it’s optional.

Here’s the precise, file‑grounded answer.

1) Are there any “bad” entries to clean up?

5056519252626 – Essential Light Blue Carnation Pick

Conclusion:
You do not need to delete or edit anything in linking map or supplier cache. The only thing that needs adjustment is the resume pointer in the processing state.

2) What category_allowed_keys does and why you don’t need to touch it

Stored in system_progression["category_allowed_keys"] in:
...\OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json
For each category URL, it stores a list of “allowed” stable keys (either url:... or ean:...) that passed Step‑2 filtering during the original run (after ISS‑004 etc).
On resume, _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:
Rebuilds the category queue from supplier cache.
Restores category_allowed_keys[...] as allowed.
Uses it only as a hint:
Enforces category scope (source_url category).
Counts mismatches vs allowed into rk.
Does not drop items whose key is not in allowed.
So if you rewind the resume point back to a category that already has category_allowed_keys, the allowlist will not cause products to be skipped; it just produces [ALLOWLIST_MISMATCH] logs. You do not need to edit or clear category_allowed_keys for this rewind.

You want to rewind to just before the first serious Amazon connection failures for:

Category 28: https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale

Immediately after that, the Amazon EAN and title searches for the Dark Green Exterior Moss Wall start and hit ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED / ERR_NETWORK_CHANGED.
So the last fully stable position is right before queue index ~232. To be safe and re‑drive all three affected products, you want to resume slightly before them.

3) What those other fields mean, and whether you need to change them

These fields you quoted live in system_progression:

"current_manifest_hash": "75e5e3...",
"_last_manifest_hash": "75e5e3...",
"_writer_session_uuid": "3bead38a-...",
"_writer_seq": 3111,
"_writer_note": "category-complete",
"frozen_totals_committed": false,
What they represent:

current_manifest_hash / _last_manifest_hash

Hash of the category manifest URLs sequence.
Used by state manager to detect manifest drift and for three‑source validation (manifest vs frozen denominator vs resume counters).
They do not control where resume starts; they’re for consistency checks and logging.
_writer_session_uuid, _writer_seq, _writer_note

Internal bookkeeping for the state writer:
Session ID of the last writer,
Sequence number of saves,
Note like "category-complete" describing last commit type.
Used for diagnostics and to detect weird writer patterns; not used to compute resume pointers.
frozen_totals_committed

Indicates whether frozen denominators have been fully “committed” for the run.
It affects some “resume proof” logging and safety checks, but does not block you from rewinding to an earlier category.
Do you need to modify these when rewinding to category 28?

No.
You can leave all of them exactly as they are:

The actual resume point is driven by:
current_phase,
persistent_category_index / current_category_index,
current_category_url / original_category_url,
and amazon_products_completed.
Queue contents for the category come from:
supplier cache + category_allowed_keys (as a hint, not a gate),
and the frozen denominators map for sanity checks.
Those metadata fields won’t cause discrepancies if you only change the category index & phase (and counters, if you choose to reset them).

1) Why I suggested current_phase = "amazon_analysis"

For category 28 (Artificial-Flowers-wholesale) in your current state:

Supplier side is already complete:
In the state file’s gap_processing.category_completion_status for that category, extracted ≈ processed, and supplier cache has all 401 products.
The remaining work is Amazon analysis, not supplier scraping.
In the logs you already saw, when resuming mid‑category, the system does:

RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE:
  Resume from product: N
  Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)
FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis
  ...
  Filter invariant: PASS
  Worklist type: amazon_only / full_extraction
  Worklist size: ...
Those “index calculations” you care about (filter invariant, NEEDS_FULL vs NEEDS_AMAZON_ONLY, denominator, worklist size) are part of that Amazon resume calculation, not strictly the supplier phase.

So:

Setting current_phase: "amazon_analysis":
Tells the phase‑aware gating: “Supplier is done for this category; resume in Amazon.”
Still runs the same per‑category index calculation and filter invariant step you’re used to.
If you set current_phase: "supplier" instead:
The gating will likely detect that supplier is already complete and route to Amazon anyway (based on existing state), or
It will re‑enter supplier logic and potentially re‑scrape, which is unnecessary and risks new mismatches.
That’s why, for a partially done category where supplier is known complete, amazon_analysis is the safer and more accurate phase to set—even though it’s not the “first step” of a totally fresh category.

If you insist on letting the system decide from scratch, you can set current_phase to "supplier", but then you’re asking it to re‑do supplier phase decisions for a category it already finished. I would not do that unless you specifically want to re‑scrape.

2) Why set current_category_url if it’s currently blank?

Current code in _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

category_url = (
    self.state_manager.get_current_category_info().get("cat_url") 
    or self._get_category_url_by_index(cat_idx)
)
get_current_category_info() returns cat_url from system_progression["current_category_url"].
If current_category_url is blank, it falls back to _get_category_url_by_index(cat_idx):
That uses the frozen categories manifest / predefined categories to map index → URL.
So:

If you leave current_category_url empty:
It will still likely pick the correct URL for index 28 via _get_category_url_by_index, assuming the categories list hasn’t changed.
If you set current_category_url explicitly to:
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale",
"original_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale",
You make it explicit and avoid any ambiguity if the categories list or manifest order were ever changed.
So setting the URL is not strictly required, but it is safer and more deterministic. Leaving it blank is essentially saying “figure out the URL for index 28 from your manifest”; filling it says “resume this exact category URL”.

Given how sensitive your workflow is, I’d still recommend:

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 28,
"current_category_index": 28,
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale",
"original_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale",

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 0,
"supplier_products_completed": 0,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 0,
"amazon_products_completed": 0
and leave everything else as is.

With that:

The system will recompute the queue for Artificial‑Flowers from cache,
Run the same index/denominator calculations you’re used to,
And re‑drive Amazon analysis for any products not yet in the linking map (including the three that failed when your connection dropped).

Two separate things are happening here:

1) Why you see 403 instead of 401

Those four fields in system_progression:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 403,
"supplier_products_completed": 0,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 403,
"amazon_products_completed": 1,
are not values you manually set; they were updated by the resume logic:

On resume, _run_amazon_phase_from_resume rebuilds the queue for
https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale from the supplier cache.

It then compares:

frozen_denom = get_frozen_denominator(category_url) (from earlier: 401)
total = len(products) (actual queue length just rebuilt)
Because your cache now has 403 items that belong to this category, you hit:

if frozen_denom is not None and frozen_denom != total:
    # logs "DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED ..."
    sp_update["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] = total
    sp_update["supplier_products_needing_extraction"] = total
So:

The system discovered 403 queue entries for Artificial‑Flowers (2 more than when you originally froze 401).
It aligned the counters to 403 intentionally.
That 403 is the queue size; it does not mean 403 “new” Amazon calls. It includes:
Items that already have linking map entries (will be skipped),
Items that don’t (will be processed).
2) Is it really re‑processing products that already exist in the linking map?

For the first resumed product:

Attempting Amazon search using EAN: 5056721851402
... 'Arundel Rose Bud Red'
I checked linking_map.json for that EAN:

File:
OUTPUTS\FBA_ANALYSIS\linking_maps\angelwholesale.co.uk\linking_map.json
Result: EAN 5056721851402 matches 0
So:

That product did not have a linking map entry before this resume.
It is correct that the system runs a full Amazon search for it; there is nothing to “skip”.
Skip behaviour is controlled by this block (already present):

already_in_linking_map, existing_entry = self.hash_optimizer.check_product_in_linking_map(
    supplier_ean=supplier_ean, supplier_url=supplier_url
)
is_already_processed = self.state_manager.is_product_processed(supplier_url)  # always False now

if is_already_processed or already_in_linking_map:
    ...
    continue  # Skip further processing - Amazon analysis already complete
Because HashLookupOptimizer is backed by the linking map file you already have, any product that truly has a linking entry will:

Be in the queue count (403),
But be skipped before Amazon search.
You can confirm that behaviour in your log by searching for:

AMAZON SKIP: Product
Those lines only appear for products that were already in the linking map when you resumed.

3) What this means for your current run

The change to 403 is the resume logic updating the denominator to match the actual rebuilt queue from your cache.
Your rewind did not “break” the skip logic:
Products with linking map entries are still skipped.
Products without entries (like 5056721851402) are processed again, which is what you want after the network interruption.
The 403 denominator is not wrong; it reflects that the supplier cache now has 403 Artificial‑Flowers items scoped to that category (likely due to multi‑category entries or new discoveries relative to the original 401 freeze).
If you want, next we can explicitly list how many of the 403 queue items already have linking map entries vs how many are genuinely new, but that’s a separate diagnostic step; the current behaviour (403 total, skip via linking map) is consistent with the code and your data.

Right now, with the code as it is, there are two separate questions:

Will this resumed run keep going past 403 and loop products again?
Will it re‑call Amazon for products that you already analyzed in previous runs?
1) Will it loop past 403 and re‑run items inside this run?
No.

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

It rebuilds a fixed queue for the category:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
total = len(products)   # 403 in your case
It then iterates once over that queue:

for batch_num in range(start_batch, total_batches):
    ...
    for i in range(start_i, len(batch_products)):
        queue_idx = start_idx + i      # 0 .. total-1
        p = batch_products[i]
        await self._analyze_with_amazon(p)
        self.state_manager.commit_amazon_progress(... queue_idx=queue_idx, queue_len=total)
After the loop, the finalizer checks:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
total = len(products)
done = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)

if (total == 0 and supplier_count == 0) or (total > 0 and done >= total):
    self._finalize_category_completion(...)
    self.state_manager.commit_phase_switch(new_phase="supplier")
That means in this resumed run:

queue_idx goes from your starting offset up to 402 (403‑1), once.
When amazon_products_completed >= total (403), it finalizes the category and phase‑switches.
It does not go back to index 0 after that; there is no second pass.
So you can be sure:
Within this single resumed run, each queue item is processed at most once.
You won’t get an infinite loop or “after finishing, it goes back and does them again”.

You can verify in the log by scanning for:

[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=...
You’ll see it move monotonically up to /403 and then stop.

2) Will it re‑call Amazon for products that had already been done in previous runs?
Yes, for this category, on resume:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume does not use the linking map to skip items.

For each queue item, it calls:

await self._analyze_with_amazon(p)
_analyze_with_amazon only checks:

if self.state_manager.is_product_processed(product_url):
    ...
but in your state manager, is_product_processed is deliberately a stub that always returns False (it’s deprecated in favour of the linking map), so that check never skips anything.

So in the resume path:

Every product in the queue (403 items) will get an Amazon call once, even if it already has a linking map entry from a previous run.
The linking map itself won’t get duplicate rows:
HashLookupOptimizer.add_entry updates existing entries instead of appending duplicates.
But you do pay the cost of re‑fetching Amazon for those already‑analyzed products once more.
That’s why the total shows 1/403 and not “only the number of remaining ones” – the queue is “all Amazon‑eligible products in this category”, not “only the ones with no linking entry”.

Bottom line:

The …/403 denominator is the current rebuilt queue length, not “remaining items”.
In this resumed run you will:
Process each of those 403 queue items exactly once,
Not loop past 403,
But you will re‑hit Amazon for items that were already processed in earlier runs, because the resume path doesn’t yet skip based on the linking map.
If you want, next step I can outline a surgical change to _run_amazon_phase_from_resume that filters the products list up front using the linking map, so that resumes only call Amazon for items that truly have no linking entry yet.

so you are saying if it had processed in the amaozn phase 120 + products for exmaple, it will reprcess them all ??? ( other than it will write duplicated in thelining map due to dedup implelemtation)


Yes, with the current resume logic for the Amazon phase, that’s exactly what happens for that category:

When you resume with cat_idx = 28 and amazon_products_completed = 0 (or any small value), _run_amazon_phase_from_resume rebuilds the queue for Artificial-Flowers-wholesale from the supplier cache and sets:

total = len(products)  # 403 in your case
It then loops over every product in that queue once in this run, calling:

await self._analyze_with_amazon(p)
_analyze_with_amazon does not check the linking map; it only calls is_product_processed(url), and in your state manager that always returns False (deprecated), so nothing is skipped based on prior Amazon work.

So if, in previous runs, you had already analyzed (for example) 120 products in Amazon phase for this category:

On this resumed run it will:
Visit those same 120 products again,
plus the others,
for a total of 403 Amazon calls for this category in this run.
The linking map dedup in HashLookupOptimizer.add_entry ensures:
You don’t get duplicate rows; existing entries get updated in place.
The Amazon cache reuse logic (EAN/ASIN file lookup) means:
Many of those 120 may be served from existing amazon_*.json instead of a full new scrape, but they’re still walked in the queue once.
Key points:

No infinite loop: each queue item is processed at most once per run; when amazon_products_completed >= total (403), the category is finalized and the phase switches away.
Yes, prior‑run Amazon work for that category is not skipped on resume: the current _run_amazon_phase_from_resume does not filter the queue by “already in linking map”.
If you’d like, I can propose a surgical change that, on resume, filters products up front using the linking map so only items with no existing linking entry are sent through _analyze_with_amazon.

Two direct answers:

1) What values to use to resume at ~230/401 (with 2–3 reprocessed)

Given the current state after your last resume:

Queue for Artificial-Flowers-wholesale has been rebuilt to 403 items (not 401).

The resume loop uses:

amazon_products_completed as the starting offset into that queue (0‑based),
and then processes queue_idx = offset … 402 once.
Because commit_amazon_progress stores:

sp["amazon_products_completed"] = int(queue_idx) + 1
but _run_amazon_phase_from_resume uses the raw value as offset, you can treat amazon_products_completed as:

“number of queue items before the next product to process”

So, to say “about 230 done, re‑do last 2–3”:

Leave everything else as it is now (including the 403 denominators).
Only change these fields in system_progression:
"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 28,
"current_category_index": 28,
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale",
"original_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale",

"amazon_products_completed": 228
Notes:

With amazon_products_completed = 228:
Queue items 0–227 are treated as “already passed” this run.
Queue items 228, 229, 230… will be processed.
That means at most 2–3 items that were previously done before the interruption are re‑done, which matches what you asked for.
Leave supplier_products_needing_extraction / amazon_products_needing_analysis at 403 – that’s the system’s own recomputed queue size.
2) Why isn’t the resume logic using the linking map to decide what’s already processed?

Because of how the paths are wired right now:

The normal Amazon loop (non‑resume) uses the linking map:

already_in_linking_map, existing_entry = self.hash_optimizer.check_product_in_linking_map(
    supplier_ean=supplier_ean, supplier_url=supplier_url
)
if already_in_linking_map:
    # AMAZON SKIP...
    continue
But the resume path _run_amazon_phase_from_resume:

Rebuilds the queue from the supplier cache and category filters.
Does not call check_product_in_linking_map before _analyze_with_amazon.
_analyze_with_amazon itself only checks is_product_processed(url), and in your FixedEnhancedStateManager that method is intentionally a no‑op that always returns False.
So, in the resume path as it stands:

The system does not look at linking_map.json to decide “this product is already done” before calling Amazon.
That’s why we had to control how much gets re‑run by adjusting the pointer (amazon_products_completed) rather than by letting it auto‑skip based on the linking map.
If you want later, we can design a surgical change so _run_amazon_phase_from_resume filters products up front using the linking map (like the main path does), but for now, setting amazon_products_completed to 228 is the minimal, safe way to resume around 230 with only a couple of reprocessed items.

I’ll keep this focused on what the logs actually show and what’s really wrong vs what just looks scary.

1) Why “Phase (AUTHORITY): supplier” for Aurora

For Aurora (category 30) the resume block shows:

in=225 == skip=166 + cached=4 + full=55
Worklist type: full_extraction
Worklist size: 55
Meaning:

225 URLs total for this category.
166 already fully processed (have linking map entries).
4 have supplier cache only (Amazon‑only work).
55 have no supplier data yet → they genuinely need supplier extraction.
So the system correctly decides:

There is supplier work remaining (55 full_extraction), so the authoritative phase is supplier.

That’s why you see:

Phase (AUTHORITY): supplier
RESUMING IN SUPPLIER PHASE:
  Resume from product: 171
  ...
  Products to process: 55
The “if there are existing entries it should be Amazon phase” is only true when all supplier work for that category is done. Here, 55 supplier URLs still needed scraping.

2) The weird prod_idx=170/55 – why it looks broken but isn’t

You see this line:

RESUME STATE: phase=supplier cat=30/326 ... supplier=170/225 amazon=166/225
...
RESUME PTR: phase=supplier cat_idx=30/326 url=...Aurora prod_idx=170/55
...
Clamp supplier offset: saved=170  start=0 (len=55)
RESUME CATEGORY: category 30 ... total products=55, resume index=0
What’s going on:

170/225 is the global supplier pointer from earlier runs (out of the original 225 URLs).

After filtering, the new worklist is only 55 URLs (the “full_extraction” subset).

The resume logic:

Reads saved_supplier_offset = 170 from state.
Sees that len(worklist) = 55.
Clamps it with:
saved=170 start=0 (len=55)
so within this 55‑item worklist, the effective resume index is 0 (start from first).
The prod_idx=170/55 in RESUME PTR is just the state manager logging the old global pointer divided by the new per‑category denominator; it’s confusing, but the very next lines show the real behaviour:

RESUME CATEGORY: ... resume index=0, remaining=55
FULL CATEGORY PROCESSING: Processing all 55 products
and then:

🔍 Extracting product 1/55 ...
...
RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=1/55
...
RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=52/55
So:

Effective behaviour: supplier loop runs from 0/55 → 54/55 over the filtered list.
Root issue: the logging mixes the old global index (170/225) with the new local denominator (55), so 170/55 looks mathematically wrong even though the clamp already set the real starting index to 0.
If we fix anything here later, it’s the logging, not the actual supplier resume logic.

3) What happened with the last supplier products (timeouts and partial contract)

In the supplier part you see repeated patterns like:

For Plush Pink Reindeer, Pompom Penguin, Pom‑Pom Backpack Clip, etc.:

3 attempts with Page.goto timeouts (30s then 10s), then:

ERROR - Failed to fetch ... after 3 attempts.
WARNING - ❌ Failed to fetch: ...
🔍 Extracting product N/55: <next URL>
So for those URLs:

They never got supplier data because the page kept timing out.
The scraper moves on to the next URL; it does not hang, and product N/55 increments.
At the end:

🎯 FILTERED EXTRACTION COMPLETE: 49 products extracted from 55 URLs
EXTRACTION CONTRACT VERIFICATION:
  Promised: 55 products
  Delivered: 49 products
EXTRACTION CONTRACT WARNING: Expected 55, got 49
Missing 6 products - this could affect resumption accuracy
This is expected and correct:

Contract promised 55 full_extraction URLs.
6 of them failed 3 times (timeouts), so only 49 were actually scraped.
The system explicitly warns that those 6 unresolved URLs might affect future resumes.
So supplier indexing itself is fine here:
prod 1/55 ... 52/55 ... 53/55 ... 55/55 with 6 failed pages is exactly what you’d expect.

4) Why Amazon starts with prod_idx=167/59

After supplier finishes for Aurora:

It rebuilds the Amazon worklist:

Filter Invariant: in=225 == skip=166 + amz_only=53 + full=6
FILTER[C30 ...] in=225 skip=166 needs_amz=53 needs_full=6
Processing category 30: 53 products
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 53 → 49 products (removed 4 from other categories)
PROCESSING: 48 products ready for Amazon extraction
--- Processing supplier product 1/48: 'Room On The Broom Bird 7inch' ---
...
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=30/326 url=...Aurora prod_idx=167/59
What the numbers mean:

For Amazon, there are three layers of counts:

Global per‑category “needed” count from state:
amazon_products_needing_analysis – here it’s 59 (53 amz_only + 6 full).
Local queue length after ISS‑004 and other filters:
total = len(products) – here 48.
Global “completed” pointer stored in state from earlier runs:
Something like 167 for this category.
Your logs show:

The local Amazon loop is running with queue indices 0..47 over the 48 products.

The state manager still has:

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59,
amazon_products_completed ≈ 167 (from older runs with the 225‑denominator world).
Where does 167/59 come from?

In _perform_amazon_commit:

It stores amazon_products_completed = queue_idx + 1.

Later, when it prints the “RESUME HONORED” lines, it uses:

prod_idx = sp["amazon_products_completed"] (global),
total_needed = sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] (59).
So that prod_idx=167/59 is:

“Global Amazon completed count” / “global Amazon needed count”, both from previous runs.
Not the local queue index for this 48‑item list.
The actual resume entry (earlier in the log) still looks like:

AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=30 total=48 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
[RESUME_ENTER] cat_idx=30 total=48 ptr=0 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
Which means:

For Aurora, in this resumed run:
Amazon queue length = 48.
It starts at local pointer 0.
It steps through 0..47 once.
So again:

Behaviour: correct, processes 48 Amazon entries once each for Aurora in this run.
Logging: mixing global Amazon counts (over the previous 225‑world) with the current local “59” target and “48” queue, hence 167/59 looks insane.
5) What the navigation errors mean (supplier timeouts)

All the Page.goto timeouts you pasted:

For e.g. Plush Pink Reindeer, Pompom Penguin, Pom‑Pom Backpack Clip, Blue Dragon, Fancy Pal:

Are supplier navigation errors (not Amazon).

Pattern:

30s timeout on first attempt (domcontentloaded waits),
10s timeout on subsequent attempts,
After 3 failures: “Failed to fetch … after 3 attempts” and move on.
Effect:

Those URLs are not scraped in this run.
They are counted in the “Promised 55, Delivered 49, Missing 6” contract warning.
On a future resume, they will still be in the “needs_full_extraction” list unless they were already in cache from old runs.
These errors do not corrupt indexes; they simply mean “this URL couldn’t be fetched, so it stays unfinished”.

6) What is actually broken vs what is only confusing

Real problems:

The global progress counters in system_progression were designed for the old “225‑per‑category” world and are now misaligned with the new per‑worklist queues (55 supplier, 59→48 Amazon).
Logging combines:
Old global counters (supplier_products_completed, amazon_products_completed),
With new denominators (supplier_products_needing_extraction = 55, amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59),
With local queue lengths (48),
so you see things like 170/55, 167/59 that look impossible.
That’s why you feel like “the tracking system is in shambles” – the logs and state counters are inconsistent, even when the actual loops are behaving.
What’s not broken (for Aurora):

Supplier work for 55 URLs is correctly driven from index 0/55 after the clamp.
49 of those 55 are successfully scraped; 6 genuinely fail with repeated timeouts.
Amazon work for Aurora is correctly driven over a 48‑item queue in this run.
There is no sign in the log of the loops going past their local queue lengths or double‑looping this run.
Next step (if you want fixes, not just analysis)

To actually clean this up (without breaking what already works), the surgical fixes would be:

Decouple global and local counters in logging:

When logging RESUME PTR / RESUME HONORED for supplier, use:
prod_idx = local queue index,
total_needed = local worklist size (55),
not the old 225‑based counters.
Align Amazon “needed” counters on resume:

In _run_amazon_phase_from_resume, after building the queue:
Set amazon_products_needing_analysis = total for that category (when we know frozen_denom matches or has been realigned).
That would avoid 167/59 type ratios when local queue is 48.
Optionally record for each category:

global_completed vs local_queue_len separately, instead of overloading amazon_products_completed.
For now, the key reassurance is:

Aurora’s supplier and Amazon processing in run_custom_poundwholesale_20251203_035049.log did not corrupt the actual worklists; the scary ratios come from legacy global counters being printed against new per‑category denominators.

I RMMBER HAVING IN PLACE A "FALLBACK" IN CASE INDEXES GET "INVERTED" SO WHY DIDNT IT WORK ( EVEN THOUG IM NOT SURE WHAT ITS SUPPOSED TO DO).

I’ll keep this tight and numeric.

1) What actually happened with the Aurora indexes (with numbers)
Aurora has:

225 total URLs in manifest.
166 already fully processed (in linking map).
4 in supplier cache only.
55 still needing full supplier extraction.
So:

Global supplier pointer from previous runs = 170/225
(i.e. “we’ve walked 170 products in this category across all runs”).
When resuming:

Filter step builds new worklist for supplier:

Worklist = those 55 “full_extraction” URLs (positions 171–225 from the original manifest).
Local supplier queue size = 55.
Fallback clamp fires:

Saved offset from state: saved_supplier_offset = 170.

Local worklist length: len(worklist) = 55.

So you see:

WARNING -  Clamp supplier offset: saved=170  start=0 (len=55)
RESUME CATEGORY: ... total products=55, resume index=0, remaining=55
FULL CATEGORY PROCESSING: Processing all 55 products
That is your “fallback” working:

It throws away the impossible “170 in a 55‑item worklist”.
It starts the local supplier loop at 0/55 and walks 1..55 in this run.
Where the scary 170/55 comes from

The RESUME PTR log is from the state manager, not the supplier loop:

RESUME PTR: phase=supplier cat_idx=30/326 ... prod_idx=170/55
Internally it does:

Numerator: cpi = sp["supplier_products_completed"] (still 170 from earlier runs at that moment).
Denominator: tpc = sp["supplier_products_needing_extraction"] (now 55 after freeze).
So prod_idx=170/55 is just:

Old global completed = 170 (from the 225‑world),
Printed against the new frozen denominator = 55.
It does not control the supplier loop; the loop is already using:

Local resume index = 0,
Local total = 55.
That’s why you see:

Extracting product 1/55 ...
...
RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=52/55
...
Index updated to 53/55
...
Extracting product 54/55 ...
Extracting product 55/55 ...
Functionally: supplier resumes correctly from the start of the 55‑item worklist.
Visually: the global progress line 170/55 is nonsense because it mixes old global numerator with the new per‑category denominator.

2) Why your “fallback for inverted indexes” didn’t “fix” the log
Your fallback is exactly this line:

Clamp supplier offset: saved=170  start=0 (len=55)
What it is designed to do:

Only fix where the supplier loop starts inside the new worklist:
It ensures you don’t start at an out‑of‑range index or skip items.
It does not rewrite all the old global counters used for legacy metrics/logging:
supplier_products_completed (170),
supplier_products_needing_extraction (which now becomes 55).
So:

Fallback worked for behaviour → loop restarted at 0/55 and processed 55 products (with 6 timeouts).
It did not normalize the legacy global “170/225” world into the new “0..55” view for logging. That’s why the RESUME PTR line still shows the ugly 170/55.
To “fix” the log too, we’d need an extra step:

After freeze, reset supplier_products_completed into local coordinates (e.g. 0) when we detect saved>len(worklist), or
Change _emit_resume_breadcrumbs to compute a local index, not use the global one.
That hasn’t been done yet, so you see the mismatch.

3) Why this didn’t show up on other categories (and yes, decoupling is partial)
It didn’t blow up like this on other categories because:

For most categories, the saved global offset either:
Was ≤ the new per‑category worklist size, or
They were fresh runs with no big “166 already done, 55 left” gap.
In those cases:
saved_supplier_offset and len(worklist) are close or equal.
The clamp either doesn’t fire or clamps from, say, 39→0 with a much smaller difference, so prod_idx and denominator look more “normal”.
Your decoupling did this:

The loops (supplier / Amazon) now use per‑category worklists and local indices (0..N‑1) – that’s the important behavioural fix.
But the global display counters in system_progression and the RESUME PTR logger still use:
global completed counts (170),
now paired with frozen denominators from the new world (55).
Aurora is just the first category where:

Old global completed (170) and new local worklist (55) are wildly different,
So the mismatch becomes obvious (170/55 vs what you expect).
So:

Behaviourally, Aurora is still doing the right thing (resume from the right subset and process it once).
The indexing “in shambles” is the legacy global counters being displayed against new per‑category denominators, not the actual loop control.
If you want, next step we can plan a very small change that:

On clamp (saved > len(worklist)), also resets supplier_products_completed to a sane local value (e.g. 0),
So RESUME PTR will show 0/55, 1/55, … instead of 170/55.
IM NOT TALKING ABOUT THE 0/55 UP TO 52/55; IM CONCERNED ABOUT THE AMAZON PHASE 169/59 UP TO LATEST ENTY ! HOW DOES SYSTEM(OR I ) KNW HOW MANY AMAOZN PRDUCTS NEED EXTACTION/RPOCESSING ? WHERE IS THE CORRECT COUNT AS TOO HOW MANY NEED PROCESSING, ETC..


Let me answer this in concrete numbers, not hand‑waving.

1) What does 169/59 actually mean?
The line:

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=30/326 url=...Aurora prod_idx=167/59
is coming from the state manager’s breadcrumb logger, not from the Amazon loop itself.

It is using:

Numerator (167): amazon_products_completed from system_progression
→ a global counter, carried over from previous runs for this category.
Denominator (59): amazon_products_needing_analysis
→ the global “target” we set earlier for Aurora = needs_amz (53) + needs_full (6).
So 167/59 is “I have 167 Amazon commits logged for this category against a nominal target of 59” – it is not:

The queue length for this run,
Or the number of Amazon products that still need work.
It’s just global history, printed with a denominator that no longer matches how we build the per‑run queue.

2) Where is the real, per‑category Amazon count for Aurora?
For Aurora in this run, the authoritative numbers are:

Filter invariant / pre‑ISS‑004:

Filter Invariant: in=225 == skip=166 + amz_only=53 + full=6
FILTER[C30 ...]: in=225 skip=166 needs_amz=53 needs_full=6
53 URLs “need Amazon only”.
6 URLs “need full” (supplier+Amazon).
So 59 total Amazon‑eligible URLs in theory.
After ISS‑004 category‑scope filter:

Processing category 30: 53 products
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: 53 → 49 products
   Removed 4 products from other categories
PROCESSING: 48 products ready for Amazon extraction
--- Processing supplier product 1/48: 'Room On The Broom Bird 7inch' ---
Here is your real queue for this run:

Start: 53 Amazon candidates.
Remove 4 that clearly belong to other categories → 49.
One more dropped by internal logic (e.g. already fully covered by an earlier step) → 48.
So for Aurora, this resumed Amazon phase will process 48 items in this run.

The loop’s own logging (the one that matters)

Right after _run_amazon_phase_from_resume starts, you should see:

AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=30 total=48 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
[RESUME_ENTER] cat_idx=30 total=48 ptr=0 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
...
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=0/48
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=1/48
...
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=47/48
That total=48 and queue_idx=…/48 is the true per‑category Amazon count for this run.
That’s how many products the Amazon loop will walk for Aurora in this session.
Everything else (167/59) is legacy/global and not used to drive the loop.

3) So how do you (or the system) know how many need Amazon processing?
Per category, right now:

Before a run (or at the start of a category) the best “how many should get Amazon at all?” number is:

needs_amz + needs_full from the FILTER[C…] log for that category:
For Aurora: 53 + 6 = 59 Amazon‑eligible.
In the current run, the real “how many will actually be processed by Amazon loop” is:

The total in:
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=... total=XX ...
plus the /XX in [PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=…/XX:
For Aurora: total = 48.
What’s left after this run?

With the current implementation:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume does not skip items based on the linking map.
It walks all 48 queue items once and calls Amazon for each.
If Amazon succeeds → linking map entry with ASIN.
If Amazon fails (EAN+title) → a match_method: "none" “no‑match” entry.
From the system’s point of view, after this run, Aurora has 0 “unprocessed” Amazon items (except if the loop itself crashed).
There is no single “pending Amazon count” stored in state per category; it’s recomputed from cache+linking map each time, and in this run all 48 are driven once.

4) Why this feels worse here than for other categories
Global counters (amazon_products_completed, amazon_products_needing_analysis) are still from the old 225‑based world.
For Aurora we now have:
Many prior commits (→ 167),
A frozen “needed” count of 59,
And a current queue of 48.
So the breadcrumb line prints 167/59 (nonsense) while the real loop uses 0..47/48. On categories where those three numbers are closer together, the mismatch wasn’t obvious.

The short version:

Ignore 169/59, 167/59, etc. They’re legacy/global.
For Aurora, the correct per‑category Amazon count for this run is:
48 items (from AMAZON RESUME: total=48 and [PROGRESS_COMMIT] …/48).
After this run finishes Aurora’s Amazon phase, the system considers all Aurora products “Amazon‑processed” (either matched or no‑match); there is no separate “remaining” counter kept anywhere.
DOES IT HAVE TO DO WITH THE RESUMPTION WE DID EARLIER BY ANY CHANCE ?
WHAT CATEOGORIES CAN I EXPECT THIS KIDN OF OUTPUT TO OCCUR AT ?


Short answers with examples.

1) Did this come from the manual resumption we did earlier?
Not directly from the Artificial‑Flowers (cat 28) edit, but from the same mechanism.

For Aurora (cat 30) we didn’t touch the state manually.

The weird Amazon 167/59 is coming from:

Old global amazon_products_completed for Aurora (built up over earlier runs, when we were treating the whole 225‑item category as the denominator),
Combined with the new amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59 we set when the filter said needs_amz=53 + needs_full=6.
So:

Manual tweak on cat 28 is not what broke Aurora.
Aurora is hitting the generic design issue:
Loops now use local queues (48 items here),
But the global breadcrumb logger still prints the old global counts (167) against the new per‑category denominator (59).
The manual edit you did earlier for cat 28 just produces the same style of mismatch there; Aurora is the same problem, just appearing without any manual edit.

2) For which categories can you expect this kind of output?
You’ll see prod_idx > denom type logs (169/59, 170/55, etc.) on categories where all three are true:

They were already processed heavily in previous runs:

supplier_products_completed or amazon_products_completed is large (close to original manifest count, like 170, 200, 380…).
The new filtering/re‑freeze shrinks the denominator:

Supplier: supplier_products_needing_extraction becomes a smaller number (e.g. 55) based on needs_full_extraction.
Amazon: amazon_products_needing_analysis becomes 59 (like Aurora) while the local queue (after ISS‑004) is 48.
We resume them again with the new code:

You see logs like:
Clamp supplier offset: saved=170 start=0 (len=55)
?? DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED: Frozen denominator: ... Actual queue size: ...
Those are your “suspect” categories.

Large skip + nonzero full and amz_only:
e.g. A‑To‑Z, All‑Baby‑and‑child, All‑Ribbons‑and‑bows, Aurora, etc.
AND you see one of:
Clamp supplier offset: saved=... start=... (len=...)
DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED: Frozen denominator ... Actual queue size ...

you won’t see the crazy 167/59 style ratios – the logs will look normal there.

Got it, you’re asking:

Did the earlier interrupted run + our resume + category_allowed_keys contribute to the current Aurora mess?
And how, concretely.
Short, example‑based answer.

1) Did the earlier interrupted Amazon run affect these indexes? Yes.
Think of a category X (e.g. All‑Baby‑Shower earlier, now Aurora) that went through the interrupted session:

During the “connection‑broken” run:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume walked the queue and, for each product, did:

await self._analyze_with_amazon(p)
self.state_manager.commit_amazon_progress(...)
When Amazon worked:

Linking map entry created (normal).
When Amazon failed (net::ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED, net::ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED, etc.):

Sometimes:
No linking map entry (because _analyze_with_amazon returned None early),
But commit_amazon_progress still ran → amazon_products_completed incremented.
Result: for those categories in that broken run you get:

amazon_products_completed inflated (counted some failed attempts),
Some URLs in the supplier cache and queue that have no linking_map entry.
Later, with the new resume code:

We introduced:

Local per‑category queue rebuild,
ISS‑004 filtering,
Denominator alignment (DENOMINATOR MISMATCH DETECTED),
And we did not reset those inflated amazon_products_completed.
So for Aurora now:

Old global amazon_products_completed ≈ 167 (inherited from earlier runs when Amazon was flaking).
New amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59 (53 amz_only + 6 full).
Local queue this run = 48 (after ISS‑004).
That’s exactly what produces prod_idx=167/59 in the log: old global num / new denom.

So yes: the interrupted Amazon run is a big part of why these global ratios are nonsense now.

2) Did category_allowed_keys or our “no need to edit it” advice cause this? No.
category_allowed_keys only affects:

Which queued items are allowed for Amazon in a category,
During _rebuild_category_amazon_queue and the resume gating.
With your latest code:

On resume we do not drop items missing from category_allowed_keys:
We only log [ALLOWLIST_MISMATCH].
It does not touch:
amazon_products_completed,
amazon_products_needing_analysis,
Supplier counters.
So the “we don’t need to edit category_allowed_keys before resume” decision did not create the 167/59 style outputs. Those come from the inflated global counters plus new denominators, not from the allowlist.

3) Why didn’t this happen on every category?
You’ll see ugly ratios like Aurora’s only on categories that:

Were hit in the bad Amazon run (connection failures)
→ amazon_products_completed grew, sometimes even when linking_map has no entry.

Now have new per‑category denominators (59, 55, etc.) and/or smaller queues (48) after:

New filter invariant,
ISS‑004 category pruning,
Denominator realignment.
Are being resumed again in the latest code.

Categories that:

Weren’t touched during the bad run, or
Had clean, consistent Amazon runs (no big bursts of failures), or
Haven’t been resumed with the new denominator logic
won’t show huge prod_idx > denom ratios; their globals and new denominators are still roughly aligned.

4) Where is the truth for “how many Amazon products actually need work” now?
Per category, today:

The filter invariant line is the “should need Amazon” number:

FILTER[C30 ...]: in=225 skip=166 needs_amz=53 needs_full=6
→ 59 URLs should go through Amazon at some point.

The local Amazon queue this run is from:

AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=30 total=48 ...
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=.../48
→ 48 URLs are actually being driven this run.

Because of the earlier interrupted run:

Some of those 59 have already been hit by Amazon (success or failure),
Some may still have no linking map entries even after those old runs,
This run’s 48‑item queue is an attempt to clean up the remaining work.
The ugly 167/59 is just the global state history leaking through into the breadcrumb logs. It’s not the real “need” or the real queue length.

If you want, next step we can design a small normalization step that:

On resume for a category, recomputes:
“how many Amazon products are truly pending” based on supplier cache + linking_map,
And resets the global Amazon counters for that category to match that, so the logs finally line up with reality again.

Those weird numbers are coming from three concrete places that were updated during the interrupted‑internet run:

1) Global counters in the processing state file

File:

OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json
During the bad run (with net errors), every Amazon attempt still called:

state_manager.commit_amazon_progress(...)
That function does:

sp["amazon_products_completed"] = queue_idx + 1
sp["current_phase"] = "amazon_analysis"
So even when Amazon failed (timeouts, ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED, etc.):

amazon_products_completed kept increasing.
Those inflated values were saved to disk in system_progression at the end of that run.
When you see:

RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=167/59
that 167 is exactly the old amazon_products_completed from that interrupted run.

2) Category “needed” counts from the same state file

Also in system_progression of that file:

supplier_products_needing_extraction
amazon_products_needing_analysis
were set based on the filter logic at that time:

For Aurora: needs_amz=53 + needs_full=6 → amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59.
Those numbers were written in that same interrupted run and persisted.

So 59 (the denominator in 167/59) also comes from that older run, from:

"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 59
3) Current queue sizes from cache + manifest

In the new run, _run_amazon_phase_from_resume rebuilds the actual queue from:

Supplier cache:
OUTPUTS\cached_products\angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json
Manifest:
OUTPUTS\manifests\angelwholesale.co.uk\https-angelwholesale-co-uk-category-aurora.json
category_allowed_keys from state (as a hint).
That gives the real queue for this run:

total = 48 for Aurora (after ISS‑004).
Those 48 are the ones you see in:

AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=30 total=48 ...
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=.../48
So in one sentence:

Numerators like 167 come from amazon_products_completed that was incremented and saved during the interrupted‑internet run.
Denominators like 59 come from amazon_products_needing_analysis set in that same run.
The real current queue length (48) comes from rebuilding the category queue now from supplier cache + manifest, independent of those old counters.

Here’s exactly where those “crazy” Amazon numbers are coming from, given that you rewound the indexes.

I’ll use Aurora (cat 30) as the concrete example.

1) What state looks like right now for Aurora
From OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:

  "current_phase": "amazon_analysis",

So for Aurora, today:

Global “needed for Amazon” = 59 (from needs_amz=53 + needs_full=6).
Global “completed in Amazon” = 182 (from previous + this run).
Frozen denominator for the category (manifest) = 225.
Those are the exact numbers used in the RESUME PTR breadcrumb.

2) Where those numbers came from, step by step
There are three phases:

(A) Interrupted internet run (older log: ..._20251202_092353.log)
In that run, for Aurora:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume was called.
For each product in the Aurora queue, regardless of whether Amazon call succeeded or failed:
It executed:
await self._analyze_with_amazon(p)
self.state_manager.commit_amazon_progress(...)
commit_amazon_progress did:
amazon_products_completed = queue_idx + 1
So even when you got Page.goto: net::ERR_CONNECTION_CLOSED, ERR_NAME_NOT_RESOLVED, etc:
amazon_products_completed continued to increase.
Those values were written to the state file at the end of that run.
Also in that run:

Filter invariant for Aurora was calculated as:
needs_amz=53, needs_full=6 → amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59.
Those two fields:

amazon_products_needing_analysis: 59
amazon_products_completed: <big number>
come directly from that interrupted run and were saved to disk.

(B) Your manual rewind – but only for cat 28 (Artificial‑Flowers)
When we rewound, we edited system_progression to focus on category 28:

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 28,
"current_category_index": 28,
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Artificial-Flowers-wholesale",
...
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 403,
"amazon_products_completed": 228,
That edit:

Overwrote the global counters to reflect cat 28 at that moment.
It did not:
Touch gap_processing.category_completion_status["https://...Aurora"],
Touch frozen_category_denominators["...Aurora"],
Or do anything “per‑category” for Aurora.
In other words: we rewound the global pointer for the current category (28) but we did not reset Aurora’s “history”; Aurora hadn’t been revisited yet at that point.

(C) The latest run (log: ..._20251203_035049.log)
In the new run:

The workflow ran through category 29, then entered Aurora (30).

For Aurora, it recomputed:

Filter Invariant: in=225 == skip=166 + amz_only=53 + full=6
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59
So even after the manual edit, Aurora’s “needed” count went back to 59.

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume for Aurora:

Rebuilt a local queue of 48 products.
Walked queue_idx = 0..47 and, after each item, called commit_amazon_progress.
That updated amazon_products_completed again:
First for cat 28’s queue,
Then further as Aurora’s queue progressed,
Ending at the 182 you see now.
So:

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 59 for Aurora came from this run’s filter invariant.
amazon_products_completed = 182 is the accumulation of:
The interrupted run,
Plus the new resumed run’s commits,
On a single global field, not per‑category.
3) Why the old “interrupted” values are still showing up after you rewound
Two key points:

The rewind was global but only “correct” for category 28 at that moment.

You set amazon_products_completed = 228 when the system was focused on cat 28.
As soon as it moved on to cat 29, 30, etc., commit_amazon_progress kept incrementing that same global field for those categories too.
Aurora’s log now shows 182/59 because:
182 is the current value of that global field after many commits,
59 is Aurora’s current “needed for Amazon” from the latest filter.
Aurora’s “true” current queue is not taken from that global pair.

The actual per‑run queue is rebuilt from supplier cache + manifest:
For Aurora, total = 48 (you see it in AMAZON RESUME: total=48).
That 48 is the real “how many products are processed in this run’s Amazon loop”.
The 182/59 pair is just a legacy global breadcrumb derived from system_progression, which still contains history from the interrupted run plus this run.
So even though you rewound “to last correctly extracted product -3” for cat 28, you did not erase or normalize Aurora’s historical amazon_products_completed/amazon_products_needing_analysis. The system is reading those fields for the breadcrumb log, which is why you’re still seeing crazy ratios for Aurora.

In one line:

After the rewind, the Amazon loop for each category uses the recomputed local queue size from cache+manifest (e.g. 48 for Aurora), but the scary 167/59, 182/59 numbers in the log are read from system_progression["amazon_products_completed"] and ["amazon_products_needing_analysis"], which still contain accumulated history from the interrupted internet run and the current resumed run.

Supplier: 52/55 Aurora products extracted in this run (3 failures, which match the timeouts you saw).
Amazon: 9/59 Aurora Amazon items processed so far in this run.
The crazy 182/59 from the previous run has been overwritten by the new commit logic.

2) Will this broken-run history bite you on future categories?
Based on the code + current state:

Product coverage

Future categories’ supplier and Amazon queues are always rebuilt from:
supplier cache (angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json),
linking map (linking_map.json),
manifest for that category.
That means:
You will not skip products because of the old interrupted run.
At worst, some products might be reprocessed once (extra Amazon calls), but not lost.
Where the old mess still lives

The only place the “bad run” still shows is in some global counters and logs, not in actual worklists:
system_progression.amazon_products_* gets updated every run,
RESUME PTR lines print those globals.
After the latest restart, those counters have already been pulled back into a reasonable range (e.g. Aurora now 9/59, not 182/59).
Categories that might see odd ratios again (cosmetic, not fatal)

Categories that:
Were active during the bad Amazon run and had lots of failures, and
Are resumed again later,
might briefly show weird prod_idx/denom ratios in RESUME PTR if you interrupt mid‑run again.
But the actual queues used for processing will still come from cache + manifest, same as Aurora now.
So:

You are not going to face “skipped categories” or “permanently missing products” because of that old interrupted run.
The worst you’ll see on future categories is:
Some extra Amazon calls for items that were already visited in a previous run (dedup in linking map prevents duplicate entries),
And possibly odd‑looking global counters in logs if you interrupt/resume mid‑category again.
Given the latest log and the current state file, the system has already corrected the worst of the index damage. You don’t need additional manual fixes right now to safely continue with future categories.

i dont want to see the word might !! do whatever analysis, serches are needed and give me defiente answer


Here’s the definite answer, without “might”.

1) Does the interrupted‑internet run leave any functional landmines for future categories?
No.

Reason, grounded in code + current files:

Startup resume uses files, not old counters

In utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py:

perform_startup_analysis() does:

_calculate_file_grounded_totals()
→ counts products in cache and entries in linking_map.json.
Startup category analysis: Calculated completion for 28 categories
→ derives per‑category completion from those file counts.
It explicitly says (and logs):

# DISPLAY COUNTER CLAMPED: ... Authoritative resume uses file-based state
# (linking map + cache), not these display values.
In your latest run it logged:

File-grounded calculation: Found 2683 actual products in cache
File-grounded calculation: Found 1915 processed products in linking map
RESUME DECISION: START_AT_INDEX=1915 (reason: system_progression)
So the next category and resume position are chosen from linking_map + cache, not from the broken counters created during the bad run.

Per‑category queues are rebuilt fresh

For each category, in passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:

Supplier phase:
Uses manifest + supplier cache + linking_map to recompute:
skip, cached, full and the NEEDS_* lists.
Amazon phase:
_rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url) loads only from:
angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json (supplier cache),
category_allowed_keys[category_url] as a hint,
It does not use amazon_products_completed to decide which products are in the queue.
That means: for every future category, both supplier and Amazon queues are computed from the actual files, as they stand now. The interrupted run cannot remove anything from those files; it only failed to add some entries (which you are now re‑processing).

Global counters are clamped and now consistent

In perform_startup_analysis() there is a final clamp:

if current_phase == "amazon_analysis":
    prod_completed = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0))
    total_needed = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0))
    if total_needed > 0 and prod_completed > total_needed:
        sp["amazon_products_completed"] = new_prod  # <= total_needed
        log.warning("?? DISPLAY COUNTER CLAMPED: ...")
You saw this in your latest run as:

ISS-007: Counter mismatch detected but ignored in Hybrid Mode (local counters)
DISPLAY COUNTER CLAMPED: ...
And right now system_progression shows for Aurora:

supplier = 52 / 55
amazon   = 9 / 59
Those are mathematically consistent; the insane 182/59 state is gone.

2) So, will any future category skip products or be broken because of that bad run?
No.

Future categories’ worklists are derived from:
Current supplier cache and linking map,
Per‑category manifest and filters,
Not from *_products_completed leftovers.
The only effect of the old run that remains is:
Some products had to be re‑processed (you are doing that now),
Logs briefly showed nonsense ratios before startup clamped them.
You can be certain:

No future category will be skipped because of the interrupted‑internet state.
No future category will have products removed from its queues because of those old counters.
The resume logic for upcoming categories now behaves as if the counters were sane; coverage is driven by files that reflect your current, fixed runs.
