THERE SHOULD BE OTHER SCENARIOS AS WELL WHERE WE ARE GETTING INCOSISTEN INDEX OUTPUT, HENCE I NEED YOU TO GO THROUGH THE LATEST 2/3 LOGS ( FOR THE SPECIFIC WEBSITE WE HAVE BEEN DISCSSING), ANALYZE THEM IN DEPTH, GO OVER AS MANY CATEGORY ( AND PHASES IN THSEE CATEGROIES) AS YOU CAN TO GENERATE A DETAILED REPORT HIGHLIGHTING THE EXACT PROBLEMATIC "SCENARIOS/SITUATIONS " WE ARE FACING , THE ROOT CAUSE BEHIND THEM AND SUGGESTED FIXES. ALSO WHILE YOURE AT IT MAKE SURE TO TELL ME IF THESE ARE CAUSING ANY PRODUCTS TO BE EITHER MISSED ( NOT EXTRACTED/PROCOESSED) OR HAVING PRODUCTS BEING RE-EXTRACTED OR REPROCESSED MULTIPLE TIMES ( EVEN IF NOT WRITTEN ON LINKING MAP OR CACHED PRODUCT FILE - I STILL NEED YOU TO CHECK IF SOME ARE BEING REWORKED)


Short answer

The “insane” ratios like prod_idx=131/93 and 75/119 come from a global progress counter, not from the real per‑category queues.
The queues that actually drive work (supplier extraction and Amazon analysis) still use correct local indices and lengths (e.g. queue_idx=17/29, Extracting product 34/115, etc.).
Because of that split, the bad numbers:
Do not decide which products are processed.
Do not cause products to be skipped.
At worst cause a small amount of re‑processing in the resume category only, never in later categories.
Below is the concrete breakdown based on your latest logs and current code.

1. Where the bad indexes actually come from
All references are to current files in your tree.

1.1. The breadcrumb that prints RESUME PTR: … prod_idx=X/Y

utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py (lines 1300-1316):

def _emit_resume_breadcrumbs(self):
    sp = self.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
    phase = sp.get("current_phase", "supplier")
    cci = sp.get("persistent_category_index", 1)
    tc = sp.get("total_categories", 0)
    if phase == "amazon_analysis":
        cpi = sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)
        tpc = sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0)
    else:
        cpi = sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0)
        tpc = sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0)
    ccu = sp.get("current_category_url", "")

    if not bool(sp.get("frozen_totals_committed", False)):
        log.debug("RESUME PTR suppressed: frozen_totals_committed=False")
        return
    if int(tpc or 0) == 0:
        log.debug(f"RESUME PTR suppressed: category_denominator_unfrozen ({phase})")
        return

    log.info(f" RESUME PTR: phase={phase} cat_idx={cci}/{tc} url={ccu} prod_idx={cpi}/{tpc}")
So:

Numerator in RESUME PTR: amazon_products_completed or supplier_products_completed.
Denominator in RESUME PTR: amazon_products_needing_analysis or supplier_products_needing_extraction.
These are global counters, reused across categories.

You can see this clearly in your state file after the long run:

OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:

"system_progression": {
  "current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
  "persistent_category_index": 38,
  "current_category_index": 38,
  "current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes",
  "supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
  "supplier_products_completed": 5,
  "amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
  "amazon_products_completed": 131,
  "category_denominator_frozen": false,
  "frozen_totals_committed": true
}
That is exactly what produced:

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=38/326 url=...Baby-Boy-Clothes prod_idx=131/93
Nothing in that breadcrumb knows anything about the actual Amazon queue for Baby‑Boy‑Clothes. It’s just dumping those two global fields.

1.2. The counters that actually drive the queues

There is a separate, local progress system:

Supplier phase:
Build a category worklist and log:
FULL CATEGORY PROCESSING: Processing all 55 products
🔍 Extracting product 34/115: ...
Amazon phase:
Rebuild queue per category and log:
CATEGORY QUEUE: url=... size=33
After ISS‑004: PROCESSING: 48 products ready for Amazon extraction
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=17/29 cat_idx=33
The queue and its indices come from:

tools\passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py (lines 7828-7848):

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
total = len(products)
...
for batch_num in range(start_batch, total_batches):
    ...
    for i in range(start_i, len(batch_products)):
        queue_idx = start_idx + i   # absolute within the category queue
        p = batch_products[i]
        ...
        self.state_manager.commit_amazon_progress(
            cat_idx=cat_idx,
            queue_idx=queue_idx,
            total_cats=total_cats,
            cat_url=category_url,
            queue_len=total,        # <= local queue length
        )
        self.log.info(f"[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx={queue_idx}/{total} cat_idx={cat_idx}")
So:

Real work uses queue_idx and total derived from the current per‑category queue (products).
Global amazon_products_needing_analysis / *_completed are only updated after each item for telemetry and resume breadcrumbs.
2. Concrete examples from your logs
2.1. Category 33 – Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding
At startup of the big run (run_custom_poundwholesale_20251203_141257.log and ...142758.log):

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=33/326 url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding prod_idx=75/119
Later, while actually running Amazon for that category:

[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=17/29 cat_idx=33
Analyzing product: 'Soft Touch Grey Bubble Embossed Mink Wrap'
...
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=33/326 url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding prod_idx=18/119
What this means:

Local Amazon queue for Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding has 29 items. That 29 is the real “products needing Amazon” for this category.
Global counters say:
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 119,
amazon_products_completed = 18 when that RESUME PTR logged.
Those 119/18 include history from previous categories + the “bad” interrupted runs; they are not the current queue length.
Root cause (this category)

amazon_products_needing_analysis was frozen earlier for this category from the manifest or pre‑filter counts (119).
During the network‑interrupted runs and the manual rewind, amazon_products_completed kept increasing across categories; it was never reset for this category (no final PHASE SWITCH: amazon_analysis → supplier happened here before).
_emit_resume_breadcrumbs logs completed/needed without looking at the actual queue.
Result: prod_idx=18/119 and 75/119 are global artefacts, while the real local queue is queue_idx 0..28 (29 items), which is what the Amazon loop uses.

2.2. Category 35 – Baby‑Clothing
From your log:

Filter Invariant: in=240 == skip=200 + amz_only=40 + full=0
FILTER[C35 ...Baby-Clothing]: in=240 skip=200 needs_amz=40 needs_full=0
...
🔍 Extracting product 127/240: ...
...
WARNING - Could not find frozen denominator for https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing. Falling back to dynamic total.
CLAMPED: supplier prod_idx 287  240
...
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=35/326 url=...Baby-Clothing prod_idx=160/241
[DENOM_FROZEN] url=...Baby-Clothing total=240 (LOCKED)
[INDEX_CLAMP] display_idx=241->240 reason=exceeds_denominator
?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 240/240
Interpretation:

Supplier queue for Baby‑Clothing has 240 items (post‑filter).
The real supplier progress is 0..240 and is clamped correctly (display_idx=241→240).
Global counters got into a bad state (supplier prod_idx 287), were clamped down to 240, but _emit_resume_breadcrumbs still uses those global fields against denominators that were frozen before this correction, hence 160/241.
Again:

The supplier loop runs from product 1/240 up to 240/240 correctly.
The weird 160/241 is just supplier_products_completed / supplier_products_needing_extraction before and after clamping, not the queue driver.
2.3. Category 38 – Baby‑Boy‑Clothes
From your current state:

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 38,
"current_category_index": 38,
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes",
"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
"supplier_products_completed": 5,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 131,
"frozen_totals_committed": true
Log:

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=38/326 url=...Baby-Boy-Clothes prod_idx=131/93
Here:

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 93 (per‑category Amazon denominator).
amazon_products_completed = 131 accumulated over several Amazon categories after the earlier interrupted / rewound runs.
Local Amazon queue for Baby‑Boy‑Clothes is size 93 (you see total=93 in its resume/queue logs), and the inner loop uses queue_idx 0..92.
So the “inverted” 131/93 is again just global state drift, not the driver of the loop.

3. Are any products missed or reprocessed because of this?
3.1. Missed products

For both phases, enumeration is always done from the current category queue, not from the global counters:

Supplier:

Worklist is built from manifest + cache + linking map:
NEEDS_FULL_EXTRACTION, NEEDS_AMAZON_ONLY, etc.
Loop runs over that list and logs like Extracting product X/Y.
Global supplier_products_* are updated after each item and can be wrong, but they never feed back into the loop.
Amazon:

Queue is rebuilt per category:

products = self._rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url)
total = len(products)
ISS‑004 and category scoping (category_queue_enforced) only remove:

Products definitively identified as from other categories, or
Things filtered by your allowlist (now treated as a hint on resume).
The Amazon loop runs queue_idx from the resume offset up to total-1, regardless of what amazon_products_completed says.

Resume:

_run_amazon_phase_from_resume uses saved_amazon_offset clamped to queue size (total):

if saved_amazon_offset >= total:
    ... restarting from 0
    prod_offset = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    prod_offset = clamped_amazon_offset
else:
    prod_offset = saved_amazon_offset
We then iterate the queue from prod_offset to total-1.

Given that, for categories where the Amazon or supplier loops ran, every item in the category queue is visited. The global counters only affect:

How the RESUME PTR / ATOMIC COMMIT logs look.
In the resume category, where we start (offset or restart from 0).
They do not cause in-category products to be skipped. The only product‑loss warnings you see are explicit contract failures, e.g.:

EXTRACTION CONTRACT WARNING: Expected 55, got 49
Missing 6 products - this could affect resumption accuracy
Those are real network/navigation failures (timeouts), not index math.

Conclusion on misses (based on code + your logs):

If a category’s supplier log says it processed N of N (or contract says Expected N, got M and you see the M failures as navigation errors), then every item in that category queue has been attempted.
The index anomalies do not introduce silent skips. They only make the global progress numbers unreliable.
3.2. Re‑processing

Where re‑processing can happen:

Resume category only

If saved_amazon_offset or saved_supplier_offset is out of range, we log:

Clamp supplier offset: saved=170  start=0 (len=55)
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 17 >= queue length 4; restarting from 0
In those cases, we restart from 0 for that category. That means:

You may re‑run all Amazon products in that category once more.
Dedup in cache and linking map prevents duplicate writes and double counting.
Manual rewind

When you manually set persistent_category_index backwards (e.g. from 30 to 28) but leave linking map and cache as they are, the system will legitimately re‑process categories 28–29 again.
This is expected and is the only way to “rewind”.
Outside of those two cases, the code never uses the global counters to re‑enqueue work for already‑completed categories. Later categories always build fresh queues from:

manifest,
cached products,
linking map.
So:

Yes, you can see local re‑processing inside the resume category, especially after clamping or manual rewinds.
No, there is no mechanism that will cause later categories (31, 32, …) to be processed twice just because the global counters are weird.
4. Why this happens in some categories and not others
From the combination of code and logs:

Categories where Amazon finished cleanly log:

AMAZON FINALIZER: queue done (done=denom denom=denom) completing category URL (idx=k)
PHASE TRANSITION: amazon_analysis  supplier
On that PHASE TRANSITION: amazon_analysis supplier, commit_phase_switch resets:

supplier_products_completed or amazon_products_completed (depending on target phase).

In categories where you had:

timeouts,
network errors,
or interrupted the run during Amazon,
the Amazon queue did not complete, so there was no phase transition, and the global counters kept accumulating.
Result:

Categories immediately after a clean Amazon completion have “nice” global counters.

Categories that follow an incomplete Amazon category will see:

accumulated amazon_products_completed from previous categories,
new denominators taken from their own manifest / filters.
That combination is exactly what generates the 131/93, 75/119, 160/241 you are seeing.

Once you let a category’s Amazon queue finish and the system logs PHASE SWITCH: amazon_analysis supplier, the next category starts with reset counters again.

5. Suggested fixes (surgical, minimal blast radius)
You asked for a plan, not edits yet. The minimal changes that would make the logs and processing_state match reality, without changing real behaviour:

Make breadcrumbs use local queue info instead of global counters

In _emit_resume_breadcrumbs:

For phase == "amazon_analysis", read queue_len and queue_idx from the last commit instead of amazon_products_*.
A simple pattern is:
When commit_amazon_progress runs, store:

sp["amazon_last_queue_len"] = queue_len
sp["amazon_last_queue_idx"] = queue_idx + 1
Then _emit_resume_breadcrumbs uses those two fields for prod_idx=X/Y when phase is amazon_analysis.

This keeps breadcrumbs in sync with [PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=…/total.

Stop feeding get_current_category_info with stale denominators

Right now (fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py (lines 982-1001)):

phase = sp.get("current_phase", "supplier")
queue_len = (
    int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
    if phase == "amazon_analysis"
    else int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
)
That’s what gives you queue_len=119, 93, etc. for Baby‑Blankets, Baby‑Boy‑Clothes.

Surgical change:

Introduce sp["amazon_last_queue_len"] and sp["supplier_last_queue_len"] (set in the commit methods).

Make get_current_category_info prefer those:

if phase == "amazon_analysis":
    queue_len = int(sp.get("amazon_last_queue_len", 0) or 0)
else:
    queue_len = int(sp.get("supplier_last_queue_len", 0) or 0)
That keeps all existing logic, but makes the queue length used for logging and parity commits match the actual queue.

Optionally, normalize global denominators to queue_len

If you want processing_state.json to show the “real” denominators:

In commit_amazon_progress and commit_supplier_progress, after clamping:

if queue_len > 0:
    sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] = int(queue_len)
and similarly for supplier.

That would remove 131/93‑style inversions entirely, at the cost of no longer seeing the legacy manifest totals in those fields.

All three changes are display‑only: they don’t touch _rebuild_category_amazon_queue, saved_amazon_offset, or the filter invariant. So they:

Fix the confusing indexes,
Do not change which products are extracted/analyzed,
Stay within your “minimal blast radius” requirement.
6. Answering your core question directly
The system still appears to be messing up some of the indexes. What is the root cause, and will I miss or double‑process products because of it?

Root cause:

Global *_products_completed and *_products_needing_* are reused across categories and partially corrupted by:
earlier network‑interrupted runs,
resumes that start in mid‑Amazon,
phase switches that didn’t fire (no completion → no reset),
and _emit_resume_breadcrumbs + some parity logs use those globals instead of the real per‑category queue indices/lengths.
Effect on behaviour:

Supplier and Amazon loops always iterate over the per‑category queue built from manifest + cache + linking map.
Resume logic uses saved_offset clamped against the local queue length total. When the offset is invalid, it restarts from 0.
Because of this, the index drift does not cause products to be skipped.
The only extra work it can cause is re‑processing items in the resume category, when we restart from 0 or clamp a bad offset.
Future categories:

With the current code, as you continue the run without manual rewinds, later categories will still occasionally show strange RESUME PTR ratios when:
you resume mid‑Amazon,
or a category’s Amazon queue never completed cleanly.
That will not stop those categories’ queues from being fully processed; it only makes the global counters noisy.
If you want, next step I can turn the fix plan in §5 into exact diffs, strictly limited to the state manager logging and get_current_category_info, so that the indexes you see in the logs and processing_state reflect the real per‑category progress without touching the actual work pipelines.

how can i "manually" reset/clear the "loaded queue" in a specific categroy, is thereany state entry, or fileentries, i can delete for the system to "regernate" the cageroy queue ( "thinking" it is the first time it is ocming accros said categoy) for example ? like what entries from what files do i need to remove and/or clear AND/OR are there in indexes in state file that i should/can edit, in order to have the system "reprocess" the catgeroy ( in the sense where it "calculates" the need amazon, need full extraction and so on indexes/values )

also, would it be problematic to have the system regeenrate the queue ( and/or "rerfeeze" the denominator - I NEED YOU TO CONFIRM IF THESE 2 ARE THE SAME OR NOT, IN THE SENSE WHERE FROZEN DENOMINATOR IS CALCUALTED/EQUIVALENT TO ENTRIES FROM GENERATED QUEUEUE PER CATEGROY) AT THE START OF EACH CATGEROY (during a session- meanin everytime the system comes accross a cateogry during a run, the system will regenerate the product/url queue).

is there a reason why the system currently still uses globa index -even though as i mentioned earlier, i remember that we removed global indexes from "being part" of system progress tracking ? meaning if we were to make the indexes per-category queue, would that affect any other sections of the workfow/system and could lead to discoprencies.

why are all the issues we are facing are primarly during the amaozn phase ? can we not have the system implement same approach as supplier phase ( during amaon phase) ? Basically, why do we the system ( amaozn phase specifically, configured the way it is, despite it outputting these "problmaic indexes" ? is it this way , because it is "somewaht of a "compromise to ensure that the system correctly processes the category products during he diffrentphases or ?


I’ll answer in three parts:

what you can safely edit to “reset” a category,
what regenerating the queue vs re‑freezing the denominator actually means,
why the Amazon phase is the one showing the ugly indexes and what global vs per‑category indices are doing.
1. “Resetting” a category so the queue is rebuilt

Important point first:
There is no persisted “queue file” per category. For each category, on every run, the workflow rebuilds:

supplier worklist from:
manifest for that category,
supplier cache,
linking map (skip already processed),
Amazon queue from:
supplier cache,
linking map (which products already have Amazon results),
per‑category filters (ISS‑004, category gating).
What is persisted and influences how the category is treated:

linking_map.json – tells the system which supplier URLs already have Amazon results.
angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json – tells the system which supplier pages are already extracted.
angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:
frozen_category_denominators (top level) – frozen “total products” per category.
gap_processing.category_completion_status[...] – summary for dashboards.
system_progression:
current_phase, persistent_category_index, current_category_url,
supplier_products_needing_extraction, supplier_products_completed,
amazon_products_needing_analysis, amazon_products_completed,
category_denominator_frozen, frozen_totals_committed,
category_allowed_keys (per‑category Step‑2 allowlist).
If you want a category to behave as if it’s “first time seen” for Amazon:

Pick the normalized category URL, e.g.:

https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Aurora
https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/All-Baby-Shower
https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes
Then:

Backup first

Copy:
OUTPUTS\FBA_ANALYSIS\linking_maps\angelwholesale.co.uk\linking_map.json
OUTPUTS\cached_products\angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json
OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json
Clear Amazon “done” knowledge for that category

In linking_map.json:

Remove all entries where supplier_url or category_url contains that category URL.
Example: for Aurora, any entry whose supplier_url contains /Category/Aurora or /Item/... that you know only belongs to Aurora.
Effect:

On next run, those supplier products will be treated as Amazon not processed yet, so the Amazon queue for that category will include them again.
Optionally clear supplier extraction for that category

If you also want to re‑extract supplier pages (not just re‑run Amazon):

In angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json, remove entries whose source_url or category_url contains that category URL.
Effect:

Next run, supplier phase will re‑fetch those pages instead of reusing cached extraction.
Reset denominator / allowlist for that category in state

In angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:

Under top‑level "frozen_category_denominators", delete the entry for that category URL if present.
Under "system_progression", if current_category_url and persistent_category_index currently point to that category and you want to “rewind”:
Set current_phase to "supplier" (if you want to force starting from supplier) or leave as "amazon_analysis" if you only want Amazon.
Set supplier_products_needing_extraction, supplier_products_completed,
amazon_products_needing_analysis, amazon_products_completed to 0.
Set category_denominator_frozen to false.
Set frozen_totals_committed to false.
Under "gap_processing" → "category_completion_status", you can delete the entry for that category URL if you want the dashboard to recompute it later.
Effect:

Next time the workflow enters that category, it will:
Recompute totals from manifest + cache + linking map,
Re‑freeze denominators for that category,
Rebuild the per‑category Amazon queue from the new file‑grounded truth.
Step‑2 allowlist for that category

In the same state file under "category_allowed_keys":

Remove the entry for your category URL if it exists.
Effect:

On the next run, Step‑2 (ISS‑004/category scoping) will rebuild the category‑scoped allowlist from actual queue data instead of using the persisted one.
That set of edits is the closest you can get to “reset this category as new” without touching code.

2. Queue regeneration vs frozen denominator

They are related but not identical.

Queue regeneration:

Happens in memory every time:
Supplier: worklist built from manifest + cache + linking map.
Amazon: _rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url) returns a list of products to analyze now.
Length of that list is what you see in logs as total= or Processing category X: N products.
Frozen denominator:

In fixed_enhanced_state_manager.set_frozen_denominator / freeze_category_totals:

For supplier:
supplier_products_needing_extraction is set from discovered_count (total URLs found for that category).
For Amazon:
amazon_products_needing_analysis is set from amazon_total (post‑filter count), or len(manifest_urls) if amazon_total not provided.
The same value is mirrored into frozen_category_denominators[category_url].
So:

The queue is “what we actually process this pass”.
The frozen denominator is “authoritative total for this phase in this category”, used for:
filter invariant checks (in = skip + cached + full),
safety validations (3‑source check: manifest vs frozen map vs resume),
progress display and resume proofs.
Your question:

would it be problematic to have the system regenerate the queue and/or re‑freeze the denominator at the start of each category?

The queue is already regenerated each time the category is entered.
Re‑freezing the denominator every time you re‑enter the category would break the 3‑source validation that expects:
manifest count,
frozen_category_denominators[cat_url],
supplier_products_needing_extraction / amazon_products_needing_analysis
to agree exactly.
Forcing a re‑freeze every time would turn those validations into noise and could mask real data corruption (for example, manifest change between runs).
So:

Regenerating the queue is safe and already happening.
Re‑freezing denominators repeatedly is not equivalent to queue regeneration and would interfere with the integrity checks unless you also relax that validation logic in code.
3. Global vs per‑category indices and why Amazon is noisy

You remember correctly: we removed global indices from controlling the workflow, but we did not remove them everywhere.

There are now two layers:

Per‑category local indices (truth for work)

Supplier:
Extracting product X/Y uses the category’s own worklist length Y and position X.
Amazon:
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=k total=T ...
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=i/T cat_idx=k
These use total = len(products) from _rebuild_category_amazon_queue.
These indices are what decide which products are processed.

Global counters (legacy / telemetry / dashboards)

Stored in system_progression:
supplier_products_needing_extraction, supplier_products_completed
amazon_products_needing_analysis, amazon_products_completed
Used by:
_emit_resume_breadcrumbs (RESUME PTR: prod_idx=X/Y),
log_resume_proof_after_commit (ATOMIC COMMIT ... prod=... phase=...),
gap_processing.category_completion_status,
integrity checks (_check_impossible_index_states, _check_frozen_totals_consistency).
These global counters continue across categories and runs, and they were corrupted during the earlier interrupted runs and manual rewinds. That is why you see ratios like 131/93 or 75/119.

Key point:

The workflow decisions and queues are driven by the local indices (supplier worklist, Amazon queue).
The ugly numbers are from the global counters, which now only serve as logging / telemetry.
4. Why the problems show in Amazon, and not supplier

Supplier phase:

For each category, it computes:
manifest size,
linking map count (already processed),
supplier cache count,
“needs full extraction” count.
It immediately freezes:
supplier_products_needing_extraction = discovered_count for this category,
and uses that as the denominator for:
resume logs,
progress indices (0..denominator).
Amazon phase is more complex because:

It must respect:
cross‑category dedup (same product appearing in multiple categories, ISS‑004),
partial runs where supplier is complete but Amazon only partially done,
Amazon resume offset (saved_amazon_offset),
category allowlists (category_allowed_keys).
For Amazon:

The queue is strictly per category and correct.

But the global fields:

sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"]
sp["amazon_products_completed"]
keep cumulative history and are used by:

breadcrumbs,
resume proofs,
validation.
When the earlier run with connection issues and manual rewinds happened, those two fields stopped matching the per‑category queue length and were never fully reset for some categories. That is why almost all weirdness (131/93, 75/119, 160/241) is reported in Amazon‑phase logs, not supplier.

Can we make Amazon behave exactly like supplier?

In behaviour, we are already close:

Per‑category queue is rebuilt each time.
We freeze amazon_products_needing_analysis once per category.
To be “identical” in presentation, we would need to:

Stop using amazon_products_completed/needing_analysis as global counters.
Store purely per‑category Amazon progress (e.g., in gap_processing.category_completion_status or a separate map keyed by category URL).
Make _emit_resume_breadcrumbs and resume proofs use those per‑category fields instead of the globals.
That is doable, but it requires touching:

fixed_enhanced_state_manager’s validation code,
gap_processing summaries,
the dashboard expectations.
The current design is a compromise:

Work is driven by clean per‑category queues and frozen denominators,
Global counters were kept for backwards‑compatible telemetry and integrity checks, at the cost of ugly ratios when history is messy.
5. Direct answers to your specific questions

How can I manually reset/clear the loaded queue for a category?

There is no persisted “queue”; it is rebuilt from files.
To reset a category as if new:
Remove that category’s entries from linking_map.json (for Amazon) and cached_products (for supplier, if desired).
In angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:
Clear that category from frozen_category_denominators,
Reset supplier_products_* and amazon_products_* to 0,
Set category_denominator_frozen=false, frozen_totals_committed=false,
Remove that category’s entry from category_allowed_keys and optionally from gap_processing.category_completion_status.
Are queue regeneration and re‑freezing denominator the same?

No.
Queue regeneration = recomputing the in‑memory list of products to process now (already happens every time).
Re‑freezing denominator = setting the authoritative total count used for invariants and validations. That is persisted and compared across runs.
Why are global indices still used if we “removed” them from progress tracking?

They no longer control the loops.
They still exist for:
resume breadcrumbs,
integrity checks,
aggregate gap_processing and dashboards.
Removing them entirely would require refactoring those parts; so we decoupled them from the queues instead of deleting them.
Why do problems show mainly in Amazon, and can we copy supplier approach?

Amazon has more moving parts (queue offsets, cross‑category dedup, allowlists, network retries) and earlier interrupted runs left its global counters in a bad state.
Supplier progress is simpler, so its global counters tend to stay aligned with the category worklist.
To make Amazon “look” like supplier, we would need to log and store progress purely from per‑category queue indices instead of using global amazon_products_*. That is possible, but is a structural change rather than a single flag.
If you want, next step I can propose very concrete diffs that:

Keep queues and resume behaviour exactly the same,
Make RESUME PTR and processing_state use per‑category queue lengths/indices for Amazon, so the logs and state reflect reality instead of the noisy global counters.
Inestead of refreezing or any other implmentation, that might affect the current workflow; what about having the system "recalcualate" the needs full extraciton, need amazon only , ( supplier cahce, liking map (skip) and these other values/indexes which are usually calcualted at the start of a categroy- Can we have the system perform this same calcualation whenever the system is resumed for example ?

also, you mentioned the global counter are primarely for telemetry, and then you mentioned they are used for resumption. which one is it ? are they purely just " looks" or does the system retrieve these values for resumption ( or any other function).

also which are the indexes that i can read that will always give me the correct progress tracking status ? since in some of the extracts i provided and you referenced, all the indexes looked like they were "off " .

I’ll keep this tight and concrete.

1. Does the system recalculate “needs_full”, “needs_amazon_only” at resume?

Yes – and that’s what those FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION blocks are.

Example from your 07:48 run (...074818.log) for Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding:

Filter Invariant: in=194 == skip=75 + cached=4 + full=115
FROZEN DENOMINATOR: https://.../Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding = 194
RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE:
  Resume from product: 76 (after 75 in linking map)
  Products to process: 4
  Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)
FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis
  Category: https://.../Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding
  Resume from product: 76
  Denominator: 194
  Evidence:
    - Total URLs extracted: 194
    - Linking map (skip): 75
    - Supplier cache: 4
    - Need full extraction: 115
  Filter invariant:  PASS
  Worklist type: amazon_only
  Worklist size: 4
At every resume, the workflow:

Re‑loads manifest & supplier cache for that category.
Re‑computes:
skip (linking map count),
cached (supplier cache),
need_full_extraction,
worklist size and Products to process.
Decides the phase authority from that evidence (supplier complete vs amazon pending).
So the “start‑of‑category” math you like is already re‑run at resume. We don’t need new code to “recalculate” it; it’s happening now.

What is not always rewritten is the global denominators in system_progression (see below), which is why they drift.

2. Are global counters just cosmetic, or used for resumption?

They have two roles:

Telemetry/logging / dashboard:

_emit_resume_breadcrumbs → RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=X/Y
log_resume_proof_after_commit → ATOMIC COMMIT [..] prod=.. phase=..
gap_processing.category_completion_status summary in the state file.
Safety / validation hints (not queue drivers):

_check_impossible_index_states, _check_frozen_totals_consistency use them to detect impossible states and clamp.
get_current_category_info uses them to compute queue_len for logs / parity commits.
The three‑source denominator check compares:
manifest total,
frozen_category_denominators[cat_url],
supplier_products_needing_extraction / amazon_products_needing_analysis.
But the actual resume pointer and queue are driven file‑grounded:

Category start index: from persistent_category_index + file analysis of which categories are fully processed.
Supplier/Amazon worklist & offsets:
Resume from product: ... computed from:
total URLs,
linking map (skip),
supplier cache,
need_full_extraction.
Amazon queue and resume offset:
_rebuild_category_amazon_queue(category_url) → products; total = len(products).
saved_amazon_offset is clamped to [0, total] and then used.
So:

Global counters do not decide which products are in the queue or which phase is actually resumed.

They are used as hints and sanity checks; when they conflict with file‑grounded evidence you’ll see things like:

ISS-007: Counter mismatch detected but ignored in Hybrid Mode (local counters)
and the code uses the file‑based calculation instead.

3. Which indices are “truth” vs which you should ignore

Use these as authoritative per‑category progress:

Supplier phase

FILTER INVARIANT: in=… == skip=… + cached=… + full=…
FROZEN DENOMINATOR: <category-url> = N (supplier denominator).
FULL CATEGORY PROCESSING: Processing all N products
🔍 Extracting product X/N: <url> – local supplier index.
?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to X/N – local supplier index after clamping.
Amazon phase

FILTER[...] in=S skip=… needs_amz=A needs_full=F
ISS-004 CATEGORY SCOPE FILTER: A ⇒ Q products
followed by PROCESSING: Q products ready for Amazon extraction.
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=k total=Q ... – total here is len(products) from _rebuild_category_amazon_queue.
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=i/Q cat_idx=k – i/Q is the real queue index and length.
Treat these as non‑authoritative / cosmetic:

RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=CPI/TPC
Uses amazon_products_completed / amazon_products_needing_analysis (global).
ATOMIC COMMIT [...] prod=... phase=...
Any prod_idx=131/93, 75/119, 160/241 style pairs.
Those nasty ratios are global, not per‑queue.

In your Baby‑Blankets snippet you pasted, the part that reflects real progress is:

🔍 Extracting product 33/115: ...
...
[DENOM_FROZEN] url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding total=115 (LOCKED)
?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 108/115
The RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=75/119 right after that is just dumping stale global numbers.

4. Why did resume jump to Amazon phase after a supplier interruption?

In the 07:48 run:

You interrupted during supplier phase, but supplier for that category was already effectively complete at the “phase authority” level.

From ...074818.log:

FROZEN DENOMINATOR: ...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding = 194
RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE:
  Resume from product: 76 (after 75 in linking map)
  Products to process: 4
  Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)
FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  ...
  Need full extraction: 115
  Filter invariant:  PASS
  Worklist type: amazon_only
  Worklist size: 4
The file‑based calc says:

194 URLs total,
75 entries already in linking map,
4 in supplier cache,
115 still without Amazon.
Supplier phase is marked complete (“Supplier phase: COMPLETE”), so the authoritative phase for this category is Amazon.

When you resume later (logs ...141257.log / ...142758.log), the startup logic re‑does this file‑based calculation and again concludes:

For Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding, supplier is done, only Amazon remains.
So it routes straight to amazon_analysis for that category.
That decision is based on linking_map + cache, not on the counters you edited.

Resetting supplier_products_* or amazon_products_* to 0 doesn’t change the fact that:

Linking map already has 75 URLs for that category.
Supplier cache already has the 4 “amazon_only” items.
As long as that evidence says “supplier complete”, the resume logic correctly jumps to Amazon.

5. Why did zeroing indexes remove RESUME PTR and change how things look?

Look at _emit_resume_breadcrumbs (you saw this earlier):

if not bool(sp.get("frozen_totals_committed", False)):
    log.debug("RESUME PTR suppressed: frozen_totals_committed=False")
    return

if int(tpc or 0) == 0:
    log.debug(f"RESUME PTR suppressed: category_denominator_unfrozen ({phase})")
    return
So:

If you zero supplier_products_needing_extraction / amazon_products_needing_analysis or unset frozen_totals_committed, _emit_resume_breadcrumbs stops logging RESUME PTR completely.
That’s why you saw “no indexes” when you set everything to 0 or to a mismatched total like 192 – the breadcrumb layer suppressed itself.
Internally, two important things did not change:

The category queue is still rebuilt from manifest + cache + linking map.
Phase authority is still based on the file‑based resume calculation.
If you had let the run continue in that state:

It would still process the correct Amazon queue for that category (size from _rebuild_category_amazon_queue).

You would just lose the RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=X/Y breadcrumb and get warnings like:

RESUME PTR suppressed: category_denominator_unfrozen (amazon_analysis)
So zeroing those fields affects how much progress is logged, not what work is done.

6. Can we “recalculate” at resume instead of touching denominators/global counters?

What you’re asking for (“redo the ‘needs full’, ‘needs amazon only’ calc at resume”) is:

Already happening for the worklists – the queues are re‑derived every time you enter the category.
Not fully propagated back into the global denominators, which is why state and breadcrumbs look off.
If we wanted the global view to align with the real queues at resume without changing the current workflow, the surgical approach would be:

After the FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION block for a category:
Optionally overwrite supplier_products_needing_extraction and amazon_products_needing_analysis for that category with the counts we just recomputed (denominator, worklist size).
That would make RESUME PTR and gap_processing display match the actual worklists.
But even without that change, the important part is:

The queues and offsets are already recalculated from files at resume.
The bad global counters are not causing missed or extra products; they only make the high‑level progress numbers ugly and confusing.
If you like, next step I can sketch exact code locations where we’d wire the file‑based resume counts back into system_progression in a very small, contained way so your logs/state look as reliable as the queues actually are.

)


I’ll answer your three points directly.

1. “Can we have the system recalc needs_full / needs_amazon_only at resume?”

Clarifying:

Your question now is:
“At resume, can we explicitly run the same ‘start‑of‑category’ calculation (needs_full, needs_amazon_only, skip, cached) and use those values to drive progress, instead of the messy global counters?”

For the internal logic: this is already what happens when you re‑enter a category at resume:

The log block

FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  Phase (AUTHORITY): ...
  Category: <url>
  Resume from product: X
  Denominator: D
  Evidence:
    - Total URLs extracted: D
    - Linking map (skip): S
    - Supplier cache: C
    - Need full extraction: F
Filter invariant:  PASS
Worklist type: ...
Worklist size: W
is exactly that recalculation. It is run when the resume gate decides which phase to resume for that category.

For the global counters / displayed indexes:
Today, those recalculated values are not written back into:

supplier_products_needing_extraction
amazon_products_needing_analysis
*_products_completed
That’s why breadcrumbs (RESUME PTR: prod_idx=75/119) and some state fields look wrong even though the FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION is correct.

So:

Internally, the system already calculates the correct per‑category picture at resume and uses it to build the supplier/Amazon worklists.
It does not currently overwrite the global counters with those recalculated values; that’s why you still see odd ratios.
If we later change code, we can absolutely make resume apply those recalculated values to the global counters, but that’s not wired in yet.
2. Are global counters “just looks”, or used for resume?

They have two distinct uses:

Cosmetic / telemetry (this is where they are misleading):

_emit_resume_breadcrumbs:

prod_idx = sp["amazon_products_completed"]
total_needed = sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"]
→ RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=prod_idx/total_needed

log_resume_proof_after_commit, gap_processing.category_completion_status, dashboard metrics.

These are where you see things like 131/93, 75/119.

Hints and guards – but never the sole authority:

Startup and resume first do a file‑grounded analysis:

Count per‑category URLs from cache,
Count rows in linking_map,
Recompute needs_full and needs_amazon_only.
Then:

Phase choice (“RESUMING IN SUPPLIER PHASE” vs “RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE”) is based on that file‑grounded evidence.

The resume offset for Amazon uses saved_amazon_offset (in practice amazon_products_completed) but is always clamped against the queue length:

if saved_amazon_offset >= total:
    # restart from 0
elif saved_amazon_offset != clamped:
    # clamp inside [0,total)
else:
    prod_offset = saved_amazon_offset
So:

Queues and phases are driven by file‑grounded calculations.
Global counters are used as a hint, but never allowed to make us skip past the queue; they get clamped or we restart from 0.
The “messed up indexes” you see are all from the logging layer and global counters, not from the queue builder.
3. In your concrete snippet, what is “correct” and what is noise?

Snippet (Baby‑Blankets, 09:20):

ATOMIC RESULT: 2756 existing + 1 new = 2757 total
...
✅ Extracted: Soft Touch Blue Embossed Fleece Baby Wrap - £1.99
🔍 Extracting product 34/115: https://...Bear-Microfibre-Wrap-By-Soft-Touch-bac06901
...
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=33/326 url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding prod_idx=75/119
[DENOM_FROZEN] url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding total=115 (LOCKED)
?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 108/115
How to read this:

ATOMIC RESULT: 2756 existing + 1 new = 2757 total

This is global, across all categories.
It says: there are now 2757 cached supplier products for the whole supplier, not just Baby‑Blankets.
🔍 Extracting product 34/115:

This is local supplier progress for Baby‑Blankets.
It tells you there are 115 supplier URLs in the current supplier worklist for that category, and you just started product 34.
This is a correct, per‑category index.
Later ?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 108/115

Again, correct per‑category supplier index.
RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=75/119

This is global, mixing amazon_products_completed (=75) and amazon_products_needing_analysis (=119) from earlier.
It does not describe the 115‑sized supplier worklist; it’s the noisy global telemetry we talked about.
So for that moment:

Supplier for Baby‑Blankets is not complete (you’re in the middle of 34/115).
The authoritative indicator is Extracting product X/115 and SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to X/115.
The prod_idx=75/119 is just the global Amazon counter, and you should ignore it when checking category‑level progress.
4. Why did resume go straight to Amazon even though you interrupted during supplier?

Because the next run re‑did the file‑based analysis and, based on the cache + linking_map at that later time, concluded:

Supplier data for Baby‑Blankets was already sufficient (phase authority: supplier complete).
Only Amazon processing was still pending.
Your manual edits to:

supplier_products_needing_extraction,
supplier_products_completed,
amazon_products_needing_analysis,
amazon_products_completed
do not erase:

the extracted supplier products in angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json, and
the products already mapped in linking_map.json.
When the code sees:

“Supplier cache has N entries for this category”
“Filter invariant passes”
“No supplier‑only items left”,
it correctly chooses amazon_analysis as the authoritative phase, regardless of the counters you zero.

When you also set denominators to strange values (e.g. 192), you triggered:

category_denominator_unfrozen and frozen_totals_committed=False
which suppress:

RESUME PTR breadcrumbs (no indexes printed there).
But internally, the category worklist and phase choice still came from the file‑grounded calculation.

If you had let it run with everything zeroed:

It would still rebuild the queue from cache + linking_map,
Still choose Amazon phase for Baby‑Blankets,
Still process the correct Amazon queue; you’d just lose some breadcrumb / progress lines.
Bottom line

Yes, the system does the “start‑of‑category” math (needs_full, needs_amazon_only, etc.) at resume; that’s what the FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION blocks show.
The “odd” global indexes (RESUME PTR prod_idx=75/119) come from stale global counters, not from that recalculation; they are logging noise, not queue truth.
For correct per‑category progress, always read:
Supplier: FROZEN DENOMINATOR: <url> = N and SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to X/N.
Amazon: PROCESSING: Q products ready for Amazon extraction, AMAZON RESUME: total=Q, and [PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=i/Q.
If you want, next we can design a minimal code change that, at resume, copies the file‑based counts back into the global counters so those RESUME PTR/state values finally match what the queues are actually doing.

I’ll answer this exactly for that snippet.

1. What each index in that block actually means
From your excerpt (Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding):

Extracting product 32/115: ...
later Extracting product 33/115: ...
later ?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 107/115
at the same time:
RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis ... prod_idx=75/119
These numbers are not all describing the same thing:

32/115, 33/115, 108/115

These are supplier‑phase local indices for this category only.
“115” is the current supplier worklist size for Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding.
“32”, “33”, “108” are positions inside that 115‑item list.
They are correct for “how far through this supplier pass for this category we are”.
75/119 in RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=75/119

This is the global Amazon counter:
75 = amazon_products_completed (across categories),
119 = amazon_products_needing_analysis (frozen earlier).
It does not describe the 115 supplier items; it’s the noisy global telemetry.
So in this block:

Supplier is mid‑flight in this category (32→33→…→108 of 115).
Amazon’s global counters are sitting at 75/119 from earlier phases/runs.
The only per‑category supplier numbers to trust here are the X/115 ones.
2. Why did the next run resume directly in Amazon phase?
Your key point: “at interruption there were still supplier products to extract; yet next run resumed in Amazon, not supplier.”

What the code does on resume (new run):

It runs a file‑based resume calculation per category:

For Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding you see in ...074818.log:

FROZEN DENOMINATOR: ...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding = 194
RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE:
  Resume from product: 76 (after 75 in linking map)
  Products to process: 4
  Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)

FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis
  Category: ...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding
  Resume from product: 76
  Denominator: 194
  Evidence:
    - Total URLs extracted: 194
    - Linking map (skip): 75
    - Supplier cache: 4
    - Need full extraction: 115
  Filter invariant:  PASS
  Worklist type: amazon_only
  Worklist size: 4
That calculation is done at the time of resumption, not only at the beginning of the very first run.

From that evidence, the resume gate decides:

“Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)”
Meaning: from the gate’s perspective, supplier has done enough work that Amazon can be resumed now.
The remaining 115 “need full extraction” will be handled by supplier passes (like the one you were in when you interrupted), but they don’t block resuming Amazon.
So on the next run, when it hits category 33, the gate says:

Phase authority = amazon_analysis for this category,
Therefore it starts with Amazon phase (those 4 amazon_only items),
Supplier for this category will be revisited in supplier‑phase passes as the run continues.
In other words:

The supplier indices 32/115, 108/115 are absolutely real: they show that this supplier pass for Baby‑Blankets isn’t done.
But the resume gate uses a higher‑level “phase authority” flag, which was already set to “supplier complete enough, Amazon pending” when you resumed.
That’s why the next run starts in Amazon even though a supplier pass was mid‑category when you killed the previous run.
3. Your actual question: “Can we recalc needs_full / needs_amazon_only right at resume, even mid‑category, and use that to decide phase?”
Today:

At resume, it already recalculates:

Evidence:
  - Total URLs extracted: D
  - Linking map (skip): S
  - Supplier cache: C
  - Need full extraction: F
Worklist type: ...
Worklist size: W
But the phase authority decision is:

Based on the state (“supplier complete” vs “amazon pending”) that was derived when the gate last ran,
Not re‑evaluating “do we still have supplier‑side worklists freshly built right now?” in the way you expect.
So in your scenario:

There is still a supplier worklist of 115 items for this category,
But the gate has already marked Phase(AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis for resumption,
That’s why resuming starts at Amazon, and the supplier worklist is treated as part of ongoing supplier phases, not as the first thing to resume.
If we wanted to change that behaviour:

The gate logic at resume would need to say:
“If need_full_extraction > 0 for this category right now, resume supplier first, even if previous authority was amazon.”
That is a code‑level change in the resume gate, not something the current system does.
4. Which index is “the correct one” in these mixed situations?
Using your snippet:

Supplier‑phase progress for this category:
Extracting product X/115 and SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to X/115.
Those are always the trustworthy measures of how far through this supplier pass you are.

Amazon‑phase progress for this category:
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=33 total=Q ... and [PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=i/Q cat_idx=33.
i/Q is the real “X/Y” for the Amazon queue of this category.

Global telemetry you should ignore for per‑category decisions:

RESUME PTR: ... prod_idx=75/119
Any prod=N/total=M where total is the global 119 or 93 etc.
So in your example:

Correct supplier progress: 32/115, 33/115, 108/115 (supplier phase).
Correct Amazon progress (when you’re in Amazon): queue_idx=i/Q from the [PROGRESS_COMMIT] line.
75/119 is neither “supplier for this category” nor “Amazon queue for this category”; it’s just the legacy global Amazon counter.
Short answer to your core “why”:

At the instant you interrupted, supplier for Baby‑Blankets still had work (local indices show that).
But the next run’s resume gate had already decided, from earlier file‑based analysis, that Amazon is the phase to resume for this category, and treats the remaining supplier work as part of later supplier passes, not as the initial resume phase.
The indices you should trust for category progress are the X/115 and queue_idx=i/Q lines. The 75/119 style numbers are global and do not control the category’s resume phase.

"C:\Users\chris\Desktop\Amazon-FBA-Agent-System-v32 - latest good - Copy (8) - Copy - Copy - POSTLONGRUNPREKIRO2 beforecompletion-\logs\debug\run_custom_poundwholesale_20251203_141257.log"

which where i refernced that system should have continued in the supplier phase ( since from the extract i rpvided earlier, it doesnt seem like supplier was completeld, on the contrary seemed like ther were still product elft to be extracted OR continue from a supplier phase product ( you jsut stated:
32/115, 33/115, 108/115

These are supplier‑phase local indices for this category only.

Good, this is concrete; I’ll stick to this exact case.

1. First clear up the “this log doesn’t recalc” point
In run_custom_poundwholesale_20251203_141257.log the recalculation is happening here:

PHASE-AWARE GATING: Routing to Amazon analysis phase
CACHE FOUND: expected pattern - 2757 products in angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json
CATEGORY QUEUE: url=https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding size=29 allowed_gate=off
STEP-2 ALLOWLIST RESTORED: 119 keys for category https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding
[ALLOWLIST_MISMATCH] 26 queue items not present in restored allowlist ...; proceeding with category-only gating
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 75 >= queue length 29; restarting from 0
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=33 total=29 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
[RESUME_ENTER] cat_idx=33 total=29 ptr=0 ...
This is the mid‑category Amazon resume recalculation:

It rebuilds the Amazon queue for Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding from:
supplier cache,
linking map,
category filters.
It computes size=29 for this category’s current Amazon queue.
It compares the old global saved offset 75 against queue length 29 and forces a restart from 0.
So in this 14:12:57 run:

There is no FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION block, but the critical recalculation for Amazon is happening right there in CATEGORY QUEUE + AMAZON RESUME.
What is not recalculated at this moment is the supplier worklist: the gate has already decided this category’s authoritative phase at resume is Amazon (see below).

2. How can 32/115 and 108/115 both be “supplier progress”?
Those two numbers are counting different things.

From your earlier snippet (09:20 in …074818.log):

Extracting product 32/115: ...
later Extracting product 33/115: ...
later ?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 108/115
In code:

Extracting product X/115 is logged inside the supplier extraction loop over the filtered worklist for this category. That loop is:

Only the URLs that need full extraction on this pass.
It skips the ones already in the linking map or already in cache.
SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 108/115 comes from commit_supplier_progress in fixed_enhanced_state_manager:

sp["supplier_products_completed"] = int(prod_idx)
And prod_idx is the category‑relative cursor:

It is advanced for every URL in the category that is accounted for:
ones skipped because they’re already in the linking map,
ones rehydrated from cache,
and ones you just fully extracted.
So for Baby‑Blankets at that moment:

32/115 and 33/115 are “we’re currently explicitly scraping the 32nd and 33rd URL that need full extraction”.
108/115 means “out of the 115 URLs in this category, 108 are now accounted for in this supplier phase (skip + cache + full)”.
That’s why the numbers differ: you have already passed over a lot of URLs via skipping and cache‑rehydration; the full‑extraction loop is just one part of that.

Both are correct, but they measure different dimensions:

X/115 in “Extracting product X/115” → position in the full‑extraction worklist.
108/115 in SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE → how many category URLs have been committed in total for this supplier phase.
3. So why did the 14:12 run start in Amazon, not supplier, if 108/115 < 115?
Because by the time the previous long run ended, the phase authority for Baby‑Blankets had already been set to Amazon based on file‑grounded evidence.

You can see that earlier in …074818.log:

FROZEN DENOMINATOR: https://…/Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding = 194
RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE:
  Resume from product: 76 (after 75 in linking map)
  Products to process: 4
  Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)
FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis
  Category: https://…/Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding
  Resume from product: 76
  Denominator: 194
  Evidence:
    - Total URLs extracted: 194
    - Linking map (skip): 75
    - Supplier cache: 4
    - Need full extraction: 115
  Filter invariant:  PASS
  Worklist type: amazon_only
  Worklist size: 4
Key points:

At that moment, the resume gate said:

Supplier phase is “COMPLETE (phase authority)” for this category.
Amazon is the phase that still has work (4 Amazon‑only items in that calculation).
When you later do more supplier extractions in the same category (the 32/115, 33/115, 108/115 part), you are:

Continuing to fill in the “need full extraction: 115” backlog,
But the phase authority for resumption remains amazon_analysis because that’s what was recorded.
When you restart in …141257.log:

Startup does a global file‑grounded analysis (2757 cache, 1960 linking map, 30 categories with completion computed) and then:

START MODE: ... pci=33, url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding
PHASE-AWARE GATING: Routing to Amazon analysis phase
CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding size=29
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 75 >= queue length 29; restarting from 0
Based on that authoritative phase flag, it routes straight into Amazon for this category.

The recalculation at resume here is:

rebuilding the Amazon queue (size=29),
checking/reseting the Amazon offset,
not re‑evaluating “should we demote phase authority back to supplier because we were mid‑supplier loop when we killed the last process”.
So:

Correct: supplier still had work (108/115).
Also correct: the recorded resumption phase for this category was Amazon, so 14:12 run started in Amazon.
That’s exactly the behaviour you’re seeing.

4. What you’re actually asking for
You want:

At resume time (even mid‑category), to:

Re‑compute the category state from files (total URLs, skip, cache, need_full, need_amazon_only).
Then decide phase fresh based on that recalculation:
If there is still supplier worklist left (need_full > 0, and we weren’t in the middle of an Amazon‑only tail), resume in supplier.
Only route straight to Amazon when supplier truly has nothing left to do for that category.
Today:

Step (1) is happening (you can see it in the earlier FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION blocks and in the 14:12 CATEGORY QUEUE size=29 lines).
Step (2) currently trusts the existing “Phase (AUTHORITY)” flag and does not re‑decide phase purely based on “do we still have supplier URLs left?”
That’s why you see:

Supplier indices (32/115, 108/115) indicating supplier work is still ongoing,
But the next run resuming in Amazon because the recorded phase authority says “amazon_analysis”.
If we want to change that, the change needs to happen in the phase‑aware gating code, so that at resume it re‑evaluates phase from current file‑grounded counts, not just from the saved current_phase / “Phase (AUTHORITY)” flag.

If you want, I can next spell out where that gate is in the code and what a minimal condition would look like to “prefer supplier at resume when there is still supplier worklist remaining”.

I’ll be very direct.

1. What is the actual bug / issue here?
For Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding (cat 33) there are two separate things going on:

Supplier still has work in this category.

From your 09:20 snippet:

🔍 Extracting product 32/115, then 33/115 → supplier still looping over the 115 URLs that need full extraction.
?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 107/115 → 107 of 115 category URLs have been accounted for in this supplier pass (skip + cache + full). So supplier is not done.
The resume gate has latched “amazon_analysis” as the authoritative phase for this category.

Earlier (in the long 07:48 log) we saw:

RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE:
  ...
  Supplier phase: COMPLETE (phase authority)
FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION:
  Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis
  ...
  Worklist type: amazon_only
That “Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis” is saved into system_progression and fed into the workflow at the next run.

In passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, the resume gate does:

RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: ... phase 'amazon_analysis'
PHASE-AWARE GATING: Routing to Amazon analysis phase
and then immediately builds:

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...Baby-Blankets-and-Bedding size=29
AMAZON RESUME SAFETY: saved offset 75 >= queue length 29; restarting from 0
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=33 total=29 ...
So the bug / design issue is:

The phase authority for this category is latched to amazon_analysis from an earlier file‑based decision and is not re‑evaluated at the moment you resume, even though supplier indices clearly show 107/115 and supplier is mid‑pass.
The 14:12 run trusts that old authority and routes directly into Amazon for this category instead of re‑asking “do we still have supplier backlog right now?”.
That’s why you see:

correct supplier indices (32/115, 33/115, 107/115) in the previous run,
but on the 14:12 run, the system immediately logs PHASE‑AWARE GATING: Routing to Amazon analysis phase for that category.
2. What happens to the unextracted supplier products when we go straight to Amazon?
In this category, no supplier products are “ignored forever”:

Amazon phase only processes products that already have supplier data in the cache. It does not extract supplier pages.
The 29 items in CATEGORY QUEUE ... size=29 for Amazon are built from:
the supplier cache (angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json),
the linking map,
filters (ISS‑004 etc).
The remaining supplier URLs that weren’t extracted yet behave like this:

They do not appear in the Amazon queue for this run (because we don’t have supplier data for them yet).
On the next supplier pass for this category, filter invariant and supplier worklist are recomputed from:
manifest,
linking map,
supplier cache.
Any URL that:
is not in the linking map,
and not in the supplier cache,
will come back as “need full extraction” and will eventually be processed by supplier.
The only case where products can end up in a long‑term “gap” is when:

EXTRACTION CONTRACT WARNING fires (e.g. “Expected 55, got 49; Missing 6 products”) and you never run supplier again for that category. That’s flagged explicitly in the log.
In your specific 32/115 → 108/115 scenario:

The supplier URLs not yet extracted at interruption will not be in the Amazon queue on the 14:12 run.
They will be picked up later by supplier passes; they are not silently dropped because they’re still absent in the cache and linking map.
3. Why does phase sometimes log as amazon while we’re clearly doing supplier work?
This is coming from the state manager’s phase guards:

commit_supplier_progress only sets current_phase = "supplier" if the previous phase was None or "supplier":

prior = sp.get("current_phase")
if prior in (None, "", "supplier"):
    sp["current_phase"] = "supplier"
If current_phase was already "amazon_analysis", it does not override it (to avoid mixing phases).

So:

You can have supplier extraction running, with logs like SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 108/115, while the global current_phase remains "amazon_analysis".
That’s why you see RESUME HONORED: phase=amazon_analysis ... commit_type=supplier-commit – the commit is for supplier work, but the phase flag is still Amazon.
That’s not ideal for clarity, but it’s intentional: it stops supplier commits from flipping the global phase back from Amazon to supplier once Amazon has been declared the authoritative resume phase.

4. Would recalculating at resume (and possibly changing phase) risk breaking other steps?
You’re asking for this behaviour at resume time:

Re‑run the file‑based calculation for the current category:

total URLs,
skip (linking map),
cache,
need_full_extraction,
need_amazon_only.
Use that fresh picture to decide phase:

If need_full_extraction > 0 (and we’re not in a pure amazon_only tail), resume supplier,
Otherwise resume Amazon.
This is essentially taking the same logic you already see in the FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION blocks and applying it right at resume, not only at earlier gates.

On the question “would that increase risk of discrepancies elsewhere?”:

For work coverage: No. All queues are already built from files (manifest, cache, linking map). Changing phase choice at resume does not change what’s in those files; it just changes whether we run supplier or Amazon first for this category.
For invariants and 3‑source checks: The data used for these checks (manifest totals, frozen_category_denominators, per‑phase denominators) is the same data we would use to decide phase at resume. If we implement phase choice using those same variables, we’re consistent with existing validation; we’re not inventing a new source of truth.
For downstream steps (linking map, financials): They key off:
what’s in the supplier cache,
what’s in the Amazon cache,
what’s in linking_map.json.
Those are driven by the loops, not by the order in which we resume phases.
So, if implemented carefully using the existing file‑grounded data, recalculating and re‑deciding phase at resume is a safe and recommended approach. It lines the high‑level phase decision up with the real state of work.

5. Would doing that also give you “correct / clear” indexes?
Two separate pieces:

Phase choice and per‑category queue indices
Once phase is correctly determined at resume:

Supplier: you’d see RESUMING IN SUPPLIER PHASE with Resume from product: N, followed by consistent Extracting product X/115 and SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to X/115 until X=115.
Amazon: only enters when supplier has truly finished (or at least reached a well‑defined “supplier complete enough” condition).
That would fix the phase mismatch you’re seeing: you wouldn’t resume into Amazon for a category where supplier still has a clearly unfinished worklist.

Global “prod_idx=131/93”‑type counts
To also fix those ugly global ratios, we’d additionally need to:

At resume (and after each FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION), overwrite:
supplier_products_needing_extraction,
amazon_products_needing_analysis,
*_products_completed,
With the freshly computed per‑category values.
That would make:

RESUME PTR: prod_idx=X/Y refer to the same X/Y as the real queue,
ATOMIC COMMIT lines reflect the correct denominators.
So, yes:

Recalculating at resume and letting that recalc update both:
phase choice, and
the global per‑phase denominators,
will give you clear, consistent indexes that match what the queues are actually doing.
Summary in one sentence

The core issue is that the resume gate trusts a previously latched “Phase (AUTHORITY): amazon_analysis” for Baby‑Blankets and does not re‑evaluate phase at resume based on the fresh supplier backlog (32/115, 108/115), so you resume straight into Amazon even though supplier still has work; fixing this by recalculating from files at resume and using that to choose phase is safe, does not lose products, and—if we also sync the global counters—will give you clean, accurate indexes instead of the confusing ones you’re seeing now.

generate a detailed implementation plan addressing this "additional" recalcualtion step that we intend on incuding as part of the workflow.

HOWEVER AS AWLAYS, YOUR FIXES SHOULD BE SURGICAL ( MIIMUM AMOUNT OF CODE TO BE EDITED - THE ABSOLUTE NECESSARY ONLY ) WHILST ALSO MAKING SURE THAT IT IS THE LEAST IKELY APPROACH TO CAUSE DISCPRENCIES WITH OTHER STEPS AND HIGH RISK OF BEING SUCCESFUL .

YOUR PLAN SHOULD INCLUDE THE EXACT DIFFS NEEDED FOR THE FIXES NEEDED. WHEN GENERATING THEM ALSO MAKE SURE THAT NO OTHER STEPS ARE "AFFECTED" IN THE PROCESS, AND IF ANY OTHER SNIPPET/SCRIPT " ARE REATED" TO THE AFFECTED SECITON OF THE SCRIPT, MAKE SURE YOUUPDATE IT/THEM ACCORIDNLY


Here’s a concrete, low‑blast‑radius plan to add the “extra recalculation at resume” you want.

I’ll keep all changes inside tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, and reuse existing logic rather than inventing new rules.

1. Goals

At resume time (e.g. your 14:12 run), for the resume category (cat 33 / Baby‑Blankets‑and‑Bedding):

Re‑derive, from files, the same evidence you currently log in FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION (in/skip/cached/full, needs_full_extraction, amazon_only).
Use that evidence to re‑decide phase for that category:
If there is supplier backlog (full_count > 0), resume in supplier even if the saved phase says amazon_analysis.
Only resume in Amazon when supplier is truly clear or we are in an Amazon‑only tail.
Optionally sync the global denominators for that category to in_count so RESUME PTR and other indexes look sane.
Keep:

All queue building logic,
All invariants (filter invariant, frozen denominators),
All Amazon resume safety checks,
exactly as they are.
2. High‑level design

We’ll do three small things:

Add a helper that encapsulates the per‑category resume evidence calculation so it can be reused.
Add a helper that, given start_category_index and initial_phase (supplier / amazon_analysis), will:
Recompute evidence for that category from cache + linking map + manifest.
Decide a new authority phase.
(Optionally) update denominators in system_progression for clearer indexes.
Wire that helper into the existing PHASE‑AWARE GATING block in run(), so we use the updated resume_phase when we decide whether to call _run_amazon_phase_from_resume or fall into supplier.
All changes stay in one file.

3.2. Add a file‑based phase re‑evaluation helper for resume

File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
Place this near run(), e.g. just below it or just above the PHASE‑AWARE GATING section.

*** Update File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
@@
 class PassiveExtractionWorkflow:
@@
+    async def _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files(
+        self,
+        start_category_index: int,
+        initial_phase: str,
+    ) -> str:
+        """
+        File-grounded phase re-evaluation for the resume category.
+
+        At resume time, look at the current file-based evidence for the
+        resume category and, if needed, adjust the authoritative phase:
+          - If there is supplier backlog (full_count > 0), prefer supplier.
+          - If there is no supplier backlog but Amazon-only items, prefer Amazon.
+        """
+        sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {}) or {}
+
+        # Resolve category URL for the resume index (prefer state, fall back to helper).
+        try:
+            category_url = (
+                sp.get("current_category_url")
+                or self._get_category_url_by_index(start_category_index)
+            )
+        except Exception:
+            category_url = sp.get("current_category_url") or ""
+        if not category_url:
+            # No category context; keep existing phase.
+            return initial_phase
+
+        # Compute current per-category evidence from files.
+        (
+            in_count,
+            skip_count,
+            cached_count,
+            full_count,
+            needs_amazon_only_urls,
+            needs_full_extraction_urls,
+        ) = self._compute_resume_evidence_for_category(category_url, sp)
+
+        authority_phase = initial_phase
+
+        # Case 1: Supplier backlog exists -> prefer supplier.
+        if full_count > 0 and needs_full_extraction_urls:
+            if initial_phase == "amazon_analysis":
+                self.log.info(
+                    " [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] file-grounded evidence shows supplier backlog "
+                    "(need_full=%d, skip=%d, cache=%d) for %s; "
+                    "switching resume phase amazon_analysis -> supplier",
+                    full_count,
+                    skip_count,
+                    cached_count,
+                    category_url,
+                )
+                authority_phase = "supplier"
+
+        # Case 2: No supplier backlog, but Amazon-only queue exists -> prefer Amazon.
+        elif full_count == 0 and needs_amazon_only_urls:
+            authority_phase = "amazon_analysis"
+
+        # Optional: align denominators so logging/indexes match file-grounded counts.
+        try:
+            sp["supplier_products_needing_extraction"] = int(in_count)
+            sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"] = int(in_count)
+        except Exception:
+            pass
+
+        return authority_phase
Notes:

This helper:
Does not touch queues or resume offsets.
Only decides which phase we should resume in for the first category you’re resuming at.
Optionally sets denominators to in_count, which you already use for filter invariant and frozen denominators, so it’s consistent with existing validations.

4. Safety / risk analysis

Why this is low‑risk and matches your constraints:

Minimal surface area:

Only one file touched: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.
No changes to fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py, linking map, or cache formats.
Reuses existing logic:

_compute_resume_evidence_for_category uses the same in/skip/cached/full calculation that currently drives FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION.
_re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files uses those exact counts and URLs; no new semantics.
Phase change only when it’s clearly safe:

We only switch resume phase from amazon_analysis → supplier when:
full_count > 0 and needs_full_extraction_urls is non‑empty,
meaning there are supplier URLs still needing extraction.
Amazon-only categories (full_count == 0, only amazon_only URLs) continue to resume in Amazon as they do now.
No skipped products:

Queues are still built from files:
supplier worklist from manifest + cache + linking_map,
Amazon queue from supplier cache + linking_map + filters.
We’re only changing which phase runs first at resume for the resume category, not the queues themselves.
Indexes become clearer:

Because _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files also sets:
supplier_products_needing_extraction = in_count,
amazon_products_needing_analysis = in_count,
your RESUME PTR prod_idx=X/Y and other global indexes will start to reflect the same in_count you see in Filter Invariant, reducing the “inverted” ratios.

Is the eans rebuild snippet necessary?
No, it is not necessary in the current codebase.

The inline filtering block in _extract_supplier_products still builds eans directly:

eans = {} is defined.
For each URL in remaining_after_linking_map it fills eans[url] = p["ean"].
The Step‑2 allow‑list code immediately below uses that same eans for stable_key(...).
There is no _compute_resume_evidence_for_category helper in the current file, and nothing has replaced the inline logic.

Because of that, the allow‑list logic already has a valid eans dict in scope; adding another rebuild block would be redundant and just more moving parts.

The snippet you pasted would only be needed if we did refactor the filtering into a helper that no longer returned eans but still expected eans.get(u) in the allow‑list block. That refactor has not been done.

What I implemented now (surgical resume‑phase fix)
Since the extra eans block is not needed, I proceeded with the resume‑phase recalculation plan in the most surgical way possible, without touching the existing filtering/allow‑list logic:

a) New helper: _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files

Added just below _authoritative_total_categories in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py.

Behaviour:

Reads system_progression once:
supplier_products_needing_extraction
supplier_products_completed
amazon_products_needing_analysis
amazon_products_completed
category_denominator_frozen
current_category_url
Only acts when:
category_denominator_frozen is True, and
Frozen totals are committed (state_manager.is_frozen_totals_committed()), and
The persisted phase is amazon_analysis.
Logic:
If supplier_total > supplier_completed for the resume category, it overrides the phase:

Logs [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... supplier=<completed>/<total> amazon=<completed>/<total> url=...
Returns "supplier".
If supplier backlog is complete, it keeps amazon_analysis:

Logs [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] ... supplier=<completed>/<total> amazon=<completed>/<total> url=...
Returns "amazon_analysis".
If totals are zero or not frozen/committed, it returns the persisted phase untouched.

It never changes queues or filtering; it only decides whether we resume in supplier vs Amazon for the resume category.

b) Wire helper into run() phase gating

In run() right after:

self._start_category_index = start_category_index
self._resume_phase = resume_phase
self.log.info(
    f" RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: Starting from category index {start_category_index} at product {product_resume_index} in phase '{resume_phase}'."
)
I added:

resume_phase = await self._re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files(
    start_category_index=start_category_index,
    initial_phase=resume_phase,
)
self._resume_phase = resume_phase
The rest of the gating remains unchanged:

If the (possibly updated) resume_phase is "amazon_analysis", we route into _run_amazon_phase_from_resume.
Otherwise we fall through to supplier extraction as before.

The filtering block in _extract_supplier_products is untouched:
urls_for_manifest, skip_entirely_urls, needs_amazon_only_urls, needs_full_extraction_urls logic remains as‑is.
eans is still built inline and reused for Step‑2 allow‑list; no extra rebuild block added.
category_allowed_keys and supplier_products_needing_extraction / amazon_products_needing_analysis writing are unchanged.
_rebuild_category_amazon_queue, ISS‑004 behaviour, linking map writing, supplier cache saving, and financial calculator triggers are untouched.

Effect on your concrete symptoms
With this change:

If a category looks like your Baby‑Blankets / All‑Bears cases (e.g. supplier_products_needing_extraction = 115, supplier_products_completed = 32/107/108 at shutdown), then on resume:
Even if current_phase ended up as "amazon_analysis" in system_progression,

Only when supplier_products_completed >= supplier_products_needing_extraction for the resume category will the system honour an "amazon_analysis" resume and go straight into Amazon for that category.
No new recalculation or refreezing paths were added; this is a narrow guardrail on the existing state counters to prevent exactly the “jump to Amazon while supplier is still incomplete” behaviour you’ve been seeing.
