Did the new resume‑phase logic run and override here?
No, in this specific state it stayed inactive by design, so there is no [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] / [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] line in the log.

From the current state (angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json):

"system_progression": {
  "current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
  "persistent_category_index": 38,
  "current_category_index": 38,
  "current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes",
  "category_denominator_frozen": false,
  "supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
  "supplier_products_completed": 5,
  "amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
  "amazon_products_completed": 1,
  "frozen_totals_committed": true,
  ...
}
The helper _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files(...) does:

Immediately returns initial_phase if category_denominator_frozen is False.
Only does the supplier‑backlog check (and logs [RESUME_PHASE_*]) when:
category_denominator_frozen == True,
frozen totals are committed, and
the persisted phase is amazon_analysis.
Here category_denominator_frozen is false, so the helper returns "amazon_analysis" unchanged and logs nothing. The implementation is present and called, but for category 38 it is intentionally a no‑op because the trusted, frozen per‑category denominator is not set yet.

Also note: the log you pointed at (run_custom_poundwholesale_20251204_023527.log) is for dkwholesale.com, not angelwholesale.co.uk, so that run would not show angel‑specific resume logs anyway.

Why these numbers: 88 / 93 and 5 / 1?
These four numbers are consistent with the filtering logic in _extract_supplier_products:

The pre‑extraction filter for a category computes three counts from urls_for_manifest:

skip_entirely_urls → products already in the linking map (fully done, both phases).
needs_amazon_only_urls → products present in the supplier cache (supplier done, Amazon still needed).
needs_full_extraction_urls → products that still need supplier extraction + Amazon analysis.
The resume logic then sets:

supplier_total = len(needs_full_extraction_urls)
amazon_total   = len(needs_amazon_only_urls) + supplier_total

if not sp["category_denominator_frozen"]:
    sp["supplier_products_needing_extraction"] = supplier_total
    sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"]     = amazon_total
    # completed counts left as‑is
It also runs a “fast‑forward” for completed supplier products when resuming in supplier phase:

total_skipped_supplier = skip_count + cached_count  # linking-map + cache
if persisted_phase == "supplier" and total_skipped_supplier > current_sup_completed:
    sp["supplier_products_completed"] = total_skipped_supplier
Given your state:

supplier_products_needing_extraction = 88
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 93
The only consistent interpretation is:

len(needs_full_extraction_urls) = 88
→ 88 products still require full supplier extraction (supplier backlog).
len(needs_amazon_only_urls) = 5
→ 5 products already have supplier data in the cache, but still need Amazon analysis.
So:

supplier_total = 88
amazon_total = 5 + 88 = 93
That matches the two “total” fields exactly.

The completed counters:

supplier_products_completed = 5

This is the fast‑forward result: 5 products in this category are already either in the linking map or cache (for supplier), so supplier completed was raised to 5 to avoid re‑extracting them on resume.

amazon_products_completed = 1

This means, for this category, 1 product from the 93‑item Amazon queue has already been processed during the current/previous runs.

So:

Supplier: 5 done out of 88 needing extraction (plus any fully skipped via linking‑map, which don’t show in this numerator because they aren’t “needing extraction” at all).
Amazon: 1 done out of 93 needing analysis.
The asymmetry (88 vs 93) is simply “full‑extraction backlog” vs “full‑extraction backlog + Amazon‑only backlog”, not a bug in itself.

Did the new resume‑phase override run for this angel run?
Yes, the new helper is being called, but for this run it returned early without changing the phase, so you don’t see any [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] / [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] lines.

Reason (from the state you pasted):

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 38,
"category_denominator_frozen": false,
"frozen_totals_committed": true,
"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
"supplier_products_completed": 5,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 1
The helper has this guard:

If category_denominator_frozen == False → return initial_phase immediately (no logs, no override).
For category 38 this flag is still false, so the helper exits immediately and leaves resume_phase = "amazon_analysis". That is why your log only shows:

RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: ... phase 'amazon_analysis'.
and nothing about [RESUME_PHASE_*] – the override is intentionally only active once the per‑category denominator has been frozen and committed.

Why 5 completed / 88 total (supplier) and 93 total (amazon)?
Those numbers come directly from the two‑step filter in _extract_supplier_products for that category:

Step 1 (linking map) and Step 2 (cache) partition the manifest URLs into:

skip_entirely_urls → already in linking map (fully done; never “needing extraction”).
needs_amazon_only_urls → in supplier cache, still need only Amazon.
needs_full_extraction_urls → still need supplier + Amazon.
From your state:

supplier_products_needing_extraction = 88
→ len(needs_full_extraction_urls) == 88
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 93
→ len(needs_amazon_only_urls) + len(needs_full_extraction_urls) == 93
so here len(needs_amazon_only_urls) == 5.
supplier_products_completed = 5 is the “fast‑forward” supplier count set when this category was last entered in supplier phase, based on:

total_skipped_supplier = skip_count + cached_count  # linking‑map + cache
sp["supplier_products_completed"] = total_skipped_supplier
For this category that worked out to 5 “supplier‑complete” items (all in cache/linking‑map).

amazon_products_completed = 1 means 1 of the 93 Amazon‑needed products has already been analyzed.

So, in plain terms for cat 38:

5 products are already supplier‑complete (cached / linking map).
88 still need supplier extraction.
Those same 88 plus the 5 cache‑only items (total 93) need Amazon analysis.
1 out of those 93 has been completed in Amazon so far.
Why didn’t the new recalculation override run for the other supplier (dkwholesale) you mentioned earlier?
Two separate things:

The re‑calculation of “needs_full”, “needs_amazon_only” etc. runs inside _extract_supplier_products each time a category is entered. That still happens for every supplier and every category; you see it in logs as:

Filter Invariant: in=... == skip=... + cached=... + full=...
RESUMING IN SUPPLIER PHASE / RESUMING IN AMAZON PHASE
FILE-BASED RESUME CALCULATION: ...
The new helper _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files only runs once at workflow startup, and only intervenes when:

category_denominator_frozen == True,
frozen totals are committed, and
persisted phase is "amazon_analysis".
In the dkwholesale run (run_custom_poundwholesale_20251204_023527.log):

It was effectively a fresh run for that supplier:
No frozen per‑category denominator yet (category_denominator_frozen is false / unset).
No meaningful supplier backlog recorded for a resume category.
Because those guards weren’t satisfied, the helper returned the persisted phase unchanged and never logged [RESUME_PHASE_*].
So in both the angel run you just showed and the earlier dkwholesale run:

The implementation is present and being called, but
It correctly chose not to override anything because the state didn’t yet meet the “safe to trust file‑based denominators” conditions (frozen denominator flag off).

Yes, the new helper is being called, but for this run it returned early without changing the phase, so you don’t see any [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] / [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] lines.


Why didn’t the new override run here? (“returned early”)
In the 05:22:21 angel run (run_custom_poundwholesale_20251204_052221.log), the flow is:

run() reads state:

RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: Starting from category index 38 at product 131 in phase 'amazon_analysis'.
Immediately after that, it calls:

resume_phase = await self._re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files(
    start_category_index=start_category_index,
    initial_phase=resume_phase,
)
Inside _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files the first guard is:

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {}) or {}

if not sp.get("category_denominator_frozen"):
    return initial_phase or "supplier"
In your state extract for this resume:

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 38,
...
"category_denominator_frozen": false,
"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
"supplier_products_completed": 5,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 1,
"frozen_totals_committed": true
category_denominator_frozen is false, so the helper hits that if and immediately returns initial_phase ("amazon_analysis"), without doing the backlog check and without logging [RESUME_PHASE_*].

That is exactly what I meant by “returned early”: the helper was called, looked at category_denominator_frozen, saw False, and exited at the top, so the phase stayed "amazon_analysis" and the only resume log you see is the original RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED.

“If we are in Amazon phase, why isn’t the denominator frozen?”
Because in this codebase there are two different notions:

A per‑category frozen denominator held by the state manager:

Set via state_manager.set_frozen_denominator(category_url, discovered_count).
Read via state_manager.get_frozen_denominator(category_url).
This is what drives logs like:
FROZEN DENOMINATOR: Category X ...
Could not find frozen denominator for <url>. Falling back to dynamic total.
A coarse boolean flag system_progression["category_denominator_frozen"]:

Written in a separate block further down in _extract_supplier_products:
Only when we go through that block and we are not already marked frozen.
It’s a legacy “we’ve already defined totals for this category” switch, not the primary source of truth.
Because of earlier edits and some resume/rewind scenarios, your category 38 is in a mixed state:

The file‑grounded totals (88 / 93) have been calculated and written to system_progression:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
and frozen_totals_committed is true, so the state manager considers totals “hardened”.

But the legacy boolean category_denominator_frozen for this category is still false, because the specific branch that sets it to True was never hit (or was hit before we rewound/edited and then got cleared). Being in amazon_analysis phase does not automatically guarantee that that flag is true.

My helper is currently conservative: it only overrides phase when both:

category_denominator_frozen is true, and
frozen totals are committed, and
persisted phase is amazon_analysis.
In your example, you are in Amazon phase, but the legacy flag is still false, so the helper deliberately does nothing rather than guessing from a possibly inconsistent state.

Why 88 / 93 with 5 / 1 (recap in this scenario)
Those numbers are coming from the same pre‑extraction filter:

For this category:

needs_full_extraction_urls length = 88 → supplier backlog.
needs_amazon_only_urls length = 5 → cache‑only, Amazon‑still‑needed.
So:

supplier_products_needing_extraction = 88
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 88 + 5 = 93
supplier_products_completed = 5 (fast‑forwarded skips/cache for supplier)
amazon_products_completed = 1 (one Amazon item finished).
That part is behaving consistently; what’s missing for the helper to kick in is just the category_denominator_frozen flag being set for this category.

I’ll keep this very concrete and example‑driven.

1. What the new fix actually does (in simple terms)

At resume, the helper does exactly this:

sp = system_progression

# 1) Bail out if the category totals aren’t marked as “frozen”
if not sp.get("category_denominator_frozen"):
    return initial_phase

# 2) Bail out if frozen totals haven’t been committed
if not state_manager.is_frozen_totals_committed():
    return initial_phase

# 3) Only care if we’re about to resume in Amazon
if initial_phase != "amazon_analysis":
    return initial_phase

supplier_total    = sp["supplier_products_needing_extraction"]
supplier_done     = sp["supplier_products_completed"]
amazon_total      = sp["amazon_products_needing_analysis"]
amazon_done       = sp["amazon_products_completed"]

# 4) If supplier backlog exists, force supplier
if supplier_total > supplier_done:
    return "supplier"    # + log [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN]

# 5) Otherwise keep Amazon
return "amazon_analysis"  # + log [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED]
So it only overrides when:

category_denominator_frozen == True,
frozen totals are committed, and
supplier_products_needing_extraction > supplier_products_completed,
and persisted phase is amazon_analysis.
If any of those is not true, it keeps the phase as is and logs nothing.

2. Example A – “healthy” category where override WILL fire

Imagine category C1 has this in system_progression at resume:

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 10,
"current_category_url": "https://.../Category/C1",
"category_denominator_frozen": true,
"frozen_totals_committed": true,

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 50,
"supplier_products_completed": 30,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 50,
"amazon_products_completed": 10
Step‑by‑step:

category_denominator_frozen == true → helper continues.
frozen_totals_committed == true → helper continues.
initial_phase == "amazon_analysis" → helper continues.
Supplier backlog: 50 > 30 → backlog exists.
Result:

Helper logs [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... supplier=30/50, amazon=10/50 ...
Helper returns "supplier".
run() routes into supplier phase for this category, not Amazon.
This is the “expected” behaviour you want.

3. Example B – your actual category 38 (why it did nothing)

Your state for angel category 38:

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 38,
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes",

"category_denominator_frozen": false,
"frozen_totals_committed": true,

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
"supplier_products_completed": 5,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 1
Walk through the helper:

Check category_denominator_frozen:

It is false → helper immediately does:

return initial_phase  # "amazon_analysis"
It does not even look at 88 > 5.

It does not log [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] or [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED].

run() then sees resume_phase == "amazon_analysis" (unchanged) and routes straight into Amazon.

So:
The fix didn’t “misbehave” here – it refused to act because the legacy “denominator frozen” flag is still false for this category, even though the counts 88 / 93 exist.

4. Can this happen for future categories?

Yes. It will happen for any category that meets all of these conditions at resume:

current_phase in state is "amazon_analysis",
There is supplier backlog (supplier_needing > supplier_completed),
But category_denominator_frozen is false.
For such a category:

The helper will not override.
The system will resume in Amazon, even though supplier still has backlog.
For categories where:

category_denominator_frozen == true and frozen_totals_committed == true,
the helper will honour the backlog and force supplier when needed, as in Example A.

5. Why is category_denominator_frozen false in your case?

In this codebase:

The real per‑category totals are in the state manager (via set_frozen_denominator / get_frozen_denominator).
The boolean system_progression["category_denominator_frozen"] is a second, legacy flag set in a separate block later in _extract_supplier_products.
Because of earlier rewinds / interrupted runs, you now have a mixed state for category 38:

The counts (88 / 93) have been set from filtering.
frozen_totals_committed is true.
But the legacy category_denominator_frozen flag never got flipped to true (or was reset).
The helper is currently using that flag as its gate, so it decides “totals are not trustworthy enough to override phase” and exits.

6. What can you do so the system behaves as intended?

There are two levels of “fix”:

A. Short‑term, for this specific category (manual state fix)
If you want the next resume for category 38 to honour supplier backlog and start in supplier phase:

In OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json, under "system_progression":

Set:

"category_denominator_frozen": true
Leave the counts as they are:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
"supplier_products_completed": 5,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 1
On the next resume at cat_idx 38:

Helper will see:
category_denominator_frozen == true
frozen_totals_committed == true
supplier_needing 88 > supplier_completed 5
It will log [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] and return "supplier".
run() will route into supplier phase instead of Amazon for that category.
This is the minimal change to make the new logic actually take control for this specific case.

B. Longer‑term, code‑level improvement (not applied yet)
Right now the helper trusts category_denominator_frozen. A safer rule (if you later want me to change code) would be to use:

frozen_totals_committed == True and
counts where supplier_needing >= supplier_completed and amazon_needing >= amazon_completed
and ignore the legacy boolean. That would:

Use the same condition the state manager already uses to say “totals are hardened”.
Avoid this kind of mismatch where the counts are correct but the flag is stale.
I have not made that change yet; I’m just describing it so you know what would be needed if you want the behaviour to be robust even in edge cases like this.

7. Summary in one sentence

The reason your new resume fix didn’t flip the phase for category 38 is simple: the helper is deliberately gated on category_denominator_frozen == true, and for that category the flag is still false, so it exits without touching the phase; if you set that flag to true (or we later adjust the helper to trust the counts instead of that flag), resuming will correctly go back to supplier whenever there is backlog.

only then will supplier_needing > supplier_completed),
fro most categories ( specially that we have exisiting entries in both linkigmap and cached produts files) as soon as system switches to amaozn extraction/phase, the indexes for total needed are mostly never the same
hence the fix you implemented is for a very specifc use case.

You’re right on both points:

In its current form, the new helper only kicks in in a narrow situation:
“phase says amazon_analysis AND the legacy category_denominator_frozen flag is true AND there is supplier backlog”.
That means some real‑world resume cases (like your category 38) still slip through because the flag is false even though the counts clearly show backlog.
So yes: right now it mainly helps the “clean” case and is too conservative.

Can we run this recalculation on every resume and adjust indexes?

Yes — and that’s actually the safer, more general way to do it.

The robust behaviour you want at resume is:

For the resume category (whatever phase we stopped in, supplier or Amazon):

Re‑derive the reality from files:
urls_for_manifest for that category (from manifest or fresh crawl).
skip_entirely_urls from linking map.
needs_amazon_only_urls from supplier cache (stable_key).
needs_full_extraction_urls (the rest).
From these, compute:
supplier_total = len(needs_full_extraction_urls)
amazon_total = len(needs_amazon_only_urls) + len(needs_full_extraction_urls)
Update system_progression for that category from those fresh numbers:

supplier_products_needing_extraction = supplier_total
amazon_products_needing_analysis = amazon_total
supplier_products_completed = skip_count + cached_count
(fast‑forward: anything in linking map or cache does not need supplier work).
amazon_products_completed stays whatever was already done (clamped to amazon_total).
Decide phase from those recomputed counts, not from stale flags:

If supplier_total > supplier_products_completed → resume in supplier.
Else if amazon_total > amazon_products_completed → resume in amazon_analysis.
Else → category is effectively done; move on.
This approach:

Works whether the previous interrupt was in supplier phase or Amazon phase.
Works even if category_denominator_frozen is wrong or missing.
Makes the *_needing_* and *_completed counters line up with actual file‑grounded backlog every time you resume.
So to answer your question directly:

Yes, we can have the system perform this calculation any time it gets resumed (for the resume category), and adjust indexes accordingly.
It would mean changing the helper so it always runs this file‑based recomputation for the resume category and bases the phase decision on that, instead of trusting category_denominator_frozen and the old counters.
If you want, next step I can draft a surgical diff that:

Factors the existing pre‑extraction filtering logic into a helper that can be called at resume time for just one category.
Uses that helper in run() at resume to:
Recompute needs_full / needs_amazon_only,
Adjust supplier_* / amazon_* counters for the resume category,
Then choose supplier vs Amazon based purely on those recomputed values.

I’ll answer in 3 parts: why the first fix was narrow, what happens to frozen denominators, then a precise plan with diffs for the broader behaviour you want.

1. Why the first fix was “narrow”

When we first added _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files, we had two competing constraints:

You wanted minimal‑blast‑radius edits.
The state we saw in your logs was sometimes inconsistent (e.g. counters and flags not lining up after manual state edits, network failures, rewinds).
So I made a conservative choice:

Only override phase when the state looked very safe and stable:
category_denominator_frozen == True
frozen_totals_committed == True
persisted phase is amazon_analysis
and supplier_total > supplier_completed.
That guarantees we don’t accidentally flip phase based on half‑baked numbers.
The downside (that we now see clearly in your real runs) is: if the boolean flag is stale (false) but the counts are correct (like 88/5 for category 38), the override never fires. That’s why it behaved like a very specific scenario only.

Now that we’ve seen multiple concrete cases where the counts are trustworthy but the flag isn’t, we can safely widen the logic to rely directly on the counts.

2. Frozen denominators vs “needed” counts

There are two things here:

Real frozen denominator (state manager)

Set by state_manager.set_frozen_denominator(category_url, discovered_count) when we first discover all URLs for a category.
This is the “true total number of URLs seen” (e.g. 225 for Aurora, 63 for All‑Baby‑Shower, 403 for Artificial‑Flowers).
We do not want to change this after it’s set; it’s meant to be an immutable snapshot.
Per‑phase “needed” counters in system_progression

supplier_products_needing_extraction
amazon_products_needing_analysis
These come from the filter:
supplier_total = len(needs_full_extraction_urls)
amazon_total   = len(needs_amazon_only_urls) + len(needs_full_extraction_urls)
These can be less than the frozen denominator because:
Some URLs are fully done (in linking map → skip_entirely).
Some URLs are cached and need only Amazon.
So:

Frozen denominator (e.g. 225) = total URLs the site had for that category at discovery.
Supplier “needing” (e.g. 55) = subset that still require supplier extraction.
Amazon “needing” (e.g. 53, 33, 93…) = subset still needing Amazon analysis.
It is expected that:

After supplier work + filtering, supplier_products_needing_extraction and amazon_products_needing_analysis no longer equal the frozen denominator.
That does not mean the frozen denominator changed; it just means the backlog shrank.
In the wider fix I’m about to propose:

We will not touch the state manager’s frozen denominators at all.
We will only read and reason over the per‑phase “needing/completed” counters in system_progression.
3. New, broader behaviour: recalc at every resume (for resume category)

Your target behaviour:

Whenever you resume (no matter where you were interrupted: supplier or Amazon), for the resume category:

Use the latest file‑based numbers (which are already stored in supplier_products_needing_extraction, supplier_products_completed, amazon_products_needing_analysis, amazon_products_completed) to decide:
Is there still supplier backlog? → resume in supplier.
Else, is there Amazon backlog? → resume in Amazon.
Do this without depending on category_denominator_frozen.
This is a less special‑case, more robust rule.

Given what’s already in the code, we can achieve that with very small changes:

We do not re‑implement filtering from scratch.
We do not modify any freezing logic or state manager methods.
We only adjust _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files to:
Always run for the resume category.
Base its decision purely on the four counters in system_progression.
Clamp obviously insane values locally (e.g. completed > needed) before deciding.
Below is the concrete implementation plan.

Implementation Plan (surgical, minimal changes)
Goal: Make _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files:

Always active for the resume category on startup.
Use supplier_products_needing_extraction / supplier_products_completed and amazon_products_needing_analysis / amazon_products_completed directly.
Ignore category_denominator_frozen for the phase decision.
Never touch frozen denominators in the state manager.
All changes are in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, inside the helper we recently added.

Diff 1 – Remove the over‑strict guards
Current top of helper (simplified):

    async def _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files(...):
        sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {}) or {}

        # If totals were never frozen/committed, do not attempt to infer anything.
        if not sp.get("category_denominator_frozen"):
            return initial_phase or "supplier"
        if hasattr(self.state_manager, "is_frozen_totals_committed"):
            try:
                if not self.state_manager.is_frozen_totals_committed():
                    return initial_phase or "supplier"
            except Exception:
                # If the check itself fails, fall back to the persisted phase.
                return initial_phase or "supplier"

        current_phase = initial_phase or "supplier"

        # Only consider overriding when we are about to route into Amazon.
        if current_phase != "amazon_analysis":
            return current_phase
Change to:

@@
-        # If totals were never frozen/committed, do not attempt to infer anything.
-        if not sp.get("category_denominator_frozen"):
-            return initial_phase or "supplier"
-        if hasattr(self.state_manager, "is_frozen_totals_committed"):
-            try:
-                if not self.state_manager.is_frozen_totals_committed():
-                    return initial_phase or "supplier"
-            except Exception:
-                # If the check itself fails, fall back to the persisted phase.
-                return initial_phase or "supplier"
-
-        current_phase = initial_phase or "supplier"
-
-        # Only consider overriding when we are about to route into Amazon.
-        if current_phase != "amazon_analysis":
-            return current_phase
+        current_phase = initial_phase or "supplier"
+
+        # Only consider overriding when we are about to route into Amazon.
+        # For all other phases we honour the persisted value.
+        if current_phase != "amazon_analysis":
+            return current_phase
Effect:

The helper now always runs its logic whenever phase is amazon_analysis, regardless of category_denominator_frozen or frozen_totals_committed.
That immediately covers your category 38 example: it will now look at 88/5 and override to supplier.
Diff 2 – Normalize and clamp counters (local only)
Current block:

        supplier_total = int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
        supplier_completed = int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0)
        amazon_total = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
        amazon_completed = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
Change to:

@@
-        supplier_total = int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
-        supplier_completed = int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0)
-        amazon_total = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
-        amazon_completed = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
+        supplier_total = max(0, int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0))
+        supplier_completed = max(0, int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0))
+        amazon_total = max(0, int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0))
+        amazon_completed = max(0, int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0))
+
+        # Clamp completed counters to their denominators for decision purposes,
+        # so we don't invert X/Y when reasoning about backlog.
+        if supplier_total > 0 and supplier_completed > supplier_total:
+            supplier_completed = supplier_total
+        if amazon_total > 0 and amazon_completed > amazon_total:
+            amazon_completed = amazon_total
Effect:

We never make decisions based on “160/241”‑style inverted numbers; we clamp them locally first.
We do not write these clamped values back into system_progression, so we don’t risk changing state semantics; they’re just used to decide phase.
Diff 3 – Keep the backlog logic, but now always active
The rest of the helper stays as you already have it:

        # If there is no recorded work for either phase, keep the persisted phase.
        if supplier_total <= 0 and amazon_total <= 0:
            return current_phase

        # Supplier backlog exists when the completed count is strictly less than
        # the denominator. In that case, supplier must run before Amazon.
        if supplier_total > supplier_completed:
            try:
                cat_url = sp.get("current_category_url") or ""
                self.log.info(
                    " [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] File-based supplier backlog detected for "
                    f"category index {start_category_index}: "
                    f"supplier={supplier_completed}/{supplier_total}, "
                    f"amazon={amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}, url={cat_url}. "
                    "Overriding phase 'amazon_analysis' -> 'supplier' for safe resumption."
                )
            except Exception:
                self.log.info(
                    " [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] File-based supplier backlog detected; "
                    "overriding phase 'amazon_analysis' -> 'supplier' for safe resumption."
                )
            return "supplier"

        # No supplier backlog: respect the persisted Amazon phase.
        try:
            cat_url = sp.get("current_category_url") or ""
            self.log.info(
                " [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] Supplier backlog complete for resume category "
                f"index {start_category_index}: "
                f"supplier={supplier_completed}/{supplier_total}, "
                f"amazon={amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}, url={cat_url}. "
                "Proceeding with 'amazon_analysis' as persisted."
            )
        except Exception:
            self.log.info(
                " [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] Supplier backlog complete for resume category; "
                "proceeding with 'amazon_analysis' as persisted."
            )

        return current_phase
Now, on every resume where:

Phase is amazon_analysis, and
supplier_products_needing_extraction / supplier_products_completed are set,
the helper will:

Either log [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] and force supplier (when backlog exists).
Or log [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] and keep Amazon (when supplier is complete).
Again: no changes to real frozen denominators in the state manager.

4. What this gives you in practice

For a category like your 38 example:

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 88,
"supplier_products_completed": 5,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 1
On resume:

Helper sees:

supplier_total = 88, supplier_completed = 5.
88 > 5 → supplier backlog exists.

It logs:

[RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] File-based supplier backlog detected for category index 38:
supplier=5/88, amazon=1/93, url=...
Overriding phase 'amazon_analysis' -> 'supplier' for safe resumption.
It returns "supplier".

run() routes into supplier phase for category 38 first, as you expect.

And this will happen for any future category where those counts in system_progression show supplier backlog at resume time, regardless of what category_denominator_frozen says.

Frozen denominators (225, 403, etc.) remain untouched and continue to represent the original discovered total URLs for each category.

before you proceed , what i was refering to here, wasnt the latest fixes we implemented, i meant when we were updating fixing the system and introduced these approaches, why dd you not do the same as what we are trying to do currently ( recalcualte at every resume) ; was there maybe a reason, or was it a compromise or ?
i want to know, in order to be sure that going along with this updated approach will cause anydiscprenices in other sectios of the workflow/logic


You’re asking about the earlier design choices, not the new helper we just added. I’ll keep it focused on that.

1. Why the earlier fixes were “one‑time per category” and not “recalc on every resume”

Looking at the code and history, you can see the intent:

The big “FILE‑BASED RESUME CALCULATION” block lives inside the supplier loop, where we already have:

urls_for_manifest
skip_entirely_urls
needs_amazon_only_urls
needs_full_extraction_urls
That block:

Computes in_count / skip_count / cached_count / full_count.
Sets supplier_products_needing_extraction, amazon_products_needing_analysis.
Does fast‑forward of completed counts.
Builds the Step‑2 allow‑list.
After that, the original architecture assumed:

“We’ve done the heavy file‑based calculation once for this category; from now on, stored counters in system_progression are the truth for this category.”
The resume gating in run() was only meant to read those stored fields, not to call back into filtering or to recompute anything.
When we added the earlier fixes (fast‑forward, hybrid fixes), we stayed within that model:

We fixed how the numbers were written (e.g. fast‑forward counts, corrected denominators).
We did not change the gating at startup to recompute, because that would mean:
Calling category‑level logic from run().
Risk of double‑counting or interfering with category queue construction.
Much bigger blast radius touching both supplier and Amazon flows.
So the earlier choice was a conservative compromise:
“Make the per‑category calculation correct and stable the first time it runs; then trust it on resume.”
It looked sufficient until your more complex real‑world scenarios (manual rewinds, network drops, categories with mixed progress) exposed where those stored counters and flags can drift.

2. Will the updated “recalc for resume” approach cause discrepancies elsewhere?

For what we just discussed (the new plan):

We are not talking about re‑running the heavy pre‑extraction filter from run().
We are not touching:
State manager’s real frozen denominators (set_frozen_denominator / get_frozen_denominator).
Linking map writing.
Supplier cache and category queues.
ISS‑004, allow‑list, or Amazon queue building.
The plan I outlined only does this, once at startup, for the resume category:

Reads four existing fields:

supplier_products_needing_extraction
supplier_products_completed
amazon_products_needing_analysis
amazon_products_completed
Clamps them locally so they’re sane (no 241/240 style inversions).

Decides phase:

If supplier_needing > supplier_completed → resume in supplier.
Else → resume in amazon_analysis (if there is Amazon backlog).
Crucially:

It does not write anything back to system_progression.
It does not change frozen denominators in the state manager.
All the existing category‑level calculations and queues still use the same code paths as today.
So the risk is limited to one decision point:

“Should we start this run in supplier or Amazon for the resume category?”
Everywhere else, the workflow stays exactly as it is now.

3. Why this broader approach is now reasonable (and wasn’t done earlier)

Earlier, we were missing two things that we now have:

Evidence that flags can be wrong while counts are still useful
Your examples (like category 38 with 88/5 supplier and 93/1 Amazon but category_denominator_frozen = false) show:

The four counters in system_progression are often more reliable than the boolean flag.
Resuming based on the phase alone can send us straight to Amazon with obvious supplier backlog.
A clear, minimal place to hook in
Now that _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files exists and is already called once at startup, we have:

A single, central place where we can adjust only the phase decision.
No need to thread new data through multiple methods or touch queue construction.
Given that:

Using these four counters just to decide “supplier vs Amazon first” is now the least intrusive way to get the behaviour you expect, with:
No changes to per‑category queues.
No changes to frozen denominators.
No changes to what gets written in linking map or cache.
If you want, I can now turn that into the concrete diffs from my previous message, so you get:

Phase decided from supplier_needing/completed on every resume.
Frozen denominators untouched.
Existing category‑level logic and indexes preserved.
surgically proceed with your suggested implenetation ( fix) plan, make sure your throughly follow the diffs lsited. once you complete integrating the plan, revist all the"affected files, scripts, paths, or whatever it may be to make sure that the planwas followed in full, correctly ( and integrated in the correct sction/snippets of each script/fole).

Why the first fix was “narrow”
When we first added _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files, we had two competing constraints:

You wanted minimal‑blast‑radius edits.
The state we saw in your logs was sometimes inconsistent (e.g. counters and flags not lining up after manual state edits, network failures, rewinds).
So I made a conservative choice:

Only override phase when the state looked very safe and stable:
category_denominator_frozen == True
frozen_totals_committed == True
persisted phase is amazon_analysis
and supplier_total > supplier_completed.
That guarantees we don’t accidentally flip phase based on half‑baked numbers.
The downside (that we now see clearly in your real runs) is: if the boolean flag is stale (false) but the counts are correct (like 88/5 for category 38), the override never fires. That’s why it behaved like a very specific scenario only.

Now that we’ve seen multiple concrete cases where the counts are trustworthy but the flag isn’t, we can safely widen the logic to rely directly on the counts.

Frozen denominators vs “needed” counts
There are two things here:

Real frozen denominator (state manager)

Set by state_manager.set_frozen_denominator(category_url, discovered_count) when we first discover all URLs for a category.
This is the “true total number of URLs seen” (e.g. 225 for Aurora, 63 for All‑Baby‑Shower, 403 for Artificial‑Flowers).
We do not want to change this after it’s set; it’s meant to be an immutable snapshot.
Per‑phase “needed” counters in system_progression

supplier_products_needing_extraction
amazon_products_needing_analysis
These come from the filter:
supplier_total = len(needs_full_extraction_urls)
amazon_total = len(needs_amazon_only_urls) + len(needs_full_extraction_urls)
These can be less than the frozen denominator because:
Some URLs are fully done (in linking map → skip_entirely).
Some URLs are cached and need only Amazon.
So:

Frozen denominator (e.g. 225) = total URLs the site had for that category at discovery.
Supplier “needing” (e.g. 55) = subset that still require supplier extraction.
Amazon “needing” (e.g. 53, 33, 93…) = subset still needing Amazon analysis.
It is expected that:

After supplier work + filtering, supplier_products_needing_extraction and amazon_products_needing_analysis no longer equal the frozen denominator.
That does not mean the frozen denominator changed; it just means the backlog shrank.
In the wider fix I’m about to propose:

We will not touch the state manager’s frozen denominators at all.
We will only read and reason over the per‑phase “needing/completed” counters in system_progression.
3. New, broader behaviour: recalc at every resume (for resume category)

Your target behaviour:

Whenever you resume (no matter where you were interrupted: supplier or Amazon), for the resume category:

Use the latest file‑based numbers (which are already stored in supplier_products_needing_extraction, supplier_products_completed, amazon_products_needing_analysis, amazon_products_completed) to decide:
Is there still supplier backlog? → resume in supplier.
Else, is there Amazon backlog? → resume in Amazon.
Do this without depending on category_denominator_frozen.
This is a less special‑case, more robust rule.

Given what’s already in the code, we can achieve that with very small changes:

We do not re‑implement filtering from scratch.
We do not modify any freezing logic or state manager methods.
We only adjust _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files to:
Always run for the resume category.
Base its decision purely on the four counters in system_progression.
Clamp obviously insane values locally (e.g. completed > needed) before deciding.
Below is the concrete implementation plan.

Implementation Plan (surgical, minimal changes)
Goal: Make _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files:

Always active for the resume category on startup.
Use supplier_products_needing_extraction / supplier_products_completed and amazon_products_needing_analysis / amazon_products_completed directly.
Ignore category_denominator_frozen for the phase decision.
Never touch frozen denominators in the state manager.
All changes are in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, inside the helper we recently added.

Diff 1 – Remove the over‑strict guards
Current top of helper (simplified):

async def _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files(...):
    sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {}) or {}

    # If totals were never frozen/committed, do not attempt to infer anything.
    if not sp.get("category_denominator_frozen"):
        return initial_phase or "supplier"
    if hasattr(self.state_manager, "is_frozen_totals_committed"):
        try:
            if not self.state_manager.is_frozen_totals_committed():
                return initial_phase or "supplier"
        except Exception:
            # If the check itself fails, fall back to the persisted phase.
            return initial_phase or "supplier"

    current_phase = initial_phase or "supplier"

    # Only consider overriding when we are about to route into Amazon.
    if current_phase != "amazon_analysis":
        return current_phase
Change to:

@@

   # If totals were never frozen/committed, do not attempt to infer anything.
   if not sp.get("category_denominator_frozen"):
       return initial_phase or "supplier"
   if hasattr(self.state_manager, "is_frozen_totals_committed"):
       try:
           if not self.state_manager.is_frozen_totals_committed():
               return initial_phase or "supplier"
       except Exception:
           # If the check itself fails, fall back to the persisted phase.
           return initial_phase or "supplier"
   current_phase = initial_phase or "supplier"
   # Only consider overriding when we are about to route into Amazon.
   if current_phase != "amazon_analysis":
       return current_phase
   current_phase = initial_phase or "supplier"
   # Only consider overriding when we are about to route into Amazon.
   # For all other phases we honour the persisted value.
   if current_phase != "amazon_analysis":
       return current_phase
Effect:

The helper now always runs its logic whenever phase is amazon_analysis, regardless of category_denominator_frozen or frozen_totals_committed.
That immediately covers your category 38 example: it will now look at 88/5 and override to supplier.

Change to:

@@

   supplier_total = int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
   supplier_completed = int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0)
   amazon_total = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
   amazon_completed = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
   supplier_total = max(0, int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0))
   supplier_completed = max(0, int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0))
   amazon_total = max(0, int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0))
   amazon_completed = max(0, int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0))
   # Clamp completed counters to their denominators for decision purposes,
   # so we don't invert X/Y when reasoning about backlog.
   if supplier_total > 0 and supplier_completed > supplier_total:
       supplier_completed = supplier_total
   if amazon_total > 0 and amazon_completed > amazon_total:
       amazon_completed = amazon_total
Effect:

We do not write these clamped values back into system_progression, so we don’t risk changing state semantics; they’re just used to decide phase.

Implementation is now in place exactly as per your plan, and only in the intended helper.

Here’s what I changed and verified:

Removed the over‑strict guards
In tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py, inside _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files I deleted the category_denominator_frozen / is_frozen_totals_committed early‑return block and simplified the top:

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {}) or {}
current_phase = initial_phase or "supplier"

# Only consider overriding when we are about to route into Amazon.
# For all other phases we honour the persisted value.
if current_phase != "amazon_analysis":
    return current_phase
This means: as soon as we’re resuming in amazon_analysis, the helper always runs its backlog logic, regardless of the legacy boolean flag.

Normalized and clamped counters (local only)
I replaced the raw int(...) reads with clamped versions:

supplier_total = max(0, int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0))
supplier_completed = max(0, int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0))
amazon_total = max(0, int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0))
amazon_completed = max(0, int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0))

# Clamp completed counters to their denominators for decision purposes
if supplier_total > 0 and supplier_completed > supplier_total:
    supplier_completed = supplier_total
if amazon_total > 0 and amazon_completed > amazon_total:
    amazon_completed = amazon_total
These clamped values are only used inside the helper to decide the phase.
Nothing is written back into system_progression, so we’re not changing any stored state.
Backlog logic left as‑is (now always active for Amazon resumes)
The rest of the helper is unchanged and sits immediately after the clamping:

If both totals are 0 → keep the phase.
If supplier_total > supplier_completed → log [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... and return "supplier".
Otherwise → log [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] ... and keep "amazon_analysis".
So on every resume where resume_phase == "amazon_analysis":

The helper now looks at supplier_products_needing_extraction / supplier_products_completed and will force supplier whenever backlog exists, even in cases like your category 38 where the old category_denominator_frozen flag was false.
Run‑level wiring left intact
The call site in run():

resume_phase = await self._re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files(
    start_category_index=start_category_index,
    initial_phase=resume_phase,
)
self._resume_phase = resume_phase
is unchanged and still sits immediately after RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED.

No other methods (_extract_supplier_products, _rebuild_category_amazon_queue, state manager calls, frozen denominator logic) were touched.

So the net effect is exactly what you asked:

Phase decision at resume is now based only on the four counters in system_progression for the resume category, with simple clamping.
It runs on every amazon_analysis resume, not just when a legacy flag happens to be true.
Frozen denominators and all other workflow steps are untouched.

You’re right: in that run the system still went straight into Amazon extraction for category 38. Here’s exactly why, and what I’ve now changed.

1. What actually happened in that log

From run_custom_poundwholesale_20251204_063912.log:

At startup:
RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: Starting from category index 38 at product 3 in phase 'amazon_analysis'.
[RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... supplier=5/88, amazon=3/93, url=... Overriding phase 'amazon_analysis' -> 'supplier' for safe resumption.
PHASE-AWARE GATING: Routing to supplier extraction phase
So:

_re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files did override the phase to supplier.
run()’s PHASE‑AWARE gating also switched and logged “Routing to supplier extraction phase”.
Then a bit later:

HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled (resume_context=True)
...
Processing chunk 1: categories 1-1
PHASE INTEGRITY GUARD: Amazon phase incomplete (done=3/93)
FORCING AMAZON RESUME to complete category first
...
CATEGORY QUEUE: url=...Baby-Boy-Clothes size=8 ...
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=38 total=8 ...
Analyzing product: 'Kids Royal Blue waterproof Rain Suit 5-6y - by Result'
...
Searching Amazon by EAN: 5055192342082 ...
So the sequence is:

Top‑level gating: “go to supplier”.

First call into _extract_supplier_products for hybrid mode hits:

# --- PHASE INTEGRITY GUARD ---
if not await self._ensure_phase_integrity():
    return []  # Block supplier work until Amazon complete
_ensure_phase_integrity looked at system_progression["current_phase"] == "amazon_analysis" and saw Amazon incomplete (3/93).

That guard logged PHASE INTEGRITY GUARD... and forced Amazon resume, calling _run_amazon_phase_from_resume(...).

Supplier extraction for category 38 was blocked; Amazon resumed instead.

So you’re absolutely right: even though the override ran, the internal phase integrity guard overruled it and forced Amazon for this category.

2. What I’ve just changed (so this doesn’t happen again)

The problem is that _ensure_phase_integrity was still using only system_progression["current_phase"] to decide, ignoring the override made by _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files.

I’ve now changed _ensure_phase_integrity to respect the effective resume phase we computed:

Old:

async def _ensure_phase_integrity(self):
    sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
    if sp.get("current_phase") == "amazon_analysis":
        total = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
        done = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
        if total > 0 and done < total:
            self.log.warning(" PHASE INTEGRITY GUARD: Amazon phase incomplete (done=.../...)")
            self.log.warning(" FORCING AMAZON RESUME to complete category first")
            rp = type("RP", (), {"cat_idx": sp.get("persistent_category_index", 1),
                                 "prod_idx": done})()
            await self._run_amazon_phase_from_resume(rp)
            return False
    return True
New (surgical change only inside this helper):

async def _ensure_phase_integrity(self):
    """Ensure we don't start supplier work while Amazon is incomplete"""
    sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
    # Use the effective resume phase when available so that an explicit
    # supplier-first override at startup is honoured and not immediately
    # cancelled by this guard.
    effective_phase = getattr(self, "_resume_phase", sp.get("current_phase"))
    if effective_phase == "amazon_analysis":
        total = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
        done = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
        if total > 0 and done < total:
            self.log.warning(f" PHASE INTEGRITY GUARD: Amazon phase incomplete (done={done}/{total})")
            self.log.warning(" FORCING AMAZON RESUME to complete category first")

            rp = type("RP", (), {
                "cat_idx": sp.get("persistent_category_index", 1),
                "prod_idx": done
            })()
            await self._run_amazon_phase_from_resume(rp)
            return False
    return True
Key points:

We now use:

effective_phase = getattr(self, "_resume_phase", sp.get("current_phase"))
_resume_phase is what run() sets after calling _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files (so it includes your supplier‑first overrides).

If _resume_phase is "supplier", the guard returns True immediately and does not force Amazon.

For runs where there’s no override, _resume_phase is just the persisted phase and the guard behaviour is exactly as before.

Everything else is unchanged:

We did not touch frozen denominators.
We did not touch queue building, ISS‑004, or Amazon resume.
We only made _ensure_phase_integrity aware of the override, so it doesn’t fight against the new logic.
With this in place:

On the next resume for category 38:
_re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files will again log [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... 'amazon_analysis' -> 'supplier'.
_resume_phase will be "supplier".
_extract_supplier_products will call _ensure_phase_integrity → see effective_phase == 'supplier' → return True without forcing Amazon.
Supplier extraction for the backlog of 88 URLs in that category will proceed as intended.

I’ll go through your 5 points in order, using the latest state + log, then summarise what the system is actually doing now.

1) Does the system always re‑extract product URLs at resume?

From run_custom_poundwholesale_20251204_064911.log:

For category 38 (“Baby‑Boy‑Clothes”) after resume:

[RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... supplier=5/88, amazon=4/93 ... Overriding phase 'amazon_analysis' -> 'supplier'
...
CHUNK CATEGORIES: ['https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes']
PERFORMING SUPPLIER SCRAPING from 1 categories
...
Scraping category: https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes
...
Button pagination complete: 224 unique URLs collected
Discovered 224 product URLs for category: https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing-Baby-Boy-Clothes
MANIFEST CREATED: Saved 224 URLs to ..._manifest.json
So behaviour is:

For the resume category, if we are about to run supplier phase (either because the persisted phase was supplier or because we overrode from amazon_analysis), the system re‑scrapes the supplier category and rebuilds the manifest. That is what you see here: 224 URLs collected again.

For categories before the resume index, in the same run, you see:

ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 38
PROGRESS TRACKING: Extracting from 0 categories in 0 batches
which means the hybrid loop “touches” those categories but does not re‑scrape them (no _collect_urls_only loop is entered because the cursor skips them).

If we resume directly in Amazon phase (no override), supplier scraping is not run for that category at resume; Amazon queues are rebuilt from:

the supplier cache (angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json),
the linking map,
the stored allowlist.
So:

Yes: whenever supplier phase runs for a category (including the resume category), the URLs are re‑collected from the site and the manifest is refreshed.
But for a pure Amazon‑only resume, the system does not re‑scrape supplier URLs; it uses cached data.
2) The “37 categories / repeated chunk checks” – useful or useless?

In this log you see many blocks like:

Processing chunk 5: categories 5-5
CHUNK CATEGORIES: ['https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-favours-wholesale']
...
CACHE FOUND: 3132 products available for progress tracking
ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories starting from index 38
PROGRESS TRACKING: Extracting from 0 categories in 0 batches
What’s going on:

Hybrid mode is enabled (HYBRID PROCESSING MODE: Enabled ... chunk size: 1 categories).

The loop goes through every category in order (chunks 1, 2, …), but for each chunk it calls _extract_supplier_products with the current resume cursor (_start_category_index = 38).

Inside _extract_supplier_products, we compute cursor_urls based on the global resume index:

For chunks whose category index < 38, cursor_urls is empty:
Hence ENUMERATING FROM CURSOR: 0 categories.
No supplier scraping is actually performed for those categories.
So these repeated “chunk” logs are:

The hybrid engine checking each chunk against the resume cursor and deciding “this is before the resume point, so skip”.
They are not completely useless: they guarantee that if the resume pointer ever moves backwards or forwards, the same code path will handle it consistently.
But they do not mean the categories are being re‑scraped; they’re mostly control‑flow logging noise.
The “Startup category analysis: Calculated completion for 35 categories” you saw in earlier runs comes from fixed_enhanced_state_manager doing a one‑time file‑grounded scan (linking map + cache + config) to classify which categories are complete; that is a real integrity check.

3) When does this new resume behaviour apply?

With your latest code:

On every resume, run() does:

RESUMPTION POINT CONFIRMED: category 38, product X, phase '...'
[RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... (only when persisted phase was amazon_analysis and supplier backlog exists)
PHASE-AWARE GATING: Routing to supplier extraction phase   # or Amazon based on result
Then, before supplier extraction, _extract_supplier_products calls _ensure_phase_integrity, which now uses the effective phase:

effective_phase = getattr(self, "_resume_phase", sp.get("current_phase"))
if effective_phase == "amazon_analysis":
    # may force Amazon resume
So behaviour by scenario:

Scenario A – interrupted in supplier phase
Example: system stopped mid‑supplier in category 10.

current_phase persisted as "supplier".
_re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files sees initial_phase = 'supplier' → no override.
_ensure_phase_integrity sees effective_phase = 'supplier' → does not force Amazon.
Result: system resumes in supplier for category 10 and re‑scrapes that category; after supplier is complete, it goes to Amazon for that category.
Scenario B – interrupted in Amazon, but supplier backlog exists
This is your category 38 case.

State before resume (previous run):
current_phase = 'amazon_analysis' and supplier_needing > supplier_completed (now 80/2).
Helper logs [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] ... 'amazon_analysis' -> 'supplier'.
_resume_phase is set to 'supplier'.
_ensure_phase_integrity sees effective_phase 'supplier' → no Amazon force.
System re‑scrapes Baby‑Boy‑Clothes (224 URLs), recalculates needs_full/needs_amazon_only, then continues supplier work for that category.
Scenario C – interrupted in Amazon, supplier backlog complete
Example: supplier totals for category N are 0/0 or done/done.

current_phase = 'amazon_analysis', supplier_needing <= supplier_completed.
Helper logs [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] and keeps 'amazon_analysis'.
_ensure_phase_integrity sees effective_phase 'amazon_analysis' and, if amazon_done < amazon_total, can force Amazon resume first.
System resumes directly in Amazon for that category.
So: this behaviour runs every time you resume, and the phase choice (supplier vs Amazon) is now always based on the four counters in system_progression for the resume category, not on the stale category_denominator_frozen flag.

4) The “131/93” kind of indices – what do they actually mean?

From the state file:

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 80,
"supplier_products_completed": 2,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 131,
And logs:

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=38/326 ... prod_idx=131/93
RESUME STATE: phase=amazon_analysis cat=38/326 url=... supplier=5/224 amazon=131/224
LINEAGE: ... total=80
There are two different “denominators” in play:

224 = number of URLs discovered for category 38 in this run (Discovered 224 product URLs).
80 = len(needs_full_extraction_urls) for that category in this run (supplier backlog). That’s what we lock as [DENOM_FROZEN] total=80.
93 = amazon_products_needing_analysis for that category – initially len(needs_amazon_only) + len(needs_full) at the time of filter (old value still persisted).
131 = global Amazon completed count across this supplier session, not just this category.
So when you see 131/93:

The numerator is global Amazon completion (amazon_products_completed) – it constantly increases as the run progresses across many categories.
The denominator (93) is the last persisted “total Amazon needing analysis” for the resume category.
That’s why it looks inverted.

For understanding how many products in this category still need Amazon:

The real numbers are:

CATEGORY QUEUE: ... size=8 when Amazon resumptions are built.
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=38 total=8 ... first_offset=....
So:

93 was the original backlog for this category when filter ran earlier.
Many of those have already been processed and written to the linking map / cache (possibly in previous runs).
The remaining queue at resume time is 8 items (the queue size), not 93 or 131.
Think of:

80 = supplier backlog now (80 products still need supplier extraction).
93 = historical “Amazon needing analysis” for that category, now partially consumed.
131 = global Amazon completions; it’s more of a telemetry counter, not a clean per‑category progress fraction.
So yes: 131/93 is “expected” in the sense that it reflects how those two counters are defined; it’s not the value you should use to read per‑category progress.

Override fired for category 38.

For category 38, URLs are re‑collected and filtered.

Amazon queues are built from cache and allowlist when Amazon phase is entered.
Some counters (like 131/93) remain “telemetry‑style”:
They’re not per‑category progress fractions; they’re global numbers paired with a category denominator.
This is pre‑existing behaviour; your new fixes didn’t introduce it.
Functional impact: low, as long as you read queue sizes and needs_* counts for real decision‑making.
Given the current code + this run’s logs and state, the new resume logic (phase override + phase‑integrity guard) is working as designed:

It prevents the “jump straight into Amazon while supplier backlog exists” regression.

I’ll split this into two parts: how to read the current numbers, then how we can add a clearer, read‑only “status” line with minimal risk.

1) How to interpret the current indexes

Using your latest state:

Key points:

Supplier phase (per‑category)

supplier_products_needing_extraction = 80
→ this is the number of URLs in needs_full_extraction_urls for Baby‑Boy‑Clothes now (i.e. supplier backlog in this category).

supplier_products_completed = 2
→ number of those 80 that have been processed in the current pass.
The 5/224 in the log (supplier=5/224) is a different view: 5 URLs out of the full 224 discovered have supplier data (linking map + cache) — not just the 80 that still need full extraction.

So for supplier in this category, right now:

Total supplier backlog: 80
Completed (of that backlog): 2
Remaining: 78
You can always compute:

supplier_done   = min(supplier_products_completed, supplier_products_needing_extraction)
supplier_need   = supplier_products_needing_extraction
supplier_left   = max(supplier_need - supplier_done, 0)
Amazon phase (per‑category vs global)

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 93
→ initial “needs Amazon” count from filter: len(needs_amazon_only) + len(needs_full) when it ran for this category. Some of those have already been processed in earlier sessions.

amazon_products_completed = 131
→ this is a global counter across all categories for this supplier, not just Baby‑Boy‑Clothes.

That’s why you see 131/93 and 131/224 in logs; those are telemetry, not a clean per‑category ratio.

For per‑category Amazon status, the reliable numbers are:

CATEGORY QUEUE: ... size=8
→ current Amazon queue length for Baby‑Boy‑Clothes at this resume.

AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=38 total=8 ...
→ same “total” as queue size.

During processing, you’ll see [RESUME_ENTER] and subsequent “Analyzing product ...” log lines; together they show how many of those 8 are being worked.

So for Amazon in Baby‑Boy‑Clothes at this resume:

Remaining Amazon items in this category: 8
As it runs, it will process those 8; the global amazon_products_completed will move from 131 upwards, but that counter is “total Amazon done across all categories”, not “this category only”.
2) Having clearer, “read‑only” status ratios

Yes, we can add clearer progress logs without touching any of the logic or indexes. The safest way is:

Do not change how counters are stored or used.
Only add log lines that:
Read existing values.
Clamp them to sane ranges when printing (so you don’t see 131/93).
Explicitly say “this is supplier for this category” vs “global Amazon”.
A simple, low‑risk enhancement would be inside _is_category_fully_processed, which already reads these four fields:

def _is_category_fully_processed(self, category_url: str) -> bool:
    sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})

    supplier_needed = int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
    supplier_done   = int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0)
    amazon_needed   = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
    amazon_done     = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
    ...
We can add just logging here, like:

    # Clamp for reporting only
    s_done = min(max(supplier_done, 0), max(supplier_needed, 0))
    a_done = min(max(amazon_done, 0), max(amazon_needed, 0))

    self.log.info(
        f" CATEGORY PHASE STATUS: {category_url}\n"
        f"   Supplier: {s_done}/{supplier_needed} "
        f"(remaining={max(supplier_needed - s_done, 0)})\n"
        f"   Amazon  : {a_done}/{amazon_needed} "
        f"(global_done={amazon_done}, remaining≈{max(amazon_needed - a_done, 0)})"
    )
This:

Never writes back to sp.
Makes it explicit that:
Supplier ratio is per‑category backlog.
Amazon ratio shown is “clamped per‑category” and also shows the global done count (global_done).
We can also add a similar, optional read‑only log right after resume:

In _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files, where we already log [RESUME_PHASE_OVERRIDDEN] / [RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED], we’re printing:

supplier={supplier_completed}/{supplier_total},
amazon={amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}
Those messages are already giving you a clean snapshot for the resume category at startup, based on clamped values (we clamp there now).

So from a tracking point of view, after these extra logs you’d have:

At resume: a clear supplier/Amazon status snapshot for the resume category.
During category processing: optional CATEGORY PHASE STATUS lines whenever _is_category_fully_processed is called.
For Amazon per‑category, the queue size (CATEGORY QUEUE size=...) plus [RESUME_ENTER] and “Analyzing product ...” give you the live picture.

I’ll keep this focused on what you asked:

how to interpret the existing indexes,
what “readable” tracking would look like in the logs,
and a surgical implementation plan (diffs only) that adds read‑only progress logs + state summaries without changing core workflow.
1. How to interpret the current indexes (for the example state)
From the latest state file for angelwholesale:

OUTPUTS\CACHE\processing_states\angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:system_progression

"current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
"persistent_category_index": 38,
"current_category_index": 38,
"current_category_url": "https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes",
"total_categories": 326,
"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 80,
"supplier_products_completed": 2,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
"amazon_products_completed": 131,
...
Definitively:

supplier_products_needing_extraction = 80

Number of URLs in this category that need full supplier extraction (after filtering: skip + cache removed).
This is per‑category, not global.
supplier_products_completed = 2

Number of those 80 that have been processed and committed so far for this category.
Remaining supplier backlog for the category is 80 - 2 = 78.
amazon_products_needing_analysis = 93

Total number of URLs in this category that need Amazon analysis when the filter ran (amazon‑only + full‑extraction).
It is a denominator for “how many should eventually go through Amazon” for this category.
amazon_products_completed = 131

Global Amazon counter across all categories, used as a pointer for resume.
It does not tell you “131/93” for this category; it tells you “131 Amazon queue items have been processed in total so far”.
For per‑category Amazon progress, the reliable log sources are:

CATEGORY QUEUE: url=... size=Q → Q = queue length for that category.
AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=38 total=Q start_batch=... first_offset=...
[RESUME_ENTER] cat_idx=38 total=Q ptr=P ... → P is the starting pointer within that category’s queue.
During processing:
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=K/Q cat_idx=38 → current queue index K (0‑based) and total Q.
So, for category 38 “Baby‑Boy‑Clothes”:

Number of URLs needing supplier extraction now: 80 (78 remaining).
Number of URLs needing Amazon analysis overall: 93 (for this category).
Current Amazon per‑category progress: read from the queue logs (CATEGORY QUEUE, AMAZON RESUME, [PROGRESS_COMMIT]), not from amazon_products_completed alone.
2. What “readable tracking indexes” would look like (log examples)
Using your example category (38, Baby‑Boy‑Clothes), here is exactly how the new log lines would look after the change, assuming:

Supplier: needing=80, completed moves from 2 → 3.
Amazon queue for this category: total=8, current queue index moves from 0 → 1.
2.1 Supplier phase – per‑product example
After processing one supplier product in this category:

[DENOM_FROZEN] url=https://angelwholesale.co.uk/Category/Baby-Clothing/Baby-Boy-Clothes total=80 (LOCKED)
?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to 3/80
SUPPLIER PROGRESS STATUS [baby-boy-clothes]: 3/80 (remaining=77)
AMAZON PROGRESS STATUS [baby-boy-clothes]: 1/93 (global_done=131, remaining≈92)
3/80 = per‑category supplier progress (3 done, 77 left).
1/93 ≈ Amazon progress relative to this category’s Amazon backlog (first item processed in this category; global_done still 131 here just as an example).
2.2 Amazon phase – per‑queue‑item example
When resuming Amazon for Baby‑Boy‑Clothes with queue length total=8, pointer starting at ptr=0:

After committing the first queue item (queue_idx=0):

AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx=38 total=8 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
[RESUME_ENTER] cat_idx=38 total=8 ptr=0 start_batch=0 first_offset=0
[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx=0/8 cat_idx=38
AMAZON CATEGORY PROGRESS [baby-boy-clothes]: 1/8 (remaining=7, global_done=132)
1/8 = first Amazon queue item done for this category.
global_done=132 reflects amazon_products_completed after this commit.
Key guarantee you asked for:
Whenever the category’s indices are “clean” (no clamping), the new SUPPLIER PROGRESS STATUS and AMAZON CATEGORY PROGRESS ratios will use the same numerators/denominators as the existing [DENOM_FROZEN] and [PROGRESS_COMMIT queue_idx=.../total] lines. So when those internal numbers are correct, the new lines will show identical ratios in a more readable form.

3. State file additions (read‑only)
At the moment your state file already has:

"total_products": 3132,          // total cached products
"successful_products": 2327,     // linking_map entries
...
"system_progression": {
  "supplier_products_needing_extraction": 80,
  "supplier_products_completed": 2,
  "amazon_products_needing_analysis": 93,
  "amazon_products_completed": 131,
  ...
}
These already mean:

total_products = number of cached supplier products (length of angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json).
successful_products = number of linking map entries (processed products).
On top of these, we can safely add read‑only fields inside system_progression:

"supplier_progress_ratio_readable": "2/80",
"amazon_progress_ratio_readable": "1/93"
with the numerators/denominators computed exactly the same way as the logs above (and clamped locally to avoid inverted X/Y).

And, if you want explicit wording at the top of the state file, we can add two extra root‑level read‑only fields:

"cached_products_count_readable": 3132,
"linking_map_entries_readable": 2327,
These just mirror total_products and successful_products and do not change any logic.

Re‑ordering so system_progression appears right after metadata is also safe, because JSON dumping in Python respects insertion order; this affects only human reading, not runtime behavior.

4. Surgical implementation plan (diffs only, no edits applied yet)
All edits stay confined to tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py and the state‑manager save path. No existing counters, queues, or frozen denominators are changed; we only add logging and read‑only fields.

                if isinstance(authoritative_total, int) and authoritative_total > 0:

1. Changes in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py

All edits are read‑only (logging and summaries); no workflow logic was changed.

1.1 _create_product_progress_callback – supplier + Amazon readable logs

Inside the inner progress_callback, in the operation_type == "supplier_extraction" branch, I added a small block after the existing supplier index logs.

New behaviour per supplier product:

Still logs:

[DENOM_FROZEN] url=... total=DENOM (LOCKED)
?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE: Index updated to X/Y
?? PROGRESS: Product X processed ... (when product_data is present)
Now also logs:

SUPPLIER PROGRESS STATUS [baby-boy-clothes]: X/Y (remaining=Y-X)
AMAZON PROGRESS STATUS [baby-boy-clothes]: A/B (global_done=amazon_products_completed, remaining≈B-A)
where:

X/Y = the same supplier display index and denominator you already see in ?? SUPPLIER STATE UPDATE.

A/B =

B = amazon_products_needing_analysis for the current category.
A = clamped version of amazon_products_completed (0 ≤ A ≤ B), used only for this display (does not change state).
Slug [baby-boy-clothes] is derived by _generate_category_slug(current_category_url).

1.2 _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files – readable ratios in system_progression

Right after clamping the totals:

if supplier_total > 0 and supplier_completed > supplier_total:
    supplier_completed = supplier_total
if amazon_total > 0 and amazon_completed > amazon_total:
    amazon_completed = amazon_total
I added:

A read‑only category progress ratio:

cat_idx = int(
    sp.get("current_category_index")
    or sp.get("persistent_category_index")
    or start_category_index
    or 1
)
total_cats = int(
    sp.get("total_categories", 0)
    or self.state_manager._authoritative_total_categories()
    or 0
)
sp["category_progress"] = (
    f"{cat_idx}/{total_cats}" if total_cats > 0 else f"{cat_idx}/0"
)
Two readable per‑category phase ratios:

sp["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"] = f"{supplier_completed}/{supplier_total}"
sp["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"] = f"{amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}"
These three fields live under system_progression and mirror what the helper is using to decide the phase:

"category_progress": "38/326" – cat index / total categories.
"supplier_progress_ratio_readable": "2/80" – supplier done / needed for this category.
"amazon_progress_ratio_readable": "1/93" – Amazon done / needed for this category (after clamping).
They are read‑only and do not drive behaviour.

1.3 _run_amazon_phase_from_resume – Amazon category progress logs

After computing queue length and resume pointer and logging:

self.log.info(
    f" AMAZON RESUME: cat_idx={cat_idx} total={total} "
    f"start_batch={start_batch} first_offset={first_offset}"
)
self.log.info(
    f"[RESUME_ENTER] cat_idx={cat_idx} total={total} ptr={prod_offset} "
    f"start_batch={start_batch} first_offset={first_offset}"
)
I added a resume‑time summary:

AMAZON CATEGORY PROGRESS [baby-boy-clothes]: ptr/total (remaining=total-ptr, global_done=amazon_products_completed)
Where:

ptr = queue pointer for this category (0‑based, from prod_offset).
total = queue length for this category.
global_done = amazon_products_completed from state (global).
Inside the inner loop, just after:

self.log.info(f"[PROGRESS_COMMIT] queue_idx={queue_idx}/{total} cat_idx={cat_idx}")
if queue_idx == prod_offset:
    self.log.info(f"[RESUME_HONORED] queue_idx={queue_idx}")
I added per‑item category progress:

AMAZON CATEGORY PROGRESS [baby-boy-clothes]: (queue_idx+1)/total (remaining=total-(queue_idx+1))
So you now get:

Queue‑style detailed numbers (queue_idx/total) as before.
A clean 1‑based 1/8, 2/8, … line for the category’s Amazon queue.
Again, these are logs only; they don’t change queue behaviour.

2. Changes in utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py

All changes are about summaries; they do not alter how resumption or denominators work.

2.1 _initialize_state – add read‑only summary fields

In the top‑level state structure I added:

At root level:

"total_products": 0,
"cached_products_count_readable": 0,
...
"successful_products": 0,
"linking_map_entries_readable": 0,
These two “*_readable” fields are just mirrors of total_products (cache size) and successful_products (linking map entries). They start at 0 and get synced during startup analysis and any expensive save.

I did not move keys around beyond this; the system_progression block remains in the same place for existing files (reordering existing JSON would be intrusive).

2.2 perform_startup_analysis – keep summaries in sync

After startup computes file‑grounded totals:

self.state_data["total_products"] = file_grounded_data["total_products"]
self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"]
I added:

self.state_data["cached_products_count_readable"] = self.state_data["total_products"]
self.state_data["linking_map_entries_readable"] = self.state_data["successful_products"]
So on each fresh session startup, your processing state will show:

"total_products": 3132,
"cached_products_count_readable": 3132,
"successful_products": 2327,
"linking_map_entries_readable": 2327,
2.3 save_state – expensive path also updates readable summaries

In the “expensive path” where save recomputes file‑grounded totals during startup:

file_grounded_data = self._calculate_file_grounded_totals()
self.state_data["total_products"] = file_grounded_data["total_products"]
self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"]
I added the same mirror updates:

self.state_data["cached_products_count_readable"] = self.state_data["total_products"]
self.state_data["linking_map_entries_readable"] = self.state_data["successful_products"]
This keeps the read‑only counts aligned whenever totals are recomputed.

They no longer exist in _initialize_state, perform_startup_analysis, or the save path. Only the original canonical fields remain:

"successful_products": 2327,
