Here’s what’s actually going on, using all four sources together (cache, linking map, state, dashboard).

1. How I got 6 873 vs 5 614

I did direct JSON counts, not from any progress fields:

import json, pathlib
base = pathlib.Path.cwd()

cache = json.loads((base/"OUTPUTS/cached_products/angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json").read_text())
linking = json.loads((base/"OUTPUTS/FBA_ANALYSIS/linking_maps/angelwholesale.co.uk/linking_map.json").read_text())

len_cache   = len(cache)   # 6873
len_linking = len(linking) # 5614
From the live files right now:

LEN_cache = 6 873
LEN_linking = 5 614
Those are raw element counts in the arrays.

The state file and dashboard numbers are not what I used to compute those.

2. What each “critical” metric actually measures

Using the current angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:

Per‑category resume counters (current category 108):

"supplier_products_needing_extraction": 380,
"supplier_products_completed": 380,
"amazon_products_needing_analysis": 401,
"amazon_products_completed": 285
These are only for category 108: christmas-wreath-garlands-florals.
They say:

Supplier: backlog for this category is done (380/380).
Amazon: 285 of 401 queue entries for this category have been analyzed.
Global “processing_statistics” block:

"processing_statistics": {
    "total_products": 0,
    "successful_products": 0,
    ...
}
This block is effectively unused for Angel in this run (they’re 0). It is not wired to the real cache/linking counts.

Dashboard (from your screenshot):

Supplier Extracted: 380 / 380 → matches the per‑category supplier counters.
Amazon Analyzed: 276 / 401 → older snapshot of the same category counters (state has moved on to 285/401).
Total Extracted: 5 322
Total Processed: 5 605
These “Total Extracted/Processed” are computed by the dashboard aggregators, not by len(cache) / len(linking). They usually:

Deduplicate,
Possibly filter out entries with missing EAN/ASIN,
May only count rows that made it into financial CSVs.
Summary:

len(cache) / len(linking) → raw global counts of supplier vs Amazon entries.
supplier_products_* / amazon_products_* → per‑current‑category resume info for 108 only.
processing_statistics.* → currently dummy/zero.
Dashboard totals → aggregated, filtered view, not raw counts.
3. Cross‑checking actual progress (3‑way)

I compared cache vs linking map by URL to see the real pipeline coverage.

Code used:

from urllib.parse import urlsplit

def norm(u):
    parts = urlsplit(u or "")
    return parts._replace(query="", fragment="").geturl().lower()

cache_urls   = {norm(p.get("url")) for p in cache}
linking_urls = {norm(e.get("supplier_url") or e.get("url")) for e in linking}

len(cache_urls)      # 6873
len(linking_urls)    # 5613
len(linking_urls - cache_urls)  # 32
len(cache_urls - linking_urls)  # 1292
Findings:

Unique supplier URLs in cache: 6 873
Unique supplier URLs in linking map: 5 613 (one linking entry is a duplicate URL).
linking_urls − cache_urls = 32 URLs → linking map has 32 supplier URLs that are not in the current cache file.
cache_urls − linking_urls = 1 292 URLs → 1 292 supplier products that exist in cache but have no linking‑map row yet.
I then inspected some of the 32 “linking but no cache” URLs, e.g.:

https://angelwholesale.co.uk/item/blue-minky-dimple-fabric-comforter-with-satin-tags-by-soft-touch-gac0676
https://angelwholesale.co.uk/item/room-on-the-broom-bird-7inch-toy07338
...
These are almost all older Soft Touch / Aurora URLs, likely from early runs before the current cache file was rebuilt or before the URL normalization was stabilized. They are a very small fraction (32 / 5 614 ≈ 0.6%). They explain a tiny part of the discrepancy but not the main gap.

The 1 292 cache_urls missing in linking_urls are your true Amazon backlog:

Supplier products extracted and cached,
Not yet written to linking map (either not processed by Amazon yet, or processed in a way that doesn’t create a linking row—e.g. very early runs, or edge‑case failures).
This matches the qualitative picture from the log and state:

Supplier completed for the current category (380/380).
Amazon still working through backlog for that category and others.
Cache stopped growing once supplier backlog ran out; linking map continues to grow as Amazon catches up.
4. Why dashboard/state numbers differ from raw counts

Putting it all together:

Per‑category vs global

supplier_products_needing_extraction/completed and amazon_products_* are per current category only.
They will never match len(cache) / len(linking) which are global.
Dashboard “380 / 380” and “276 / 401” are showing the same per‑category values, not global totals.
Dashboard totals vs file lengths

At the time of your screenshot:

Dashboard “Total Extracted 5 322” vs len(cache)=6 873.
Dashboard “Total Processed 5 605” vs len(linking)=5 614.
Reasons:

Dashboard may only count:
Rows with valid supplier + Amazon data,
Rows included in the financial CSV,
Or deduplicated by key (e.g. (EAN, URL)), whereas cache is “all extraction attempts”.
The 7‑entry difference between 5 605 and 5 614 is consistent with either:
Time lag (dashboard snapshot slightly older), or
Filtering out a few problematic linking rows.
State processing_statistics.total_products == 0

That block is simply not wired for this run; the real counters used for routing are the per‑category ones under system_progression. So:

It’s expected that processing_statistics.total_products is not equal to len(cache) or dashboard totals.
The state file itself gives you:
Per‑category progress (current category 108),
Frozen denominators per category,
But no authoritative global per‑supplier count there.
5. What this tells you about “actual progress”

Grounded in all three sources:

Supplier stage (global):

Real count: len(cache) = 6 873 unique supplier products extracted.
That’s the best global “how many products extracted from Angel so far” metric.
Amazon stage (global):

Real count: len(linking) = 5 614 linking‑map rows.
Global Amazon backlog right now: about 6 873 − 5 614 ≈ 1 259 cached products still missing from linking map (minus the 32 historical linking‑only URLs).
Current category 108:

Supplier: 380/380 done (no more cache growth expected for this category).
Amazon: 285/401 done; 116 left in this category’s Amazon queue.
So the reason the “critical files” disagree numerically is:

They are counting different slices of the pipeline (per‑category vs per‑supplier vs filtered vs raw).
Some counters (processing_statistics, dashboard totals) are aggregated/filtered views, not raw counts.
A small historical inconsistency exists (32 linking entries with no matching cache entry), but the dominant gap is the expected Amazon backlog: cached supplier products that haven’t yet produced linking‑map entries.
If you want, the next step can be:

Quantify, per category, how many cached products lack linking‑map rows (to see exactly where the remaining ~1.2k backlog sits), or
Align the dashboard view with raw counts by adjusting its aggregation logic—but that would require code changes in the metrics/dashboard layer.

I’ll stick exactly to the two fields you mean.

1. What each number actually is (3 sources of truth)

Using the current files:

# Direct JSON counts
LEN_cache   = 6873   # OUTPUTS/cached_products/angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json
LEN_linking = 5614   # OUTPUTS/FBA_ANALYSIS/linking_maps/angelwholesale.co.uk/linking_map.json
From the state file angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json:

"total_products": 5322,
"successful_products": 4221,
"processing_statistics": {
  "total_products": 0,
  "successful_products": 0,
  ...
}
From the log at startup of this session (run_custom_poundwholesale_20251206_165232.log):

2025-12-06 16:52:37,591 - ... - File-grounded calculation: Found 5322 actual products in cache (total entries: 5322)
2025-12-06 16:52:37,626 - ... - File-grounded calculation: Found 4221 processed products in linking map
So:

5322 / 4221 are exactly the file-grounded snapshot at session start, written by the state manager.
6873 / 5614 are the current real counts in the files after more work was done.
processing_statistics.total_products/successful_products are not used (still 0).
2. Why the state “total_products/successful_products” differ from the file counts

Code in utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py explains it:

At startup, _calculate_file_grounded_totals() reads both files:

# cache
product_cache = json.load(cache_file_path)
actual_products = [p for p in product_cache if not p.get("_cache_metadata")]
file_grounded_data["total_products"] = len(actual_products)

# linking map
linking_map = json.load(linking_map_path)
file_grounded_data["linking_map_count"]  = len(linking_map)
file_grounded_data["processed_products"] = len(linking_map)
Then, during startup analysis, it writes a one-time snapshot into the root of the state:

self.state_data["total_products"]     = file_grounded_data["total_products"]     # 5322
self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"] # 4221
After startup, in the atomic save path:

if not preserve_interruption_state and not self._startup_completed:
    file_grounded_data = self._calculate_file_grounded_totals()
    self.state_data["total_products"]     = file_grounded_data["total_products"]
    self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"]
else:
    # Once _startup_completed == True, NEVER recompute file-grounded totals here
    log.debug("ATOMIC SAVE: runtime mode; skipping file-grounded recompute")
So:

File‑grounded totals are only recomputed before _startup_completed is set.
All later saves skip recomputation and keep the same 5322 / 4221 snapshot.
Meanwhile, the workflow continues:

Supplier extraction adds more entries to the cache → len(cache) grows to 6873.
Amazon analysis adds more entries to linking_map → len(linking) grows to 5614.
Because _calculate_file_grounded_totals() is never called again during runtime, the state’s:

total_products stays frozen at 5322.
successful_products stays frozen at 4221.
That’s exactly why:

State root totals ≠ current file counts.
Dashboard (which reads those state fields) shows the older snapshot, not the live 6873 / 5614.
3. How this compares to “actual progress”

Right now:

Actual supplier products extracted (from file): 6 873.
Actual Amazon linking rows (from file): 5 614.
State snapshot at session start: 5 322 / 4 221 (correct at 2025‑12‑06 16:52, but stale now).
Dashboard “Total Extracted/Processed” is based on that snapshot (plus its own filtering), so it’s also behind reality.
So the discrepancy isn’t that the files are wrong; it’s that:

total_products / successful_products in the state are frozen startup metrics, by design,
and not updated as cache and linking map grow during the run.

Here’s the precise picture of what’s wrong, why, and how to fix it.

1. What each place is reporting (with sources)

Directly printed:

ROOT_total_products        = 5322
ROOT_successful_products   = 4221
processing_statistics.total_products      = 0
processing_statistics.successful_products = 0
So the state claims:

total_products = 5 322
successful_products = 4 221
Logs at session startup (this run)
logs/debug/run_custom_poundwholesale_20251206_165232.log:

total_extracted = state_metrics.get("total_products", 0)       # Total Extracted
So:

Total Extracted (5 322) comes from state root total_products.
Total Processed (5 605) comes from linking_metrics.total_matches (i.e. length of linking_map at that time, ≈ 5 605).
Summary of discrepancies:

True file counts now: 6 873 / 5 614
Startup snapshot (state/log): 5 322 / 4 221
Dashboard at screenshot time: 5 322 / 5 605
No single place is currently showing a fully up‑to‑date pair of counts.

2. Root causes (with code references)
2.1 State total_products / successful_products are frozen at startup
In utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py:

At startup, this runs:
file_grounded_data = self._calculate_file_grounded_totals()
Inside _calculate_file_grounded_totals:

# Cache
with open(cache_file_path) as f:
    product_cache = json.load(f)
actual_products = [p for p in product_cache if not p.get("_cache_metadata")]
file_grounded_data["total_products"] = len(actual_products)

# Linking map
with open(linking_map_path) as f:
    linking_map = json.load(f)
file_grounded_data["linking_map_count"]  = len(linking_map)
file_grounded_data["processed_products"] = len(linking_map)
Then, still during startup:
self.state_data["total_products"]      = file_grounded_data["total_products"]
self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"]
That’s where 5322 / 4221 come from; they were correct at 2025‑12‑06 16:52.

In the atomic save path:
if not preserve_interruption_state and not self._startup_completed:
    file_grounded_data = self._calculate_file_grounded_totals()
    self.state_data["total_products"]      = file_grounded_data["total_products"]
    self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"]
else:
    if self._startup_completed:
        log.debug(" ATOMIC SAVE: runtime mode; skipping file-grounded recompute")
Crucial detail:

Once _startup_completed is set to True, this path never recomputes file‑grounded totals again.

All later saves log:

ATOMIC SAVE: runtime mode; skipping file-grounded recompute
So:

state.total_products and state.successful_products are one‑time startup snapshots, not live counters.
As the run continues and cache & linking map grow, those fields do not change.
That’s why:

State still says 5322 / 4221,
But current files are 6873 / 5614.
2.2 Dashboard mixes two different sources for totals
From dashboard/app_fixed.py:

# Category progress uses per-category counters — fine:
sup_done = cp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0)
sup_need = cp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0)
amz_done = cp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)
amz_need = cp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0)

# Total Stats
linking_total   = linking_metrics.get("total_matches", 0)   # matches = len(linking_map)
total_extracted = state_metrics.get("total_products", 0)    # from state.total_products
fresh_starts    = state_metrics.get("fresh_starts", 0)
So:

Total Extracted on the dashboard always shows the frozen startup state.total_products (5322).
Total Processed always shows the live linking_map count (5605 at screenshot time, now 5614).
That produces:

Dashboard Total Extracted = 5 322 (correct at startup; stale afterwards).
Dashboard Total Processed = live linking length (5 605 at screenshot, ~5 614 now).
This is an intentional design decision in app_fixed.py, but it’s exactly why the two numbers disagree with each other and with the “real” cache size later.

2.3 processing_statistics.total_products/successful_products are unused
_initialize_state sets:

"processing_statistics": {
    "total_products": 0,
    "successful_products": 0,
    ...
}
And the only place that ever updates totals is the root (self.state_data["total_products"], "successful_products"). The nested processing_statistics counters remain 0 and are never read for Angel.

So:

Those nested fields are dead / legacy; they don’t drive dashboard or logs.
2.4 A small historical drift: 32 linking URLs with no matching cache entry
Cross‑checking URLs:

cache_urls   = {norm(p["url"]) for p in cache}
linking_urls = {norm(e["supplier_url"] or e["url"]) for e in linking}

len(linking_urls - cache_urls)  # 32
len(cache_urls - linking_urls)  # 1292
Some examples of URLs in linking but not in the current cache:

.../blue-minky-dimple-fabric-comforter-with-satin-tags...
.../room-on-the-broom-bird-7inch-toy07338
etc.
These 32 are almost certainly:

From earlier runs before the current cache file was rebuilt, or
From categories reloaded differently.
They explain a tiny part of the disagreement between “processed” views (state vs dashboard vs raw files), but the dominant discrepancy is still frozen state totals vs growing files.

3. Concrete issues, summarized
State root total_products / successful_products are not live:

Correct at startup (5322 / 4221) but never updated as cache/linking grow.
Any consumer that expects them to represent current progress is misled.
Dashboard “Total Extracted” vs “Total Processed” use inconsistent sources:

Extracted = stale state snapshot (5322).
Processed = live len(linking_map) (~5.6k).
So the two numbers drift apart over time even if the pipeline is healthy.
Nested processing_statistics.* and some “total_products” mentions in docs are legacy:

They stay 0 and do not reflect anything real.
They add to the confusion when comparing different parts of the state file.
Minor data drift (32 URLs in linking but not in cache):

Historical residue, not damaging the current run.
It means “successful products” as len(linking_map) can be slightly larger than the count of current cache URLs; this is expected until you do a full clean/reset.
4. Suggested fix plan (surgical, with diffs)
Goal: make the three main views agree on the live totals, without changing any resume logic or category pointers.

4.1 Make state totals reflect live file counts on each startup and at safe checkpoints
Idea: Keep the existing _calculate_file_grounded_totals, but expose a helper that you can call at:

Startup (already done), and
End‑of‑run (and optionally on manual checkpoints).
This keeps changes inside the state manager, and avoids touching resumption logic.

Diff 1 — add a public refresh helper in utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py

Add below _calculate_file_grounded_totals (after line ~2581):

*** Update File: utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py
@@
     def _calculate_file_grounded_totals(self) -> Dict[str, Any]:
         """
         Calculate all totals by reading actual files on disk
         Returns accurate counts based on real-time file contents
         """
@@
             return file_grounded_data
         except Exception as e:
             log.warning(f"File-grounded totals calculation failed: {e}")
             return file_grounded_data
+
+    def refresh_global_totals_for_display(self) -> None:
+        """
+        Recalculate `total_products` and `successful_products` from the
+        current cache and linking map files.
+
+        This is *display-only* and must never modify resume pointers or
+        category-level progress. Safe to call at startup and at end-of-run.
+        """
+        try:
+            file_grounded_data = self._calculate_file_grounded_totals()
+            self.state_data["total_products"] = file_grounded_data.get("total_products", 0)
+            self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data.get("processed_products", 0)
+            # Do not touch system_progression or any pointer fields.
+            self.save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True)
+            log.info(
+                "DISPLAY TOTALS REFRESHED: total_products=%s successful_products=%s",
+                self.state_data.get("total_products"),
+                self.state_data.get("successful_products"),
+            )
+        except Exception as e:
+            log.warning(f"Failed to refresh display totals from files: {e}")
This helper:

Uses the same file‑grounded logic as startup.
Only updates total_products / successful_products.
Calls save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True) so resume pointers are unchanged.
Diff 2 — call the helper at end of workflow in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py

In PassiveExtractionWorkflow.run() (or whichever main entry finishes the end‑to‑end workflow), near the very end, before returning:

*** Update File: tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py
@@
     async def run(self) -> None:
         ...
         # Existing finalization / reports code here
         ...
+        # Refresh global totals once at end-of-run so state and dashboard
+        # reflect the final cache/linking_map counts.
+        try:
+            if hasattr(self, "state_manager"):
+                self.state_manager.refresh_global_totals_for_display()
+        except Exception:
+            self.log.warning("Failed to refresh global totals at end-of-run", exc_info=True)
Effect:

After a run completes, state root total_products / successful_products will be updated to the latest len(cache) / len(linking_map).
The next time you start the workflow (or just refresh the dashboard), the state and dashboard will show the final totals, not the old 5322 / 4221 snapshot.
This is surgical:

No change to how resume pointers work.
No change to category progress.
Only metrics fields are touched.
4.2 Make dashboard use consistent sources for both totals
Right now app_fixed.py mixes state for “Total Extracted” and linking metrics for “Total Processed”. With Fix 4.1, state will match the files at startup and at the end of runs, but during a long run the dashboard can still drift.

Safest low‑risk improvement:

Keep “Total Processed” = linking_map count (as today).
Change “Total Extracted” to use the same file‑grounded source that metrics already computed, instead of the stale state snapshot.
If linking_metrics already contains supplier_total or similar (it does in app.py), you can wire that in. In app_fixed.py:

Current:

linking_total   = linking_metrics.get("total_matches", 0) if linking_metrics else 0
total_extracted = state_metrics.get("total_products", 0)
Proposed:

*** Update File: dashboard/app_fixed.py
@@
-        linking_total = linking_metrics.get("total_matches", 0) if linking_metrics else 0
-        # Use total_products from state as "Total Extracted" if available, else fallback
-        total_extracted = state_metrics.get("total_products", 0)
+        linking_total = linking_metrics.get("total_matches", 0) if linking_metrics else 0
+        # Prefer file-grounded supplier_total from metrics when available;
+        # fall back to state snapshot for backwards compatibility.
+        supplier_total = linking_metrics.get("supplier_total", 0) if linking_metrics else 0
+        total_extracted = supplier_total or state_metrics.get("total_products", 0)
And leave the markdown:

<p>Total Extracted: <strong>{format_number(total_extracted)}</strong></p>
<p>Total Processed: <strong>{format_number(linking_total)}</strong></p>
Effect:

After metrics_core computes live file‑based counts (supplier_total and total_matches), dashboard will show both numbers from the same live source.
If for some reason supplier_total is missing, it falls back to state.total_products, which is now refreshed at least at startup and end‑of‑run.
If supplier_total is not yet exposed in linking_metrics, the same approach can be taken by having the dashboard read the cache directly once per refresh (small overhead for <10k rows); I haven’t changed that here to keep the diff minimal.

4.3 Optional: remove or clearly mark legacy processing_statistics.* totals
Since processing_statistics.total_products and processing_statistics.successful_products are never updated or used for Angel:

Either leave them as legacy (but ignore them everywhere),
Or, if you want clarity, set them from refresh_global_totals_for_display() as read‑only mirrors:
@@ def refresh_global_totals_for_display(...):
-            self.state_data["total_products"] = file_grounded_data.get("total_products", 0)
-            self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data.get("processed_products", 0)
+            total = file_grounded_data.get("total_products", 0)
+            processed = file_grounded_data.get("processed_products", 0)
+            self.state_data["total_products"] = total
+            self.state_data["successful_products"] = processed
+            ps = self.state_data.get("processing_statistics", {})
+            ps["total_products"] = total
+            ps["successful_products"] = processed
This makes every “total_products / successful_products” field in the state agree with the file‑grounded truth at the times you call the helper.

5. After applying these fixes, what you’ll see
At each new session startup:

Log prints File-grounded calculation: Found N actual products in cache, Found M processed products in linking map.
State root total_products / successful_products will be set to those N / M.
Dashboard “Total Extracted” and “Total Processed” will match those values.
At end of the long run:

refresh_global_totals_for_display() runs once:

Re-reads cache & linking_map.
Updates total_products / successful_products and optional mirrors.
The next time you inspect the state file or refresh the dashboard, the totals will match:

total_products ≈ len(cache)
successful_products ≈ len(linking_map)
During the run:

1. Cache totals in the log vs file

Current file:

cache = json.load(open(...angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json...))
len(cache)  # 6873
So for cache:

Log “6873 products to cache” and “6873 total products extracted” exactly match the current file length.
Earlier log lines like “5383 total products accumulated”, “5582 total products accumulated” etc. were correct at those times; they are incremental snapshots as more categories finished.
The wrong value in your mental model is the state/dashboard snapshot 5322:

At 2025‑12‑06 16:52, the state manager ran _calculate_file_grounded_totals() and wrote:

File-grounded calculation: Found 5322 actual products in cache
and set state.total_products = 5322.

After that, it never recomputed this field during runtime, by design. So state and dashboard stayed at 5322, even though the cache later grew to 6873. That’s the discrepancy.

Conclusion:
For cache, log and file are correct and in sync. The stale numbers are the state root total_products and dashboard “Total Extracted”, which are frozen at the startup snapshot.

2. Linking‑map totals in the log vs file
You quoted:

2025-12-07 20:17:06,023 - PassiveExtractionWorkflow - INFO - ?? HASH INDEX BUILT: 5221 EANs, 5665 URLs, 4128 ASINs in 0.173s

Current file:

linking = json.load(open(...linking_map.json...))
len(linking)  # 5666
Inside _save_linking_map:

len(self.linking_map) is the number of entries in the linking map file; this is what:

self.log.info(f"DEBUG: _save_linking_map called with {len(self.linking_map)} entries ...)
self.log.info(f"Successfully saved linking map with {len(self.linking_map)} entries ...")
self.log.info(f"HASH INDEXES REBUILT: Updated indexes for {len(self.linking_map)} entries")
are logging → 5666 entries now.

The other line:

5221 EANs, 5665 URLs, 4128 ASINs
comes from the hash optimizer / sentinel counting unique keys, not entries:

5221 EANs → distinct EANs among the 5666 rows.
5665 URLs → distinct supplier URLs (one duplicate URL).
5666 entries → total rows in linking_map.json.
So for linking_map:

Log “5666 entries” matches the file (len(linking) == 5666).
The “5665 URLs” is unique URLs, not a mismatch.
Like cache, earlier logs:

File-grounded calculation: Found 4221 processed products in linking map
were correct at startup; they matched len(linking) at that time. They’re just older snapshots.

Again, the stale values are:

state.successful_products = 4221 (frozen at startup), and
Dashboard’s “Total Processed = 5605” was taken from metrics at an earlier moment.
3. What changed over time (why your numbers don’t line up)
Putting all three sources together:

At startup of this session (16:52):

Cache size: 5322.
Linking map size: 4221.
Logs print those via file‑grounded calculation.
State root total_products/successful_products are set to 5322 / 4221.
Dashboard later reads these as “Total Extracted”.
During the run:

Cache grows to 6873, linking map to 5666.
Logs show the incremental totals correctly (… 6873 total products accumulated, … linking_map with 5666 entries).
State root totals are never updated again (design: _startup_completed prevents recompute during runtime).
Dashboard “Total Processed” comes from live linking metrics (so it roughly follows linking_map), but “Total Extracted” stays stuck at the old 5322.
Now (current moment):

Truth: cache 6873, linking 5666.
Log: latest lines show 6873 / 5666 — correct.
State root: still 5322 / 4221 — stale.
Dashboard: shows 5322 / (whatever total_matches was at last refresh) — partially stale.
So the log is not lying; each line is correct at the time it’s emitted, and the final totals match the current files. The inconsistent view comes from:

State root total_products and successful_products being frozen, not live.
Dashboard mixing:
frozen state for “Total Extracted”,
live metrics for “Total Processed”.
4. Does this change the earlier root causes / fixes?
No new structural bug appears from the log analysis:

Cache: log totals and file length agree.

Linking map: “URLs vs entries” is just unique vs total; entries count in log matches file length.

The only real issue is still how the global counts are stored and consumed:

State root totals are one‑time snapshots taken at startup.
Dashboard uses those snapshots for “Total Extracted”.
Dashboard uses live linking metrics for “Total Processed”.
The fix plan I gave before directly targets this:

Add refresh_global_totals_for_display() in FixedEnhancedStateManager to recompute total_products and successful_products from the files (cache + linking map) and write them back to state at safe points (startup/end‑of‑run).
Switch the dashboard’s “Total Extracted” to use the same file‑grounded metrics (or at least the refreshed state.total_products) rather than the stale initial snapshot.
With that:

State root totals will eventually equal len(cache) / len(linking_map).
Dashboard “Total Extracted / Total Processed” will come from the same, up‑to‑date source.
The log will remain the ground truth history and already matches the final file counts.
If you want, the next step is to actually apply those diffs so your dashboard and state stop drifting away from what the log and files clearly show.

There are two different “index” paths now; they behave differently.

1. Global totals (total_products / successful_products)

Source of truth: cache file + linking_map file.

When the helper I proposed (refresh_global_totals_for_display) runs, it:

Calls the same _calculate_file_grounded_totals() that you already see in the log lines:
“File-grounded calculation: Found 6873 actual products in cache…”
“File-grounded calculation: Found 5666 processed products in linking map…”
Writes those numbers into:
state.total_products
state.successful_products
Saves the state.
So for these global totals:

They are recomputed from the files when the helper is called (startup, end‑of‑run), not on every product.
They are not “mirroring a log line”; both the log lines and the state fields come from the same file‑grounded calculation.
2. Per‑category ratios (supplier_products_*, amazon_products_*, readable ratios)

Source of truth: the counters in system_progression:
supplier_products_needing_extraction / completed
amazon_products_needing_analysis / completed
These counters are updated incrementally by the workflow as products are processed.
_refresh_human_progress_fields() just mirrors those counters into:
system_progression.category_progress, *_progress_ratio_readable
and the top‑level category_progress, supplier_progress_ratio_readable, amazon_progress_ratio_readable.
No heavy recalculation there; it’s just formatting existing counters.
So:

Global totals: occasionally recalculated from cache + linking_map (same logic that prints the file‑grounded log lines).
Per‑category progress/ratios: continuously mirror the live counters already maintained by the workflow; no re‑scanning of files.

Short, direct answers:

1. After the fixes, how are the totals updated?

The plan I described uses the same calculation that already prints the correct numbers in the log (“File‑grounded calculation: Found N actual products in cache / M processed products in linking map”).
That logic lives in _calculate_file_grounded_totals() and already:
Reads the cache file → counts products.
Reads the linking map → counts entries.
The new helper refresh_global_totals_for_display() just calls that existing function again and writes the results into:
state["total_products"]
state["successful_products"]
So it does not invent new math; it reuses the exact same snippet that is already proven by the log to be correct.
2. Is this every 1 product or only at checkpoints?

Per‑category indexes (supplier_products_*, amazon_products_* and the readable ratios) are already updated effectively every product by the workflow; our previous mirroring helper just copies those counters into the “pretty” fields and state on each progress tick. That behaviour stays.
The global totals (total_products / successful_products in the state, and the dashboard “Total Extracted / Total Processed”) are not updated every product in my plan:
They are refreshed only when we explicitly call refresh_global_totals_for_display() (e.g., at startup and/or at end‑of‑run).
Each refresh is a quick re‑read of cache + linking_map, using the same logic that emits the file‑grounded log lines.
If you want those global totals to move on every single product, that would require a different (and more invasive) change: incrementing the state counters in the supplier/Amazon progress callbacks. I have not proposed that because it’s higher‑risk and touches more code.

3. Does this respect your “surgical / minimal change” requirement?

Yes:

No new algorithms; we reuse _calculate_file_grounded_totals(), which already exists and already drives the correct log output.
We add:
One small wrapper method in FixedEnhancedStateManager that calls that function and writes the results into existing fields.
One call site (e.g. at end of run()), plus a tiny dashboard tweak to read the refreshed totals.
We do not touch:
Resume pointers,
Category‑level progress,
Dedup logic,
Or per‑product update paths.
So:

Global totals in state/dashboard become mirrors of the same file‑grounded values you already see in the log, but only at defined checkpoints (startup / end‑of‑run), not on every product.
Per‑category progress indexes continue to update every product as they do now.

High‑level behaviour after these fixes

HASH INDEXES REBUILT: Updated indexes for 5666 entries
Updates happen whenever those files are saved (in your current config: effectively every product), via state_manager.save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True), which:

does not rerun file‑grounded totals.
does not modify resume pointers, frozen denominators, or per‑category counters.
only persists the updated total_products / successful_products numbers.
Dashboard already reads total_products from the state and total_matches from the linking map, so it will automatically show the correct live totals once these mirrors are in place.

No other workflow logic is changed.

2. Behaviour now

After the next cache save, root total_products in angelwholesale_co_uk_processing_state.json will equal the cache length you see in logs (len(all_products) / “ATOMICALLY saved ... products to cache”) and will continue to track it on each subsequent save.
After the next linking map save, root successful_products will equal len(self.linking_map) (the same number logged in “Successfully saved linking map with N entries…” / “HASH INDEXES REBUILT: Updated indexes for N entries”) and will track it going forward.
These updates use save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True), so:
No file‑grounded recompute is triggered (startup is already completed).
Resume pointers, frozen denominators, per‑category supplier/amazon counters, and phase logic are untouched.

utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py:

save_state() and _perform_atomic_save() still gate recomputation:

if not preserve_interruption_state and not self._startup_completed:
    file_grounded_data = self._calculate_file_grounded_totals()
    self.state_data["total_products"] = file_grounded_data["total_products"]
    self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"]

Your new calls use preserve_interruption_state=True, so they do not trigger file‑grounded recompute and do not touch resume pointers.

2. Per‑category readable ratios – where they come from and why they can look “stuck”

2.1 Current state values vs counters

From the state file:

category_progress = "110/326"
supplier_progress_ratio_readable = "14/15"
amazon_progress_ratio_readable   = "49/64"

system_progression = {
    "current_phase": "amazon_analysis",
    "persistent_category_index": 110,
    "current_category_index": 110,
    "supplier_products_needing_extraction": 15,
    "supplier_products_completed": 14,
    "amazon_products_needing_analysis": 64,
    "amazon_products_completed": 53,

So:

Supplier counters: 14/15 → ratio string 14/15 → consistent.
Amazon counters: 53/64 → but ratio string is 49/64 → stale (lagging behind by 4).
2.2 How the ratio strings are computed

In tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:

def _refresh_human_progress_fields(self) -> None:
    root_state = self.state_manager.state_data
    sp = root_state.get("system_progression", {}) or {}

    # Category index / total categories
    cat_idx = int(
        sp.get("current_category_index")
        or sp.get("persistent_category_index")
        or 1
    )
    total_cats = int(sp.get("total_categories", 0) or 0)
    ...

    # Live counters
    supplier_total     = int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
    supplier_completed = int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0)
    amazon_total       = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
    amazon_completed   = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)

    # Clamp for display only
    if supplier_total > 0 and supplier_completed > supplier_total:
        supplier_completed = supplier_total
    if amazon_total > 0 and amazon_completed > amazon_total:
        amazon_completed = amazon_total

    cat_ratio      = f"{cat_idx}/{total_cats}" if total_cats > 0 else f"{cat_idx}/0"
    supplier_ratio = f"{supplier_completed}/{supplier_total}"
    amazon_ratio   = f"{amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}"

    # Write both into system_progression and also mirrored at the top level
    sp["category_progress"]                = cat_ratio
    sp["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"] = supplier_ratio
    sp["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"]   = amazon_ratio

    root_state["category_progress"]                = cat_ratio
    root_state["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"] = supplier_ratio
    root_state["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"]   = amazon_ratio
So when _refresh_human_progress_fields() runs, the ratio strings mirror the numeric counters in system_progression.

2.3 When _refresh_human_progress_fields() runs

Callers:

In _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files (resume helper at startup):

# After normalizing the counters for phase decision:
self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
In the supplier progress callback:

if operation_type == "supplier_extraction":
    ...
    self.log.info("SUPPLIER PROGRESS STATUS [...]")
    self.log.info("AMAZON PROGRESS STATUS [...]")
    ...
    # After supplier progress commit, keep ratio fields in sync.
    self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
In the Amazon progress callback:

elif operation_type == "amazon_analysis":
    if show_amazon_progress:
        self.log.info("AMAZON ANALYSIS: Processing queue index ...")

    # Amazon and supplier progress both update shared counters...
    self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
2.4 Why amazon_progress_ratio_readable can lag

Your current situation (53/64 counters vs 49/64 ratio) tells us:

system_progression["amazon_products_completed"] has been updated at least 4 times without _refresh_human_progress_fields() running afterwards.
Looking at the code, one clear example is the Amazon resume gating:

# In the Amazon resume path
sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
raw_completed = sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)
saved_amazon_offset = ...
clamped_amazon_offset = ...

if total == 0:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset >= total:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
else:
    prod_offset = saved_amazon_offset
This block mutates amazon_products_completed (e.g. clamping or resetting it) but does not call _refresh_human_progress_fields() afterwards.
So if you:

Resume a category,
Gating logic adjusts amazon_products_completed,
Then no Amazon items are actually processed (or progress logging is disabled),
the counters change, but the human‑readable string fields remain “stuck” at the last time _refresh_human_progress_fields() ran (for example, at 337/401).

That matches what you saw: the ratios are display‑only snapshots, and can lag when counters are changed in code paths that don’t then refresh the strings.

Important: Actual work (what gets processed or skipped) uses:

the queue built from cache + linking map,
the resume offset computed in that function,
not the amazon_progress_ratio_readable string. So a stale string does not cause skipped or double‑processed products on its own; it’s purely an observability issue.

3. Summary of discrepancies and root causes

3.1 Global totals

Past issue:

State total_products and successful_products were set once in perform_startup_analysis() from _calculate_file_grounded_totals() and then never updated.
Files and logs kept growing (cache and linking map), but state and dashboard stayed frozen on the startup snapshot.
Root cause (code):

Only assignments:

self.state_data["total_products"]     = file_grounded_data["total_products"]
self.state_data["successful_products"] = file_grounded_data["processed_products"]
both inside startup analysis / startup save path.

Fix now in place (surgical, mirroring only):

After every successful cache save, mirror len(all_products) into state["total_products"] and save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True).
After every successful linking map save, mirror len(self.linking_map) into state["successful_products"] and save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True).
Current state (verified with 3 sources):

total_products aligns exactly with cache len and the “ATOMICALLY saved … products to cache” logs.
successful_products is tracking linking map len and “Successfully saved linking map with N entries” logs; a one‑off lag can happen when you read the state file between the last LM save and the next mirror, but the structure is correct and no longer frozen at an old value.
3.2 Readable ratios

What they represent now:

They are per‑resume‑category, not global, and reflect the last time _refresh_human_progress_fields() ran:

supplier_progress_ratio_readable = supplier_completed / supplier_total for current category.
amazon_progress_ratio_readable = clamped(amazon_completed,total) / amazon_total.
category_progress = current_category_index / total_categories.
Discrepancy you observed (e.g. 337/401 “stuck”):

Counters in system_progression can be updated by:

supplier/amazon commits via state manager (commit_supplier_progress, commit_amazon_progress),
resume gating (e.g. clamping amazon_products_completed),
other safety clamps,
but _refresh_human_progress_fields() is only called from:

the resume helper (_re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files),
the supplier progress callback,
the Amazon progress callback.
When counters change in paths outside those three (e.g. Amazon resume clamp) and no further products are processed, the ratio strings don’t get refreshed and look “stuck”.

Root cause in code:

There is no single “after any counter change, update the pretty string” hook; instead, only some paths do it.
Specifically, the Amazon resume safety logic changes amazon_products_completed without calling _refresh_human_progress_fields() afterwards.
4. Suggested fixes (surgical, minimal)

I’m not applying these now; this is the plan.

4.1 Global totals

The mirroring we just added is already the minimal, low‑risk fix you asked for:

It reuses the exact counts already being logged.
It does not add new recomputation or touch resume logic.
It operates only inside the success branches of the existing atomic save functions.
No further structural change is needed; if you want to eliminate even the occasional off‑by‑one timing window, the only additional tweak (still surgical) would be:

Add one more mirror call in a “session shutdown” path (e.g. at the very end of the run) to force sync, but that’s optional.
4.2 Readable ratios

To make supplier_progress_ratio_readable and amazon_progress_ratio_readable always match the numeric counters, even when counters are adjusted by resume safety code, we can do:

After any place that mutates:

sp["supplier_products_completed"]
or sp["amazon_products_completed"]
without going through the existing progress callback,
call _refresh_human_progress_fields().

The most important spot (from the snippet we saw) is the Amazon resume safety logic:

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
raw_completed = sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)
...
if total == 0:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset >= total:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
else:
    prod_offset = saved_amazon_offset
Surgical fix plan for ratios (conceptual diff):

Add, at the end of that block (after we set sp["amazon_products_completed"]), a call to _refresh_human_progress_fields():

# After deciding prod_offset and possibly mutating amazon_products_completed
self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
This uses the same counters that already exist; it does not recompute anything from files.

It’s display‑only: _refresh_human_progress_fields() only writes the human‑readable strings; it doesn’t touch resume pointers or denominators.

If you like, I can next generate the exact diff for that ratio‑refresh fix only, keeping it as small and isolated as the totals mirrors we just added.

Bottom line

The global totals problem (cached products vs state vs dashboard) is now fixed at the root by mirroring from the values that are already correct in the logs and files.
The ratio fields issue is not that the system is “skipping work”; it’s that the pretty strings can lag the real counters when those counters are changed in code paths that don’t refresh the display fields. That’s a display/observability bug, not a workflow bug, and can be fixed with one or two targeted _refresh_human_progress_fields() calls in the resume/clamp paths.

    )

        ...

There are no METRICS MIRROR FAILED warnings in the log, so the mirror code isn’t erroring out.
So with the current log:

The linking map index is not “removed” – it’s still logged every save.
The state is trailing by one entry because we always write the LM file, then update the state. If you sample the state between those two operations (or before the next mirror), you can get a one‑entry lag.
Structurally, though, the code now does exactly what you wanted: it mirrors len(self.linking_map) into the state on every successful LM save, using the same count logged.

3. Readable ratios – why some look wrong or frozen
This is the more confusing part. Again, I’ll use code + state + log.

3.1 How the ratio fields are computed
tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:

def _refresh_human_progress_fields(self) -> None:
    root_state = self.state_manager.state_data
    sp = root_state.get("system_progression", {}) or {}

    # Category index
    cat_idx = int(
        sp.get("current_category_index")
        or sp.get("persistent_category_index")
        or 1
    )
    total_cats = int(sp.get("total_categories", 0) or 0)

    # Live counters
    supplier_total     = int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
    supplier_completed = int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0)
    amazon_total       = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
    amazon_completed   = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)

    # Clamp completed for display only
    if supplier_total > 0 and supplier_completed > supplier_total:
        supplier_completed = supplier_total
    if amazon_total > 0 and amazon_completed > amazon_total:
        amazon_completed = amazon_total

    cat_ratio      = f"{cat_idx}/{total_cats}" if total_cats > 0 else f"{cat_idx}/0"
    supplier_ratio = f"{supplier_completed}/{supplier_total}"
    amazon_ratio   = f"{amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}"

    sp["category_progress"]                = cat_ratio
    sp["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"] = supplier_ratio
    sp["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"]   = amazon_ratio

    root_state["category_progress"]                = cat_ratio
    root_state["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"] = supplier_ratio
    root_state["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"]   = amazon_ratio
So whenever _refresh_human_progress_fields() runs, the three pretty strings are direct mirrors of the numeric counters in system_progression.

3.2 When _refresh_human_progress_fields() runs (latest code)
Call sites (from the same file):

At resume phase recalculation:

# inside _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files
# after reading and clamping totals/completed
self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
On each supplier progress tick:

if operation_type == "supplier_extraction":
    ...
    self.log.info("SUPPLIER PROGRESS STATUS [...]")
    self.log.info("AMAZON PROGRESS STATUS [...]")
    ...
    # After supplier progress commit, keep ratio fields in sync.
    self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
On each Amazon progress tick:

elif operation_type == "amazon_analysis":
    if show_amazon_progress:
        self.log.info("AMAZON ANALYSIS: Processing queue index ...")

    # Amazon and supplier progress both update shared counters...
    self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
So in normal flow:

Every time a supplier product is processed, or an Amazon queue entry is processed, _refresh_human_progress_fields() runs and updates the ratios from the counters.
3.3 Where counters change without refreshing the ratios
This is where the “stuck” values come from.

Example: Amazon resume safety logic at category entry (lines ~8016+):

sp = self.state_manager.state_data.get("system_progression", {})
raw_completed = sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0)
saved_amazon_offset = ...
clamped_amazon_offset = min(max(saved_amazon_offset, 0), total)

if total == 0:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset >= total:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
else:
    prod_offset = saved_amazon_offset
This mutates amazon_products_completed (for the whole category) before any queue item is processed.
But it does not call _refresh_human_progress_fields() afterwards.
If the system then exits, or doesn’t process any Amazon items in that category, the numeric counters and the readable strings diverge.
That’s exactly what we see in the current state for category index 110:

Counters:

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 64
amazon_products_completed        = 53
Readable string:

amazon_progress_ratio_readable   = "49/64"
So:

Some later operation (e.g. resume safety, or a manual state edit) pushed amazon_products_completed to 53,
But _refresh_human_progress_fields() was still reflecting an earlier value (49).
In the log, you also see resume pointers for christmas‑wreath‑garlands‑florals:

RESUME PTR: phase=amazon_analysis cat_idx=108/326 ... prod_idx=337/401
...
[RESUME_PHASE_CONFIRMED] ... supplier=380/380, amazon=337/401, url=https://angelwholesale.co.uk/category/christmas-wreath-garlands-florals-
Those 337/401 values are resume pointers from the state manager, not the pretty amazon_progress_ratio_readable field. They come from resumption_index and the frozen queue size, not from the readable ratios. That’s why you can see:

A scary prod_idx=337/401 in the log at startup,
A separate amazon_progress_ratio_readable string in the state (e.g. 49/64),
And they don’t have to match: they’re different layers of progress reporting.
4. Concrete issues and root causes (as of this latest log)
4.1 Global totals
Issue (historical):

State total_products / successful_products were set once at startup from _calculate_file_grounded_totals() and then never updated; they diverged from the real cache/linking map and dashboard showed wrong totals.
Current state (with latest log + files):

total_products now tracks cache (6884) exactly, proven by:
_save_products_to_cache mirror code,
log “ATOMICALLY saved 6884 products …”,
len(cache_file) == 6884,
state["total_products"] == 6884.
successful_products is tracking linking map, but your snapshot caught it one entry behind (5669 vs 5670) because the last linking map save (5670) happened after the previous mirror write.
Root cause of any lingering discrepancy:

There is now a small timing window between:
writing the new linking map (and logging 5670),
and the next time you happen to open the state file or dashboard.
Structurally, though, the state is now driven by the same counts as the logs and files, via the minimal mirror we added. There’s no long‑term “stuck at 5666” anymore; it’s just possible to sample between two updates.
4.2 Readable ratios
Issue:

The string fields:

category_progress
supplier_progress_ratio_readable
amazon_progress_ratio_readable
are not guaranteed to match the underlying counters whenever those counters change outside the supplier/Amazon callbacks (especially in resume safety/clamp code).

Example from current state:

amazon_products_completed = 53, amazon_products_needing_analysis = 64
but amazon_progress_ratio_readable = "49/64".
Root cause:

_refresh_human_progress_fields() is called:
once at resume entry (via _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files),
on each supplier tick,
on each Amazon tick.
But some code changes the counters without calling it afterwards (e.g. Amazon resume clamp sets amazon_products_completed before any Amazon tick runs).
If the session stops or you inspect state between future ticks, you see stale ratios.
5. What to fix (conceptually), staying surgical
You asked me not to edit code now, so this is just the fix plan.

5.1 Global totals (already fixed in code, just to be clear)
We already implemented the minimal solution:

Mirror len(all_products) into state["total_products"] inside _save_products_to_cache after a successful cache save.
Mirror len(self.linking_map) into state["successful_products"] inside _save_linking_map after a successful linking map save.
This uses the same counts that appear in your log lines.

It does not touch resume pointers or frozen denominators.

It updates these metrics effectively every product in your current config.

No further change is required here to align state/dashboard with the latest counts, apart from the minor timing window.

5.2 Readable ratios (needed to stop the “stuck” strings)
To make:

supplier_progress_ratio_readable
amazon_progress_ratio_readable
always reflect the actual counters in system_progression:

Keep _refresh_human_progress_fields() as the single writer of those string fields.
Call it in all code paths that change counters, not just in the callbacks.
The most important additions, based on the latest log and code:

At the end of the Amazon resume safety logic (where we clamp amazon_products_completed):

# After setting sp["amazon_products_completed"] and computing prod_offset
self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
Similarly, if there are any other places where:

sp["supplier_products_completed"] = ...
sp["amazon_products_completed"]   = ...
are mutated directly (without going via commit_supplier_progress / commit_amazon_progress), also follow them with _refresh_human_progress_fields().

This is:

Minimal: we’re not recalculating anything, just mirroring existing counters.
Safe: _refresh_human_progress_fields() only updates display fields; it does not change resume pointers, denominators, or queues.
Exactly what you asked for: the ratio fields become live mirrors of the same counters that drive the rest of the workflow.

    if linking_map_path.exists():

        self.hash_optimizer.build_indexes(self.linking_map)

            f" HASH INDEXES REBUILT: Updated indexes for {len(self.linking_map)} entries"

                state["successful_products"] = len(self.linking_map)

        except Exception:
            ...
This is global, not category‑specific. When the resumed category’s Amazon work appends entries to self.linking_map, the next _save_linking_map call sees the larger len(self.linking_map) and writes that number directly into state["successful_products"].

Why you saw 5669 in the state when the file/log showed 5670

At the time we inspected the state:

successful_products = 5669
But the log shows we then had a save to 5670, and later saves beyond that (5671, 5689, 5692,...).

State is only updated when _save_linking_map calls save_state(...). If you open the state file between two such saves, you’ll see the prior value. That’s why you saw 5669 while the last log line already had 5670 — you were looking between the mirror writes.

There is no path where:

the linking map grew,
_save_linking_map ran,
and successful_products stayed at an older number.
The mirror code runs in the same success block as the “Successfully saved linking map with N entries...” log you pasted.

So for the resumed category:

New matches from christmas-wreath-garlands-florals are appended into self.linking_map.
That increases len(self.linking_map).
On the next _save_linking_map call, that global length is written into successful_products.
Those products are absolutely counted in the global successful total; they are not excluded just because this is a resume category.
3. Why ratios like amazon_progress_ratio_readable can look wrong
You pointed at:

"amazon_progress_ratio_readable": "337/401"
during the resume at category 108, and later values like "49/64" vs counters 53/64.

Again, three sources:

Counters in state (system_progression) for the current category:

amazon_products_needing_analysis = 64
amazon_products_completed        = 53
Readable ratio fields in state:

amazon_progress_ratio_readable   = "49/64"
Code that computes the ratio:

_refresh_human_progress_fields() in tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py:

amazon_total     = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
amazon_completed = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)
...
amazon_ratio = f"{amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}"
sp["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"] = amazon_ratio
root_state["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"] = amazon_ratio
Root cause of mismatch:

amazon_products_completed is updated in several places:

via state manager commits during normal Amazon processing,

and in the Amazon resume safety block (clamping/resetting), e.g.:

if total == 0:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset >= total:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = 0
elif saved_amazon_offset != clamped_amazon_offset:
    sp["amazon_products_completed"] = clamped_amazon_offset
...
_refresh_human_progress_fields() is only called:

once at resume entry,
on each supplier tick,
on each Amazon tick.
So if:

the resume safety code adjusts amazon_products_completed, and
no Amazon tick runs afterwards (or you inspect state between ticks),
then:

the numeric counters update,
but the pretty string amazon_progress_ratio_readable stays at the last value when _refresh_human_progress_fields() was called (e.g. 337/401).
This is why the resume category’s ratio can appear “stuck”, even though:

the counters are moving (prod_idx in the log, amazon_products_completed in system_progression),
and global successful_products reflects the actual linking map size.
Important: those readable ratios are display only – they are never used to decide what gets processed or skipped. Processing uses:

the Amazon queue,
the offset prod_offset computed in the resume logic,
and the linking map/cache.
4. Summary, focusing only on the resume category
For category 108 (christmas-wreath-garlands-florals):

The run you pointed to resumes in Amazon phase at prod_idx=337/401 and moves forward.
New Amazon matches from that category are appended to the global linking map.
Each time _save_linking_map runs (on a batch boundary or end of run), the global linking map size (e.g. 5667 → 5668 → 5669 → 5670 → …) is:
logged in “Successfully saved linking map with N entries…”,
written into state["successful_products"] via the mirror we added,
and later used by the dashboard to show “Total Processed”.
There is no special case that excludes resume categories from successful_products. If the link is in linking_map.json, it is counted.

The mismatches you’re seeing now are:

tiny timing gaps between file/log vs state snapshots for successful_products (one save apart), and
stale display‑only ratios (amazon_progress_ratio_readable) that are not refreshed after certain counter adjustments (like resume safety clamps).
If you want, the next precise step is:

add a single _refresh_human_progress_fields() call at the end of the Amazon resume safety block, so the ratio fields always track the counters even right after a resume, without touching any resume logic or totals.

I can see the concern is that, after the fixes we merged, the resume category no longer writes any entries into the linking map and the “linking map saved” log lines at run startup (like in the 04-16 log) disappeared. I also understand that the latest run log (2025‑12‑08_072316) and state/counter outputs no longer show those startup linking-map saves. I dug through the code and logs:

The only places we ever append entries to self.linking_map are inside the supplier loop (when self.hash_optimizer.add_entry(entry) returns True) and the Amazon processing path. When the run restarts on category 108, it skips to Amazon phase right away; those existing categories had their linking-map entries created before the interruption. After resumption, the Amazon phase loop should still append new entries for the remaining products, but the state/logs show no “linking map save” occurring during the single-category resume.

The log you pointed to at 08‑07 vs 08‑04 demonstrates that when the run started fresh (08‑04), it immediately saved the linking map (entries 5667 → 5668 → 5669). In the later run (08‑08), the linking map saves happen only later, after the resumed category’s Amazon work processes a few products, but there’s no “startup” save because _save_linking_map is now only triggered by the periodic commit inside _run_amazon_phase_from_resume (every linking_map_batch_size products) and at the very end. During a resume that immediately jumps into the Amazon phase and processes just a handful of products before you stop, it’s possible that the periodic batch threshold isn’t reached, so no linking-map save occurs before you inspect the log. That’s why the start-of-run log lines vanish—they now appear only when enough Amazon products are processed to trigger _save_linking_map.

As for “linking map entries from the resume category aren’t counted,” the logs for 08‑04 and 08‑08 both show the Sentinel: linking map size verified (N-1 -> N) messages. Each time _save_linking_map runs, the sentinel verifies the increased length, so any new products the resume category produces are saved and counted—just within the periodic batch schedule. If you stop before the batch rebuild runs, you won’t see the new value reflected in successful_products, but once the next _save_linking_map happens (either from hitting the batch size or at the end of the run), the new linking-map entries are there and mirrored into the state.

So the “missing” behavior is really due to the fact that the resume run jumps straight into the Amazon phase and, unless it processes enough products to hit the periodic linking-map batch size, _save_linking_map doesn’t execute until later. The fix would be to ensure that whenever we finish an Amazon batch—especially during resume—we flush the linking map regardless of batch size, so resumed categories persist their entries immediately. That is a surgical change: add one _save_linking_map(self.supplier_name) call at the end of the resume-phase processing loop (after we process the Amazon worklist) whenever there were new Amazon matches, so the linking map and successful_products reflect them even in short resume runs.

Would you like me to generate the precise diff for that (mirror in-hit) change, ensuring the linking map is saved “at least once” after the resume category finishes its pending Amazon items?

Findings

Global totals still mirrored – the cache-log-state triplet for total_products still matches the values you keep referencing (cache file & “ATOMICALLY saved … 6884” log vs. total_products=6884). The linking-map mirror also still runs inside _save_linking_map (len(self.linking_map) → state["successful_products"]) so the line you quoted from the 2025‑12‑08 04:22… / 04:25… log is the same code path that writes successful_products. The only visible difference between the 08‑04 log and the 08‑08 log is that in the resumed run we no longer emit linking-map saves immediately at start because _save_linking_map is only invoked when the periodic batching condition (overall_product_index % linking_map_batch_size == 0) is satisfied or during the final completion phase (see lines 3022‑3054). On the first full run (04‑16) the newly found entries bumped the counter early enough to hit that if block at startup; on the resume run (08‑07/08) the job jumps straight into Amazon with few new entries, so the batch threshold or final-flush hasn’t fired yet, hence no “Successfully saved linking map …” at the exact start. The underlying function is still there.

“Linking map entries from the resume category aren’t counted” – they are counted, but only after _save_linking_map executes. The sentinel log shows the count increasing (5666→5667→…→5670). Because the resume run processed very few Amazon products before you checked, the periodic save hadn’t triggered yet, so the most recent successful_products in the state (5669) lagged by one entry behind the file/log (5670). That lag disappears as soon as the next save runs.

Critical bug: when the run resumes in Amazon and you stop it before hitting the linking-map batch/normal completion (which happens quickly in a short resume), none of the new entries produced by the resumed category ever get flushed to disk or mirrored into successful_products. That’s why the resume category “adds nothing” to the linking map from the state/dash perspective even though Sentinel still sees the global count advance once the save eventually fires.

Ratio fields remain stale for resumed category because _refresh_human_progress_fields() is not called after resume-safety clamping (lines ~8016‑8047). The log shows the resume pointer stepping through 337→339→340/401, but amazon_progress_ratio_readable stayed at “49/64” because the display-only fields weren’t refreshed after those counter updates.

Root causes

_save_linking_map only runs:

every linking_map_batch_size products processed (lines 3022‑3028)
at the very end (_save_linking_map called after complete_processing)
→ Resumed runs that process fewer products never reach either checkpoint, so new linking-map entries (and therefore successful_products) “stick” in memory and never hit disk/state/log.
_refresh_human_progress_fields() isn’t invoked immediately after the resume safety logic clamps amazon_products_completed (lines 8016‑8047). Those adjustments mutate the counters, but the displayable ratios remain at the last time _refresh_human_progress_fields() executed (e.g. “49/64” even though amazon_products_completed = 53), leading to “stuck” progress strings for the resume category.

Suggested fixes (surgical, low blast radius)

Force a linking-map flush whenever the resume category processes something, even if it’s only one product:

After the Amazon resume loop finishes (or after each batch_products iteration), check if batch_products produced any new linking-map entries (e.g. via self.linking_map length or a flag). If so, call _save_linking_map(self.supplier_name) immediately instead of waiting for linking_map_batch_size or final completion.
This ensures that resumed categories write their new entries to disk and update successful_products and the dashboard even within short sessions.
The diff would insert a single _save_linking_map call near the end of the Amazon processing loop (around lines 2963‑3050), guarded by if new_entries_added. It stops short of altering any other resume logic.
Refresh the ratio fields whenever the resume logic mutates counters:

Immediately after the section that clamps/resets amazon_products_completed (around lines 8016‑8047), add:

self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
This keeps amazon_progress_ratio_readable in sync with the actual counters right after resumption, so you no longer see stale “49/64” values.

No other logic needs to change—only the display fields are updated.

If you want me to produce those exact diffs, I can do that next.

State Review

The per-product log lines you referenced for startup linking-map saves now occur only when _save_linking_map actually executes (periodic batch or final save), so a short resume run that processes only a few Amazon items may not produce those lines until the first flush happens. Key Issues & Fixes Going Forward

Linking-map entries from the resume category are not always persisted

Evidence: log run_custom_poundwholesale_20251208_034051.log shows no “Successfully saved linking map…” lines right at the resume start—only later, once a batch flush occurs. Sentinel logs confirm the linking-map length only grows when _save_linking_map runs, meaning the resumed Amazon work adds entries but they stay in memory until index-length % linking_map_batch_size == 0 or until final completion.
Root cause: _save_linking_map is only invoked inside the periodic block (lines 3022–3028) or the final completion phase. During a resume run that processes only a handful of products, the batch threshold is never hit, so no save (and hence no state mirror) happens for those entries.
Fix: After processing each Amazon batch during resume (i.e., inside the Amazon loop once batch_products is consumed), call _save_linking_map(self.supplier_name) if any new linking-map entries were added. This ensures resumed categories flush their new matches even in a short session, keeping the logs, file, and successful_products in sync.
amazon_progress_ratio_readable lags the actual counters

Evidence: processing_state still reports amazon_progress_ratio_readable = "49/64" shortly after resume, even though system_progression["amazon_products_completed"] = 53 (and the log resume pointer is 337/401). The ratio field is only updated when _refresh_human_progress_fields() runs, which currently happens only in the resume helper and in supplier/Amazon callbacks.
Root cause: The resume safety logic that clamps/reset amazon_products_completed (lines ~8016–8047) updates the counter but does not call _refresh_human_progress_fields(), so the display string keeps showing the old value until the next supplier/Amazon tick happens.
Fix: Add self._refresh_human_progress_fields() immediately after the block that mutates amazon_products_completed in the resume safety logic (after prod_offset is determined). That way, the ratio fields are always refreshed to match the counters even before any further Amazon items are processed.
No other missing change

All planned modifications from earlier in this thread are still implemented: the cache save now mirrors len(all_products) into total_products, the linking-map save mirrors len(self.linking_map) into successful_products, the human-readable ratios are refreshed in the two callbacks plus the helper, and those helper calls remain in the resume logic. There is no evidence the files reverted to an older version; every change we’ve discussed remains present.

New helper _refresh_human_progress_fields added

New method inside PassiveExtractionWorkflow (just after _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files):

def _refresh_human_progress_fields(self) -> None:
    """
    Mirror the current numeric counters in system_progression into the
    human-readable ratio fields in both system_progression and at the
    top level of the processing state.
    """
    try:
        root_state = self.state_manager.state_data
        sp = root_state.get("system_progression", {}) or {}

        cat_idx    = int(sp.get("current_category_index") or sp.get("persistent_category_index") or 1)
        total_cats = int(sp.get("total_categories", 0) or 0)
        if total_cats < 0:
            total_cats = 0

        supplier_total     = int(sp.get("supplier_products_needing_extraction", 0) or 0)
        supplier_completed = int(sp.get("supplier_products_completed", 0) or 0)
        amazon_total       = int(sp.get("amazon_products_needing_analysis", 0) or 0)
        amazon_completed   = int(sp.get("amazon_products_completed", 0) or 0)

        # Clamp for display only
        if supplier_total > 0 and supplier_completed > supplier_total:
            supplier_completed = supplier_total
        if amazon_total > 0 and amazon_completed > amazon_total:
            amazon_completed = amazon_total

        cat_ratio      = f"{cat_idx}/{total_cats}" if total_cats > 0 else f"{cat_idx}/0"
        supplier_ratio = f"{supplier_completed}/{supplier_total}"
        amazon_ratio   = f"{amazon_completed}/{amazon_total}"

        # Write into system_progression
        sp["category_progress"]                = cat_ratio
        sp["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"] = supplier_ratio
        sp["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"]   = amazon_ratio

        # Mirror to top level of state
        root_state["category_progress"]                = cat_ratio
        root_state["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"] = supplier_ratio
        root_state["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"]   = amazon_ratio
    except Exception:
        # Display-only; never break workflow
        return
Linking map save: mirroring successful_products into state

In _save_linking_map(self, supplier_name: str):

The “ATOMIC SAVE” log is now inside the try block (indentation change only):

#  ATOMIC SAVE: Use Windows Save Guardian with alt-temp strategy
self.log.info(
    f" ATOMIC SAVE: Attempting to save {len(self.linking_map)} entries using atomic strategy..."
)
After verifying the file exists and rebuilding hash indexes, a new block mirrors the linking-map length into the state:

# NEW: Mirror global successful_products into state using the same count.
# This keeps dashboard/state totals aligned with the linking map file.
try:
    if hasattr(self, "state_manager") and self.state_manager is not None:
        state = self.state_manager.state_data
        state["successful_products"] = len(self.linking_map)
        # Preserve interruption state; do not trigger startup recompute.
        self.state_manager.save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True)
except Exception as e:
    self.log.warning(f" METRICS MIRROR FAILED (successful_products): {e}")
The sentinel shrinkage call was only reflowed (no behavioral change):

self.sentinel_monitor.check_linking_map_shrinkage(current_size, previous_size)
# became
self.sentinel_monitor.check_linking_map_shrinkage(
    current_size, previous_size
)

New mirror block after successful cache save:

# NEW: Mirror global supplier total into state for dashboards/metrics.

try:
    if hasattr(self, "state_manager") and self.state_manager is not None:
        state = self.state_manager.state_data
        state["total_products"] = len(all_products)
        # Preserve interruption state so no startup recalculation is triggered.
        self.state_manager.save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True)
except Exception as e:
    self.log.warning(f" METRICS MIRROR FAILED (total_products): {e}")
The stable_key call for new products was expanded into a multiline call (same logic):

k_new = stable_key(
    normalize_url(product.get("url")),
    self._valid_ean_for_key(product.get("ean")),
)

        f" AMAZON ANALYSIS: Processing queue index {product_index}/{authoritative_total}"
    )

# system_progression; keep the human-readable ratios in sync.
