root_state["category_progress"]
root_state["supplier_progress_ratio_readable"]
root_state["amazon_progress_ratio_readable"]
Replaced it with:

self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
Effect

Behavior is the same or better: instead of calculating ratios in one place, we centralize the logic in a helper that:
Reads system_progression counters.
Clamps completed ≤ total for display.
Writes ratios into both system_progression and root keys.
Assessment

Keep.
It simplifies and de‑duplicates ratio handling.
Applies the same logic everywhere _refresh_human_progress_fields() is called.
Purely display; does not affect resume or totals.
2. New helper _refresh_human_progress_fields
What it does

Reads system_progression counters and current_category_index/total_categories.

Builds:

category_progress
supplier_progress_ratio_readable
amazon_progress_ratio_readable
Writes them into:

system_progression[...]
root state_data[...] convenience fields.
Effect

Gives a single, consistent source of truth for the “pretty” progress strings.
Allows you to call it from different contexts (resume helper, callbacks) without duplicating code.
Assessment

Keep.
It’s a clean abstraction for display‑only fields.
Overhead is tiny (a few ints and string formats).
It’s the right place to fix “stale ratio” problems by calling it after any counter changes.
3. _save_linking_map: mirror successful_products into state
What changed

After a successful linking‑map atomic save and hash index rebuild:

if hasattr(self, "state_manager"):
    state = self.state_manager.state_data
    state["successful_products"] = len(self.linking_map)
    self.state_manager.save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True)
Effect

Every time the linking map file is successfully saved, successful_products in the state is updated to the same value as:
len(self.linking_map)
“Successfully saved linking map with N entries …” log.
Dashboard’s “Total Processed” now follows the real linking‑map size instead of staying stuck at the startup snapshot.
Assessment

Keep.
This is exactly the fix for your “dashboard total processed is wrong” complaint.
It’s minimal: reuses the count already computed and logged.
Cost is an extra state write per linking‑map save (which already happens relatively infrequently, controlled by linking_map_batch_size).
4. _save_products_to_cache: mirror total_products into state
What changed

After a successful cache atomic save and in‑memory cache sync:

if hasattr(self, "state_manager"):
    state = self.state_manager.state_data
    state["total_products"] = len(all_products)
    self.state_manager.save_state(preserve_interruption_state=True)
Effect

total_products in the state now matches:
len(all_products) (cache length),
“ATOMICALLY saved 6884 products … to cache” log,
len(angelwholesale-co-uk_products_cache.json).
Assessment

Keep.
Fixes “Total Extracted” in the dashboard; before this, it was frozen at the startup value.
Uses existing counts; no extra scanning.
One extra state write per cache save; in your config you’re already saving every product, so overhead is minor relative to the scraping work.
5. _create_product_progress_callback: call _refresh_human_progress_fields on every tick
What changed

In supplier_extraction branch, after periodic cache save:

self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
In amazon_analysis branch, after queue index log:

self._refresh_human_progress_fields()
Effect

Whenever supplier or Amazon progress moves, the readable ratio fields are updated to reflect the current counters.
This is what makes supplier_progress_ratio_readable track “14/15”, “380/380”, etc., while the run is active.
Assessment

Keep, but this is purely cosmetic.
It doesn’t change workflow, only how often ratios are refreshed.
It adds a tiny bit of CPU each tick, but that’s negligible compared to network I/O.
6. FixedEnhancedStateManager.perform_startup_analysis: set successful_products from linking_map_count
What changed

After computing linking_map_count from disk:

linking_map_count = file_grounded_data.get("linking_map_count", 0)

# NEW: Initialize successful_products + display metrics from linking map
self.state_data["successful_products"] = linking_map_count
ud = self.state_data.setdefault("user_display_metrics", {})
ud["successful_products"] = linking_map_count
Effect

At startup, before any new processing, successful_products is seeded from the linking map already on disk.
That gets overwritten later by the mirrors in _save_linking_map, but it ensures the initial value is correct before the first save happens.
Assessment

Harmless/optional.
It aligns the initial successful_products with the pre‑existing linking map, which is coherent.
Once the run starts, the dynamic mirror in _save_linking_map keeps it up to date; this startup seed is mainly to avoid a “0” or stale value at the very beginning.
You could remove this and rely solely on the runtime mirror; behaviour after the first _save_linking_map would be the same.
Which changes are not responsible for the resume‑category linking map issue?
Important:

None of these patches change when _save_linking_map is called.
That is controlled by the existing logic around linking_map_batch_size and the final save at the end of run().
The resume‑category issue you’re seeing (short resume run, no disk write yet, or delayed “Successfully saved linking map…” logs) is due to that batching logic, not the new mirrors.
If we want the resume category’s new entries to always be persisted, the change we need is separate:

Ensure _save_linking_map(self.supplier_name) runs at least once after the resume category’s Amazon work completes, even if we didn’t hit the periodic batch threshold.
That is a new fix, and I haven’t applied it yet. The six changes above are the only ones currently in the code.

can i run the system for now with no issues (despite indexes fucing up slightly ?


Yes, you can run it now, with two caveats:

Core workflow is fine. Scraping, Amazon analysis, financials, and resume logic are still driven by the cache + linking map + processing_state counters, not by the pretty ratio fields. The “fucked up” ratios (amazon_progress_ratio_readable, etc.) are display‑only.

Resume + short runs: After a resume, new Amazon matches from the resume category are only flushed to disk when _save_linking_map runs (periodic batch or end‑of‑run). If you stop very soon after resuming—before the first linking‑map save—you can end up with some new matches still only in memory. If you let it run until you see at least one new “Successfully saved linking map with N entries …” in the log after resuming, those entries are safely persisted and counted.

So for normal, longer runs: you’re safe to continue. Just avoid stopping immediately after a resume if you want to guarantee all new matches from that category are written out.

were sny of the 6 changes you lsited in you above response, impeleted during this chat ? list the ones that were not


Short answer: from Git’s point of view, all six changes I listed are part of the current uncommitted edits, and none of them were present in the last committed version. I identified them by comparing your working copy to the last commit, not by rereading the chat.

How I identified them

I ran git diff --name-only to see which code files changed: only
tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py and utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py.
Then I ran git diff -- tools/passive_extraction_workflow_latest.py and
git diff -- utils/fixed_enhanced_state_manager.py and read the full hunks. Every behavior change I described comes directly from that diff output.
That means: everything in those diffs (the mirrors, _refresh_human_progress_fields, the startup seeding of successful_products) did not exist in the last commit and was added/modified afterwards. Some of them were created via my apply_patch calls in this chat; if you haven’t edited those files manually outside this conversation, then they were all introduced during this chat.
Which of the 6 changes were not pre‑existing

Relative to the last committed version, all six changes I listed are new:

Replacing inline ratio writes in _re_evaluate_resume_phase_from_files with a call to _refresh_human_progress_fields.
Adding the _refresh_human_progress_fields helper itself.
Mirroring successful_products = len(self.linking_map) in _save_linking_map.
Mirroring total_products = len(all_products) in _save_products_to_cache.
Calling _refresh_human_progress_fields() from the supplier and Amazon progress callbacks.
Seeding self.state_data["successful_products"] and user_display_metrics["successful_products"] from linking_map_count in perform_startup_analysis in FixedEnhancedStateManager.
There are no other code changes in those two files beyond these hunks; everything else in the diff is formatting or logging reflow.

So: if you roll back to the last commit, all six go away; if you keep the current working copy, all six are in effect.
